# Volume_4_in_the_Academic_Press_TERRESTRIAL_ECOLOGY_SERIES

**Original File:** `Volume_4_in_the_Academic_Press_TERRESTRIAL_ECOLOGY_SERIES.pdf`
**Total Pages:** 341
**Processed on:** 2026-03-03T03:46:15.137914

根据您提供的文档索引，以下是对其中涉及的几个关键概念和模型的简要解释：

1. **森林生态生理学**：这是研究植物与周围环境之间相互作用的科学。文档涵盖了树木生长、养分循环、水分运动以及光合作用等主题。

2. **树木生长模型**：这些模型用于预测单个树木或整个林分的生长过程。文档中提到了以下类型：
   - 经验模型（如CanSPBL和Ponderosa Pine模型）
   - 混合模型（如PROMOD-NITGRO混合模型）
   - 基于过程的模型（PBM，例如BIOMASS、CABALA、FOREST-BGC、BIOME-BGC、ITE Edinburgh模型）

3. **基于过程模型（PBM）**：这些模型考虑了植物生理学、生态学和遗传学的原理来模拟树木生长。文档中提到的PBM包括：
   - BIOMASS
   - CABALA
   - CenW
   - DIFFEQ
   - FOREST-BGC 和 BIOME-BGC
   - ITE Edinburgh 模型
   - PROMOD
   - PROMOD-NITGRO 混合模型

4. **养分循环**：这是涉及植物、土壤和大气之间营养物质交换的过程。文档讨论了以下内容：
   - 生物地球化学循环（如氮的固定）
   - 地球化学循环（如养分的释放和重新分配）

5. **水分运动**：这包括水分在植物体内以及从土壤到大气中的流动。文档涉及以下方面：
   - 水势
   - 蒸腾作用
   - 水分利用效率

6. **遥感技术**：这是一种收集关于地球表面信息的方法，通常用于监测森林、土地覆盖和气候变化。

7. **地理信息系统（GIS）**：这是存储、分析和展示空间数据的技术。文档中提到了使用GIS来辅助树木生长模型的应用。

8. **树木结构动态**：这是研究树木群体结构和生长过程的学科。文档涉及以下内容：
   - 树木冠层面积指数
   - 根系统
   - 密度（疏伐）

9. **气象因素**：这些影响植物生长的环境条件，包括温度、湿度、太阳辐射和风速。

10. **木材产品**：这是从树木中获得的材料，用于建筑、家具和其他用途。

文档涵盖了上述主题的多个方面，提供了对森林生态生理学领域深入研究的概述。

Acknowledgements
I
n the course of preparing this book, a number of people read and commented on the drafts of
various chapters; some did so in detail, providing us with invaluable comments, queries and
suggestions. Most of these people are busy professional scientists and university teachers, and
their time is valuable. We therefore greatly appreciate the gift of some of it. In particular, we thank Ram
Oren (Duke University, NC), Belinda Medlyn (Macquarie University, NSW), Philip Smethurst
(CSIRO, Hobart, Tasmania), Torgny Na¨shlom (Swedish University of Agricultural Sciences, Umea¨,
Sweden), Auro Almeida (CSIRO, Hobart, Tasmania) and Richard Waring (Oregon State University
Corvallis, OR) who all read and provided detailed comment on the draft of various chapters. As well as
reading and providing comment on chapter drafts, Dick Waring also drew our attention to relevant
papers and provided copies of many that we had difficulty in obtaining. Auro Almeida provided
comment on drafts and made available data from his work in Brazil, as well as copies of papers we
could not access over the world wide Web. He spent significant amounts of time with PJS discussing his
work and its implications. Luke Esprey provided comment on a chapter and some insights into forestry
in South Africa. Mike Ryan checked our statements about some aspects of respiration to ensure that
they were consistent with conventional wisdom. Chris Beadle, Dale Worledge and Tony O’Grady
(CSIRO, Tasmania) also made available various climatic and soil temperature data, and Vanessa
Haverd (CSIRO, Canberra) the data for one set of canopy profiles shown in Figure 2.11. We acknowl-
edge permission from Cambridge University Press to reproduce Figure 9.3 from Jones (1992) ‘Plants
and Microclimate’ as our Figure 2.8, and from Oxford University Press to use Figures 3 and 4 from
Cˇ erma´k et al. (2007) (Tree Physiology 27, pp. 181–198) as our Figure 7.7. We would also like to thank
Elsevier and Tree Physiology for making available to us free subscriptions to their electronic resources,
supplemented by the services of Messrs Google and Wiki.
vi

--- Page 4 ---

Preface
T
his book was originally planned as a second edition of ‘Physiological Ecology of Forest
Production’, which was published by Academic Press in 1986. Encouraged by friends among
the community of tree ecologists, physiologists, and foresters who were generous in their
appreciation of the original, I approached Academic Press and we negotiated a contract. Academic
Press was, a little later, bought out by Elsevier, but they honoured the contract, and the editors
at Elsevier have been patient with our inability to meet the submission date that was originally
agreed upon.
My initial outline owed much to the earlier book, but I was painfully aware of how much progress
had been made in our field in the quarter century that had passed since that was written. This was
clearly not going to be a revision, but rather an almost completely new book. It was also clear to me that
I needed help: I needed a coauthor who was familiar with the field of physiological ecology and
modelling, with particular reference to trees and forests, who had the skills necessary to make up for my
shortcomings, and who was interested and had the time to give to the business of writing a technical
book. Fortunately, Peter Sands, who had recently retired from the CSIRO (Commonwealth Scientific
and Industrial Research Organisation, Australia), met all those criteria. We had done some work
together and knew each other well, and he responded positively and enthusiastically to my invitation to
join me in the task.
Peter had worked with various types of models relating to forestry for many years and had been
involved with a number of projects where he provided the mathematical skills needed by biologists,
ecologists, and foresters. He had rewritten the software for the 3-PG model that I had produced with
my great friend and colleague Dick Waring from Oregon State University, and that flexible and
powerful software has been a key factor in the success of that model, which is mentioned frequently
and discussed in detail in this book. The fact that Peter lives in Tasmania and I live in the Blue
Mountains in New South Wales was no obstacle to our collaboration: we had broadband internet
connections and that is all you need nowadays. In extremis we could resort to the telephone, but
seldom needed to. It has been a real pleasure and a stimulating experience to work with Peter; the book
we have produced is the result of a completely equal partnership and equal, mutual, effort. There have
been few significant disagreements and those were resolved without stress or problems.
In the preface to the 1987 book, I said that my objective was to demonstrate how a quantitative
approach to whole-plant physiology, coupled with some knowledge of the physics of plant environ-
ments, allows us to analyze the way trees grow, and the way environmental factors and management
actions affect growth. That objective has been extended, in this book, to include consideration of the
models that have been developed to describe many of the processes that underlie and govern the way
trees grow. It is these models that provide the tools with which knowledge and understanding can be
translated into the quantitative predictions needed by managers and decision-makers concerned with
forest productivity, hydrology, and water balance or carbon sequestration.
Our target audience is teachers and graduate students in forestry, ecology, and ecophysiology.
Although our (limited) knowledge of undergraduate courses around the world in these and associated
fields suggests that the text is probably not pitched at a level where it will be useful to undergraduates,
there is no reason why some undergraduate courses should not be able to make use of it. We also
hope that the book will be useful to established scientists who may find that the coverage of areas
outside their specialties is of value, and to forest managers who want to understand their forests at a
deeper level. The book is wide-ranging and, for biologists and foresters unaccustomed to think in
vii

--- Page 5 ---

mathematical terms, the treatment of various processes may sometimes seem dense. However, we hope
you will persevere. The properties and behaviour of many of the equations can be explored in the
spreadsheets and other material on the PHYSECOLWeb site Elsevier established specifically to accompany
this book, and where the code for the 3-PG model can also be found and downloaded without charge.
We have had in mind, throughout the writing of this book, the idea that the various (first order)
processes that contribute to, and determine, the way trees respond to their environment, and grow,
should be considered in relation to the process of growth (biomass accumulation) as a whole.
The individual biophysical processes are usually studied, as far as possible, in isolation, but the growth
of a tree is a complex process of interactions and feedbacks, so we need to consider the system as whole.
The framework within which we can do that is a process-based model that constitutes an attempt to
describe growth in quantitative, mathematical terms. So, all the chapters dealing with individual
processes are set within such a framework, and in the second last chapter, we deal in detail with
the 3-PG model.
As always, with exercises such as this, a number of people have contributed their time and expertise
to help us avoid factual mistakes and to offer suggestions about matters where our treatment was
inadequate. In most cases, these people are busy professional scientists for whom it is not a trivial
matter to commit valuable time to reading and commenting on draft chapters of someone’s new book.
We are very grateful to those who did this; they helped considerably to improve the product but bear no
responsibility for our mistakes and shortcomings. These friends and colleagues are named in the
Acknowledgements, but here I want to thank, particularly, Dick Waring, who not only offered
comment, advice, and encouragement, but also did his best to make sure that I kept abreast of the
literature. He drew my attention to numerous papers I might otherwise have missed and frequently
obtained and sent to me pdf copies of papers that I could not otherwise get access to. Since I do not
have access to a library, this was assistance of immense value.
It is customary, at this point in the preparation of a book, for the author to thank his family for
tolerating long periods during which he was antisocial, not available for family activities, and generally
focussed on a project in which they had no part. However, things are a bit different in my case. Being
retired, the writing project has provided me with an activity that kept my brain active and kept me in
touch with my friends in the scientific community, which has been an important part of my life for 40
years. My children have long since left home and my wife, Diana, is generally quite happy to see me
pecking away on the computer (she has her own, so there is no conflict on that front), provided I do not
use it as an excuse to escape domestic and gardening duties. She is always supportive and, although she
contributed little to this book, without her it would not have been written.
Joe Landsberg
‘Withycombe’, Mt Wilson NSW
April 2010
I fully endorse Joe’s comments about our work on this book! It has indeed been a pleasure and
inspirational. Above all, it was a real joy to have the opportunity to immerse myself in science, simply
for its own sake, free of the need to justify what I was doing to some organisation, and free of
organisational politics. Thanks, Joe, for this opportunity! It has also been a pleasure to be on this
journey with Joe and to build on our working relationship through the sharing of anecdotes in our
lives, the doings of our families, and the varied climates of our physical environments. There has been
far more to writing this book than the science you are going to find herein!
I came to plant physiology via a ‘sea change’ into mathematical modelling of natural systems in
several divisions of the CSIRO. This was after 10 years as a theoretical physicist developing and applying
techniques to analyze—and design—optical image forming systems and to model the eyes of various
animals. In the process, I became a bit of a Jack of All Trades—but Master of None—in various areas.
My first project in that new phase of my career was a collaboration with Dick Hughes developing a
viii
Preface

--- Page 6 ---

model of the population dynamics of that pesky little irritant of the Australian bush: the bush fly
(Hughes and Sands 1979). This was my introduction to the nature of biological data and how radically
it differs from the data captured by physicists and engineers. It was also my first taste of working
alongside a biologist, and I am grateful to Dick for making that, too, such a rewarding and pleasurable
experience. At the same time, my team leader in CSIRO was Peter Benyon, a modeller who hailed from
weapons research, who taught me the importance of looking at the whole system—not just the bits that
were the current flavour of the month. Peter is no longer with us, but his respect for other disciplines
and rigour remain a part of my way of thinking.
During the writing of this book, I suffered detached retinas in both of my eyes, one of which is still
by no means satisfactory. This unfortunate excursion into the practical aspects of my early academic
interests led to the need for a significant extension of the contract for delivering this book. For this we
are grateful to our editors at Elsevier for their understanding and flexibility.
Finally, I would like to express my thanks to my family, especially to my wife Krista, for her support
and patience with my, at times, annoyingly one-pointed approach to life, and to my grandchildren who
have had a rather elusive Old Grump for a while. However, I still had to take my share of household
responsibilities!
Peter Sands
39 Oakleigh Av.
Taroona, Australia 7053
For the PHYSECOL Web site, go to http://www.elsevierdirect.com/9780123744609

Table of Symbols
W
e define here the symbols with their units, and common subscripts, as used in this book. All
symbols are defined in the text where they are first used, with their units given, and are
sometimes redefined—as a reminder—later. We have not necessarily included in this list
symbols that occur only once in the book. We have also listed generic symbols, that is, with subscripts
omitted, and in separate lists biophysical constants with their standard values, and subscripts and their
meaning.
The units given are usually the base units, whereas when values are given prefixes are used. For
example, the base unit of assimilation rate is mol m2 s1 but values are typically given in
mmol m2 s1, and the base unit of water potential is Pa but values are usually given in MPa. Some
quantities have different units depending on their context. A common example is conductance: if it is a
molar flux its units are mol m2 s1, whereas if it is a mass flux its units are (usually) m s1.
While writing the book we tried to be consistent with conventional use in the literature concerned
with environmental physics and plant ecophysiology, insofar as there is any consensus among users in
this matter. However, because of the range of material covered, it has been difficult to avoid using the
same symbol or subscript to denote different variables on numerous occasions. For example, H denotes
heat flux in Chapter 2, but is also used to denote stand or canopy height, and a parameter in the
temperature response of the photosynthesis model. We have decided to accept this problem because
many symbols are widely used in particular contexts and changing them may lead to greater confusion
than does duplication!
We also rely on a certain degree of commonsense and use of context when interpreting a symbol. For
instance, A stands for an area and for an assimilation rate, and subscripts B implies bole or basal and C
canopy. Thus A is leaf assimilation rate, AB is basal area, AC is canopy assimilation rate.

1.4 The PHYSECOL Website
12
Physiological ecology is concerned with the way physiological processes are affected by, and interact
with, environmental conditions to determine the state and behaviour of an ecosystem. Our aims in this
book are to present clear descriptions of the most important of the physiological processes involved in
the growth of trees, and to describe the way these processes are affected by weather and the condition of
the plants, and so determine tree growth. The book is not about the practical business of forest
management, but we believe that it is important that those involved in that business understand tree
physiology and the way tree growth is determined by physiological processes.
The physiological processes involved in the growth of terrestrial plants are, for the most part,
common to all of them. There are some differences between the biochemical processes in the dark
reactions of photosynthesis in so-called C3 and C4 plants, but we will not be concerned with those here.
Nor are we concerned with ‘classical ecology’ – with plant associations and why they occur where they
do, or with the characteristics of complex ecosystems. But the principles we present are applicable in
any ecological situation.
Some people concerned with managing wood-producing forests tend to take the view that tree
physiology is not important to them. Dealing with forests being managed for wood production they
Physiological Ecology of Forest Production
Joe Landsberg and Peter Sands
ISSN 1936-7961, DOI: 10.1016/S1936-7961(11)04001-2
Copyright # 2011 Elsevier Inc.
All rights reserved.

--- Page 17 ---

have to make decisions about matters such as harvesting and re-planting schedules, weed control in
young stands, possibly disease control and so on. Physiological processes at the level of cells or organs
(leaves, roots, etc.) may seem to be far removed from the practical world of ‘hard hat’ forestry, and in
some senses they are, but they underlie the behaviour and responses of the system being managed, and
it is always easier to predict the behaviour of a system, and manage it, if you understand the way it
works, and the way it is likely to respond to particular events. These may be natural events – such as
drought, exceptional heat or cold, weed infestation or insect attack – or management actions such as
fertilization or thinning. And those responsible for managing large forest areas, who are concerned
with wood flow to mills over each season, with decisions about which areas are to be re-planted
and how, and with standing stocks, need to know how much wood is ‘out there’, in the area they are
responsible for, and how fast the trees are growing and are likely to grow. Quantitative models
are important tools for these managers, and good quantitative models depend on knowledge about
tree physiology. So we are not concerned with the physiology alone, but also with how it can inform
practical models of tree and stand growth.
1.1 SOME BACKGROUND ON FORESTS
Forests are among the most important terrestrial ecosystems. They are conveniently classified into
diverse ‘forest biomes’, based on the major climatic zones (tropical, temperate and boreal) and the
physiognomy of the vegetation (broad-leaved evergreen, broad-leaved deciduous and needle-leaved
evergreen conifer; Landsberg and Gower 1997). There is enormous variation in the structure of
forest stands, which range from multi-layered tropical forests, through deciduous forests, where
most of the leaves fall at the end of summer, to evergreen coniferous forests with their characteristic
clumped canopies, occurring mainly at higher latitudes. Natural forests may include a wide range of
species – species diversity in undisturbed tropical forests is among the highest of any ecosystem on
earth – or species diversity may be low, as in the boreal forests. Logging, fires and regeneration may
reduce species diversity, creating almost mono-specific even-aged stands, and of course plantations are
single species, uniform stands.
1.1.1. Goods and Services
Forests provide a number of ‘goods and services’. Natural forests are widely regarded as of great
aesthetic value: they feature in the folklore and fairy tales of many cultures; forests are beautiful,
mysterious, primeval; in some countries access to the forests to camp, find mushrooms or simply to
walk and enjoy them is a right entrenched in law. They contribute enormously to the beauty of
landscapes, and tropical forests provide a living to (sadly, remnant) native peoples in Africa, Indonesia,
Papua New Guinea and the Amazon. In some countries natural forests are protected, in others
economic pressures are bringing about their destruction. Some natural forests are managed for
wood production but much of this now comes from plantations, which are the simplest forest
ecosystem: mono-specific, even-aged, genetically uniform – although this varies – and managed as a
crop. Much of what we have to say applies most easily to plantations.
The modern concern with sustainability and renewable fuels has led to increased attention to bio-
energy plantations, usually based on fast-growing tree species, grown in very high population densities
with the objective of producing the maximum possible amount of woody biomass in the shortest
possible time. These hardly qualify as forests, but since the product is derived from young trees the
principles of forest ecophysiology apply to them. Some forests are maintained in catchments to ensure
2
Chapter 1

--- Page 18 ---

that stable supplies of clean water are maintained and, increasingly in this age of human-induced
climate change, to which CO2 is a major contributor, the value of forests as systems that absorb and
sequester CO2 is being recognised. Since most forests grow over long periods (there was a saying that
foresters need not be too concerned about the consequences of their decisions since they generally die
before those consequences become apparent), they are likely to be affected by changes to the climate in
the areas where they are growing and their rates and patterns of growth will probably be quite different
from those that have been observed over previous growth cycles. But we note that the growth cycle
(rotation time) of modern plantation forests is usually a great deal shorter than that of earlier years.
Let’s examine those ‘goods and services’ in a little more detail. It is almost impossible to assess the
economic worth of forests where their primary value is aesthetic, and we will not be concerned with
that aspect, although the physiological processes and the principles we discuss in this book apply to
forests preserved for their scenic beauty as much as to any other forest ecosystem.
1.1.2. Wood Products
Wood is the most obvious product from forests. It is of enormous international importance, being used
for making paper, in building and construction, for energy production – which is a matter of releasing
for human use the energy stored by photosynthesis – and various other purposes. The most basic sub-
division of wood-producing forests is between those managed to produce sawlogs for timber building
and associated uses, and those managed for pulpwood, the basic material used in papermaking. To
produce sawlogs, trees must be large, so stands managed for sawlog production must be allowed to
grow for longer than those where the product is to be pulpwood, for which the logs do not need to
be large. The diameter size distribution of the trees in stands to be harvested for pulpwood
is not important, and it does not even come into consideration in bio-energy plantations, but it
is important in sawlog stands, where management in terms of thinning practices is aimed at optimising
that characteristic. Valuable hardwood logs have been extracted from (natural, unmanaged)
tropical forests, probably for centuries. That industry is now tainted by illegal logging and destructive
practices – both legal and illegal – but this, again, is not a concern of this book. Nor are the destructive
logging practices in Tasmania, where mature (old-growth) natural forests are destroyed to
produce wood chips for export and processing for high-quality paper, although the authors note
here their concern about this industry.
1.1.3. Water
There is a widespread view that forests are the best vegetation cover for water catchments: they keep
run-off to minimum rates, water infiltrates the soil easily, groundwater flows are stable and forests
prevent damaging floods and landslips except under extreme conditions. Many of the world’s impor-
tant catchments are forest-covered. However, the introduction of plantations to catchments that were
previously covered in pastures, for example, will almost certainly result in reductions in the water
output from the catchments (see, for example Brown et al. 2007). There is no question that water losses
from forests are higher than from short vegetation and Calder (2007) notes that the conventional
wisdom, which assumes that forested catchments are ‘ a good thing’, may not be right for areas in, for
example Africa and India, where water is a limiting resource for human communities. Establishment of
plantations in catchments in such places may reduce the water available to the communities to a
damaging extent. In South Africa the water use by plantations – particularly eucalypt plantations – is
becoming an important issue because of their impacts on the water yield of catchments, particularly
during dry periods, when plantations reduce groundwater flows and catchment yields that are already
Introduction
3

--- Page 19 ---

low (Dye 2001, Versfeld 2004). It is therefore appropriate that we should base our consideration of tree
water use, the water relations of trees and the effects of water supply on growth, on the basic
hydrological equation, which contains terms for run-off and deep drainage and provides insights
into the interaction between forest stands, rainfall and the soil.
1.1.4. CO2 Sequestration
Overshadowing all decisions about forests (as well as many other matters affecting our lives) in
the world of the twenty-first century, is the probability of significant changes to the global climate,
caused by the anthropogenic emissions of ‘greenhouse gases’. Of these CO2 is one of the most
important. It is also the fundamental component in the process of photosynthesis, the primary
biological process that underlies all plant growth. Forests are important components of the global
carbon cycle: they cover about 40106 km2, of the earth’s land surfaces, of which about 25106 km2
are boreal and tropical forests and about 0.4106 km2 are plantations (FAO 2005, Forestry Paper 147).
All forests play an important role in CO2 absorption and carbon sequestration: CO2 is absorbed in the
process of photosynthesis and emitted as a result of respiration and decomposition of dead material.
There is international interest in whether the balance is positive, i.e. do forests absorb more CO2 than
they emit – are they net sinks or sources, and over what period? Factors such as age and stage of growth,
and interaction with nitrogen, are important. We deal with these processes in some detail.
Hundreds of experiments have shown that increasing atmospheric CO2 concentrations increase net
photosynthesis rates, but there are doubts about long-term effects, particularly on long-lived plants
such as trees, because of acclimation. Photosynthesis is considered in detail in Chapter 3. It suffices here
to note that carbon assimilation rate is proportional to the rate of diffusion of CO2 from the ambient
air to the sub-stomatal cavities, so it is strongly affected by stomatal control, which we measure as
resistance or its inverse, conductance. In broad-leaved trees, stomatal conductance generally increases
with increasing CO2, but this is not the case in conifers. Kirschbaum (2004) analysed photosynthetic
responses to elevated CO2 concentrations using the Farquhar et al. (1980) model and showed that, in
general, rates of photosynthesis increased with increasing temperature and CO2 concentrations, but are
highly dependent on species and growth conditions.
The responses of forests, in terms of growth and water use (affected by stomata) and the overall
effects of CO2 on plant productivity, are of much more than academic interest. Hyvo¨nen et al. (2007)
reviewed the effects of rising atmospheric CO2 on the growth of temperate and boreal forests, and
concluded that young forests will continue to act as C sinks, i.e. net absorbers of CO2, for the
foreseeable future. In a review of CO2 fertilisation experiments (FACE) in forests, Norby et al. (2005)
concluded that, overall, increased atmospheric concentrations resulted in increases in net primary
production of about 23%, relative to normal concentrations. The range of responses was large –
between 12% and 35% at the Duke FACE site alone – and dependent on nitrogen availability, which
affects carbon uptake through the influence of nitrogen on the leaf area of forests (Oren et al. 2001).
McCarthy et al. (2006) showed, at the Duke site, that above-ground net primary production responded
to CO2 only through its enhancement of leaf area duration – the integral of leaf area index (LAI) in
terms of time.
If the amount of CO2 in the atmosphere continues to increase for the foreseeable future, two
important questions arise. The first is: will increased rates of uptake by plant communities remove
significant amounts of that CO2 from the atmosphere, thereby helping to mitigate its effects on the
global climate? The second, which is related to the first, is: can we expect increased forest productivity,
for relatively long periods, as a result of the increased atmospheric CO2?
4
Chapter 1

--- Page 20 ---

1.2 MODELS AND PHYSIOLOGY
We have already asserted that it is easier to predict the behaviour of a system, and manage it, if you
understand the way it works, and the way it is likely to respond to particular events. Those who manage
forests, or make decisions about their management, need to be able to predict how forests will respond
to natural conditions or to changes caused by management actions designed to achieve specified results
in the future. They, therefore, need tools that allow useful, practical, quantitative predictions of forest
growth and responses to change. These tools are models, which are also important scientific tools.
We discuss the concepts and principles involved in models and their development in Chapter 8.
1.2.1. Importance of Physiology
Understanding the physiological processes that underlie and determine the way trees respond to these
various factors and conditions is fundamental to our ability to anticipate and predict the responses of
forests to particular situations or events. But understanding of individual processes alone cannot
provide us with the ability to predict the probable growth of trees and stands and their responses to
changes, whether management induced or environmental. For that understanding to be useful in a
practical sense, we need to set it in the framework of formal statements, or sets of statements, about
how trees grow. If we are to make quantitative predictions about tree growth and the behaviour of
forest ecosystems, then the statements we make should be expressed in mathematical terms; i.e. they
should be framed as models. So this book is concerned to provide the background physiological
knowledge needed to understand forest growth, and to set that information within the framework of
process-based models that can be used for analysis and prediction and to evaluate the effects of change.
1.2.2. The Nature of Models
Some clarification is necessary with regard to the term ‘model’. In conventional forestry a model is
commonly understood to be a statistically derived equation or set of equations, usually based on
measurements of forest stands. Such models can be used to estimate the amounts of timber in stands
with the same general characteristics as those from which the model was derived. The projected growth
curve of a stand is also, usually, estimated from equations based on the observed growth of other,
similar, stands, or it is an extrapolation of the growth curve derived from measurements made in the
stand of interest. Models of this type are completely empirical; they are derived from measurements
that have no basis in theory or the mechanisms of forest growth. They have a valuable role in forest
management, but we might expect the importance of that role to decrease because of changes in
management and climate. This book is not concerned with such models, although they will be
discussed, to some extent, in Chapter 8, where models based on biophysical processes are reviewed
and discussed.
All models, even empirical ones, are hypotheses. They describe how a system is thought to work.
As such, they can be tested by experiments or measurements designed to determine how accurately the
model predictions correspond to observed reality. In the case of empirical models it is difficult to
design tests with any great resolving power, because those models, by definition, apply only to the
systems from which they were derived. Process-based models are expected to have greater generality as
well as greater explanatory power. They describe systems in terms of underlying processes and
mechanisms that are (to varying extents), general; i.e. they apply to, or operate in, all systems of
similar type. They also serve as the frameworks we need that allow us to identify gaps in our knowledge
Introduction
5

--- Page 21 ---

and to assess the relative importance of different processes. They are powerful scientific tools and have
immense potential as practical tools.
We define process-based or mechanistic models as sets of statements, usually expressed as mathe-
matical equations, that embody our current knowledge or hypotheses about the workings of forest
ecosystems and their responses to stimuli. The equations quantify the physiological processes that
contribute to, and comprise, the growth of trees. These processes operate at different levels and interact
with one another: biochemical processes operate in cells; photosynthesis, driven by the radiation
absorbed by leaves, is affected by both the nutrient and water status of the leaves; processes such as
carbohydrate translocation operate at the level of tissues or organs; the water relations of plants are
determined by the interaction of leaves with the atmosphere and roots with the soil; nutrient uptake
from the soil is affected by soil water status as well as by soil fertility; plants in communities interact
with one another. The growth rate – the rate of accumulation of mass – varies through the plant as
allocation patterns change with environmental conditions and with the age and reproductive state of
the plant. The mass of leaves, reflected in leaf area, determines energy interception which influences the
rate of photosynthesis, while root mass and distribution are major factors in water use and nutrient
uptake.
Most physiological processes are affected by environmental conditions: changes in their rates may be
caused by changes in temperature or in the state of the plant, in terms of nutrient concentrations or
the water status or age of tissues. Processes that operate at lower organisational levels in the plant (cells,
tissues, organs) generally have faster response times than processes at higher levels (plant, stand,
ecosystem). Process-based models of forest growth deal, essentially, with dry mass production by
plant (forest) stands and the way that material is distributed within the plant, resulting in stand growth
and carbon storage.
In the final analysis every model is based on empiricism, i.e. on data that describe some process or
part of the system, usually obtained by careful experiments designed to eliminate as many extraneous
factors and influences as possible. The essential difference, therefore, between completely empirical
models such as conventional forestry models, and process-based models, is that the empiricism in
process-based models is at lower levels than in single-tree or stand models. This means that, in
formulating process-based models, decisions have to be made about the way processes are likely to
interact with one another, as well as about the relative importance of different processes. In many cases
there is not enough information to allow unequivocal decisions. Accordingly, model-builders have to
make assumptions about how the system works, based on their knowledge of the behaviour and
responses of the system as a whole and, sometimes, on (hopefully intelligent) guesswork. This is
perfectly acceptable scientific practice, provided that the assumptions are clearly stated, and the
resulting model is testable; i.e. it should be formulated in such a way that it can be tested by
measurements or experimental manipulation, either of the modelled system as a whole, or preferably
of its component sub-models. If a model is tested and its output is shown to be consistent with
empirical data, this does not mean that the model is right; it simply means that it has not yet been
shown to be wrong. However, as the range of tests and of the conditions under which a model is tested
is increased, confidence in it can also increase.
1.2.3. Complexity and Uncertainty
One of the major problems we face in process-based modelling is the level of complexity. We want
models that are testable, that have as few parameters as feasible (Occam’s Razor; Principle of Paucity),
but we are dealing with systems that are immensely complicated, with layers of organisation, and
6
Chapter 1

--- Page 22 ---

processes at different levels with different response times. If we try to model all the detail, we
will produce complex and unwieldy mathematical structures for which we will not be able to
get most of the parameter values, and which would be untestable. On the other hand, if we simplify
too much we may smooth over, or overlook, factors that have important effects on the end result, or
are involved in important interactions. The choice will be guided by pragmatism – what end result
are we looking for – as well as ‘do-ability’: can we build a useful model? The answer to that question
depends quite strongly on the objective of the exercise. If we are developing a model as a means
of organizing the information available to us, or of evaluating the relative importance of
various processes and identifying gaps in our knowledge of the system as a whole, then almost any
model will be useful. But if we are concerned to produce a tool that will provide outputs of practical
value then the choices about what to include and at what level of complexity, become much more
difficult.
When we describe the operation of a process by an equation we have to recognise that there is always
uncertainty in the parameter values and the output. All models are, to some degree, uncertain. Process-
based models contain uncertainties at the level of the physiological processes that they describe, arising
from variation in those processes, from errors in experimental measurements, and from the assump-
tions incorporated in the models. They will, therefore, generate uncertainties as a result of interactions
between processes as well as those caused by scaling up to the level of stands and ecosystems. Awareness
of those uncertainties, and the ability to keep them under control, is part of the art of the modeller.
Conventional, statistically based models, written at the level of trees or stands, contain uncertainties
arising from measurement errors and variations in the trees and stands on which the models are based.
Attempting to apply those models to situations other than those from which they were developed
produces yet more uncertainty because the new situations contain unknown elements that will add to
the inherent uncertainty in the models.
There is always variation in biological systems – between the organs and parts of trees, between
individual trees in a stand, between neighbouring stands and forests – caused by differences in soil
depth, fertility, water-holding capacity, micro- and macro-topography. Furthermore, no measurement
is perfectly accurate. Therefore when we present our experimental results and models as general-
isations, we have to recognise that the equations and relationships are approximations to any given
particular situation and condition: we need to know how far particular situations are likely to deviate
from actuality, and make a judgment about whether it is worth incorporating more detail to deal with
those particular situations. So when we use models to make decisions, or to analyse particular systems
or situations, we need some idea about the degree to which the results will correspond with reality,
which implies that we can define – or measure – ‘reality’.
1.2.4. Mathematics
This book inevitably contains a lot of mathematics, which may be of concern to some readers. We hope
that those readers will not be put off by the equations, which are simply an efficient way of describing
how the particular processes under discussion work – as we understand it - and reflecting the ideas of
the ecophysiological community at large. We have established a website, which we will refer to as
PhysEcol, to accompany this book. This site contains several spreadsheet applications that illustrate
many of the relationships discussed in this book, and which can be freely downloaded. PhysEcol is
discussed further at the end of this Chapter.
There is some danger in this exercise since the apparently precise descriptions and results provided
by mathematics can lead to the impression that our knowledge of the behaviour of these processes is
Introduction
7

--- Page 23 ---

equally precise. This is seldom the case. The equations describe how the processes (appear to) behave
and respond to the factors that influence them. The parameter values determine the quantitative level
of the responses: How much carbohydrate is produced? How much goes to each part of the plant?
What is the leaf water potential under given soil and atmospheric conditions? The equations are
statements with general applicability; they are supposed to apply to all situations and systems of the
type under consideration. But, almost universally, the parameter values are uncertain, and depend on
the plants/ecosystems on which the measurements used to determine them were made, and the
techniques used to make those measurements. Moreover, the relationships themselves are simplified
representations of reality.
However, the equations represent precise statements of our hypotheses, and they can be tested at
the level of the general statements they make. This is the most crucial and stringent test. If it can be
unequivocally demonstrated that the form of an equation is wrong, this amounts to rejection of the
hypothesis about the process concerned, which must then be re-formulated. Tests at the level of
parameter values that indicate different values apply do not disprove the hypothesis unless the
differences are so large that the whole relationship is called into question.
1.2.5. Statistical Analyses
Conventional statistics, including statistical design, appropriate replication, analysis of variance,
regression and the rest, are an essential part of ecophysiological research. These are the techniques
used to compare the effects of treatments upon the state of a system – such as a forest – at any time, to
evaluate the probability that differences are real, to fit the constants and coefficients to equations, to
evaluate the residual differences between predictions and measurements and to test whether values
differ significantly between different sets of data. Statistical techniques are also important in evaluating
the variance associated with sampling, which leads to proper sampling procedure – always essential in
research involving measurements in variable populations.
Multivariate analysis is a technique that may be valuable in hypothesis formulation or in testing the
relative importance of individual variables, but it is not a substitute for knowledge about the mechan-
isms and inter-relationships that determine the behaviour of a system. Statistical analysis cannot
provide any more knowledge about a system than is contained in the empirical data – although
inferences may be drawn from the results – and even the most cunning manipulation will not extract
information from a collection of data that are not suited to the testing of a hypothesis. Statistical
methods are often used to test the so-called ‘null hypothesis’ – that treatments or populations are not
different in the characteristic being tested. But this may be trivial and whether rejected or not, nothing
is learned about the system under study. For example, suppose we measure some property of a group of
trees, such as stem diameter. We then apply fertilizer to half the group and, after a suitable interval,
return and re-measure the stems. The null hypothesis is that fertilizer has no influence on the growth
rate of the trees, and hence their size at the end of the time interval. Statistical analysis may show that
the null hypothesis must be rejected – the probability that the differences in growth between the
fertilized and unfertilized trees are not real is so small as to be unacceptable. The differences are
therefore real and can be attributed to fertilization. This, in relation to the forest ecosystem, may
be useful information to a manager but to a biologist it is a trivial result, merely confirming what is
already known.
Of much more interest are questions such as: would the magnitude of the difference be the same on
different soil types, with a different rainfall pattern, with trees of a different age, spacing, etc.? These
questions could be answered by more experiments, but each would merely provide another example of
the phenomenon. We need to understand the functioning of the system in terms of the processes
8
Chapter 1

--- Page 24 ---

involved – the rate of uptake of nutrients under specified conditions, the movement (re-translocation)
of nutrients within trees, and growth rates in relation to the nutrient concentrations in tissues.
(We discuss some of these matters in Chapter 6.)
1.2.6. Importance of Physiological Modelling
In physiological modelling and experimentation, we usually assume that we know which physiological
processes are most important in plant growth, and which factors affect them. For example, there
seems little point at this stage in setting up experiments to test whether light (photon flux) is more
important than temperature in determining the rate of photosynthesis; the effects of the two
are thoroughly documented. Similarly, it hardly seems justifiable to expend much effort in time-series
analysis of diurnal changes in transpiration rate, tissue water potential and stem radius in trees
to demonstrate that these can described by a simple resistance–capacitance model. Such models
have already been proposed, formulated tested and found to explain adequately the observed variations
(see Chapter 7). The problem, in many cases, is to evaluate their relevance to trees and obtain
appropriate parameter values for the functional relationships between processes and the variables
that drive them.
We present, in this book, current knowledge about the most important of the physiological
processes that underlie and determine the growth of trees – and indeed most terrestrial plants – and
the way these processes are affected by environmental factors. Consistent with the arguments presented
above, we consider these processes within the framework of a process-based model, and use for this
purpose the 3-PG model first described by Landsberg and Waring (1997), with which we have both
been heavily involved over the last decade (see Sands 2004a,b; Sands and Landsberg 2002). This model
has been widely used and extensively tested (e.g. Coops et al. 1998a,b; Waring and McDowell 2002;
Sands and Landsberg 2002; Almeida et al. 2004a,b,c; Esprey et al. 2004) so its strengths and weaknesses
are well known and documented. It meets our definition of a process-based model as given above, i.e. it
deals with dry mass production by plant (forest) stands, and the way that material is distributed within
the plant, resulting in stand growth and carbon storage. It also provides a means of evaluating and
integrating physiological information and knowledge.
1.3 OUTLINE
The remainder of this chapter provides an outline of the material presented in this book.
Chapter 2 (Weather and Energy Balance) provides background information about the weather
conditions that influence, govern and constrain the growth of plants. These include radiant energy, air
temperature and humidity, wind speed and precipitation. They affect different physiological processes,
and they all vary spatially and temporally. A general knowledge of average values is not enough for
useful analysis. We will provide enough information to ensure that, when weather variables are
considered in various contexts in later parts of the book, even the reader who was initially unfamiliar
with the detail will be comfortable with them. We also provide the equations used to calculate radiant
energy and vapour pressure from other data, e.g. temperature. More detailed treatment of the
interactions between weather variables and forest canopies is provided in Chapter 5.
Radiant energy drives plant growth so we consider normal patterns of seasonal and diurnal variation
in incoming solar radiation, and provide a short discussion of the role of short-wave radiation
Introduction
9

--- Page 25 ---

(i.e. irradiance or photon flux density) as the driver of photosynthesis. Net radiation and atmospheric
humidity drive water loss by transpiration from plants. The net radiation absorbed by forest canopies
can be estimated from empirical relationships with incoming solar energy.
We consider patterns of diurnal temperature variation and seasonal temperature patterns in
different regions. Air humidity and temperature are intimately related. The measure of humidity
with which we are most concerned is vapour pressure deficit (D), and we present the equations relating
relative humidity, D and air temperature, and methods of estimating D from air temperatures.
There is a brief treatment of air flow and transfer processes (boundary layer conductances) –
important background to understanding the transfer of CO2 into, and water vapour out of, leaves,
as well as bulk transfer processes to and from canopies – which leads on to the development of the
Penman-Monteith equation, widely used to calculate rates of transpiration from plant canopies. This
anticipates information about stomatal conductance and its behaviour, provided in Chapter 3.
Chapter 3 (Physiological Processes) describes the physiological processes that are recognised as the
most important determinants of the growth of terrestrial plants – and therefore forests.
Photosynthesis is driven by light intercepted by leaves that stimulates reactions in specialised leaf
organelles called chloroplasts. There is a vast literature on this process. We provide only the informa-
tion needed to understand and interpret the current standard model describing leaf photosynthesis in
terms of irradiance, atmospheric CO2 and its diffusion through stomata into leaves (Farquhar et al.
1980; Farquhar and von Caemmerer 1982). Chapter 3 focuses on the mechanisms of photosynthesis at
leaf level, while canopy photosynthesis is dealt with in Chapter 5, where radiation interception by
stands is also discussed.
Although photosynthesis is the fundamental plant growth process, by which CO2 is fixed and
converted to carbohydrates, both photosynthesis and transpiration are controlled – perhaps modulated
is a better word – by rates of gaseous diffusion through stomata. These are the pores in leaf surfaces that
can vary their aperture and hence the resistance they impose to the movement of gases into and out of
leaves. This control is described in terms of stomatal resistance, or (more commonly) conductance
(denoted gS). We deal in detail in Chapter 3 with gS and the factors affecting it.
Respiration is the process by which carbohydrates are oxidised to CO2 and H2O; it is reasonably well
understood biochemically (see Jones 1992, for a useful summary) and is mentioned briefly at the end of
Chapter 3. However, since the level at which we deal with respiration in any detail is the carbon balance
of trees and stands, we deal with the process, at a relatively empirical level, in Chapter 5.
Chapter 4 (Stand Structure and Dynamics) deals with stand structure in terms of stem population
dynamics, height and diameter relations, diameter distributions, allometric scaling and its implications
and the foliage structure of canopies. The variations in allometric relationships between species, and
the effects of nutrition, stocking and water on those relationships (particularly in plantation eucalypts),
are examined. Allometric relationships reflect constraints on the partitioning of biomass between the
various parts of trees and, for that reason, they provide a powerful and useful tool for analysing
carbohydrate partitioning and the ratios of biomass allocation to different components. The implica-
tions of these allocation patterns, which are important in the development of stand growth models, are
explored in detail in this chapter. The development of the critical variable termed LAI, and the
distribution in space of the foliage of forest canopies are treated in some detail. The last section of
Chapter 4 provides information on the mass and distribution of root systems, relevant to the
discussions on uptake of water and nutrients as well as in relation to biomass distribution.
Chapter 5 (The Carbon Balance of Trees and Stands) is devoted to the carbon balance of canopies
and stands. There is a multitude of canopy radiation interception models in the literature, many of
which are based on similar considerations, beginning with the idea that canopies can be treated as
10
Chapter 1

--- Page 26 ---

homogeneous radiation-absorbing media. They are developed from them, becoming more complex as
they take into account the paths of rays through canopies, and the probability that those rays will – or
will not – be intercepted by foliage elements. The complexity of a model depends on the assumptions
made about canopy structure (clumped, homogeneous. . .), on whether sun angles and the time course
of solar radiation are considered, whether direct beam and diffuse solar radiation are treated separately,
the time scale of the analysis and on whether the objective is integration over time and space or
treatment of discrete intervals. Chapter 5 is not a review of such models. Rather, we present the simplest
and most easily applicable (Beers law) and examine some of its limitations, and then we also present
what we consider is the most widely applicable and useful model: the two-stream approximation with
explicit recognition of sunlit and shaded leaves. The discussion of canopy interception models provides
the basis for treatment of canopy biomass production and light-use efficiency.
In Chapter 6 (Nutrient Dynamics and Tree Growth), we discuss recent work on nutrient uptake and
modelling and deal in some detail with various components of the nutrient cycle.
All living tissues must contain some minimum concentration of nutrients to grow, or at least
maintain themselves, in a steady (respiring) state. Forest nutritional requirements are considered in
terms of nutrient uptake processes and growth in relation to nutrition. These requirements are largely
revealed by field experiments based on the idea of adding nutrients to forest (including plantation)
ecosystems at rates commensurate with the potential growth rates of the stands, and hence the rates at
which nutrients need to be supplied to meet the requirements of new material. We discuss the processes
involved in nutrient uptake by root systems and the growth of trees in relation to their nutrient status.
The final sections of Chapter 6 deal with two models of nitrogen mineralization, with a model of
nitrogen uptake and with the pragmatic fertility indices used in two (process based) forest production
models.
Consideration of plant water relations is the focus of Chapter 7 (Hydrology and Plant Water
Relations). These relations affect every aspect of plant growth, and so we include an outline of the
hydrological balance between water inputs to a system (primarily by precipitation) and losses by
interception, run-off, drainage out of the reach of roots, and transpiration. We deal with these in
Chapter 7, and consider water in the root zone in some detail. Water uptake and movement through
trees is central to the whole field, interacting with stomatal conductance, which must regulate rates of
loss at the foliage surfaces in order to prevent catastrophic embolism in the conducting tissues, with
consequent loss of water conducting capacity. The interesting phenomenon of storage in stems, while it
may not make a great contribution to the overall transpiration by trees, may well be important in
maintaining water supply to the transpiration stream, so that trees can avoid water stress at times when
demand is high. We present models of these processes and discuss stem hydraulic conductivity and its
implications. The last section of Chapter 7 deals with some of the implications of water stress.
Chapter 8 (Modelling Tree Growth: Concepts and Review) deals with modelling in general.
It provides a short discussion of the philosophy and rationale of modelling, followed by a discussion
of empirical, process-based (mechanistic) models and hybrid models. Finally a number of examples of
process-based and hybrid models are reviewed and their properties examined. The reviews assess the
strengths and weaknesses of the models and the extent to which they meet (or appear to meet)
the objectives outlined by their developers. Model testing, calibration and sensitivity analysis are
considered.
Chapter 9 (The 3-PG Process-Based Model) covers in some detail the model called 3-PG (the
acronym stands for Physiological Processes Predicting Growth) we use as a framework for a number of
the discussions in this book. There are endless possible formulations of the processes involved in forest
growth: the equations may be written to deal with them in considerable detail, with short time steps, or
the system may be described in terms of generic properties, with the processes represented in simplified
Introduction
11

--- Page 27 ---

terms, as is the case with this model. 3-PG embodies, in a relatively simple structure, the basic processes
that underlie the growth of all forests. These are: radiation interception and canopy photosynthesis,
modified by water relations and nutrition; carbohydrate (biomass) partitioning to the component
parts of trees; stem populations and the way they change with time; and the conversion of biomass into
products of interest to foresters. This model has been widely adopted and used in a number of contexts,
both as a research and a management tool. The discussion in Chapter 9 includes discussion of the
assumptions and simplifications involved in 3-PG and reviews some of the papers describing work that
has been done with it. A full working implementation of the model as a VBA program running in Excel
is available from the PhysEcol website, along with documentation and tutorial material. In the last
section of that chapter we discuss possible improvements to the 3-PG model.
In Chapter 10 (Future Developments), we outline our views about the areas in the field of plant
ecophysiology, with particular reference to forests, that we think merit particular attention in the near
future, and try to anticipate some of the developments that are likely in the coming years. Predictions
of this sort are usually notable mainly for their inaccuracy, and most scientists, teachers and leaders of
research groups will have their own ideas about what is important and where scientific resources
should be directed. Nevertheless, we feel that the exercise may be worthwhile, if only as a stimulus to
rational argument and critical consideration of the various options that exist. The resources available
for research in our field will never be adequate for the research needed to fill all the gaps in our
knowledge, and priorities have to be set when applying for grants or determining the direction of
programs. Our suggestions are influenced by our coverage of current knowledge, as outlined in this
book, as well as by our preferences, prejudices and shortcomings.
1.4 THE PHYSECOL WEBSITE
We have established a website, which we will refer to as PHYSECOL, to accompany this book. All material
on it is freely downloadable, and all code is freeware. The various applications have been developed in
Microsoft Excel 2003 running under Windows XP and Vista, and documentation is in the form of pdf
files.. However, we cannot guarantee compatibility with Mac versions of Excel, especially where Visual
Basic for Applications has been used to code macros.
PhysEcol contains spreadsheets in which many of the more important equations and relationships
described in the text of this book have been programmed. Many applications allow the various
relationships to be explored with varying parameter values and ranges of input variables, and the
outputs are graphs and/or tables that illustrate the expected behaviour of the process under consider-
ation. Other applications provide tools for fitting observed data to relationships. It is hoped these
examples will prove valuable aids for understanding or research. We do not list in this book which
relationships are so explored. Rather, PhysEcol should be seen as an evolving resource, and readers are
recommended to refer to it for additions and updates.
The PhysEcol website also includes specific models that are explicitly referred to later in this book.
The most important of these is the complete 3-PG model discussed in detail in Chapter 9, along with
various supporting documents, and ancillary spreadsheets. Others are: the implementation of the
Farquhar and von Caemmerer (1982) assimilation model coupled to a model of stomatal response to
environmental factors and intracellular CO2 concentration as discussed in Section 3.3; the Sands
(1995b, 1996) daily whole-canopy photosynthetic production model discussed in Section 5.2.4; and a
simple implementation of the Candy (1997) empirical stand growth model NITGRO described in
Section 8.2.1.

This book is concerned with analysing the growth of forest trees in terms of the physiological
processes that underlie and determine growth. The rates of these processes depend on external
conditions and interactions with other processes going on in the plants. If we could integrate the
rates and quantify the interactions between them, we could determine the state of the plant at any time
in terms of its current state as described by its mass, the distribution of that mass among the
component parts of the plant, and the condition of the plant. Therefore we need to be able to analyse
plant growth in terms of the rates at which physiological processes operate. The condition of the plant
may be specified in terms of the water or nutrient status of the plant.
The external conditions that affect the rates of physiological processes include the weather variables:
radiant energy, air temperature, humidity and wind. To provide the background necessary for analys-
ing plant growth in relation to environmental variables, or for the analysis or prediction of observed
Physiological Ecology of Forest Production
Joe Landsberg and Peter Sands
ISSN 1936-7961, DOI: 10.1016/S1936-7961(11)04002-4
Copyright # 2011 Elsevier Inc.
All rights reserved.

--- Page 29 ---

responses at a particular level, we present in this chapter information about these weather variables.
We also discuss methods of estimating their values where measurements are not available.
Nutrients and water also affect growth. We consider these later in Chapters 6 and 7.
2.1 PROCESS RATES AT DIFFERENT LEVELS
Ecosystems are inherently hierarchical, and ecology is the study of the relationships and interactions
between entities occupying the various levels. Processes at a given level depend on the levels below
them, and their properties are explained by the behaviour of entities at these lower levels. In a forest
ecosystem the first (highest) level is typically the forest or stand. (The population of animals that
browse on or live in the trees should also be included here, but this book is concerned only with the
trees themselves.) The second level is an individual tree, composed of biomass pools that are actively
involved in growth (i.e. leaves, sapwood and fine roots) and in providing structural support
(i.e. branches, stems and coarse roots). At the next level, we have organs such as buds, xylem, phloem
and meristematic material, all of which are involved in various aspects of tree growth. At a finer scale,
leaves contain stomata, and cells within leaves contain chloroplasts and the biochemical machinery of
photosynthesis.
Accordingly, we consider plant growth as a hierarchical set of processes, and envisage a mechanistic
analysis of plant growth and responses to the environment in terms of the mechanisms or processes
underlying those responses at the next level down. The response times of processes at lower levels are
shorter than those at higher levels, and the spatial scale is smaller. In particular, as Table 2.1 shows, the
lower the level, the faster the processes that occur at that level. For example, single-leaf photosynthesis
occurs on the time scale of seconds and responds to the rapidly changing light environment in the
canopy, due to sun-flecks and to the inclination of the leaf as affected by local air movement. The data
in Figure 2.1, based on Wang et al. (2001), show that the photosynthetic rates of individual leaves
respond in different non-linear ways to different environmental variables. The fitted lines in the figure
are simple empirical functions for these rates, discussed later in this book. The process of photosyn-
thesis, and photosynthesis models, are dealt with in Chapter 3.

In principle, it should be possible to describe – or model – systems at higher levels in terms of
processes one level down. Photosynthesis at leaf level requires a supply of CO2 and is affected by light,
the nutrient status of the leaves and by temperature. The rate of supply of CO2 is influenced by ambient
concentrations and controlled by stomatal conductance (see Section 3.3). The forest canopy is an
assemblage of leaves; so given information about their properties and conditions through the canopy,
we should be able to calculate canopy photosynthesis and carbon fixation rates. For a canopy,
photosynthetic production over periods of a day or longer is likely to be determined more by total

solar radiation absorbed by the canopy and its total leaf area than by fast, short-period responses to
rapidly changing variables. Modelling canopy photosynthesis and from that forest production are dealt
with in detail in Chapter 5.
It is a characteristic feature of hierarchical systems that, as we move up in the hierarchy from lower
to more aggregated levels, processes at the lower levels can usually be described by simpler relationships
at the higher level. For example, foliage (needle) length in conifers is determined by cell division and
expansion, and is hence controlled by those variables that control substrate supply, as illustrated in
Figure 2.1, and by the availability of water for maintaining cell turgor. Raison et al. (1992b) were able to
account for most of the differences in total needle length on Pinus radiata trees subject to varying
nutrition and irrigation treatments by a linear relationship with an integrated measure of moisture
stress (see Myers 1988; also Section 7.6.1). Their graph is reproduced as Figure 2.2 with a non-linear
relationship fitted to the data rather than the original linear relationship. This figure is interesting as it
illustrates how a single variable – in this case a measure of water stress – can over-ride the influence of
others, such as temperature and nutrition. This has implications in relation to the effects of water stress
on the growth of the foliage, but we will not explore these here. Water stress is itself a consequence of
the water loss by transpiration, driven by radiant energy, vapour pressure deficit and wind speed.
It is important to note that relationships describing processes are often non-linear, so the average
response over a period of time or spatial region may differ significantly from the response to the
average conditions during that period or across that region. Non-linearity is also illustrated in
Figure 2.3 with data from Benson et al. (1992), who described basal area increments (m2 ha1 yr1)
in the same trees as Raison et al. (1992a,b), by a simple, but non-linear, relationship with the same
moisture stress integral.

2.2 WEATHER FACTORS THAT AFFECT PLANT GROWTH
In this section, we outline the main weather variables that influence – indeed determine – plant growth,
and provide some background on the analysis of process rates and the state and condition of plants.
The external weather variables that affect plant growth are usually strongly correlated. For instance,
solar radiation is usually lower on cloudy or wet days, and humidity is usually lower on warm days than
on cool days. Such correlations often enable us to infer missing data for one variable from data for
other variables, and we indicate how this can be done for solar radiation and vapour pressure deficit
later in this section. Weather variables also vary diurnally, and often in a characteristic manner. This is
shown in Figure 2.4 for a tropical and temperate site. We also show later in this section that these
diurnal variations can often be represented using simple empirical relationships. Because stomata open
and close in response to both solar radiation and vapour pressure deficits, and hence photosynthesis
and transpiration are strongly coupled (see Section 3.3), detailed models of photosynthesis will have to
take the diurnal variation of weather variables into account. However, diurnal effects can usually be
neglected when photosynthesis is estimated for longer time periods, and for whole canopies (e.g. see
the model 3-PG described in Chapter 9).
2.2.1. Solar Radiation
The basis of tree growth is photosynthesis, and this is driven by the radiant energy from the sun
absorbed by the leaves, or by other photosynthetically active surfaces such as the bark of some species.
Since, as we have seen, photosynthetic production depends non-linearly on the rate of absorption of
solar energy by the leaves, it is determined by both the input rate and the total amount of radiant
energy.

Weather and Energy Balance
17

--- Page 33 ---

The flux of radiant energy from the sun exterior to the earth’s atmosphere, the so-called ‘solar
constant’, varies seasonally from about 1321 W m2 in early July to 1412 W m2 in early January due
to the ellipticity of the Earth’s orbit. The path of this radiation through the atmosphere varies diurnally
due to the changing position of the sun in the sky. In particular, the optical path length decreases with
increasing solar elevation, so radiation at the earth’s surface is highest when the solar elevation is
highest, i.e. at solar noon.
Once radiation enters the atmosphere, it is absorbed and scattered by air molecules, aerosols and
dust. These processes contribute to the blue of a clear sky and the red of a sunset but, more important

18
Chapter 2

--- Page 34 ---

in relation to our discussion, they contribute to a reduction of the direct beam radiation and to the
generation of diffuse radiation. Absorbed radiation may be partially re-radiated, but is generally lost
from the beam through conversion to other forms of energy, e.g. powering atmospheric chemistry.
Scattered radiation is absorbed or repeatedly scattered, and that portion which experiences a net
downward scattering is received as diffuse radiation. As a consequence of the repeated scattering
process, diffuse radiation has little directional variation in intensity.
Accordingly, the radiation received at any point on the earth comprises direct beam radiation
received from the direction of the sun, and diffuse radiation which is essentially independent of
direction. The ratio of direct to diffuse radiation is variable.
A general reference covering a wide range of aspects of solar radiation is An Introduction to Solar
Radiation (Iqbal 1983).
a) Types of Radiation
All material bodies radiate energy, and this includes the sun, the atmosphere, the ground and plant
parts. The nature of this thermal radiation is determined by the absolute temperature T (i.e. in Kelvin)
of the body, and its total flux ’(T) (W m2) is given by the Stefan–Boltzman law for black-body
radiation:
’ T
ð Þ ¼ esT 4;
ð2:1Þ
where e is the emissivity of the body, and s¼5.67108 W m2 K4 is the Stefan–Boltzman constant.
The emissivity e is the fraction of radiation the body emits or absorbs as compared to a perfect black-
body. For most vegetative surfaces e is in the range 0.90–0.96.
The spectral distribution of black-body radiation also depends on the temperature T (K) of the
emitter, and the peak wavelength lmax (mm) is given by
lmax ¼ 2898=T:
ð2:2Þ
The surface temperature of the sun is around 5800 K, and approximately 96% of solar radiation is in
the 0.3 (ultraviolet) to 3 mm (infra-red) window with a strong peak at lmax0.48 mm. However,
temperatures of bodies on earth are of the order of 290 K, and thermal radiation at this temperature has
a spectral distribution from 3 to 50 mm with a peak at lmax10 mm. For this reason solar radiation,
both direct and diffuse, is called short-wave radiation, while thermal radiation that originates from
bodies on the earth is called long-wave radiation.
It is also necessary to distinguish between broad-band solar radiation and photosynthetically active
solar radiation (PAR). Broad-band solar radiation is the full solar spectrum, i.e. from about 0.15–
3.2 mm. PAR, or visible radiation, falls within the spectral band from 0.4 to 0.7 mm (400–700 nm) and
is actively involved in photosynthesis. External to the earth’s atmosphere, the ratio of PAR to total solar
radiation is 0.44. However, because of atmospheric effects such as the presence or absence of clouds, the
ratio of PAR to broad-band solar radiation varies from 0.4 to 0.6, and is nominally taken to be 0.5. If the
qualifier ‘PAR’ is not used, broad-band solar radiation is always implied.
When considering processes such as transpiration or the energy balance of bodies, we are concerned
with the whole spectrum, including long-wave radiation, and use appropriate units, i.e. energy-flux
density (W m2). For photochemical processes, such as photosynthesis, the actual number of photons
(the fundamental quantum particles of radiation) is also important. Accordingly, the unit used for
photosynthetically active radiation is the mole, and one mole of photons is called an Einstein (E).
Although PAR may be expressed as energy-flux density, photon-flux density (with units mol m2 s1)
Weather and Energy Balance
19

--- Page 35 ---

has the great advantage that photosynthetic efficiency can be expressed in terms of the number of moles
of CO2 fixed per mole of photons in the visible waveband in which radiation will activate photosyn-
thesis. For comparison with many published data, the units mol and Einstein are numerically equal.
It is not strictly correct to convert directly from energy flux to photon flux, because the energy of a
photon depends on its wavelength. However, in normal sunlight 1 J of PAR contains about 4.6 mmol,
i.e. for solar radiation 1000 W m22300 mmol m2 s1 PAR.
For convenience, throughout this book, we will frequently follow the common practice of referring
to PAR as ‘light’.
b) Irradiance
The irradiance, or flux density of radiant energy, is the power incident on a unit area and is usually
expressed as W m2 (i.e. J m2 s1). In particular, direct beam solar irradiance Idir (W m2) is the
irradiance of the sun’s direct beam measured on a plane perpendicular to the beam. On the other hand,
diffuse irradiance Idif (W m2) is always measured on a horizontal plane. If the measuring surface is not
normal to a beam of irradiance Idir, then the measured irradiance I (W m2) is reduced because the
area of the surface intersected by the beam is increased. If y is the angle between the beam direction and
the normal to the surface, then
I y
ð Þ ¼ Idir cosy:
ð2:3Þ
This is known as Lambert’s cosine law. It follows that the total solar radiation I (W m2) incident
upon a horizontal surface is given by
I ¼ Idif þ Idir cosyz;
ð2:4Þ
where yz is the zenith angle of the sun, i.e. the angle between the direct beam and the normal to the
earth’s surface.
On cloudless days, irradiance varies smoothly with time-of-day. This is illustrated in Figure 2.5 for
3 days at different times of the year at a site of latitude 42.9S in south-east Tasmania, Australia. Peak
irradiance in summer is higher than in winter because sun angles are higher and the reduction in
irradiance due to the cosine law is less. Also, high sun angles reduce the optical path length of the direct
beam in the atmosphere, and hence there is less atmospheric absorption. At sunrise and sunset
atmospheric absorption is high, in part due to the low sun angle and in part due to increase in aerosols
and dust. This leads to enhanced reduction of direct beam irradiance at these times as compared to
diffuse radiation and could explain the ‘tails’ on the observed irradiance curves.
Detailed models of photosynthetic production require knowledge of the diurnal and seasonal
variation of solar radiation. This can be obtained using models for the passage of solar radiation
through the atmosphere that take into account variations of the extra-terrestrial irradiance with time of
year, diurnal changes in sun-position, and the absorption and subsequent scattering of direct beam
radiation by molecules, aerosols and dust along the optical path. One such model is that of Bird and
Hulstrom (1981), and we discuss this process in detail in Appendix 1. As illustrated in Figure 2.5, such
models can provide excellent estimates of irradiance. However, they require information about various
optical properties of the atmosphere and their temporal variation. As atmospheric optical data were
not available for the data shown in Figure 2.5, atmospheric water content was chosen so that observed
and predicted maximum irradiance agreed.
An alternative approach can be based on the empirical observation that the temporal variation of I
during daylight hours is given by the simple empirical relationship

I tð Þ ¼ Ix cos pt=he

ð2:5Þ
where Ix (W m2) is the global irradiance at solar noon, he (hours) is an effective day-length, and
t (hours) is time from solar noon. Fitting Eq. (2.5) to observed time-series data for irradiance on
cloudless days usually gives an excellent fit, e.g. see Figure 2.5. However, the fitted values for he are
consistently less than the actual day-length hd as determined from astronomical formula (see Appendix 1).
This is because atmospheric absorption of the direct beam increases with increasing path length through
the atmosphere, strongly reducing direct beam irradiance for low sun angles. Based on data from various
days of the year spanning day-lengths from 8.8 to 15.1 h,
