c) Insolation
Insolation is the amount of solar radiation received on a given surface in a given time period.
In particular, daily insolation Q (MJ m2 day1) is the solar radiation incident on a horizontal surface
per square metre integrated over a day. Insolation varies seasonally and with daily variation in cloud
cover. Figure 2.6 shows the seasonal variation of daily insolation in southern Tasmania and Aracruz, in
Brazil. The envelope curves were predicted by the Bird and Hulstrom (1981) model with atmospheric
optical properties selected to fit clear-day irradiance in winter and spring for the Tasmanian site. It is
clear that this overestimates irradiance on cloudless days in summer and early autumn, presumably
because of enhanced absorption due to dust and smoke. It is also clear that the Bird and Hulstrum
model has generally overestimated insolation at the Brazilian site, and this almost certainly reflects the
fact that skies in Tasmania are far clearer than tropical skies.

--- Page 38 ---

Total annual energy income at these sites is about 5200 MJ m2 in Tasmania and 6400 MJ m2 at
Aracruz. These values provide a good guide to the amount of energy that can be expected at various
latitudes, although this of course varies with cloudiness. Monteith and Unsworth (1990) give examples
of total energy income at various sites round the world; values range from 5 to 6 MJ m2 day1 in
winter in Europe and north America to summer values of about 30 MJ m2 day1 in Australia (and
many other places).
An excellent approximation to the diurnal variation of irradiance required in detailed photosyn-
thetic models can be obtained as follows. Integration of I(t) as given by Eq. (2.5) over daylight hours
gives insolation as
Q ¼
ðhe=2
he=2
I tð Þdt ¼ 2
p heIx:
ð2:7Þ
Accordingly, if insolation Q and actual day-length h are known, Eqs. (2.5)–(2.7) can be used to
determine the diurnal variation of irradiance for use in the photosynthetic models. This avoids the
need to determine the optical properties of the atmosphere.
If actual day-length is used in Eq. (2.7) instead of he, Ix is underestimated because he<hd, and
although total radiation absorbed by the canopy is unchanged (Q is the same), instantaneous irradi-
ance will be reduced in photosynthetic calculations. However, the concomitant reduction in photosyn-
thetic production will be to some extent made up for by the increased duration of daily photosynthesis.
This approach can be applied to cloudy days, but the errors in actual and inferred irradiance are
more marked. For example, Figure 2.5b shows the observed irradiance on a cloudy day, and compares
this with the theoretical irradiance in the absence of cloud, and with the irradiance estimated by
applying Eqs. (2.5)–(2.7). As a consequence, photosynthetic production will be overestimated during
periods when the sun is covered by cloud, and underestimated when it is clear. These errors may be
compounded by associated changes in air temperature and vapour pressure deficit, but for many
situations this approach is both useful and adequate (e.g. in the simple canopy photosynthesis model of
Sands 1995b; see Section 5.2.4).
d) Determination of Insolation in the Absence of Direct Observations
Modern meteorological stations, especially those used in a research setting, often measure insolation
directly. However, for many sites, and especially for historical data, another way of estimating insola-
tion (or irradiance), is required.
One simple method corrects an estimate of insolation Q0 at the earth’s surface on cloudless days
using observed measurements of average cloud cover, e.g. the ratio of hours of bright sunshine (hS) to
day-length (hd). The insolation Q is then given by A˚ ngstro¨m’s formula:
Q ¼ a þ bhS=hd
ð
ÞQ0;
ð2:8Þ
where a and b are empirical constants. Since Q¼Q0 on a cloudless day, aþb¼1. A˚ ngstro¨m (1924)
estimated a¼0.25 for Stockholm, with similar values for Washington, while Hounam (1963) estimated
a¼0.34 from 458 observations in all 12 months at six contrasting sites across Australia.
The A˚ ngstro¨m formula requires clear-day insolation at the earth’s surface. Prescott (1940) circum-
vented this by defining Q0 in Eq. (2.8) to be daily insolation calculated assuming the atmosphere is
transparent. This approach is now widely used. In this case aþb¼tm, where tm is the average
atmospheric transmittance (also called transmissivity). In this case both Q0 and tm can be predicted
using models such as Bird and Hulstrom (1981), or equations in Appendix A1. For the same 458
Weather and Energy Balance
23

--- Page 39 ---

observations mentioned above, and with Q0 being daily insolation assuming the atmosphere is
transparent, Hounam (1963) estimated a¼0.26 and b¼0.50 across Australia. There were local and
seasonal differences that presumably reflect variations in atmospheric conditions.
As the A˚ ngstro¨m–Prescott approach also requires hours of bright sunshine, other approaches
employ readily available data such as monthly or annual mean air temperature, humidity and
precipitation, and site factors such as latitude and altitude to modify transmissivity. Bristow and
Campbell (1984) developed a relationship between diurnal temperature extremes and daily total solar
radiation at the surface, discussed further in Section A1.4. Their rationale was that when conditions are
dry and clear the differences between maximum and minimum air temperatures increase. They used
modelled estimates of extra-terrestrial radiation and an estimate of the daily value of atmospheric
transmittance, which is strongly influenced by atmospheric water vapour. We tested their formulation,
with their parameter values, using daily observations for a year at the site in south-east Tasmania (see
Figures 2.5 and 2.6). The results were not good: the observed versus predicted regression line was
y¼0.81xþ2.9 (r2¼0.64) with a standard deviation of residuals of 5 MJ m2 day1. Comparison of
the weekly average observed and predicted radiation values indicated a better fit: y¼0.96xþ0.9
(r2¼0.93) with a standard deviation of residuals of 1.8 MJ m2 day1. These results suggest that
approaches such as that of Bristow and Campbell (1984) are likely to be site specific, and are better
suited to predicting longer term averages than daily values.
Coops et al. (2000) used a modified version of the Bristow–Campbell model, with site latitude and
altitude as well as daily maximum and minimum air temperatures, transmittance at the site and extra-
terrestrial radiation, for estimating mean monthly insolation. The transmittance is the theoretical
maximum transmittance at the site modified by factors based on diurnal temperature range and site
altitude. Their approach incorporates an empirical relationship between direct and diffuse irradiance at
the earth’s surface. Although the model is based on daily calculations, the results are estimates of
monthly average direct beam and diffuse insolation, corrected for the slope and aspect of the site. Coops
et al. (2000) tested the model at three sites in the Pacific NW, Scotland and Canberra, covering altitudes
from 80 to 850 m. The predicted and measured radiation at each site did not fall exactly on 1:1 lines: the
r2 values ranged from 0.87 to 0.98 with slopes >1 and standard errors of 0.2–3 MJ m2 day1. Overall,
errors in monthly mean insolation were up to 7% on horizontal sites and up to 13% on slopes, and the
standard error was around 2 MJ m2 day1 over any month. This performance is similar to that of the
standard Bristow–Campbell model at the Tasmanian site.
Trnka et al. (2005) compared a number of different approaches using data from the Central
European lowlands, and concluded that the A˚ ngstro¨m–Prescott approach was the most reliable,
explaining 96% of the variability in daily insolation with a mean residual of 0.1 MJ m2 day1 and
an annual root-mean-square error (RMSE) of 1.6 MJ m2 day1. Models using cloud cover and
diurnal temperature range, or daily precipitation, or only diurnal temperature range gave annual
RMSE of 2.3, 3.1 and 3.5 MJ m2 day1, respectively. In all cases, less than 86% of variation in
insolation was explained and the average residual was 0.3 MJ m2 day1.
2.2.2. Temperature
Temperature is a key determinant of the rate of metabolic processes and hence has a major impact on
plant growth, especially on carbon balance through its influence on photosynthesis and respiration.
It also affects plant development, for example through effects of temperature on the timing of bud burst
and flowering. Temperature affects the water balance of the plant, and the dynamics of soil water storage,
through its effects on evaporation and transpiration. Finally, extreme temperature induces leaf damage
that affects plant growth, disrupts enzyme systems, or induces extreme water stress and loss of turgor.
24
Chapter 2

--- Page 40 ---

We tend to think of temperature as air temperature. Energy is absorbed by the leaves, branches and
bark of a plant and by the soil, and is dissipated as sensible heat and as latent heat during transpiration
and evaporation. As a result, the temperature of these entities may differ from the ambient air
temperature, and vary differently to air temperature (see (b) below). However, air temperature is a
primary variable that determines the temperature environment of a plant, and plant processes are often
described in terms of air temperature. Professor Ram Oren (personal communication) pointed out to
us that there are plants in environments with very low air temperatures but high radiation loads, that
have architectural adaptations that cause tissue temperatures to be much higher than air temperatures.
a) Air Temperature
Air temperature is conventionally measured in a screen at a standard height of 1.4 m above the ground.
This is because thermometers must be shaded from direct sunlight, and because temperature varies
with height above the ground.
It is not widely appreciated that air temperatures may fluctuate rapidly, particularly in unstable
atmospheric conditions when heat is rising rapidly from the ground. However, for most purposes there
seems little reason to depart from using average temperatures over a selected time period as the basis
for analysing plant growth. In general, over periods of several hours or longer, the temperatures of plant
parts may follow air temperatures quite closely. Exceptions may be the trunks of trees exposed to direct
sunlight, where temperatures under the bark can rise significantly above air temperatures, and roots,
which of course are subject to soil temperatures (see (c) below).
Daily maximum and minimum temperatures are of interest in themselves because they may cause
damage to plants. The minimum temperature that plants can tolerate varies enormously. Tropical
plants will stop growing and may be damaged by temperatures as low as 10 C, while trees growing in
cold climates may be able to tolerate air temperatures as low as 40 C without serious damage. This
tolerance may be season specific: very low air temperatures in spring, for example, may cause serious
damage to buds and growing points in plants which would otherwise tolerate much lower tempera-
tures in winter. Therefore, in analysing the climatic range to which a species or provenance is adapted,
it is important to examine not only its growth patterns in relation to average temperature, but also its
ability to withstand extremes at various stages of growth.
We do not deal in this book with developmental processes. However, these are particularly relevant
in cold climates, where physiological activity is stopped by low temperatures in winter. For example,
Bergh et al. (1998) found that annual photosynthetic production of Norway spruce in Sweden was
affected by the timing of the recovery of photosynthetic capacity after winter, particularly following soil
thawing, and the decline in photosynthetic activity with the onset of severe frosts in autumn.
Where air temperatures are used in calculations relating to some aspect of plant growth, the values
used are usually averages over some time period (e.g. monthly in the 3-PG forest growth model; see
Landsberg and Waring 1997 and Chapter 9). The extent to which air temperatures fluctuate, and
therefore the amount of ‘smoothing’ that results from using averages, is not always appreciated, so we
present in Figure 2.7 a year-long record of maximum and minimum air temperatures at the same sites
as the radiation data in Figure 2.6.
b) Leaf Temperatures
Leaf temperatures are important not only because they affect the rates of physiological processes such
as photosynthesis and respiration, but also because they govern the leaf energy balance, and hence
transpiration rates. Leaf temperatures during the day are not necessarily tightly coupled to air
temperatures. Rather, they are determined by the radiation load on the leaves, and by the effectiveness
with which they can dissipate heat by transpiration and convection. Leaf temperature responds rapidly
Weather and Energy Balance
25

--- Page 41 ---

to changes in incoming radiation (the response time is of the order a minute), and may become
significantly higher than air temperatures when radiation loads are high and wind speeds low. Data
from Jones (1992) reproduced in Figure 2.8 show leaf and air temperature fluctuations in response to
changes in solar radiation.
c) Stem and Soil Temperatures
Soil temperatures are far less dynamic than air temperatures. They typically vary less than, and lag
behind, changes in air temperature, and variation in soil temperature is damped by soil depth. These
features are illustrated in Figure 2.9 by data obtained for Eucalyptus globulus growing at the Pittwater
eucalypt plantation site (A. O’Grady and D. Worledge, personal communication), a temperate site in
southern Tasmania.
d) Diurnal Variation of Temperature
Daily temperatures typically increase from a low around dawn to a maximum in the mid afternoon
before declining late afternoon and during the night to a minimum the following dawn. For many
purposes a simple empirical representation of diurnal air temperature T (C) based on daily observed

The application of Eq. (2.9) to a randomly chosen 10-day period at a site in south-eastern Tasmania
is illustrated in Figure 2.10. This is typical of the quality of the representation of diurnal temperature
variation obtained with this relationship. The maximum and minima were midnight to midnight
extremes, and sometimes the temperatures the following night are lower than the dawn minimum.
When this occurs, as it does on days 286 and 287 in Figure 2.10, the predicted pattern varies
significantly from the observed. However, if the dawn minimum is used, this issue is resolved. On
some occasions the passage of a cold front causes the daily maximum to occur at or very soon after
dawn, with falling temperatures throughout the day. However, Eq. (2.9) can be used in these cases if
appropriate choices of maximum and of tx are made.
Sometimes average temperature is required over different periods of the day. For example, the
canopy photosynthesis model of Sands (1995a,b) makes use of average temperature for daylight hours
separately before and after noon. If such data are not available, they can be estimated by integrating
Eq. (2.9) over the required periods and thus expressing the averages explicitly in terms of the daily
maximum and minimum and day-length. In particular, the average temperatures TAM from sunrise to
the time of maximum temperature, and TPM from the maximum to sunset, are given by

2.2.3. Humidity and Vapour Pressure Deficit
The amount of water vapour in the air is an important determinant of plant growth because it
determines the rate at which water is lost from leaves to the atmosphere through the stomata by the
process of transpiration. Water is used by a growing plant for several essential processes: it is used in
photosynthesis where molecules of water and carbon dioxide are combined to form organic carbon
and oxygen, the movement of water up the plant due to transpiration carries nutrients from the soil,
and water is required to maintain cell turgor and hence the structural integrity of the plant. On the
other hand, transpiration removes water from the soil, and dry soils reduce transpiration and slow
growth.
There is a beautiful web of feedbacks in which stomata ‘sense’ the water contents of the soil and the
atmosphere and open or close to control both the availability of CO2 for growth, and the loss of soil
water through transpiration. Transpiration is discussed in detail in Section 2.5.3 and stomatal control
in Section 3.2. It is enough to note here that transpiration is driven primarily by the water vapour
pressure gradient between leaves and the air, assuming that the stomatal cavities are saturated with
water vapour.
Atmospheric water content is measured by its vapour pressure e, which is the partial pressure of
water vapour in the atmosphere. The standard unit of pressure is the Pascal (Pa), although vapour
pressures are nowadays usually expressed in kPa. In a great deal of earlier literature the millibar (mbar)
was the standard unit used, where 1 mbar¼100 Pa, hence 1 kPa¼10 mbar.
In this section, we provide the basic equations for calculating air humidity and vapour pressures
from conventional wet and dry bulb temperature measurements. We also provide a method of
estimating vapour pressure when only maximum and minimum air temperatures are available.
a) Calculating Vapour Pressure and Humidity
The partial pressure of water vapour in air saturated at a particular temperature (T) is called the
saturation water vapour pressure es(T) and depends on temperature alone. A simple and accurate
expression for saturation vapour pressure is given by Dilley (1968):
es T
ð Þ ¼ 0:61078 e17:269T= 237:3þT
ð
Þ;
ð2:11Þ
where T is in C, and as given by this equation eS is in kPa. The temperature at which water begins to
condense out of a volume of saturated air is called the dew point (Td).
The partial pressure of water vapour in unsaturated air (ea, simply called vapour pressure) can be
calculated from the standard psychrometric equation
ea ¼ es Tw
ð
Þ  g T  Tw
ð
Þ;
ð2:12Þ
where Tw is the wet-bulb temperature, es(Tw) is saturation vapour pressure at Tw, and g is the
psychrometric constant. This is defined by g¼cpaP/el (Pa K1), where P (Pa) is atmospheric pressure,
cpa¼1004 J kg1 K1 is the specific heat of dry air, l¼2.454 MJ kg1 is the latent heat of vaporisation
of water and e¼0.622 is the ratio of the molecular weights of water and air. For fully ventilated
psychrometers g¼66.1 Pa K1. Full ventilation implies that radiative exchanges between the sensors
and the air stream are negligible and that forced convection is the dominant heat exchange mechanism
between sensors and the air. Therefore the sensors measure true air temperature, the rate of evapora-
tion from the wet-bulb element depends only on the vapour pressure deficit of the air, and any increase
in the flow rate does not cause an increase in the wet-bulb depression (TTw).
Weather and Energy Balance
29

--- Page 45 ---

Relative humidity Hr is the ratio of the vapour pressure of unsaturated air (ea) to saturated vapour
pressure of air at the same temperature (i.e. Hr¼ea/es(T)), and is usually expressed as a percentage.
Relative humidity only provides information about the absolute value of humidity if the temperature is
also specified. Some biological phenomena, such as the opening of seed cases, are affected by Hr. It also
influences the likelihood of forest fires, and their severity if they occur, because dry plant material
absorbs water if relative humidity is high. The amount of water in such material has a very significant
effect on the ease with which it will ignite and the temperature with which it will burn.
The specific humidity q (kg kg1) is the mass of water vapour per unit mass of air with which the
water vapour is associated. From the universal gas law and the law of partial pressures it can be shown
that
q ¼
eea
P  ea
 eea
P ;
ð2:13Þ
where e is as defined above; the approximation is valid since atmospheric pressure P is large compared
to ea. Since P100 kPa and ea is typically 1.5 kPa, q is typically of the order of 0.01 kg kg1. Specific
humidity has the advantage of being a conservative measure of water vapour concentration because it is
independent of temperature and pressure. However, the absolute humidity of air w (kg m3), given by
w ¼ raq;
ð2:14Þ
where ra (¼1.2 kg m3) is the density of air, is affected by both temperature and pressure and hence
should be avoided.
b) Vapour Pressure Deficit
When we consider transpiration, the vapour pressure difference (De, Pa) between the inside of the
leaves and the ambient air is the important variable. It depends on the vapour pressure of the air and on
the foliage temperature (Tf). Assuming that air in sub-stomatal cavities is saturated with water vapour,
then De¼es(Tf)ea.
In general, the vapour pressure deficit D (Pa) is an important measure of the drying power of air.
It is given by
D ¼ es T
ð Þ  ea ¼ es T
ð Þ 1  Hr=100
ð
Þ;
ð2:15Þ
where T is the current air temperature, es(T) is the saturated water vapour pressure at T, ea is the
current (unsaturated) water vapour pressure and Hr is the relative humidity.
If diurnal values of Hr have not been observed, D can be estimated from the diurnal temperature
variation by noting that ea is a characteristic of the air mass at the site and is observed to vary slowly
through the day in comparison with es(T). Hence, if the dew point temperature is assumed equal to the
minimum air temperature Tn, then ea¼es(Tn) and D at any time is
D T
ð Þ ¼ es T
ð Þ  es Tn
ð
Þ;
ð2:16Þ
where T is the predicted (or observed) diurnal temperature.
The daily average vapour pressure deficit Dav is obtained by averaging the diurnally varying values of D.
Because photosynthesis and transpiration are largely confined to daylight hours, Oren and Pataki (2001),
Ewers et al. (2001) and Almeida and Landsberg (2003) argue that it is more appropriate to use the vapour
pressure deficit Dday averaged over daylight hours only, as a potential determinant of photosynthetic
production and transpiration over periods of a day or more.
30

--- Page 46 ---

c) Calculating Average Vapour Pressure Deficits
If diurnal observations of air temperature and relative humidity are available, Eq. (2.15) can be used to
determine diurnal values of vapour pressure deficit. These in turn can be averaged to determine Dav or
Dday. Relative humidity is rarely reported, other than at 9 AM and possibly 3 PM, and some other
means for determining Dav or Dday is required. The form of Eq. (2.16) suggests the use of
Dav ¼ a es T
ð Þ  es Tn
ð
Þ
ð
Þ
ð2:17Þ
as an estimator of daily average vapour pressure, where a is an empirical factor.
With a¼0.5, Eq. (2.17) explained about 53% of the variation in Dav over a period of 1 year in south-
eastern Tasmania, and about 64% in Brazil. With a¼0.7, Eq. (2.17) can be used to predict Dday. In this
case 62% of the annual variation was explained in Tasmania and 75% in Brazil. This procedure is
extensively used in modelling exercises, when only air temperatures are available (see Running and
Coughlan 1988, Coops et al. 2000).
2.2.4. Wind
Strong or persistent wind may be an important factor affecting the growth of plant communities:
strong winds cause damage (wind throw) and persistent wind modifies growth patterns. Eddies and
gusts are characteristic of wind, and damage to trees often occurs because the frequency of gusts
corresponds to the oscillation frequency of the trees. Whether a tree breaks or fall depends not only on
wind force and characteristics, but also on tree height and diameter, the bending properties of the wood
and the extent of mutual protection by other trees. In relation to growth patterns, we have all seen the
distorted plants along coasts or on hillsides subject to persistent wind.
However, the most important effect of wind is its influence on the transfer of entities such as water
vapour, heat and CO2 to and from foliage and plant stands. We consider the process of mass transfer
and its consequences in Section 2.5.1.
2.3 VARIATION OF CLIMATIC FACTORS WITHIN A CANOPY
Environmental conditions within plant canopies are determined by the atmospheric conditions
pertaining at any time and their interaction with the canopy elements – mainly with foliage.
We have drawn in Figure 2.11 several representative profiles indicating the conditions that can be
expected in a dense and a sparse canopy. These are derived from Jarvis et al. (1976) and from Haverd
et al. (2009). All the profiles are drawn as smooth curves, representing hourly averages.
In the eucalyptus stand (Figure 2.11, upper panels) the LAI of the trees was about 3; understory
vegetation contributed another 1.5. The low, uniformly distributed leaf foliage mass caused all variable
values to change more or less monotonically with height. The major influence on air temperature
was probably the ground and understory vegetation, although leaf temperatures are likely to have
been higher than air temperature because of radiation absorption – net downward radiation
declined steadily due to absorption by foliage elements. VPD was lowest near the (warm) ground,
increased slightly above the understory – suggesting that transpiration there was low – and then
decreased steadily with height. Absorption of momentum was similar at all heights, so wind speed
decreased monotonically. Reduced CO2 concentrations above the understory and in the lower canopy
indicated significant photosynthesis in those layers.

FIGURE 2.11 Illustration of the profiles of key meteorological factors through the canopy of two contrasting stands. The upper profiles are based on data obtained in a 40-m tall temperate eucalyptus forest
at the Tumbarumba Ozflux site in south-east Australia (provided by Vanessa Haverd, personal communication; see also Haverd et al. 2009). The bottom row of figures are mean hourly values of
measurements made at a number of levels in a 10-m high Picea sitchensis stand near Aberdeen, Scotland. The data were obtained by digitising Fig. 13 in Jarvis et al. (1976) and using the values at the
reference height (14 m) to give the actual values. VPD was determined from observed vapour pressure and temperature using Eq. (2.15).

--- Page 48 ---

In the dense (LAI¼10) spruce stand (Figure 2.11, lower panels), the very high foliage mass in the
top third of the canopy was the dominant influence on within-canopy conditions. Radiation and
momentum absorption were highest there; boundary layer conductances (see Section 2.4.3a) were low
because of the low wind speed, so foliage and air temperatures were relatively high, resulting in
increased vapour pressure deficits. Temperature and VPD then decreased towards the damp and
heavily shaded soil. (There was no understory.) CO2 concentrations were lowest in the dense foliage
layer because of extraction by photosynthesis. There was a small increase in wind speed (u) below the
foliage layers. This was commonly observed (see Landsberg and James 1971) and is attributed to
pressure pulses or gusts that penetrate gaps in canopies and spread under the foliage. Clearly, most of
the turbulent transfer of entities entrained in the eddies moving up and down above and through the
canopy is in the region of greatest wind shear (du/dz) in the region of the canopy top.
2.4 ENERGY BALANCE, EVAPORATION AND TRANSPIRATION
The gross features of a stand of trees are strongly dependent on long-term climatic conditions.
However, on short time scales physical characteristics of the stand interact with the prevailing weather
to determine the environment, or microclimate, inside the stand. There are continual fluxes of heat,
water vapour and CO2 between various parts of trees, and the forest canopies as a whole, and their
environment. Solar radiation heats exposed surfaces and drives evaporation, including transpiration,
which in turn dissipates heat and cools the surface. Hence, the actual temperature and radiation that
trees experience, and that affect their growth and the rate they lose water, are different for different
parts of the trees.
The degree to which microclimate needs to be taken into account in a model of tree growth and
development is strongly dependent on the purpose of the model. A model of long-term stand growth
on a monthly time step and based on an average tree in the stand (e.g. 3-PG, see Chapter 9) can
generally ignore the details discussed here. However, a model predicting short-term photosynthetic
responses for a particular tree in situ will need to account for variations in microclimate. Some of the
results presented here, in particular the Penman–Monteith equation for transpiration, are of general
applicability.
In this section, we discuss the principles of radiant and heat energy balance, and energy fluxes.
We then explore the energy balance of surfaces and of a canopy, and present basic equations that enable
us to estimate the rate of evaporation from surfaces, and the rate of loss of water vapour through
stomata from leaves or from the canopy as a whole. For further information on microclimate and the
physics of plant–environment interactions, we recommend the books by Monteith and Unsworth
(1990) and Jones (1992).
2.4.1. Radiant Energy
The total radiation that falls on a body near the earth’s surface comprises both short-wave radiation
from the sun, and long-wave thermal radiation from the sky, the ground and other bodies. The long-
wave radiation is given by the Stefan–Boltzman law, i.e. Eq. (2.1). Thus, the downward flux of long-
wave radiation depends on the apparent radiative temperature of the sky, while the other fluxes depend
on the effective temperatures of the radiating surfaces. It is important to understand the nature of this
radiation environment as it is quite complex, and fairly drastic assumptions need to be made if simple
models are to be used to describe it.
Weather and Energy Balance
33

--- Page 49 ---

a) Net Radiation
The net flux ’n of all radiation across a plane is the sum of all short-wave and long-wave radiation
through this plane. For example, the net radiation crossing a horizontal plane is
’n ¼ ’S þ ’L#  ’L";
ð2:18Þ
where ’S is short-wave radiation, i.e. the sum of direct and diffuse solar radiation, and ’L# and ’L" are
incoming (downward) and outgoing (upward) long-wave radiation, respectively. Empirical observa-
tions suggest that under clear skies the downward long-wave radiation ’L# approximates that of a black-
body at a temperature about 20 C below air temperature, whereas under a cloudy sky this radiative
temperature is only 2 C below air temperature. Since the upward radiation ’L" can be approximated by
radiation from a body roughly at air temperature, net long-wave radiation is upwards, and is about
100 W m2 under clear skies, or 10 W m2 under cloudy skies. At night, ’S¼0, so on clear nights
’n100 W m2, whereas on cloudy nights this radiative cooling is significantly reduced.
For surfaces or bodies that absorb energy, including single leaves or entire canopies, net radiation ’n
is the balance between the incoming short- and long-wave radiation, outgoing reflected radiation and
thermal radiation from the body itself, determined by its surface temperature Ts. This is described by
’n ¼ 1  a
ð
Þ’S þ ’L#  ’L" Ts
ð
Þ;
ð2:19Þ
where a is the albedo, or reflection coefficient, of the surface, ’S is incoming short-wave (solar)
radiation, ’L# is incoming long-wave radiation from all sources and ’L"(Ts) is the outgoing long-
wave radiation produced by the surface at temperature Ts. In this case the net radiation ’n is the energy
retained or absorbed by the body, and used to drive processes such as metabolism, evaporation of water
from the surface, transpiration or simply to heat the body (see Section 2.4.2).
Radiation absorbed by a surface can be stored as heat, or dissipated as sensible or latent heat. Storage
of heat changes the temperature of the surface, and the rate of change of temperature and the rate of
heating S (W m2) are related by the simple relationship
S ¼ zrscps
dTs
dt ;
ð2:20Þ
where z (m) is an effective depth over which heating occurs, rs (kg m3) is the density of the surface
material, and cps (J kg1 K1) is the specific heat capacity of this material. We assume the metabolic
energy consumption is negligible.
The outward flux of long-wave radiation ’L" in Eq. (2.19) depends on the temperature Ts of the
surface but the exchange of thermal radiation changes that surface temperature, so there is an element
of circularity in the solution of the energy balance equations. Accordingly, it is often more convenient
to assume radiative losses from the surface occur as if the surface was at air temperature, as ’n then
corresponds to the net radiation measured by radiometers. The consequences of this assumption are
not generally significant.
For many purposes net radiation ’n over forests can be estimated from empirical relationships of
the form
’n ¼ an þ bn’s;
ð2:21Þ
where ’s is the total short-wave radiation, which need not be measured nearby. Jarvis et al. give values
of bn for coniferous forests which range from 0.71 to 0.91; a value of 0.8 will seldom be much in
error although
Moore (1976) gives ’n¼0.67’s(4510)W m2
for
winter
months
and
34
Chapter 2

--- Page 50 ---

’n¼0.85’s(558)W m2 for summer. Federer (1968) found bn¼0.83 for a hardwood forest while
Rauner (1976) indicated that 85% of ’s was absorbed by a dense aspen stand (i.e. bn0.85) and Kalma
and Fuchs (1976) give bn¼0.8 for a citrus orchard.
Comparison of Eqs. (2.19) and (2.21) suggest bn1a, where a is albedo, whilst an is an estimate of
the average value of net ’L and thus depends on local climate as well as forest structure. For coniferous
forests average ’L varies widely, from 6 to 126 W m2, with a median value of about 60 W m2.
There are few values available for deciduous forests other than that given by Federer (1968):
an¼89 W m2. Almeida and Landsberg (2003), in a recent detailed study of eucalyptus plantations
in Brazil, found that bn had a value of 0.82, but an had values of 8.9 and 7.8; considerably lower
than the values found in earlier studies.
b) Albedo of Forest Canopies
The albedo a, or reflectivity, of the canopy regarded as a surface, is an important aspect of canopy energy
balance (see Eq. 2.19). In general, the reflectivity of surfaces decreases with their roughness, and since forest
canopies are rough their reflectivity is low. Changes in albedo with the height of canopy elements are
illustrated in Figure 2.12 (from Stanhill 1970). Representative (mean daily) values of a are about 0.10 for
coniferous forests (Jarvis et al. 1976) and 0.16 for deciduous forests (Rauner 1976). There have been several
thorough studies of the radiation balance of forests, e.g. those by Moore (1976) on P. radiata in South
Australia, and Stewart and Thom (1973) on Pinus sylvestris in England. Moore identified variations in
albedo through the day, but these were relatively trivial and he concluded that a mean albedo of 0.110.01
could be applied to the canopy for all solar elevations. Pinker (1982) found that the albedo of a tropical
evergreen forest showed very marked diurnal variation, from about 0.17 (early morning/evening) to 0.11
midday. There were also seasonal differences. Ollinger et al. (2008) found that albedo varied linearly with
nitrogen content in the canopy, from 0.08 at 0.5%N to 0.17 at 2.5%N.
2.4.2. Energy Balance and Flux Equations
The study of energy balance is based on a fundamental law of physics: the law of conservation of
energy. Energy may be converted from one form to another, but it cannot be created or destroyed. The
radiative energy that is absorbed by a body is not lost from the system; rather, it is transformed into other
0.3
Winter barley
Natural pasture
Evergreen scrub forest
Eucalyptus plantation
Aleppo pine forest
Mixed hardwood forest
0.2
0.1
Albedo (a)
1.0
10
Vegetation height (m)
100
FIGURE 2.12 Relationship between mean albedo and the height of vegetation. The vertical lines correspond to two standard deviations
(re-drawn from Stanhill 1970). (Note that the x-axis (vegetation height) is a logarithmic scale.)
Weather and Energy Balance
35

--- Page 51 ---

formsofenergy.Forexample,absorbed solarradiationcanbeconvertedinto metabolicenergy,storedasheat
with a consequent change in temperature of the body, or dissipated as latent heat through evaporation.
The term ‘heat’ loosely refers to energy that is measured by the temperature of the body, i.e. it is the
energy inherent in the thermal motion of molecules. Sensible heat is energy that causes a temperature
change, and is transferred from one body to another through conductance or convection when there is
a difference in temperature. Latent heat is the energy absorbed or released during a change of state, for
example from liquid water to water vapour. Bodies can also interchange radiant energy, e.g. short- or
long-wave radiation can be absorbed, and then re-radiated as long-wave radiation.
When discussing the energy balance of a system it is convenient to work with the fluxes of energy,
where the flux is the amount of energy crossing unit area in unit time. Flux is measured in units of
W m2. Conservation of energy requires that the total flux of energy across the boundary of the system
is zero. The various fluxes involved in energy balance are
1.
total incoming radiation ’T, including both short- and long-wave radiation;
2.
outgoing long-wave radiant energy ’L;
3.
sensible heat transfer H from the system to its environment;
4.
the latent heat L driving evaporation;
5.
energy M expended by the system through metabolic processes such as photosynthesis and
respiration and
6.
rate of energy storage S in the system.
Energy balance then requires that
’T ¼ ’L þ H þ L þ M þ S:
ð2:22Þ
The radiant heat loss ’L is determined by the surface temperature Ts (K) of the body by the Stefan–
Boltzman formula, i.e. Eq. (2.1). The sensible heat flux H is related to the temperature difference
between the surface and the environment, and the latent heat flux by the difference in vapour pressure
at the surface and in the environment of the body. These heat fluxes are considered in the next section.
In steady state, i.e. when the surface temperature of the system is steady, the rate of energy storage S is
zero. Finally, in most of the cases with which we are concerned here, the metabolic rate M is small
compared to the other fluxes and is neglected.
2.4.3. Resistances and Conductances
The concepts of resistance to the transfer of an entity, or conductance – the inverse of resistance – are
central to the study and measurement of fluxes to and from plant surfaces, and the movement of water
within plants (see Chapter 7). We therefore present, at this point, the basic algebra associated with these
ideas. It is worth making a note about units here. Processes such as transpiration have, for the most
part, been treated historically as mass fluxes, i.e. E is in kg m2 s1, so the units of resistances are s m1
and conductances are m s1. In the case of photosynthesis – treated as the flux of CO2 into leaves
through stomata (see Chapter 3) – the units now used are molar, so assimilation rate (A) is in
mol m2 s1, the driving force for diffusion is the gradient of partial pressure, or mole fraction and
molar conductances have dimensions mol m2 s1 (see Jones 1992, pp. 55–56, for a more complete
explanation). To convert between these units, use the following formula
gðms1Þ ¼
R T þ 273

36
Chapter 2

--- Page 52 ---

where R¼8.314 J K1 mol1 is the universal gas constant, T (C) is the temperature and P (Pa) is the
pressure. At 20 C at sea level the conversion factor is 0.024 m3 mol1.
The basic physical process involved in analysing the fluxes of heat and water vapour across the
surface or boundary between two media is Fick’s law of diffusion. This states that the flux F of any
entity is proportional to its concentration difference across the boundary, i.e.
F ¼ 1
r DC ¼ gDC;
ð2:24Þ
where DC is the difference between the concentration C of the entity in the two media, r is called the
resistance of the boundary for that entity, and its reciprocal g (¼1/r) is called conductance. This
equation is a direct analogue of Ohm’s law:
current amps
ð
Þ ¼ potential difference volts
ð
Þ=resistance ohms
ð
Þ:
In Section 2.4.5, we present an alternative form of Fick’s law applicable to transport in turbulent
media, as distinct from across a boundary.
Equations analogous to Eq. (2.24) govern the transport of many entities across a surface or
boundary separating two regions where there is a difference in concentration of the entity. Examples
are: energy, momentum, water vapour, CO2 and other chemical species. The resistance r in Eq. (2.24)
measures, for example the effects of turbulence, boundary layer thickness or stomatal aperture on the
flux of heat or water. The conductance g (¼1/r) is used for many purposes.
In many cases the surface across which Fick’s law applies is physically obvious: a membrane, or a leaf
surface with stomata. A more subtle example is a boundary layer. This is a region of air adjacent to a
surface that is not mixed with the surrounding bulk air and through which there is a continuous
concentration gradient. Fick’s law applies across such boundary layers, with the flux driven by the
concentration difference across the boundary layer as a whole. Boundary layer conductance, or
resistance, is also termed aerodynamic conductance, or resistance. It depends on the thickness of the
layer and hence on anything that affects this thickness, such as wind speed or size of the object.
In other cases, the surface is a composite entity. For instance, in transpiration water vapour first
diffuses through the leaf surface (via the stomata), and then through the boundary layer on its way to
the bulk air. In this case, the resistance is made up of two resistances in series: the stomatal resistance
and the boundary layer resistance. On the other hand, the individual stomata on a leaf act in parallel.
In general, resistances in series add algebraically, whereas for resistances in parallel, it is their
reciprocals (i.e. the conductances) that add algebraically, exactly as in the electrical analogue. For
example, suppose r1 and r2 are two resistances and g1 and g2 are the corresponding conductances. If the
resistances are in series, the total resistance rT and total conductance gT are given by
rT ¼ r1 þ r2
and
gT ¼
g1g2
g1 þ g2
:
ð2:25Þ
On the other hand, if the resistances are in parallel,
rT ¼
r1r2
r1 þ r2
and
gT ¼ g1 þ g2:
ð2:26Þ
Resistance and conductance depend on the entity being transported, and on the external conditions.
For example, conductances of water vapour and CO2 through stomata differ because their molecular
weights differ. They are also affected by stomatal aperture, and hence by variables such as leaf
Weather and Energy Balance
37

--- Page 53 ---

temperature and atmospheric vapour pressure deficit. However, it is commonly assumed that the
boundary layer resistance (and hence conductance) for heat and water vapour are the same.
a) Boundary Layer Conductance
The effectiveness of leaf–air coupling is influenced by the leaf aerodynamic or boundary layer conduc-
tance (gb). This is a measure of the effectiveness with which entities such as heat or water vapour are
transferred away from leaf surfaces, or CO2 towards them. Whatever the wind speed at a point in a plant
canopy, the frictional forces between foliage surfaces and the moving air cause the layer of air in contact
with the foliage surfaces to be moving much more slowly than the bulk air. Flow within leaf boundary
layers is laminar and transfer of water vapour, heat and CO2 across them is by molecular diffusion. The
thickness of the layer depends on leaf size and shape, wind speed and turbulence in the bulk air. This is
illustrated by Figure 2.13 which shows boundary layer conductance as a function of leaf size and wind
speed. Outside the laminar layer the transfer of entities entrained in the air is by turbulent diffusion or
forced convection, a process orders of magnitude faster than molecular diffusion. It is worth noting that
the influence of wind speed varies with the density of foliage: dense foliage causes mutual sheltering so
that the effective wind speed across foliage may be significantly lower than speeds that may be measured
in open spaces (see Landsberg and Thom 1971, Landsberg and Powell 1973).
In the interests of simplification and space, we omit detailed discussion of leaf boundary layers here.
For most practical purposes approximate values of gb will suffice. They can be obtained from the
literature (e.g. Grace 1983), or estimated from the graphical presentation (nomogram) given by Jones
(1992, p. 65), who also provides a detailed treatment. Grace quotes the following formulae for gb under
forced convection, and based on engineering results derived for flat plates,
gb ¼

0:048K 0:67u0:8=d0:2

where K (cm2 s1) is the diffusion coefficient in air (K¼0.21 for heat and 0.24 for water vapour),
d (cm) is the characteristic dimension of the leaf, and u (m s1) is wind speed.
b) Stomatal Conductance
Stomata provide direct pathways between leaves and the air: they are the active interface between plants
and their atmospheric environment. Within the sub-stomatal cavities wet cells are exposed to the air
and allow the capture of CO2, but this wet surface inevitably results in the loss of water vapour through
the stomata. Stomatal resistance is a measure of the resistance to diffusion of CO2 or water vapour
molecules from the stomatal walls to the opening of the stomatal pores. Concomitantly, stomatal
conductance gS (m s1) is a measure of the rate of diffusion along this pathway. This fundamentally
important variable is discussed in detail in Chapter 3.
Once water vapour has diffused through the stomata it then has to cross the leaf boundary layer with
conductance gb, so water vapour traverses two resistances in series between its source on wet walls of the
stomatal cavities and the free air. The total conductance gV for water vapour is given by
gV ¼
gSgb
gS þ gb
ð2:28Þ
from Eq. (2.25) since gS and gb are in series.
2.4.4. Heat and Vapour Fluxes
We now apply the equation for Fick’s law (Eq. (2.24)) to two important fluxes: the sensible heat flux
from a surface into the surrounding air, and the latent heat or water vapour flux from a region of
saturated air in contact with the surface to the surrounding air. The temperatures of the surface and of
the air are denoted by Ts and Ta (K), respectively: vapour pressure of the surrounding air is ea (Pa), and
the atmospheric pressure is P (Pa). (Subscripts ‘s’ and ‘a’ are used to denote the surface and
surrounding air, respectively.)
The sensible heat of a body is the amount of energy stored in that body and is the product of its
volume, its temperature above some reference temperature (e.g. 0 C), its density, and its specific heat
capacity. The sensible heat flux H (W m2) from the surface of the system into the air is primarily
through conduction from the surface to the surrounding air, given by
H ¼ gHracpa Ts  Ta
ð

ð2:29Þ
where gH (m s1) is the boundary layer conductance for heat, ra¼1.204 kg m3 is the density of dry
air, and cpa¼1004 J kg1 K1 is the specific heat capacity of dry air.
The latent heat flux is L¼lE, where E (kg m2 s1) is the rate of evaporation and
l¼2.454 MJ kg1 is the latent heat of vaporisation of water. Since E is the flux of water vapour
from saturated air adjacent to the evaporating surface to the surrounding air, E¼gVDw, where w is the
absolute humidity and is related to vapour pressure e through Eqs. (2.13) and (2.14). It can then be

2.4.5. Energy Balance of a Surface
If Eqs. (2.29) and (2.30) for H and L are used in the energy balance Eq. (2.22), assuming M¼0 and that
the surface is in steady state with the surrounding air so S¼0, it can be shown (e.g. Monteith 1973,
Jones 1992) that the rate of evaporation of water E (kg m2 s1) from the surface is given by
lE ¼ s’n þ gHracpaD
s þ ggH=gV
;
ð2:31Þ
where gV and gH (m s1) are the conductances of water vapour and heat from the surface to the
surrounding air, D is the vapour pressure deficit of the surrounding air, and s is the derivative des(T)/dT
evaluated at the air temperature. The other symbols are as defined above.
For a wet surface there is no resistance to the transport of vapour into the boundary layer, so gVgH,
and Eq. (2.31) can then be used to estimate the potential or wet-surface evaporation rate E0, e.g. of
water that has been intercepted by a leaf during rain:
lE0 ¼ s’n þ gHracpaD
s þ g
:
ð2:32Þ
Evaporation from the soil surface is also in principle given by Eq. (2.31), but in this case there is a
resistance to transport of vapour in the soil to the soil surface that, like the leaf stomatal resistance, is in
series with the boundary layer resistance. Because this transport resistance increases dramatically as the
soil dries out, i.e. gV decreases, the ratio gH/gV increases and soil evaporation declines as the soil dries.
Evaporation from a wet leaf and from the soil are considered further in Chapter 7.
2.5 CANOPY ENERGY BALANCE AND TRANSPIRATION
In order to understand the energy balance of whole canopies it is necessary to take into account an
additional process: that of mass transfer of entities such as heat, water vapour and CO2 from the
movement of bulk air within the canopy to the atmospheric boundary layer above the canopy. The
atmospheric boundary layer is the surface region of the atmosphere, within which air flow is deter-
mined primarily by the surface friction and the vertical gradient of temperature (Kaimal and Finnigan
1994). However, despite the complexity of these turbulent transfer processes, results very similar to
Eqs. (2.29) and (2.30) are obtained for whole-canopy heat and water vapour flux and which lead to
relatively simple, widely used equations for transpiration. In this section, we present those results
required to determine the energy balance and transpiration of a canopy.
2.5.1. Wind and Transfer Processes
Transfer of entities between foliage and the atmosphere is a two-stage process. First, transfer occurs
between the foliage and the bulk air within the canopy across the leaf boundary layer, as described by
Eqs. (2.29) and (2.30). This transfer clearly depends on conditions within the canopy space, as
determined by the interaction of the canopy as a whole with its environment. The second stage is
the transfer between the bulk air and the atmospheric boundary layer above the canopy, and is
described by similar equations given later: i.e. Eqs. (2.42) and (2.43). However, in this case the transfer
is across a vertical region of space, not a discrete boundary, and is turbulent. The flux is proportional to
the product of the concentration gradient and a turbulent exchange coefficient.
40
Chapter 2

--- Page 56 ---

Turbulence can be envisaged as consisting of eddies – discrete parcels or bubbles of air, each of which
has certain properties: momentum (massvelocity); temperature (heat content); humidity (water
vapour content) and CO2 content. In air flowing over a rough surface, eddy size will tend to increase
with distance away from the surface because near the surface the air is slowed by momentum absorbed
by the roughness of the surface. Eddies displaced either upwards or downwards will conserve their
properties until they disintegrate and merge with the airstream at the level reached. We can use
momentum as a tracer to obtain estimates of the boundary layer conductance required in the various
transfer equations. Momentum transfer by eddies and momentum absorption by surfaces, and the
consequent reduction in wind speed, induce wind profiles characteristic of the surfaces. Wind profiles
reflect the change in horizontal wind speed at different heights above the surface. These are much easier
to measure than the fluxes of heat and water vapour.
The wind speed u (m s1) over open vegetation is related to the height z (m) through the empirical
relationship
u zð Þ ¼ u
k ln z  d
z0


;
ð2:33Þ
where the constant k0.41 is called von Karman’s constant, u (m s1) is called the friction velocity,
and d (m) and z0 (m) are parameters. Note that wind speed declines essentially to zero at a height
z¼z0þd. The parameter d is called the apparent reference height, or zero-plane displacement, and is
zero over open ground and some fraction of the canopy height for a crop or forest. The parameter z0 is
called the roughness length, and is a measure of the aerodynamic roughness or momentum absorbing
capacity of the surface or canopy.
The value of Eq. (2.33) is that it allows the boundary layer conductance to be estimated by an
analysis of momentum transfer between the canopy and free air. By analogy with the flux equations for
water vapour and sensible heat, the momentum flux t (kg m1 s2) is given by
t ¼ Km

so u0¼0. Based on the empirical observation that momentum flux is constant for considerable heights
above a canopy, Monteith (1973), and see also Monteith and Unsworth (1990), showed that
t ¼ ru2

--- Page 57 ---

Both d and z0 increase with crop height, but Monteith (1973) summarised data that showed these
quantities also vary systematically with wind speed. Jarvis et al. (1976) reviewed values of z0 and d
relative to canopy height H for coniferous forests: there was considerable variation but
z0/H¼0.080.05 and d/H¼0.80.09. Shuttleworth (1989) reported values of z0/H and d/H of 0.06
and 0.86 for a tropical (Amazonian) forest. In general, we can expect the upward displacement of the
wind profile to be more pronounced when canopies are dense than when they are sparse. In the limit of
an extremely dense canopy d would tend towards H, while z0 becomes progressively smaller, as it does
for a smooth land surface covered by short grass.
A survey by Kelliher et al. (1993) produced values of gb for forest canopies between about 0.07 and
0.2 m s1. Kelliher et al. plotted the values against vegetation height, as a contrast to grasslands, but
there was no consistent relationship between forest height and boundary layer conductance. The reason
my lie in the varying densities of the stands examined (see Figure 2.11 and associated comment).
2.5.2. Partitioning Absorbed Energy
Conservation of energy in a canopy requires that ’n be partitioned into latent heat L¼lE through
evaporation or transpiration, the sensible heat H absorbed by the adjacent air, and heat stored below
the surface where ’n is measured, including the ground, and denoted by G:
’n ¼ lE þ H þ G:
ð2:38Þ
Over any 24 h period G will usually be negligible and we will for simplicity neglect it in the following
analysis, although it should not be ignored in detailed short-term analyses.
Energy partitioning is often described by the ratio of H to lE, known as the Bowen ratio b:
b ¼ H=lE:
ð2:39Þ
Substituting this into Eq. (2.38) and ignoring G we obtain
lE ¼
1
1 þ b ’n
and
H ¼
b
1 þ b ’n:
ð2:40Þ
Therefore, given values for ’n and b, we can obtain estimates of lE.
The principal advantage of the Bowen ratio is that it is relatively straightforward to determine
experimentally, although the measurements required are technically demanding. The basis of the
method lies in the fact that we may write lE and H as fluxes of water vapour and heat in terms of
vertical gradients of vapour pressure and temperature. In Section 2.4.2, we presented Fick’s law for the
flux of an entity across a boundary between regions in which the concentration C of the entity differed.
Here, we are more concerned with transport within an extended medium, where concentration
varies continuously. At any point in the medium, the flux F of an entity is proportional to the product
of its concentration gradient (@C/@z) and a turbulent exchange coefficient K. If the concentration
gradient is smooth and slowly varying, this can be written as the product of the boundary layer or
aerodynamic conductance (gb) and the difference in concentration at some reference level z¼0 and
at the height z. Thus
F ¼ K @C

b ¼ g DT

where DT and De are the changes in T and e measured across the same height interval Dz. The Bowen

from measurements above a canopy.
Jarvis et al. (1976) summarized the values of b determined over coniferous forests and found that for
most forest sites, irrespective of species, b for dry canopies varies between 0.1 and 1.5 (implying lE/’n
varies from about 0.9 to 0.4) and, when the canopy is wet with rain or dew, between 0.7 and þ0.4,
where negative values of b indicate that energy is being supplied to the surface. Stewart and Thom
(1973) show that b may rise to values of 5 or even 10 for pine forests during the day.
Since b is the ratio of sensible to latent heat fluxes and high values of b occur over vegetation during the
day, they must be associated with periods when latent heat flux (i.e. transpiration) is low, at least relative to
incoming energy. In general b is lower in the morning and evening, and when there is cloud cover.
There seem to be few, if any, direct measures of b for deciduous forests, but Rauner (1976) gives
average values of ’n and lE for a number of such forests. Since Eq. (2.40) may be rewritten as
b¼’n/lE1, b can be calculated from these values. Rauner gives integrated data for summer for
deciduous forest types in northern Europe from which we obtain the average values lE/wn, and hence
b, given in Table 2.2. Since b varies non-linearly during the day (e.g. Stewart and Thom 1973), average
values should be treated with considerable caution.

2.5.3. Canopy Transpiration
The evaporation of water from plant communities is of major importance, not only to the commu-
nities themselves but also to the hydrology of their region. Indeed, water loss from plant communities
has massive consequences for life on a global scale. We deal with this in some detail in Chapter 7, but
because evaporation is driven by weather conditions, the equations that allow us to calculate it are
presented here.
The term ‘evapotranspiration’ includes transpiration, as well as the evaporation of intercepted water
on wet foliage (which in some climates may be greater than transpiration losses) and evaporation from
wet soil under plant canopies. But transpiration, the evaporation of water from sub-stomatal cavities, is
probably the most important process overall, since it affects both soil water balance and the way plants
grow.
Transpiration from the canopy of a plant community is a two-stage process: evaporation from the
sub-stomatal canopies into the canopy space in response to foliage–air vapour pressure gradients, and
mass transfer of water vapour, entrained in eddies, from the canopy space to the atmosphere above the
community. In principle, we can calculate transpiration from foliage using Eq. (2.31), and from
canopies using Eq. (2.42). However, there are complications in both cases. For instance, Eq. (2.31)
requires net radiation and temperature within the canopy, and we also need the vapour pressure
within the canopy to calculate mass flux from the reference level (z¼dþz0) to the air above the
canopy. Since these depend on transpiration from the foliage into the canopy space and the rate of
removal of the resulting vapour by turbulent transfer, we clearly need some approximations to solve the
problem.
Here, we present a combination of energy-balance mass-transfer equation first derived by Penman
(1948) and subsequently refined and modified by Monteith (1965). The well known and widely used
current version is generally known as the Penman–Monteith equation. It is based on the ‘big-leaf
approximation’ which treats canopies as a single large leaf. As such it ignores the complications and
problems that arise from the fact that canopies are 3D, that the sources and sinks for water vapour and
heat may be at different levels (see Raupach and Finnigan 1988), and that there are gradients of various
environmental factors within the canopy. Under the big-leaf approximation, canopy transpiration is
given immediately by Eq. (2.31) provided the conductances gH and gVare interpreted appropriately for
a canopy rather than a single leaf.
Water vapour from inside leaves proceeds first through the stomata, then through the leaf boundary
layer to the bulk air in the canopy and finally through the canopy boundary layer to the atmosphere
above the canopy. Similarly, sensible heat from leaves proceeds through the leaf and canopy boundary
layers in series. Accordingly, the boundary layer conductance gb is the combined conductance of the leaf
and canopy boundary layers in series, so
gb ¼
gblgbC
gbl þ gbC
;
ð2:45Þ
where gbl and gbC are the leaf and canopy boundary layer conductances, respectively. Note that, at low
wind speeds in dense plant canopies, the conductance of the combined leaf and canopy boundary
layers may be low enough to effectively uncouple the vapour pressure at the leaf surface from that in the
bulk air, so transpiration may increase the humidity of the air surrounding foliage in the canopy.
We also assume Eq. (2.37) holds, i.e. boundary layer conductances for water, heat and mass transport
are identical.
44
Chapter 2

--- Page 60 ---

a) Canopy Conductance
Since the Penman–Monteith equation is a ‘big-leaf approximation’, canopy conductance gC is used in
place of stomatal conductance gS. The stomatal conductances of all leaves in the canopy are in
parallel and hence sum algebraically, so for a canopy with n layers of foliage, each with a leaf area li
per unit ground area and an average stomatal conductance gSi, canopy conductance gC can be estimated
from
gC ¼
Xn
i¼1gSili
ð2:46Þ
and L¼Sli is the leaf area index of the canopy. Various authors have shown that an approach such as
this to integrate leaf-level stomatal conductance to whole canopy conductance can provide reliable
estimates of gC. White et al. (1999) used this approach to determine canopy conductance from leaf-
level observations of stomatal conductance and then developed a model for the environmental
response of gC (see also Section 3.2.5). Ewers et al. (2007) calculated whole canopy conductance for
Pinus taeda subject to a range of irrigation and fertilisation treatments while taking into account the
effects of systematic variation in VPD. The resulting canopy averaged stomatal conductance showed a
strong linear relationship with stomatal conductance determined directly
from porometer
measurements.
Kelliher et al. (1995) inverted the Penman–Monteith equation to obtain Eq. (2.53), given below, and
then used measured stand water use rates to determine canopy conductance gC of a number of stands.
They then showed that the ratio of the maximum canopy conductance gCx to the maximum stomatal
conductance gSx, obtained from gas-exchange observations, increased from around 1.7 for very open
canopies, to about 3 at an LAI of about 6 and then remained constant. The ratio gCx/gSx is non-zero for
open canopies because their observations of stand water use included both transpiration by the canopy,
and evaporation from the soil. We use this relationship in Chapter 9 to model canopy conductance in
3-PG.
Novick et al. (2009) examined the possibility of applying a Jarvis-type model (Jarvis 1976; see
Section 3.2.5) to model the response of canopy conductance gC to irradiance, vapour pressure deficit
and soil water status. In this context, canopy conductance gCref (mmol m2 s1) is the conductance
obtained when soil water is not limiting and the VPD is 1 kPa. From published data from a range of
forest canopies, including boreal, temperate and tropical stands, they found that 75% of the variation
in gCref could be explained by the empirical relationship
gCref ¼ 98:2 AS
AFH þ 37:3;
ð2:47Þ
where AS is the average single-tree sapwood cross-sectional area (cm2), AF is the average single-tree
foliage area (m2) and H is stand height (m). Novick et al. provide a table of values for AS¼ S/AF for
forests at 22 locations.
b) Penman–Monteith Equation for Canopy Transpiration
The Penman–Monteith equation for canopy transpiration is derived from the heat balance and sensible
and latent heat flux equations by using a linear approximation to eliminate the surface temperature,
and then partition the absorbed energy (net radiation) into sensible and latent heat transfer. Details of
the algebra and a discussion of the assumptions and implications can be found in Monteith and
Weather and Energy Balance
45

--- Page 61 ---

Unsworth (1990). The equation can be written in several forms, but the following form is similar to
Eq. (2.31) for evaporation from a surface and is the more commonly used:
lE ¼ s’na þ gbracpaD
s þ g 1 þ gb=gC
ð
Þ ;
ð2:48Þ
where ’na is the net radiation absorbed by the canopy, taking into account Beer’s Law and the
combined leaf and canopy boundary layer conductance gb is given by Eq. (2.45). Note that in this
form, gC is the conductance for the whole canopy.
If the boundary layer conductance gb is very small, then Eq. (2.48) gives the so-called ‘equilibrium
evaporation rate’ Eeq, where
lEeq ¼
s
s þ g ’na:
ð2:49Þ
This is the evaporation rate from a leaf completely decoupled from its environment by a very thick
boundary layer. On the other hand, if the boundary layer conductance is very large, the evaporation
rate is the so-called ‘imposed evaporation rate’ Eimp, where
lEimp ¼ racpagC=g


ð2:50Þ
For other values of gb, lE is bounded by these two values. To express this, Jarvis and McNaughton
(1986) introduced the quantity O defined by
O ¼
1 þ s=g
1 þ s=g þ gb=gC
ð2:51Þ
which takes values in the range 0–1, so that
E ¼ OEeq þ 1  O
ð
ÞEimp:
ð2:52Þ
They identify O as a measure of the decoupling of conditions at the leaf surfaces from those in the
atmosphere. When O!0, leaf surfaces become strongly coupled to the atmosphere, and in particular
the vapour pressure deficit at a leaf surface is identical to that of the atmosphere. On the other hand,
when O!1, leaf surfaces are poorly coupled to the atmosphere and conditions at the leaf surfaces find
their own local equilibrium.
Jarvis and McNaughton (1986) tabulate O values that range from 0.8 to 0.9 for crops such as alfalfa
and pasture, for which gb is small, down to 0.1–0.2 for forests, which are much more strongly coupled
to the atmosphere. Phillips et al. (2001), in a study on Norway spruce in Sweden, showed that O varied
from about 0 to about 0.05 as canopy conductance increased from 0 to 0.6 mm s1.
When transpiration rate and net radiation measurements are available in research projects, either on
a per tree basis – in which case we need to know the leaf area per tree, and express transpiration in
terms of unit leaf area – or on a ground area basis, Eq. (2.48) can be inverted to give estimates of canopy
conductance:
gC ¼

This is a relatively widely used procedure for inferring canopy conductance, particularly in associa-
tion with sap flow measurements (see Chapter 3).
46
Chapter 2

--- Page 62 ---

2.5.4. Eddy Correlation
It is not our intention, in this book, to deal with measurement procedures and techniques, but we
cannot avoid a brief discussion of the technique of ‘eddy correlation’, increasingly recognised as the
standard technique for producing continuous data on the fluxes of CO2, water vapour and heat to and
from extensive vegetated surfaces. Forests are important among these, as although much depleted, they
still cover about 48106 km2 of the earth’s surface. Because of the importance of forests in global
carbon and hydrological balances, data on fluxes to and from forests are particularly important.
Eddy correlation technology has recently improved to the point where the measurements are
routine. Under the umbrella of the FLUXNET program, more than 400 stations at which CO2 and
water vapour fluxes are measured have been established world-wide (Baldocchi 2008), using very
similar technology and software to log and analyse data that will increase our understanding of energy
and mass exchanges between the biosphere and the atmosphere. Information from the various
FLUXNET locations is beginning to accumulate in the technical literature, providing new quantitative
insights into those exchanges and long-term measurements of net ecosystem exchange of water vapour
and CO2. Examples can be found in Goulden et al. (1996), one of the first papers that provided
continuous measurements over a number of years, and in Law et al. (2002), who carried out a meta-
analysis of data from a subset of FLUXNET sites, as well as other papers in the special issue of
Agricultural and Forest Meteorology (2002, vol. 113). Baldocchi’s (2008) paper provides the best
overview of results to date, discussing history and background, methodological issues and presenting
and analysing a summary of the results being obtained.
Eddy correlation rests on the fact that eddies – relatively coherent, rotating parcels of air charac-
terised by properties such as momentum, temperature, CO2 and water vapour concentrations – are
vertically mixed by turbulence so that their properties are transferred between levels. Mean flux across a
plane implies correlation between the wind component normal to the plane and the entity in question
(Kaimal and Finnigan, 1994), so measurements of the vertical component of wind velocity and of the
properties being transferred provide a direct measure of flux across the plane. These measurements
require accurate, fast-response sensors: typically 3D sonic anemometers, open-path gas analysers and
thermocouple thermometers. The instrument company Campbell Scientific Inc. have a website (see
References) that provides an outline of the theory underlying the eddy correlation technique, as well as
descriptions of the instruments required, discussion of requirements for their exposure and a descrip-
tion of data processing procedures. The properties and correct deployment of eddy correlation
instruments have been the subject of a great deal of investigation and development over recent decades.
The mathematics underlying eddy covariance are relatively complex. A rigorous treatment can be
found in Kaimal and Finnigan (1994), and there are discussions of various aspects of the procedure in
Lee et al. (2004). Essentially, the flux of the entity of interest (water vapour, CO2, heat, momentum) is
computed as a covariance F between the instantaneous deviations w 0 in vertical wind speed w from its
mean value w and the instantaneous deviation s0 in concentration of some property or entity
(described in terms of its mixing ratio s¼r/ra, where r is the density of the entity and ra is air
density) from its mean value s, multiplied by mean air density
F s½ 	 ¼ raw0s0:
ð2:54Þ
The measurements are made at a single point in a plane above the surface, representing the upper
boundary of the system under study. F[s] is integrated from the ground to the measuring height and
measurements are normally averaged over about 30 min.
Weather and Energy Balance
47

--- Page 63 ---

There are a number of assumptions involved in the method, and also various difficulties relating to
site characteristics, fetch length (the ratio of the distance to a major discontinuity in surface type, e.g. a
change from forest to grassland, to the height of the instrument), signal processing and error identifi-
cation and analysis. A useful discussion of uncertainties and data interpretation is provided by Loescher
et al. (2006), and Baldocchi (2008) discusses some of the causes of error and the efforts being made to
evaluate and correct the errors.
