We discussed in Chapter 2 the concept of a hierarchy of plant growth processes and dealt in some
detail with the weather factors that act on, and interact with, plant communities. In this chapter, we
describe the major physiological processes that determine how fast, and by how much, plants grow.
The principles are the same for all mesophytic, land-based plants, including trees.
Forest productivity may be described as the net rate of dry matter production by a stand, over any
specified period. Productivity depends on the interception of radiant energy by leaves and the
conversion of this energy into carbohydrate by the process of photosynthesis, relative to the rate of
loss by respiration, the death and shedding of organs and the death of individual trees. The effectiveness
with which radiant energy is converted to chemical energy depends on the photosynthetic properties of
Physiological Ecology of Forest Production
Joe Landsberg and Peter Sands
ISSN 1936-7961, DOI: 10.1016/S1936-7961(11)04003-6
Copyright # 2011 Elsevier Inc.
All rights reserved.

--- Page 65 ---

the leaves; the ability of the leaves to draw CO2 from the atmosphere depends on the functioning of
their stomata. The carbohydrates resulting from net photosynthesis are transported to all parts of the
plant for respiration and growth.
Commercial forestry is concerned with optimising forest productivity, and ensuring that the
maximum possible amount of accumulated carbohydrate is converted into marketable product. All
silvicultural practices are, in effect, directed towards these ends. For example, practices such as thinning
are, at least implicitly, aimed at producing the optimum canopy size and structure, while fertilisation
increases leaf area and may improve the photosynthetic efficiency of leaves.
We deal with the hierarchy of processes that determine growth in plant communities at the level of
cells, tissues and leaves (e.g. photosynthesis and stomatal behaviour), whole plants (e.g. biomass
allocation) and stands (e.g. litterfall and stem mortality). We need to use knowledge of physiological
processes to explain – preferably in quantitative terms – how they respond to both external and internal
conditions, resulting in changes in the state of the plants, and hence of the community as a whole.
Consideration of physiological processes leads to the problem of the differences in response times of
processes at different levels, which range over a factor of about 100 (see Table 2.1). This means that to
simulate the change likely to take place across a single time step at level N, we may have to simulate
changes across 100 intervals at level N1. Furthermore, there may well be a number of processes at
level N1, with interactions between them, which must be simulated to calculate the state of
the system at level N after a single time step. The common procedure is to simulate state transitions,
i.e. changes to the state of a system, at level N in terms of approximate descriptions of the mechanisms
that operate at level N1.
State transitions in physical or biological systems are usually continuous, and are specified by
equations giving the rate of change of each state variable as a function of the set of state variables
and the external forces (or factors). These transition functions may be in the form of differential
equations, or in their simpler difference equation form giving the actual change that occurs over a finite
time interval. In the case of the centrally important physiological process of photosynthesis, the
equations are written in terms of our understanding of biochemical reactions, but they are written
for generic leaves, or cohorts of leaves, taken to represent the whole system (usually a forest stand in the
context of this book) and the outputs are scaled by the amount of foliage carried by the stand.
We could regard all the other processes involved – stomatal functioning, water relations, nutrient
uptake and utilisation – as constraints on carbohydrate production and transport. However, everything
is inter-related: stand structure and canopy architecture determine light interception and hence, the
energy available for photosynthesis; photosynthesis depends on the supply of CO2 diffusing through
stomata to the chloroplasts; transpiration is driven by atmospheric conditions acting on the canopy
and interacts with soil water to determine leaf water potential, which has a strong influence on stomatal
conductance, and so on. Respiration – the process of oxidation of carbohydrates to CO2 and water
vapour, which makes energy available for growth and maintenance in all living cells – can be considered
as operating at a number of levels. In leaves it is intimately linked with photosynthesis; in structural and
storage tissues it proceeds at rates determined by the mass of living tissue, and is affected by
temperature. In considering physiological processes our task, as far as possible, is to account for and
quantify these interactions and the constraints they place on carbohydrate production and
distribution.
In the case of photosynthesis this can be done by considering the primary driving variable that
determines the rate at which the process operates – in this case irradiance (photon flux density) – and
the variables that may, directly or indirectly, constrain the process. These include the rate of supply of
CO2 to the chloroplasts where the biochemical reactions take place, the rate of regeneration of a
carboxylating enzyme (ribulose-bisphosphate carboxylase), and temperature. Stomatal opening, which
50
Chapter 3

--- Page 66 ---

directly influences the rate of CO2 supply, is affected by irradiance, the leaf–air vapour pressure deficit
of the atmosphere and by leaf water potential. It is also affected by the concentration of CO2 in the sub-
stomatal cavities.
We deal with photosynthesis and stomatal conductance in some detail in this chapter. Respiration is
considered in less detail while our discussion of allocation focuses on principles and concepts.
3.1 PHOTOSYNTHESIS
Photosynthesis, the primary production process in plants, uses light energy to convert CO2
into carbohydrates. It comprises light and dark reactions that involve the removal of electrons from
water – resulting in the release of O2 – and the donation of those electrons to CO2, leading to reduced
carbon compounds with a gain in free energy. The basic equation may be written
H2O þ CO2 þ light energy ! O2 þ CH2O
where CH2O is a generic carbohydrate. These processes take place in the chloroplasts and are strongly
dependent on photon flux density and intracellular CO2 concentration.
3.1.1. Overview of Biochemistry of Photosynthesis
The primary photochemical processes in photosynthesis are the light reactions. These are initiated
when photosynthetically active radiation is absorbed by the photosynthetic pigments, thus raising the
energy level of the light harvesting chlorophyll molecules to an excited state. A specialised chlorophyll
molecule donates electrons to electron carriers, which then flow down the electron transport chain. The
energy of the electrons is used to generate the high-energy products adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and
the reduced form of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH) (see Figure 3.1). In this
process water is split to provide reactive hydrogen, with the release of O2.
The photochemical light reactions proceed at a rate that depends on the quality and intensity of
light alone. Although high, the efficiency of energy transfer is not 100% because the incident photons
span a range of frequencies and any excess of energy by a particular photon above that required for
excitation of chlorophyll acceptors can result in heat or fluorescence. However, although the light
reactions are not affected by temperature (or CO2 concentration), electron transport from chlorophyll
molecules to ATP is strongly dependent on temperature.
The dark reactions do not require light to drive them, but use the energy inherent in ATP and the
reducing power of NADPH produced in the light reactions to reduce CO2 to carbohydrate (CH2O).
The initial acceptor of CO2 is ribulose-bisphosphate (RuBP), and the reaction is catalysed by the
enzyme RuBP carboxylase (Rubisco). The first carbon reduction product in almost all tree species is the
3-carbon (C3) compound 3-phosphoglyceric acid (PGA). This triphosphate is then reduced by ATP
and NADPH in a complex sequence of reactions known as the Calvin–Benson cycle, and further
metabolised to form sugars. The RuBP substrate for the initial carboxylation reaction is also regener-
ated in the Calvin-Benson cycle. These processes depend on both intracellular CO2 concentration and
on temperature.
The above paragraphs describe the dominant photosynthetic process for cool-temperate species,
including trees, cereals, root crops and lower plants. There is a large and important group of plants,
including important crops such as maize and sugar cane, but few trees, where the first carbon reduction
Physiological Processes
51

--- Page 67 ---

product is a 4-carbon (C4) compound. A third group of plants employs crassulacean acid metabolism
(CAM) in which the initial carboxylation reaction occurs during the night and the stomata are closed
during the day but open during the night. This is important for water conservation, and the CAM
pathway is usually found in succulent plants from arid areas, but also in bromeliads such as pineapple.
The C4 and CAM photosynthetic pathways will not be considered in detail here.
In C3 plants, Rubisco constitutes a major fraction of leaf protein. Because it has a relatively low
affinity for CO2 and is competitively inhibited by an oxygen fixing enzyme (oxygenase), it has been
implicated as a factor that limits the rate of photosynthesis. The regeneration of RuBP appears to be
dependent on the concentration of CO2 at the carboxylation sites [CO2]. If this is low, CO2 assimilation
is not limited by the amount of RuBP but by the amount of the enzyme Rubisco. As [CO2] increases,
electron transport reactions, and therefore the capacity to regenerate RuBP, become limiting, i.e. the
supply of RuBP becomes limiting.
This simplified outline of the biochemistry of photosynthesis ignores many uncertainties and
sources of variation but will suffice for the present purposes. A detailed mechanistic model of
photosynthesis utilising these principles was developed by Farquhar et al. (1980) and subsequently
modified by Farquhar and von Caemmerer (1982). von Caemmerer (2000) provides a detailed
discussion of the photosynthetic biochemistry and the development of the model.
3.1.2. Gas Analysis and the Observation of Photosynthetic Data
The data upon which our understanding of photosynthetic relationships is based come primarily from
gas exchange experiments. In these experiments, a leaf – or, particularly in the case of conifers, many
leaves (needles) – is placed in a chamber in which photon flux density, leaf temperature and ambient
CO2 concentration can all be controlled. Infra-red gas analysers are used to measure the concentrations
of CO2 and water vapour in the air streams entering and leaving the chamber. From the rate of flow of
air through the chamber, it is possible to calculate the assimilation and transpiration rates. Using a

FIGURE 3.1 Diagrammatic representation of the primary processes in photosynthesis. Light energy absorbed by photosystem II (PS-II),
chlorophyll removes an electron from water, resulting in the evolution of oxygen. Energy is transferred from the excited chlorophyll
molecules to a (reduced) substance, Q. Electrons pass from Q to PS-I, some of the energy being used to generate ATP from ADP. Light
harvesting chlorophyll in PS-I provide the energy for substance X to reduce NADP – an electron carrier – to NADPH2. Ribulose-bisphosphate
(RuBP) is the initial acceptor of CO2, the reaction being catalysed by RuBP carboxylase. RuBP is regenerated. The energy of ATP is used in the
reactions in which CO2 is reduced to PGA (i.e. 3-phosphoglyceric acid) and hence to sugars.
52
Chapter 3

--- Page 68 ---

simple model (described below) based on an understanding of the gas analysis apparatus, the
intracellular CO2 concentration and stomatal conductance can be inferred from the assimilation and
transpiration rates and the ambient CO2 concentration.
Initially, gas analysis techniques involved large, and often temperamental, laboratory systems
involving air pumps, flow meters, drying and humidification sub-systems, large lamps which generated
a great deal of heat that had to be removed, temperature controllers and CO2 analysers. Output was on
chart recorders that required laborious processing. The first field systems appeared in the 1970s. Within
the last 20 years pump technology and control systems have been improved and miniaturised to the
point where accurate measurements in the field are now routine. Good commercial systems that allow
measurements to be made over short periods are now available, so the number of measurements
made – and hence sample size – can be greatly increased. There are many designs of gas exchange
systems, and we do not intend to review them. Details can be found in many of the hundreds of papers
in the literature dealing with leaf gas exchange. Of the papers we cite in this book Mott (1988) provides
a detailed description of the system he used, as does Leuning (1995), who also provides a diagram.
Modern, off-the-shelf units are virtually fully automated and provide immediate outputs of
photosynthesis, dark respiration rates (no light) and conductance values at a range of easily controlled
temperatures, light intensities, CO2 concentrations and air humidity. The result has been an avalanche
of measurements on a wide range of plants in all sorts of conditions. It is normal in gas exchange work
to use leaves attached to the plants. The essence of these systems is a small leaf chamber in which the air
is well stirred by a fan or fans to maximise boundary layer conductance. The area of leaf enclosed is
accurately known in the case of flat leaves, whereas the surface area of conifer needles is usually
determined retrospectively. Boundary layer conductance is usually measured by independent means,
for example by measuring the heat flux from heated leaf replicas or vapour flux from wet blotting
paper. Light is supplied by lamps with known spectral characteristics and measured at leaf level by
quantum sensors. Some chambers consist of diffusing spheres, with the foliage centrally mounted on
fine wire grids, so that light flux density is uniform in all directions. Leaf surface and air temperatures
are normally measured with fine wire thermocouples. Rates of air flow through the chamber must be
accurately known, and the humidity and CO2 concentrations of the input and output streams are
measured. Ambient CO2 concentration Ca and vapour pressure ea are those of the well-mixed input air
stream.
The measurements outlined above provide all the information needed to calculate stomatal con-
ductance gS (mol m2 s1), assimilation rate A (mol m2 s1), and leaf surface and intercellular
concentrations of CO2 (Cs and Ci, mol mol1, respectively). Assimilation and transpiration rates are
obtained directly from the changes in CO2 concentration and vapour pressure between the input and
outgoing airstreams – and hence the mass flux per unit leaf area can be determined (see Eqs. (2.11) and
(2.12), describing air humidity and vapour pressure, in Chapter 2). Since the leaf-to-air vapour
pressure deficit D (Pa) is known, gS can also be calculated directly; see Eq. (3.1). If CO2 concentration
Cs at the leaf surface is of interest, it can be calculated from the knowledge that the flux A of CO2 into
the leaf is across two resistances in series, i.e. A¼gb(CaCs)¼gS(CsCi) where gb is the boundary
layer conductance, so we can solve for Cs and, since gS can be obtained independently from transpira-
tion measurements, for Ci, which must always be obtained as a derived variable.
Assimilation rate is analysed below in terms of its dependence on intracellular CO2 concentration,
and its dependence on photon flux density. A plot of assimilation as a function of intracellular CO2
concentration is called an A–Ci curve, while a plot of assimilation rate as a function of photon flux
density is called a light response curve. Examples of these curves were given earlier in Figure 2.1.
In Section 3.2, conductance will be analysed in terms of its response to environmental factors such as
photon flux density, temperature, vapour pressure deficit and leaf water potential, but we outline here
the usual procedure for determining gS at leaf level.
Physiological Processes
53

--- Page 69 ---

If the flux of water vapour per unit leaf area (the transpiration rate, E) is measured, either in a
gas exchange system or using some form of diffusion porometer (see, for example Jones 1992,
pp. 140–144), the conductance for water vapour is given by:
gV ¼
EP
ei  ea
ð
Þ :
ð3:1Þ
The vapour pressure ei within the stomatal cavity is assumed to be the saturated vapour pressure es(Tl)
at the leaf temperature Tl, ea is the vapour pressure of the air adjacent to the leaf, E is the vapour flux
per unit leaf area (i.e. transpiration) and P is atmospheric pressure. In much of the older literature on
stomatal conductance the units used were m s1, but where, as is now more usual, flux density is
expressed in molar terms (i.e. E is in mol m2 s1), gV and gS have as units mol m2 s1. The
conversion formula was given in Eq. (2.23). If the boundary layer resistance (rb) is kept low by high
air flows across the leaves in the measuring equipment, it can be ignored for most purposes.
The equation for the conductance of CO2 through stomata, which essentially defines the rate of
photosynthesis, is obtained from Eq. (3.4), i.e. gS¼A/(CaCi), where A is assimilation rate, Ca is
ambient CO2 concentration and Ci is the CO2 concentration in the intercellular spaces (i.e. sub-
stomatal cavities).
The molecular diffusivities for water vapour and CO2 are different owing to the different sizes of the
molecules, and hence the conductances for these two entities differ. The ratio for stomatal conduc-
tances is gS(water):gS(CO2)¼1.65:1, while the ratio for boundary layer conductances is 1.37:1 because
of the influence of turbulence. In practice, it usually suffices to assume that the conductance for water is
about 1.6 times the conductance for CO2.
Countless experiments have analysed the gas exchange of leaves and its dependence on environ-
mental factors. In some, conditions have been controlled by the experimenter, but in many the leaves
were in situ and the conditions were ambient. In the latter case there is a huge variation in the response
of assimilation or stomatal conductance to the environmental factors taken one at a time. In this case it
is more appropriate to fit an envelope curve to the data rather than a curve through the data (e.g. White
et al. 1999).
3.1.3. Empirical Relationships for CO2 Supply and Demand
A summary of the limitations imposed on photosynthesis by diffusion of CO2 to the sites of
photosynthesis and by the carboxylating enzyme is provided by Figure 3.2. This shows the A–Ci
curve, the commonly observed relationship between CO2 assimilation rate A and the intercellular
concentration Ci (mol mol1) of CO2. Examples are given in Wong et al. (1978) for Eucalyptus
pauciflora, Watson et al. (1978) for apples and Pearcy et al. (1982) for Euphorbia species, while
Wullschleger (1993) provides a comprehensive review covering 109 species. An A–Ci relationship
describes the demand for CO2, and can often be adequately described empirically by a rectangular
hyperbola of the form
A ¼ ACx
kCC
kCC þ ACx
 Rd;
ð3:2Þ
where ACx (mol m2 s1) is the saturated value of A for high Ci, the carboxylation efficiency kC
(mol m2 s1) is the initial slope of the A–Ci curve and Rd (mol m2 s1) is a respiration term. The
value G (mol mol1) of Ci at which A¼0 is known as the CO2 compensation point, and inverting
Eq. (3.2) gives

Note that although Eq. (3.2) is often adequate it is an approximation. For a more realistic form, see
Section 3.3.3b.
According to Farquhar and Sharkey (1982), the linear portion of the A–Ci curve with slope kC
corresponds to RuBP saturated photosynthesis. In this part of the curve there is ample substrate and
any increase in Ci results in activation of more enzyme, which increases the rate at which CO2 is fixed.
However, if the rate of RuBP carboxylation is increased sufficiently, the capacity to regenerate the
substrate becomes limiting, and further increases in Ci do not lead to concomitant increases in A,
i.e. the A–Ci curve saturates for sufficiently high Ci.
The A–Ci curve is also known as the ‘‘demand function’’. On the other hand, the supply of CO2 for
photosynthesis is determined by the rate at which CO2 diffuses through the stomata to the reaction site.
If gS (mol m2 s1) is the stomatal conductance, this supply rate is given by:
A ¼ gS Ca  Ci

where Ca (mol mol1) is the ambient CO2 (molar) concentration. This corresponds in Figure 3.2 to a
line through the ambient CO2 concentration Ca and with slope gS, and is known as the ‘supply
function’. The terms ‘demand function’ and ‘supply function’ were introduced by Raschke (cited by
Farquhar and Sharkey 1982). At equilibrium, the supply and demand of CO2 must balance. In Figure 3.2

--- Page 71 ---

this occurs at the point where the supply and demand curves cross. The downward projection from
their point of intersection to the Ci-axis gives the corresponding value of Ci.
It is a simple matter to solve Eqs. (3.2) and (3.4) for this point of intersection. A quadratic
equation for Ci is obtained, with the corresponding value of A then obtained from Eq. (3.4). Since
the resulting equation only applies when gS is constant and, as we show below, gS and A are
themselves coupled, this equation is of limited practical value and we do not give it here – we
return to a more rigorous analysis of the supply and demand curves in Section 3.3. However,
Eqs. (3.2) and (3.4), in association with Figure 3.2, provide a great deal of information about leaf
photosynthesis. For example, by examining the changes in the geometry of Figure 3.2 when either Ca
or gS is increased, it can be seen that both A and Ci must increase. On the other hand, increasing kC
increases A and reduces Ci for low Ca or gS. Equation (3.4) also suggests that if CaCi was constant,
leaf carbon assimilation rate could be expected to be linearly related to the conductance, gS. This
expectation has been realised in a number of studies, e.g. Wong et al. (1979) and references cited
there, and is illustrated in Figure 3.3.
3.1.4. Farquhar and Von Caemmerer Model of Leaf Photosynthesis
Early analyses of leaf photosynthesis led to a number of empirical models, generally based on fitting
hyperbolic equations to light response curves. Charles-Edwards (1981) and the original edition of
this book (Landsberg 1986b; pp.80–81) provide a detailed treatment of the mathematics of these
models. Although the basic photosynthetic light response was a simple empirical function not based
on any understanding of the biochemical basis of photosynthesis, these models did encapsulate the
basic processes of CO2 supply as controlled by stomatal conductance. On the other hand, Hahn has
developed both steady-state and dynamic models of the details of photosynthetic biochemistry (e.g.
see Hahn 1984), but Hahn’s models operate on the time scale of seconds to minutes so they are quite
unsuitable for modelling plant growth, despite their sound biochemical basis.

56
Chapter 3

--- Page 72 ---

In recent years the model developed by Farquhar et al. (1980) and Farquhar and von Caemmerer
(1982) has become widely accepted as the definitive treatment, and is the one now generally used. This
model calculates the photosynthetic rate as the minimum of possible limitations imposed by Rubisco
activity and represented by the parameter VCmax, or electron transport as reflected by RuBP regenera-
tion and represented by the parameter Jmax. Both limitations depend on the intercellular CO2
concentration, which is determined by stomatal conductance gS (mol m2 s1). The parameters Jmax
and VCmax (both with units mol m2 s1), as well as the maximum rate of oxygenation VOmax, which is
also required, are correlated with one another and with foliar nitrogen content (Field 1983; Medlyn
et al. 1999; Kattge et al. 2009). (We discuss plant nutrient requirements and dynamics in Chapter 6.)
Since their original publication, the equations of this model have been presented in various forms by
a number of authors, including Wang et al. (1992), Wullschleger (1993), de Pury and Farquhar (1997)
and Medlyn et al. (1999). Here, we present them in their simplest useful form, and provide representa-
tive parameter values derived from some of the papers cited. Our presentation follows von Caemmerer
(2000) who provides an in-depth discussion of photosynthetic biochemistry and a description and
parameterisation of the model for both C3 and C4 plants. The model predicts leaf assimilation as a
function of photon flux density, leaf temperature and the partial pressures of CO2 and O2 in the stomatal
cavity. Although changes in O2 partial pressure are extremely unlikely in nature, laboratory examination of
assimilation under different O2 regimes has been pivotal in deciphering the photosynthetic machinery.
a) Assimilation
In the Farquhar and von Caemmerer model assimilation rate A is given by:
A ¼ min AC; Aj


ð3:5Þ
where AC and Aj (mol m2 s1) are the rates of assimilation when carboxylation is limited by Rubisco
and RuBP regeneration, respectively, and Rd (mol m2 s1) is the rate of dark respiration. Assimilation
can also be limited by the rate at which triose phosphates (e.g. PGA) are utilised in the synthesis of
carbohydrates (von Caemmerer 2000). We choose to ignore this so-called export-limited assimilation
rate Ap here, but it could occur under conditions of high irradiance and CO2 supply along with low
temperatures.
When the rate of carboxylation is limited by the amount, activation state and kinetic properties of
Rubsico, AC is given by:
AC ¼

This equation shows that Ci depends on irradiance through J, and on temperature through the other
photosynthetic parameters (see 3.1.4b). Accordingly, the range of Ci over which photosynthesis is
RuBP-saturated (Ci<Ci) or RuBP-limited (Ci>Ci) depends on irradiance and temperature.
Equations (3.5), (3.6) and (3.8) give A as a function of intracellular CO2 concentration Ci, i.e. they
define an A–Ci curve. However, although both Eqs. (3.6) and (3.8) describe rectangular hyperbolae, the
overall response given by Eq. (3.5) is not of that form: the A–Ci curve switches from one form to
another at Ci¼Ci. Hence, although it is often possible to obtain a good empirical fit of an A–Ci curve
to a single rectangular hyperbola of the form of Eq. (3.2), the resulting parameters may not reflect the
actual underlying biology of the response. Sharkey et al. (2007) discuss the assumptions associated with
the equations of the Farquhar–von Caemmerer model and provide an outline of how the parameter
values can be estimated from measurements of CO2 assimilation. They also refer to a Website where a
spreadsheet ‘estimator utility’ can be found.
b) Temperature Effects
All biochemical reactions depend on temperature because enzyme activity is temperature dependent.
This is modelled by expressing the maximum reaction rates and Michaelis–Menten constants as
explicit functions of temperature. It is common practice to use the Arrhenius equation to express
the rate K of a biological process, e.g. a single reaction where a substrate produces various products, as a
function of the temperature. The Arrhenius equation is
K T
ð Þ ¼ k0eE=RT;
ð3:11Þ
where T (K) is absolute temperature, E (J mol1) is the activation energy of the process, the constant k0
can be expressed in terms of the rate K0 observed at some reference temperature T0 (K) and
R¼8.3145 J K1 mol1 is the universal gas constant. The Arrhenius equation can be used for each
of VCmax, VOmax, KC, KO, Rd and G in the Farquhar and von Caemmerer photosynthesis model.
Parameters are given in (c) below.
58
Chapter 3

--- Page 74 ---

The Arrhenius equation is based on the following assumptions: (a) before the reaction can proceed,
the substrate has to be raised to its activation energy E, (b) the rate at which the reaction proceeds is
proportional to the number of substrate molecules with energy E and (c) the number of molecules with
a given energy is given by the Boltzman distribution (Thornley and Johnson 1990). Over temperature
ranges commonly experienced in the field, Eq. (3.11) can be approximated by a simple exponential
function of temperature of the form
K T
ð Þ ¼ K0ek TT0
ð
Þ;
ð3:12Þ
where k¼E/RT02 and K0 is the rate at a temperature T0. The Q10 of a reaction is the ratio of its rates at
temperatures that differ by 10 C. It follows that at a reference temperature of 25 C (i.e. 298 K),
Q10¼e0.0135E, where the activation energy E is in kJ mol1.
The maximum rate of electron transport Jmax is also a function of temperature. However, electron
transport is a more complex process than a typical enzymatic reaction, and its temperature dependence
is not given by a standard Arrhenius function, but has a temperature optimum. Thornley and Johnson
(1990) give an expression for the rate of a reaction where the enzyme can exist in either an active or an
inactive state, with products formed from the activated state. They assumed that the rate of product
formation is given by the Arrhenius equation, and that the two forms of the enzyme are in an
equilibrium that favours the inactive state as temperature increases. The rate is then given by:
K T
ð Þ ¼
k0eE=RT
1 þ e STH
ð
Þ=RT ;
ð3:13Þ
where S (J K1 mol1) and H (J mol1) are the entropy and enthalpy of the active state relative to the
inactive state, respectively. It is this equation that is used to express Jmax as a function of temperature.
Parameters are given in (c) below.
The rate given by Eq. (3.13) has an optimum of Kopt at a temperature of Topt (K), given by:
Topt ¼

Although Eqs.(3.11)and (3.13)havea sound thermodynamic basis, direct measurementofquantitiessuch
as E, S and H is extremely difficult. Accordingly, E/R, S/R and H/R may be treated as empirical parameters.
In fact, if Eq. (3.13) is fitted to observed data, it is better to parameterise it with Kopt, Topt, E/R and H/R.
Other forms for K(T) have been used, e.g. a parabolic function of temperature. Although these often
give a statistically very good fit, they are not based on an understanding of the underlying processes and
hence cannot be extrapolated without considerable caution.
Seasonal acclimation of the temperature dependence of photosynthesis has been observed in the
context of a simple light response obtained from, e.g. Battaglia et al. (1996), and incorporated into a
canopy photosynthetic production model by Sands (1995b, 1996).
c) Parameterisation
Values of the parameters in the Farquhar and von Caemmerer photosynthesis model are obtained by
fitting the model to photosynthetic data from gas exchange experiments run under different levels of
ambient (and hence different intercellular) CO2 and O2 concentrations, different temperatures and
Physiological Processes
59

--- Page 75 ---

different illuminations. A set of standard values at a reference temperature of 25 C and ambient O2
concentration, i.e. O¼20.5 kPa, for each of the parameters VCmax, VOmax, KC, KO, Rd and G, and the
corresponding activation energies, is given in Table 3.1 (based on Table 2.3 in von Caemmerer 2000).
There is significant variation between species, especially in Jmax and VCmax. However, common values
are often assumed across a wide range of species, and values in Table 3.1 should be used where observed
values are not available.
Farquhar and Wong (1984) gave a¼0.385, with y¼0.67; Wullschleger (1993), in his analysis of 109
species used a value of a¼0.18; Leuning et al. (1995) used a¼0.2 and Medlyn et al. (2005a) used
a¼0.2, with y¼0.95, and 0.3 with 0.71. In practice, the values of a and y have only slight effects on the
estimated value of Jmax and we use those of von Caemmerer (2000), i.e. a¼0.36, y¼0.7.
Medlyn et al. (2002a) examined the temperature variation of these parameters using published
experimental data for a number of tree and crop species, with a strong focus on tree species. They
obtained values of VCmax at 25 C for deciduous trees ranging from 27 to 90, with a mean of
66.5 mmol m2 s1; the range for evergreen trees was 41–97, with a mean of 72.9 mmol m2 s1.
The values of the activation energy E for deciduous trees ranged from 33 to 74, with a mean of
48.7 kJ mol1; for evergreen trees the range was 35–61, with a mean value of 50.8 kJ mol1.
The temperature responses of both VCmax and Jmax vary seasonally and, as we noted earlier, are
influenced by leaf nitrogen content (Medlyn et al. 1999, 2002a,b; Kattge and Knorr 2007; Kattge et al.
2009). There is significant scatter in the data for anyone tree species and the relationships (linear through
a significant scatter of points) vary from species to species. VCmax always increases with increasing leaf N
content (expressed on a leaf area basis); Jmax usually does so too, but there are exceptions.
Wullschleger (1993) provided estimates of VCmax and Jmax for tree species from his collation of data
from 109 plant species. His values for trees are reproduced in Table 3.2. Wullschleger (1993) and
Medlyn et al. (2002a) plotted Jmax against VCmax, and obtained very similar linear relationships. In the
Wullschleger data, for all the plant species he examined, the points were described by:
Jmax ¼ 1:64VCmax þ 29:1

3.2 STOMATAL CONDUCTANCE
We have referred several times to the fact that stomatal conductance, which we have already encoun-
tered in Chapter 2, is a key process that constrains photosynthesis and transpiration. This was also
illustrated in our discussion of the supply and demand A–Ci curves in Section 3.1.3.
Stomata are apertures (minute pores) in leaf surfaces that provide direct pathways between the leaf
interior and the air; they are the active interface between plants and their atmospheric environment.
The precise structure, the amount of wax and the number and distribution of stomata vary between
species. Plants with stomata on a single surface of their leaves – the underside – are called hyposto-
matous; those with stomata on both surfaces are called amphistomatous. Stomatal conductance gS is a
measure of the rate of diffusion of CO2 into the leaf or water vapour molecules out of the cavities
behind the stomata. It is a function of the number, size and degree of opening of the stomata in the
surface layers of leaves.
A useful generalised diagram of the structure of stomata and the pathways of water vapour through
them is provided by Figure 2.1 in Leuning et al. (2004). Within the sub-stomatal cavities wet cells are
exposed to the air and allow the capture of CO2, but this wet surface inevitably results in the loss of
water vapour through the stomata. A proper coupling of assimilation to atmospheric CO2 requires a
sound understanding of stomatal function and how it is related to environmental variables. In this
section, we discuss stomatal function and various empirical relationships that have been used to
represent stomatal conductance in response to environmental variables. In Section 3.3, we present a
fully coupled model of assimilation and stomatal function.
Changes in stomatal aperture, and hence gS, depend on changes in turgor pressure in the guard cells.
These are specialised epidermal cells on either side of the aperture, hydraulically linked to surrounding
TABLE 3.2

Physiological Processes
61

--- Page 77 ---

epidermal cells. If the turgor pressure in the guard cells falls, the cells tend to become flaccid and
stomatal apertures are reduced. The details of the mechanisms that cause movement of water in and
out of guard cells have been the subject of enormous amounts of research – see discussion in Jones
(1992), and references cited by him. However, this is a level too far down for our purposes, and we will
confine ourselves to a phenomenological approach, based on observations at the leaf-level of stomatal
responses to irradiance, vapour pressure deficit, leaf water potential and CO2 concentrations.
3.2.1. Stomatal Response to Irradiance
Stomatal responses to irradiance (photon flux density) are driven by photochemical processes, inter-
acting with leaf–air vapour pressure difference, temperature and leaf water status. Many of the earlier
studies on stomatal conductance, particularly those based on field measurements with porometers (e.g.
Jarvis 1976; Watts et al. 1976) showed considerable scatter because of these interactions, but the
relationship with irradiance was unequivocally established by many laboratory studies (see, for
example Warrit et al. 1980) to be hyperbolic. Warrit et al. fitted the equation
gS ¼
gmax
1 þ b=’P
ð
Þ ;
ð3:18Þ
where gmax is conductance under high light levels, ’P (mmol m2 s1) denotes photon flux density
incident on the leaf and b (mmol m2 s1) indicates the sensitivity of gS to ’P and is the value of ’P at
which gS¼gmax/2. Values of b for apple leaves varied through the growing season and were in the range
50–90 mmol m2 s1, while gS generally reached the gmax values reported in the experiments at
’P400 mmol m2 s1.
3.2.2. Stomatal Response to Vapour Pressure Deficit
Landmark studies by Lange et al. (1971) and by Schulze et al. (1972), who used early leaf chambers, and
Hall et al. (1976) showed that gs declines with increasing vapour pressure deficit D. A field survey by
Waring and Franklin (1979) showed, for eight conifer species in the Pacific North West region of the
United States, that the response was essentially universal; the data showed widely varying values of gmax
and differences between species in the point at which gS begins to fall in response to increasing D. (This
has since been explained in terms of differences in the hydraulic conductivity of stems and branches.
See comments below, and Section 7.4.3.) Whitehead et al. (1981) produced data from tropical forests
that showed similar patterns, and there have been many similar results since then. The form of the gS
versus D relationship is not always clear from experimental data: e.g. Thorpe et al. (1980) indicated a
linear decline, Lohammar et al. (1980) suggested a hyperbolic relationship, while Lloyd (1991) found
an exponential expression (see Figure 6.13 in Jones 1992 for a collection of representative curves).
Various approaches used to express the dependence of gs on VPD can be conveniently summarised by:
gS ¼
gmax 1  D=D0
ð
Þ
ðlinearÞ
gmax= 1 þ D=D0
ð

ðhyperbolicÞ
gmaxeD=D0
ðexponentialÞ

ð3:19Þ
where gmax is conductance when D¼0, D0 is the value of D at which gS¼gmax/2.
All of these relationships exhibit a decline in gS for increasing D even when D is close to zero.
However, it is also observed, e.g. Waring and Franklin (1979), that gS is unaffected by values of D below
a threshold of around 1 kPa. In fact, many of the data to which Eqs. (3.19) have been fitted actually
62
Chapter 3

--- Page 78 ---

show such a threshold (e.g. Dye and Olbrich 1993; White et al. 1999). This can be accommodated in
Eq. (3.19) by replacing D/D0 either by (D/D0)n, where n is a power 2 or by (DD1)/D0, where D1 is
the threshold and setting gS¼gmax for D	D1.
We note here the important finding of Oren et al. (1999) that stomatal sensitivity to D was
proportional to the magnitude of gS at low D (	l kPa). For canopy conductance gS they established
the relationship
gS ¼ gSref  m ln D;
ð3:20Þ
where gSref is the value of gS at D¼1 kPa and serves as a reference conductance, and m is a parameter.
The value of m is related to the regulation of leaf water potential by stomata, to prevent cavitation.
However, we note that the relationship expressed by Eq. (3.20) predicts that gS goes to zero for
moderate values of D, whereas observation suggests that gS remains non-zero but small for large D
(e.g. White et al. 1999).
3.2.3. Stomatal Response to CO2
Stomatal responses to ambient CO2 concentration Ca were established in early laboratory studies, e.g.
Heath (1949), cited by Mott (1988), Meidner and Mansfield (1968). Jarvis (1976) incorporated them
into his semi-empirical model of stomatal conductance, assuming a threshold region were there is no
response to Ca between 0 and 100 ppm followed by a linear decline to a minimum value at 1000 ppm.
(Currently, Ca380 ppm ¼ 350 mPa.)
Cowan and Farquhar (1977) proposed that stomatal control has evolved to maximise carbon gain
while minimising water loss. Wong et al. (1979) tested this experimentally and demonstrated a linear
relationship between A and gS (Figure 3.3). Mott (1988) showed unequivocally that stomata respond to
intercellular CO2 concentrations (Ci) rather than to ambient concentrations (Ca) or concentrations at
the leaf surface. This is consistent with the idea that stomatal conductance varies so that it matches the
photosynthetic capacity of leaves, but Morison and Jarvis (1983), commenting on the results of careful
leaf chamber experiments, stated that ‘‘because intercellular CO2 concentrations inhibit stomatal
opening but stimulate CO2 assimilation, there is no unique relationship between gS and A. . . . There
is no evidence to suggest that the limited correlation between gS and A (was) caused by anything other
than the parallel responses of stomata and CO2 assimilation to light . . . both light and CO2 can affect
stomata separately. The results show that the parallel responses of assimilation and conductance to
light result in constant intercellular CO2 concentrations, and not that stomata maintain a ‘constant Ci’.’’.
There is now strong evidence that stomata of broadleaf trees are responsive to high CO2, whereas
those of conifers are not. Brodribb et al. (2009) found that ‘‘All species responded to low Ca by
increasing gS but only angiosperm stomata demonstrated a significant closing response when Ca was
elevated to 600 mmol mol1. As a result, angiosperms showed significantly greater increases in water-
use efficiency under elevated Ca than the other lineages.’’ and they ‘‘suggest that the angiosperms have
mechanisms for detecting and responding to increases in Ca that are absent from earlier diverging
lineages, and these mechanisms impart a greater capacity to optimise water-use efficiency.’’
If the responses of stomata to CO2 are manifest at ecological and global scales they may be important
in relation to the water use and energy balance of plant communities as well as their productivity. The
possibilities and implications arising from these effects were explored by Field et al. (1995), although
many of their analyses were necessarily speculative, since evidence on scales larger than controlled
environment plant chambers was, at that time, scarce. More is now coming to hand from various FACE
(Free Air Carbon Dioxide Experiments), in which plants in the field are subjected to long-term increased
CO2 concentrations. In general, under elevated atmospheric CO2 concentrations it seems likely that
Physiological Processes
63

--- Page 79 ---

forests may use slightly less water per unit CO2 assimilated than agricultural crops and grassland. This is
because water use by forests is well coupled to the atmosphere, so that canopy conductance is of over-
riding importance relative to boundary layer conductance (see Eqs. (2.50)–(2.52) and the remarks
following them).
Clearly a complete model of gS responses to environmental factors has to incorporate the effects of
responses to CO2. Since this involves consideration of photosynthesis, it is presented in the context of
the coupled model discussed in Section 3.3.
3.2.4. Stomatal Response to Leaf Water Potential
We turn now to the effects of leaf water potential on stomatal conductance. Leaf water potential
provides a measure of the water status of leaves. It has units of pressure, or energy per unit volume, and
is soundly based in thermodynamic theory. It may not necessarily be the best parameter to use in
analysis of physiological functions, but it is the most common and widely measured, and other
parameters that describe the water status of plant tissue can be related to, or derived from, it. Water
potential and plant water relations in general are discussed in Chapter 7.
Because stomatal guard cells are hydraulically connected to the adjacent mesophyll cells, changes in
the water status of guard cells, caused by the evaporation of water through the stomata, are transmitted
to the surrounding leaf tissue. The rate at which water is transported from soil to roots and up water
potential gradients through the conducting tissues of plants depends on soil–root contact, and how wet
the soil is, on the resistances in the flow pathways and on the rate of water loss (see Section 7.3). From
the ecophysiological point of view, as opposed to purely physiological understanding, predictive
models of stomatal functioning will have to be part of a suite of sub-models that includes water loss
and water movement through plants. Here, we simply note the rather obvious point that if the rate of
water supply to leaves is not fast enough to replace the water lost by transpiration, the rate of water loss
must be reduced by increased stomatal resistance or the leaves will desiccate and die. If resistances to
flow in those parts of the pathway towards the distal (foliage) end were lower than those near the
beginning (uptake end), loss of water from the leaves would cause cavitation in the conducting tissues,
disrupting the water supply pathways. Stomata must, therefore, act as the regulators of plant water
potential, maintaining it at values that can be sustained by the conducting system of the trees, i.e. at
values high enough to avoid catastrophic cavitation (see Section 7.4). Landsberg and Gower (1997)
stated this explicitly: ‘‘Stomata will close when the plant cannot sustain the rate of water loss driven by
vapour pressure deficit. This suggests that all empirical relationships established between gS and D
must have been influenced by the capacity (conductivity) of the plant hydraulic system. . .’’.
It may be that inadequate recognition of the pressure regulator role of stomata in the past was, to
some extent, responsible for a number of apparently inconsistent results from various studies at the
level of leaves and canopies. Stomata generally appear insensitive to cl until some threshold (critical
value) is reached, below which stomatal closure takes place. The work of Sperry and his collaborators
(see Chapter 7 and Tyree and Sperry 1988; Sperry 1995, 2000; Sperry et al. 1998; Sperry et al. 2002) has
now made clear that the point at which stomata start to close depends on the hydraulic characteristics
of the soil–root–stem–petiole system and the ratio of root–stem conductivity to leaf area (see
Section 7.4.1 and Eq. (7.29); Oren et al. 1999; Ewers et al. 2000, 2001).
We note here a finding that has implications for much work on plant water relations, where
measurements of pre-dawn leaf water potential (cpd) have been widely used to estimate water potential
in the root zone. The assumption is that stomata are closed at night and that night-time transpiration is
zero. Therefore, during the night, plant and soil water potentials would equilibrate, and plant water
potential measured before dawn, i.e. before stomata open and transpiration begins, provide a measure
64
Chapter 3

--- Page 80 ---

of soil water potential. This has provided a means of estimating cs in the rooting volumes of trees.
Kavanagh et al. (2007) provide a discussion and review of some of the literature on this issue. However,
if stomata do not close fully at night and transpiration continues, plant–soil water potential equilibra-
tion will not occur. Dawson et al. (2007) surveyed data from a wide range of tree and shrub species
across a diversity of ecosystems and found that most species transpired at night, and that the ratio of
night- to day-time transpiration was up to 10–20%. They found that night-time stomatal conductance
was proportional to atmospheric vapour pressure deficits (D), and declined with increasing interval
since the last rain, which obviously reflected soil water content. Kavanagh et al. examined, in detail,
four species in an experimental forest in the Pacific North-West (United States) and identified non-
linear relationships between sap-flux at night and D. They found that nocturnal stomatal conductance
averaged about 33% of mid-morning conductance. They recommend that pre-dawn leaf potential
(cpd) measurements should be made when D is quite low, and that estimates of cpd be corrected for the
effects of D on conductance using the simple relationship:
cpd ¼ cs  gSnocD
ð
Þ

ð3:21Þ
where gSnoc is nocturnal stomatal conductance and gL is leaf specific conductance, which is determined
on the basis of measurements of mid-morning leaf potential and conductance following times when
nocturnal conductance was zero or very low.
In the first edition of this book (Landsberg 1986b) the general effect of leaf water potential on
stomata was described by the function
gS ¼

mechanisms that control stomatal responses of tall trees. He discusses the role and influence of guard
cell mechanics, hydraulic feedbacks and interactions between guard cells and epidermal conditions.
However, his model is too complex to be of interest at the level of trees and canopies.

3.2.5. Phenomenological Models of Stomatal Conductance
Because of the natural variation in stomatal conductance, the exact mathematical forms of the
relationships between gs and environmental factors that we use in the context of simulations at the
level of whole trees or stands are not generally very important. Statistical analyses and fitting
procedures applied to the data from studies on different species, under different conditions tend to
lead to expressions of different form, although the essential shapes of the responses are likely to be
similar. For example, Eqs. (3.18)–(3.22) could be combined to give a simple empirical model:
gS ¼
gSmaxeD=D0
1 þ cl=cl0
ð
Þn
½

 1 þ ’P0=’P
½

ð3:23Þ
in which the ‘constants’ D0, cl0, n and ’P0 obtained from different data sets would differ.
Equation (3.23) expresses stomatal conductance as the product of a maximum conductance gSmax
(obtained when conditions are optimal) and functions of each of the environmental factors, where each
function is 1 under optimal conditions.
a) The Jarvis Model
Jarvis (1976) presented an empirically based multiplicative model of stomatal responses to internal and
external environmental variables, originally parameterised using data from leaf chambers and mea-
surements made in canopies with porometers. This model has been applied mainly at the tree and
canopy level (e.g. White et al. 1999; Granier et al. 2000; Kim et al. 2008; Ward et al. 2008). The basic
equation is
gS ¼ gSmaxf’pfDfcfTfCa;
ð3:24Þ
where gSmax is the theoretical maximum conductance and the f terms denote response functions to
photon flux density ’P, vapour pressure deficit D, water potential cl, temperature T and atmospheric
CO2 content Ca. As in Eq. (3.23), each of the functions f is 1 under optimal conditions and declines to
0 as conditions depart from their optimum. Jarvis provided functional forms, and a variety of different
response functions have been fitted to this model by other people. Equations (3.18)–(3.22) could be
employed for these response functions. Ward et al. (2008) found that a Jarvis-type model with f’p and
fD described by expressions similar to Eqs. (3.18) and (3.19) described the diurnal variation in gS with
only minor bias.
A comprehensive study of the effects of leaf nitrogen N, D, and T on gas exchange in Pinus radiata
was conducted by Sheriff and Mattay (1995). They showed both light saturated assimilation rate Amax
and stomatal conductance gS as three-dimensional response surfaces modelled as multiplicative func-
tions of N, D and T. These relationships explained 86% of variation in Amax and 91% of variation in gS.
There is a threshold level of leaf nitrogen of about 0.4 mmol g1 above which Amax increases linearly
with N, with a corresponding threshold of 0.44 mmol g1 for gS. In a similar study on Eucalyptus
globulus, Sheriff and Nambiar (1991) found thresholds in the range 0.2–0.35 mmol g1, depending on
the timing and nature of nutrient application.
Studies on different systems are likely to yield different functional relationships (see, for example Kim
et al. 2008; Ward et al. 2008), but the general form for each variable will be the same. The use of gSmax – as
in Eq. (3.23) or Eq. (3.24) – is sometimes criticised on the grounds that it is difficult to establish this
value, but it has frequently been measured (see, for example Ko¨rner et al. 1979; Kelliher et al. 1995).
Data to which equations such as Eq. (3.24) are applied often come from experiments where all
factors affecting gS vary simultaneously. In that case the value of gSmax must be at least the largest
66
Chapter 3

--- Page 82 ---

observed gS, and envelope curves may be fitted to plots of gS versus each factor separately, rather than a
curve through the data points because each point is affected by more than a single factor. When this is
done the predicted gS often overestimate the observed values, indicating either that a factor affecting gS
has been omitted, or that there are interactions between the factors. From a practical point of view, it
might suffice to fit Eq. (3.24) directly to all the data in the data set. An example of this kind of analysis
can be found in White et al. (1999), where an equation of this form was applied to both single-leaf and
whole-canopy conductance of Eucalyptus globulus and E. nitens.
b) The Ball and Berry Model
At the level of leaf physiology, detailed models of the way stomata function, and their response to
varying conditions imposed on them, have been developed on the basis of the information and
understanding derived from relatively precise data from leaf chambers and gas exchange systems.
An important development in this respect was the empirical model produced by Ball et al. (1987),
which incorporated the correlation between assimilation A and gS (see Figure 3.3) and included the
effects of humidity and ambient CO2 concentrations on conductance. The equation they obtained,
which described the results of a number of gas exchange experiments, was
gS ¼ g0 þ g1AHr=Cs:
ð3:25Þ
The equation does not include effects of irradiance explicitly; these are implicit in A, which tends to 0 as
’P approaches 0. In Eq. (3.25) g0 is the ‘residual’ conductance at this point and g1 is another parameter,
Hr is the relative humidity of the air, and Cs is CO2 concentration at the leaf surface.
There are some conceptual problems with this model. Firstly, Mott’s (1988) experiments showed
clearly that stomata respond to sub-stomatal CO2 rather than Cs; secondly, stomata respond to
ambient vapour pressure deficit D rather than relative humidity. These anomalies were resolved by
Leuning (1995) who replaced Hr by a function of D, and Cs by (CsG), where G is the CO2
compensation point, i.e. the value of CO2 at which net photosynthesis is zero. So we have
gS ¼ g0 þ
g1A
Cs  G f D
ð Þ:
ð3:26Þ
Leuning chose the humidity function used successfully by Lohammar (1980), i.e. the hyperbolic
response f(D)¼1/(1þD0/D) in Eq. (3.19).
Tuzet et al. (2003) modified the model further (see also Leuning et al. 2004) substituting sub-
stomatal CO2 concentration Ci for Cs and introducing an empirical logistic function f(cl) in place of
f(D) to describe the sensitivity of stomata to leaf water potential. The resulting equation for this model is
gS ¼ g0 þ
g1A

ð3:28Þ
In this expression cl is bulk leaf water potential, cref a reference potential, and sl a parameter.
Tuzet et al. did not include the f(D) term in their model. Leuning et al. (2004) justified this omission
on the grounds that the linkage between gS and atmospheric humidity is complicated by the
Physiological Processes
67

--- Page 83 ---

dependence of stomatal conductance on leaf water potential. Consistent with this they showed that
stomatal conductance depends on D in the manner predicted by f(D) in Eq. (3.26) when soil water
potential is zero (wet soil). Under these conditions stomatal conductance begins to decline with
increasing D because as rates of flow through the system increase, cl decreases and guard cell turgor
declines.
Equation (3.27) is a valuable analytical tool that can be used to examine the behaviour of stomata at
leaf level in association with appropriate measurements (see Leuning et al. 2004), but the need for
values of A, Ci, G and cl virtually precludes the use of those equations for tree or stand scale modelling,
except where detailed physiological measurements have been made. However, the hydraulic properties
of trees can be measured, or inferred, from measurements of sap flow (see, for example Ewers et al.
2007; Ward et al. 2008) and, for many purposes, at the tree and stand level, estimates of stomatal
conductance obtained using some form of f(D) can be used to estimate canopy conductance. Novick
et al. (2009) showed that the ratios of sapwood conducting area to leaf area provide a useful surrogate
for stem hydraulic conductivity; see Section 7.4.1 and Eq. (7.29). Alternatively, soil water balances,
which can be calculated with useful accuracy, can be used to derive a measure of relative soil water
content that can be substituted for f(cl) (see Granier and Loustau 1994; also Eqs. (3.29) and (3.30),
below).
c) Parameterisation of Ball and Berry Model
Where sufficient data are available the parameters in Eq. (3.26) can be estimated statistically. For
example, Medlyn et al. (2005a), using extensive physiological data from three flux measurement sites in
Europe, obtained empirical values for g0, g1 and D0. They made the assumption – reasonable for all field
data relating to trees – that CsCa since boundary layer conductance for coniferous foliage (needles) is
very high relative to gS. The equation obtained for P. sitchensis was
gS ¼ 0:037 þ
6:33A

It is clear, from various published studies (for example Ward et al. 2008; Kim et al. 2008) as well as
the Medlyn et al. (2005a) study, that any of Eqs. (3.18)–(3.27) can probably be fitted to particular sets
of field data. They will yield parameter values appropriate to the particular data set used, but these are
unlikely to be applicable to plant communities in general. The variation in gS within plant commu-
nities, as reflected in efforts to characterise it from field measurements, is such that, in most cases, any
of a number of equations will fit the data (e.g. see Watts et al. 1976, or for more recent results,
68
Chapter 3

--- Page 84 ---

Ewers et al. 2007). But good values of gS are essential for calculating photosynthesis and transpiration,
and we need to work towards the most generally valid and applicable equation. In this respect
Eqs. (3.26) and (3.27) provide the best theoretical expressions available and some form of these
equations should be used wherever possible. The vapour pressure deficit response function may take
any of several forms; in general the response tends to be non-linear, so either Eq. (3.20) or one of the
forms in (3.19), is likely to be most appropriate.
3.3 COUPLED MODEL OF PHOTOSYNTHESIS AND STOMATAL FUNCTION
In our discussion of A–Ci curves in Section 3.1.3, in particular in the context of Figure 3.2, we
introduced the concepts of CO2 supply and demand curves. We showed that the intersection of
these two curves gave the actual assimilation rate and intracellular CO2 concentration that would
occur under those conditions. In this context, the demand for CO2 was given by Eq. (3.2), while supply
was governed by the linear relationship of Eq. (3.4).
The Farquhar and von Caemmerer leaf photosynthesis model expresses assimilation in terms of the
partial pressure Ci of CO2 in the stomatal cavity. However, it is actually the partial pressure Cc of CO2 at
the chloroplasts that drives photosynthesis, and hence it is necessary to take into account the
conductance of CO2 from the intercellular space to the chloroplasts. Jarvis (1971) describes how to
calculate this: it is essentially the initial slope kC of the demand function (see Figure 3.2) and is
therefore likely to reflect the effectiveness of enzymatic CO2 fixation. On the other hand, Farquhar and
von Caemmerer (1982) suggested that chloroplast and intercellular CO2 concentrations are effectively
the same, hence this (so-called) mesophyll conductance can be ignored. The issue of mesophyll
conductance is currently receiving renewed attention, but since Flexas et al. (2009) conclude that
‘‘the field is probably still in its infancy, and many controversial issues remain to be resolved’’, we
continue to follow the original formulation of the Farquhar and von Caemmerer model and ignore
mesophyll conductance.
In order to model assimilation rate A in terms of atmospheric conditions it is necessary to couple Ci
with atmospheric CO2 partial pressure Ca. This can be done mathematically by deriving explicit
equations for A and Ci in terms of Ca and environmental factors that affect stomatal conductance.
However, because these are fourth order polynomials, the more basic equations describing the demand
for and supply of CO2 to the stomatal cavity are solved iteratively by repeated linearisation.
In the coupled model, the coupling of Ci to Ca is done separately for RuBP-limited and RuBP-
saturated assimilation, and the actual assimilation rate is the minimum of that determined in these two
cases, exactly as in the Farquhar and von Caemmerer model. The mathematics of the coupled model
are non-trivial, and in the following section we simply outline the model and its numerical solution,
and present some results from applying it. There is a spreadsheet implementation on the PHYSECOL
website.
3.3.1. The Supply and Demand Curves
Irrespective of whether assimilation is RuBP limited or RuBP saturated, assimilation in the Farquhar
and von Caemmerer model can be written as a function of Ci of the form
A ¼ aCi þ b
gCi þ d  Rd:
ð3:32Þ
Physiological Processes
69

--- Page 85 ---

Here a, b, g and d are temperature or light-dependent quantities that can be obtained by inspection of
Eqs. (3.6) and (3.8). This is the demand for CO2; in steady state this must equal the flow of CO2 from
the atmosphere into the stomatal cavity. The value of Ci at which A¼0 is the CO2 compensation point
G, which depends only on temperature and irradiance and can be obtained from Eq. (3.32):
G ¼ dRd  b
a  gRd
:
ð3:33Þ
The supply equation determines the rate A at which CO2 diffuses into the stomatal cavity across the leaf
boundary layer and through the stomatal opening:
A ¼ gS Cs  Ci
ð
Þ
ð3:34Þ
A ¼ gb Ca  Cs
ð
Þ;
ð3:35Þ
where Ca is the ambient atmospheric p(CO2), Cs is the CO2 concentration on the leaf surface and gS
and gb are the stomatal and leaf boundary layer conductances for CO2, respectively. Stomatal conduc-
tance is assumed to be similar to the Leuning (1990) or Tuzet et al. (2003) modifications of the Ball
et al. (1987) equation, i.e.
gS ¼ g0 þ g1f
A
Ci  G ;
ð3:36Þ
where g0 and g1 are parameters, f accounts for the effects of factors such as vapour pressure deficit or
water potential on stomatal conductance, and G is given by Eq. (3.33).
When Eqs. (3.32)–(3.36) are solved for A or Ci a fourth order polynomial equation is obtained, with
the consequence that there are four possible solutions. In principle the biologically appropriate
solution can be found for this equation but we take a different approach, akin to the geometrical
solution of Figure 3.2.
3.3.2. Solution of the Supply and Demand Equations
The demand curve defined by Eq. (3.32) is a rectangular hyperbola, as in Figure 3.2, but the
supply curve obtained from Eqs. (3.34) to (3.36) is a non-rectangular hyperbola. These curves are
illustrated in Figure 3.5a, where the similarity to Figure 3.2 is quite marked. Determining the supply
rate involves solving a quadratic equation, the two solutions of which give the two branches of the
hyperbola. The biologically relevant branch is the one in the upper region of Figure 3.5a, and labelled as
‘supply curve’.
A technique for solving the coupled model involves determining the points of intersection of a series
of linear approximations to the supply and demand curves. This is illustrated in Figure 3.5b. Given
some initial value of Ci, e.g. 100 mbar in Figure 3.5b, Eq. (3.32) and the quadratic solution to
Eqs. (3.34)–(3.36) are used to determine the corresponding values of A on the supply and demand
curves. The slopes of these curves can also be determined from these equations and used to determine
lines through these points tangent to the curves as shown in Figure 3.5b. The point where these two
lines intersect gives an improved value of Ci for the intersection of the supply and demand curves. This
process is then repeated using the updated value of Ci until the corresponding values of A for the supply
and demand curves agree to within some specified tolerance. This algorithm appears to be robust, and
to converge rapidly. A suitable initial value of Ci for the algorithm is some value between Ca and G.
70
Chapter 3

--- Page 86 ---

This technique is used to determine the assimilation rate corresponding to both the RuBP saturated
and RuBP limiting cases in the Farquhar and von Caemmerer leaf photosynthesis model. The actual
assimilation rate is then the minimum of the two rates so obtained.
3.3.3. Results from Applying the Coupled Model
a) Parameter Sensitivity of Coupled Model
This simple geometrical approach to the solution of the coupled model yields insights into the
behaviour of the model as external conditions or photosynthetic parameters are changed. We outline
some of these insights here.
First, the supply curve is the same irrespective of whether photosynthesis is RuBP saturated or RuBP
limited. Therefore, conclusions based on the shape of the supply curve apply in both cases. As Ca is
increased the shape of the supply curve does not change; it is merely translated along the Ci-axis and always
crosses this axis at Ci¼Ca. As a result, an increase in Ca increases assimilation. Increasing the boundary
layer conductance gb increases the slope of the supply curve but hardly affects its location, so an increase in
gb increases assimilation up to a limit (when the supply curve is vertical for very large gb). A change in g0
hardly affects the slope of the upper branch of the supplycurve but increases the gap between the upper and
lower branches. Consequently, assimilation, intracellular CO2 and stomatal conductance are insensitive to
g0. Finally, changing g1 strongly affects conductance, and for small g0 conductance is proportional to g1.
However, decreasing g1 steepens the supplycurve and moves it to the left,resulting in a decrease in Aand Ci.
b) A–Ci and Light Response Curves from the Coupled Model
The coupled model was use to generate (a) an A–Ci curve and (b) a light-response curve for leaves with
the photosynthetic parameters given in Table 3.1, with g0¼0.01 and g1¼8, and at a temperature of
25 C. Other conditions, e.g. VPD and leaf water potential, were assumed to be optimal so the modifier

f in Eq. (3.36) is unity. The results are shown in Figure 3.6. Transition from RuBP saturated
photosynthesis to RuBP limited photosynthesis occurs at about Ca450 mbar in case (a), and at

We noted in Section 3.1.3 that A–Ci curves can be described empirically by rectangular hyperbolae,
and light responses by non-rectangular hyperbolae (see also Figure 2.1). Because of the transition from
RuBP saturated to RuBP limited photosynthesis, it is not strictly correct to describe the A–Ci and light
responses in this simple manner. However, in many cases noise or biological variation in observed
assimilation data can mask the effects of the transition from RuBP saturated photosynthesis to RuBP
limited photosynthesis and the simpler empirical relationships can be effectively used. We take
advantage of this in Chapter 5, where we develop a simple model of whole-canopy production over
an extended time period.
3.4 RESPIRATION
There are two fundamentally different forms of plant respiration: photorespiration and dark (or
mitochondria) respiration. Photorespiration is a reaction between oxygen and the enzyme Rubisco
that occurs in illuminated leaves when intracellular CO2 is low, and results in the formation of glycolate
which is quickly degraded to CO2. Dark respiration is any process by which organic substances are
oxidised to CO2 and water with the production of the ATP and reducing power (NADPH) required for
metabolic processes. It occurs in leaves that are not illuminated, and is best measured in the dark in the
absence of photorespiration. We are not concerned in this book with photorespiration: hence the term
‘‘respiration’’ always refers to dark respiration.
The respiration rate of plant tissue varies with the number of living cells, and with their metabolic
rate. Respiration rate is expressed in terms of the rate of CO2 efflux (or as an equivalent biomass loss)

discuss in Section 5.5.1 the concepts of construction and maintenance respiration of plant tissue; here
we focus on foliage respiration.
3.4.1. Temperature Dependence of Respiration
The temperature dependence of respiration can be described by the standard Arrhenius relationship,
i.e. Eq. (3.11). However, it is customary to use the empirical relationship
R T
ð Þ ¼ R0ekR TT0
ð
Þ;
ð3:37Þ
where kR is a parameter and R0 is the respiration rate at a reference temperature T0. As we discussed in
the context of Eq. (3.12), another way of expressing the rates of biological processes in relation to
temperature is the so-called Q10. This is defined as the ratio between the rates of a process at two
temperatures that differ by 10 C. From Eq. (3.37) we get
Q10 ¼ e10kR:
ð3:38Þ
Typical values of Q10 for the short-term temperature response of (dark) respiration are in the range 2–2.5.
Respiration is a process with a response time much shorter than a day (see Section 2.1), which
presumably determines the exponential relationship given by Eq. (3.38) observed in short-term data.
However, various studies have reported evidence for acclimation of the temperature response of
respiration and it is important to distinguish short-term temperature responses from long-term
responses to temperature, e.g. daily or seasonal, which are often more nearly linear than exponential,
or characterised by a low Q10, e.g. 1.3–1.5 (Gifford 1994).
For example, Linder and Troeng (1981) found hourly respiration rate was exponentially related to
temperature with Q102, but when daily respiration was plotted against mean daily temperature the
relationship was linear. They also found a pronounced variation in the rate of biomass respiration at a
given temperature in different seasons, and attributed this to temperature acclimation of the respiring
biomass, and to varying amounts of respiring tissues. Butler and Landsberg (1981) identified marked
seasonal changes in stem respiration rate, apparent as a change in the respiration R0 at a reference
temperature but not in the temperature coefficient kR, and Yokota et al. (1994) reported large seasonal
changes in R for hinoki cypress uncorrelated with temperature, as did Edwards and Hanson (1996) for
oak and maple. The high values of R coincided with periods when physiological activity was high, so
the seasonal changes can be explained as changes in the rates of growth and maintenance. Gifford
(1994) examined seasonal temperature response of respiration of various crops in Australia and found
a Q10 of 1.3–1.5.
3.4.2. Dark Respiration of Leaves
Observations of the dark respiration of leaves abound; they are a normal output of any gas exchange
experiment. We summarise in Table 3.3 a few observations of foliar respiration, standardised to a
temperature of 15 C, for conifers, deciduous and evergreen broadleaf trees. Respiration can be
expressed in many ways. Here, we have used the area-based units mol C m2 s1 to facilitate compari-
son with observed light-saturated assimilation rates, which are expressed in the same units. However,
respiration per unit leaf mass (mol C kg1 s1) is often used, as well as respiration per unit leaf
nitrogen (mol C (mol N)1 s1).
Physiological Processes
73

--- Page 89 ---

Various factors have significant effects on leaf dark respiration Rd. We have already discussed the
effects of temperature on respiration in general, and Eqs. (3.37) and (3.38) apply to Rd. Where known,
Q10 values have been included in Table 3.3. These Q10 values apply to the effects of short-term changes
of temperature, e.g. diurnal changes. However, Rd at a given reference temperature also changes
seasonally, e.g. in response to the resumption of leaf activity following the onset of Spring. These
seasonal changes are quite common, even in temperate species.
An example is the observation of the seasonal variation in the temperature dependence of Rd for E.
globulus in Portugal, determined from gas exchange data, reported by Pereira et al. (1986). They found
that Q10 was high (2.8) in winter and early autumn, but declined to 2.25 in summer and early
spring. Also, respiration rate at the average ambient temperature preceding the date when the
measurements were made increased far more slowly than indicated by the seasonal Q10: from about
0.28 mmol m2 s1 in winter to about 0.55 in summer. In fact, although other factors such as water
stress might have affected the seasonal temperature response of Rd, and this was more linear than
exponential, the Q10 for seasonal average respiration rate as a function of seasonal temperature
approximated 1.35. This is consistent with the long-term Q10 reported by Gifford (1994). As a result
of this temperature acclimation of respiration, the temperature required to invoke a particular Rd
increased with increasing seasonal temperatures.
Leaf nitrogen content also has a strong effect on respiration, with Rd increasing with leaf nitrogen
content. For example, Sheriff and Nambiar (1991) showed that Rd increased linearly with foliar
nitrogen content from about 0.45 mmol m2 s1 at a nitrogen content of 0.4 mmol g1 to about
0.6 mmol m2 s1 at a nitrogen content of 1.7 mmol g1. The ratio Rd/Amax declined with increasing
leaf nitrogen.
Generally, older leaves and leaves in a low-light environment, e.g. in the lower canopy, have lower
respiration rates than young or strongly illuminated leaves. The correlation of Rd – and also of
assimilation rate and stomatal conductance – with the general level of light intensity in canopies is
almost certainly due to a general decline in leaf nitrogen content with depth in the canopies. It is
hypothesised that the distribution of leaf nitrogen is such as to optimise whole canopy photosynthetic
production (Field 1983; Sands 1995a), driven by the fact that higher levels of nitrogen lead to higher
Amax and higher saturating light levels. It follows that less nitrogen is needed where light levels
are low, so respiration is lower. These issues are discussed in the context of E. globulus canopies by
O’Grady et al. (2008). They showed that Rd varied from 1.4 mmol m2 s1 in the upper canopy, down
