and suppressed trees, but Rd was higher in suppressed trees than in dominant trees, so the ratio Rd/Amax
was higher in suppressed trees. There was no immediately apparent explanation for this.
3.5 ALLOCATION OF BIOMASS
Allocation is the distribution to the various parts of a plant of the substrates needed for growth and
produced by the assimilation of carbohydrates in the foliage and the uptake of nutrients by the roots. This
leads to the synthesis of new biomass in the different parts of a plant. The wide range of shapes of trees of
the same species emphasises that allocation is affected by environmental conditions such as temperature,
light, wind, and nutrient and water availability. However, it is not the external environmental conditions
per se that determine allocation; rather it is how these affect the internal state of the plant.
In an in-depth review of carbon allocation in trees, Cannell and Dewar (1994) claim that ‘‘Although
there is much information on the distribution of dry matter in plants, there is surprisingly little
understanding of the mechanisms that govern carbon allocation. Research at the process level on
carbon allocation . . . has fallen far behind research on processes such as photosynthesis . . . and now
severely limits our ability to construct process-based models of whole plants’’. This state of affairs
probably prevails today, although significant success has been achieved modelling allocation.
The synthesis of carbohydrates and nutrients into new biomass is a complex process. These substrates
aretransported fromtheir source to other parts of the plant by mass flow in the vascular system.Substrate
gradients then form within the plants in response to their relative rates of supply and utilisation in
response to changing environmental conditions. As a result, different parts of plants accumulate biomass
at different rates in response to these conditions. In particular, growth of organs responsible for uptake of
a limiting resource (e.g. roots for nutrients, foliage for light) is often enhanced relative to other organs
when that resource is not limiting. This improves uptake of the resource and restores a balance between
acquisition of substrates and their uses in growth. Also, substrates can be temporarily stored in ray
parenchyma cells (part of the sapwood), and provide the resources needed for maintenance respiration
or growth during periods of short-term dormancy, such as reduced photosynthesis due to water stress, or
following seasonal dormancy where over-winter storage in deciduous species promotes new leaf growth.
The physical structure of plants must also be sufficient to overcome mechanical forces, e.g. wind
loading on the canopy, and the need for mechanical support is an important factor determining
biomass allocation. The mechanical principles determining branch and stem shape were also reviewed
by Cannell and Dewar (1994). Cambial growth appears to be stimulated by mechanical forces, but no
biochemical or biophysical explanation has been found. Growth tends to occur along stems to equalise
the stress distribution, i.e. they have the minimum mass required so they don’t break under their own
weight or wind loading.
Growth is clearly the outcome of complex balancing processes between supply and demand of the
carbohydrate and nutrient substrates for growth, and involves feedbacks between this supply and
demand. In fact, rather than being a single process in its own right, allocation is an emergent property
of a system comprising distinct processes, some of which are passive, whereas others are active and
require energy from respiration. These include:

photosynthesis and nutrient uptake by roots, to provide the source of growth substrates

the creation of sinks through the utilisation of substrates by growth processes

the supply of energy for active transport, through respiration.
Physiological Processes
75

--- Page 91 ---

long-distance transport through Mu¨nch flow, driven by concentration gradients between source
and sink

short-distance transport, comprising phloem loading at sources and unloading at sinks

temporary storage of substrates as reserves for growth under varying rates of supply.
There are models of allocation that take most or all of these processes into account. Although they
reproduce most of the observed characteristics of biomass allocation in plants, growth models based on
them tend to be complex. Source-sink models using a transport–resistance approach (Section 3.5.2a)
appear to have the most promise, but they need extensive testing against forest growth data.
3.5.1. Principles Underlying Models of Allocation
In this section, we review some of the biological and biophysical principles that are involved in biomass
allocation and hence determine the growth of the physical structure of trees. Later (Section 5.6.3), we
focus on how the principles of allometry and functional balance can be used to develop constraints on
biomass allocation that can be used in simple growth models.
a) Functional Balance
Functional balance is concerned with how the resources for growth are supplied in amounts that ensure
that the needs for growth and proper functioning of plant tissue are met. In particular, a balance is
established between leaf and root activity, as these are the sources of carbon and nutrients, respectively.
If there is an excess of substrate in one part of a plant, it may be re-translocated and used whereever
needed, or it may inhibit further synthesis or uptake of this substrate. Plants generally respond to sub-
optimal environmental conditions by changing allocation so as to minimise limitations to growth.
Bloom et al. (1985) used an economic analogy for plant growth and suggested another expression of
functional balance: that optimal growth occurs when all resources are equally limiting.
An early statement of functional balance was by Davidson (1969), who noted that the ratio of the rates
of photosynthesis and nutrient absorption in grasses and clover was proportional to the foliage-to-root
biomass ratio. He proposed that ‘‘photosynthate and absorbed minerals are allocated to growth of roots
and shoots to maintain a balanced functional economy of the plant’’, and ‘‘allocation of photosynthate is
controlled by the relative rates of root and foliage functioning, by inverse proportion’’. Not all nutrients
obey a functional balance of this form: in particular Mg and Mn, both of which are essential cofactors of
photosynthetic enzymes rather than being involved in biomass production, behave in the opposite way.
The concept of functional balance can be expressed by the following relationship
lsNWR ¼ sCWF;
ð3:39Þ
where sN is the uptake rate of nitrogen per unit root mass, sC is the carbon assimilation rate per unit
foliage or stem mass, WF and WR are the foliage (or shoot) and root mass, and l is a parameter related
to the average C:N ratio in plant material. Changes in environmental factors, or interventions such as
pruning, disturb the functional balance of plants. However, trees are observed to gradually return to a
new state of balance by changing their functioning. Ma¨kela¨ (1999) discusses how this is achieved in
models of allocation.
b) Local Determination of Growth
Plants modify their growth patterns to favour the capture of resources. These shifts can be seen as
adaptive since they promote efficient resource capture. The controls that regulate them are poorly
understood, and growth of a plant organ depends not just on local conditions, but on conditions
76
Chapter 3

--- Page 92 ---

experienced by other organs. It has been assumed that specific messengers, e.g. growth hormones,
determine allocation in response to environmental conditions. However, doubts remain as to their
identity and how they exert control at the whole-plant level.
In a review of attempts to model biomass allocation during growth, Cheeseman (1993) noted that
many models required the plant to have unreasonable knowledge about itself, e.g. of its total weight, or
require some form of integrating mechanism, such as a hormone, to carry information about the global
state of the plant. He argued that ‘‘growth and resource allocation can be based solely on local, internal
non-structural resource concentrations and simple kinetic rules, without the need for additional
mechanisms to integrate activities to the organismal level’’. In particular, by using a simple model of
plant growth with two compartments, roots and shoots, and two substrates, carbon and nitrogen, he
showed that functional balance, in the sense of Davidson (1969), was an emergent property of plant
growth based on local determinants of growth.
c) Optimality Principles
The use of optimality principles in biology has a strong theoretical appeal as they are well-established in
physics and engineering. Optimality is driven through long-term evolutionary trends, but also acts in a
more reactive way as a response to short-term environmental changes. Acclimation of photosynthesis
and respiration to seasonal temperatures is a familiar example of the latter, but optimality may also play
a role in allocation.
Early models of allocation were based on root and shoot pools, C and N substrates. They were
applied to balanced exponential growth and allocated new growth so as to maximise the relative growth
rate with respect to tissue nitrogen content (e.g. Hilbert 1990). However, allocation in trees is more
complicated than in vegetative plants because stems and coarse roots play structural and storage roles,
distinct from the acquisition of growth substrates, and must be included. The inclusion of structural
compartments greatly complicates the application of optimality principles (e.g. Kastner-Maresch and
Mooney 1994; Osone and Tateno 2003), but observed allocation responses are again predicted by the
models.
Although allocation based on optimality principles can reproduce observed allocation patterns, the
resulting models may require knowledge of the state of the whole plant, or integration over an extended
time period, and hence are difficult to reconcile with Cheeseman’s (1993) claim that allocation is the
result of purely local processes.
d) Coordination Theory
To resolve some of the problems with optimality principles, Chen et al. (1993) and Chen and Reynolds
(1997) proposed an alternative approach called coordination theory. Coordination theory assumes that
some aspect of growth is regulated by the balance between two or more distinct processes, and that this
balance is coordinated by some internal mechanism when changing external conditions induce an
imbalance. Essential features of coordination theory are a quantitative definition of the imbalance in
terms of the current state of the system, and a parameter which determines the time-scale of the plant
response to an imbalance.
Chen and Reynolds (1997) applied coordination theory to biomass allocation in a plant subject to
water stress. Balanced growth occurs when the carbon- and water-limited growth rates are equal, and
the measure of imbalance is the difference in growth rates divided by the actual growth rate. This
imbalance is regulated by changing the relative biomass allocation to shoots and roots, with the change
in this allocation controlled by the imbalance. Over the long term, leaf (or shoot) and root activities
appear to be coordinated so there is no net accumulation of free growth substrates in a plant.
Physiological Processes
77

--- Page 93 ---

3.5.2. Mechanistic Approaches to Modelling Allocation
In their review of carbon allocation in trees, Cannell and Dewar (1994) emphasised the need to divide
the tree into compartments according to function. These include physiologically active tissues, and
woody tissue. Active tissues are foliage for photosynthesis, fine roots for nutrient and water uptake, and
sapwood for translocation and short-term substrate storage. Woody tissue such as stems and coarse
roots provide physical support and, along with reproductive components such as seeds and seed
capsules, long-term non-reversible storage.
As we saw above, it is also necessary to consider both active and labile pools of carbon and nitrogen
in each organ, and a mechanism for substrate movement between pools. Therein lies the source of
much of the complexity of allocation models. We summarise below two of the most popular mecha-
nistic approaches, and review models using these in Chapter 8.
a) Transport–Resistance and Allocation
One of the most commonly used approaches to understanding and modelling allocation is the
transport–resistance paradigm (Thornley 1972). The key assumptions are:

the relative growth rate of any sink is proportional to the product of C- and N-substrate
concentrations at the location where phloem unloading occurs

the fluxes of C-substrates from leaves to the sinks, and of N-substrates from the roots to the sinks,
are driven by the concentration gradients from source to sink, and the associated hydraulic
conductance increases with the size of the plant parts traversed.
These assumptions satisfy Cheeseman’s (1993) claim that allocation is based only on local conditions
within the plant. Thornley’s original model included only shoot and root compartments. In the
Edinburgh model of forest growth Thornley (1991) implemented a transport–resistance sub-model
for allocation that included structural biomass components, as well as pools for foliage and fine roots.
Each pool comprises a structural component and a meristem, and in each meristem there are carbon
and nitrogen substrate pools. The substrate dependence of various rates is through Michaelis–Menten
kinetics.
Dewar (1993) allowed for the fact that nutrients taken up by the root are in part immediately
available for root growth and in part are transported in the xylem to the leaves, where they enter the
phloem and hence are carried to other parts of the tree. This avoids the need for separate conductances
for different growth substrates as they are carried together in the phloem by mass flow, but Dewar’s
model does not include a stem compartment.
b) Pipe Model
The functional interdependence of sapwood and foliage, first formulated as Pressler’s rule (Pressler
1865), states that the increment in bole cross-sectional area at a given height is proportional to the
foliage area above this point. This is consistent with the pipe model (Shinozaki et al. 1964), originally
proposed as a hydraulic explanation of plant form, which posits that the stem and branches of a plant
are assemblages of pipes extending from leaves through the stem into the root system, and that the area
of leaves connected to an assemblage of pipes is proportional to the cross-sectional area of that
assemblage. The pipe-model is presumed to optimise stem hydraulic conductance. However, McCulloh
et al. (2003) showed that the optimum network has a small number of wide conduits at the base that
feed an increasing number of narrower pipes further up the tree, and the distribution of pipes
resembles that of blood vessels in the cardiovascular system (Murray 1926). Measurement of the actual
78
Chapter 3

--- Page 94 ---

xylem of plants McCulloh et al. (2003) indicated that it more closely obeys Murray’s law than the
original pipe model as long as the pipes do not function as structural support.
The pipe model has been used as a framework for biomass allocation in tree growth models
(Valentine 1988; Ma¨kela¨ 1997), and explains the shape of stems (Ma¨kela¨ 2002). Trees are assumed to
comprise an assembly of active pipes that provide the conduits for growth substrates, and inactive pipes
that once supported leaves but now provide structural support. Height growth is a reflection of an
increase in pipe length due to apical growth, while basal area growth is a reflection of an increase in
total pipe cross-sectional area due to meristem activity in the cambium. Consistent with the functional
interdependence of leaf and sapwood area, the rate of supply of substrates is proportional to the cross
sectional area of a pipe, but respiration is proportional to the volume of the pipe. When respiration
exceeds supply, the pipe becomes physiologically inactive, but contributes to the mechanical support of
the tree.

From the point of view of commercial forestry the most important characteristics of forest stands
are the number and size of the stems, since these are the harvestable product. From the point of view of
the physiological ecologist the most important characteristics of forest stands are their foliage, since the
foliage is the primary interface between the trees and their atmospheric environment, and their roots,
since these are the interface between the plant and the soil. It is natural, then, to divide the components
of a stand into those concerned with its structure and with physiological function. Stands consist
of populations of stems, characterised by their height and diameter distribution. The structural
components of trees include the stems, branches and coarse roots. These determine the architecture
Physiological Ecology of Forest Production
Joe Landsberg and Peter Sands
ISSN 1936-7961, DOI: 10.1016/S1936-7961(11)04004-8
Copyright # 2011 Elsevier Inc.
All rights reserved.

--- Page 96 ---

of a stand, and generally change only slowly over time. The functional components are the foliage, sap-
wood and fine roots. These are much more responsive to the environment.
Stem populations are reasonably predictable in plantations and in even-aged re-growth forests
dominated by single species. Stem population dynamics are important to forest managers, to
modellers, and to those concerned with carbon sequestration, either in relation to climate change or
wood production. However, these dynamics are extremely hard to predict in mixed species forests
where there has been opportunity for the development of gap and understorey species. We discuss in
this chapter both density-dependent mortality – also known as self-thinning – and density-indepen-
dent mortality induced by environmental factors.
The distribution of biomass between the various components of a tree – foliage, stems, roots, etc. – is
important as this determines the potential for growth (leaves and fine roots), structural stability (stem
and coarse roots) and economic products (mainly the stem). In this book, we use the term biomass
partitioning to refer to the manner in which the total biomass of a stand or tree is distributed amongst
these components. On the other hand, we use the term allocation to refer to how new assimilates are
allocated to these different components. It is important to remember that partitioning is the outcome of
the ongoing process of allocation in response to changing environmental conditions.
We also provide a summary of the statistical relationships between stem height and diameter, and
discuss the mathematical expressions generally used to describe stem size distributions. Although these
are essentially the domain of conventional forest mensuration, they need to be considered here because
the step from process-based models of forest growth to useful practical tools is the development of
hybrid models (e.g. Battaglia et al. 1999; Ma¨kela¨ et al. 2000). These use the empirical relationships that
emerge from mensuration to convert the stem biomass predicted by a process-based growth model into
the variables useful to the forest manager.
Our physiological understanding of the processes underlying carbohydrate allocation is uncertain.
The biomass of the component parts of trees (indeed, of any plants) tend to bear fixed – or at least
stable and predictable – relationships to one another. These are called allometric relationships, and are
routinely used to estimate biomass partitioning within a tree from simple measures such as its stem
diameter. In this chapter, we discuss the use of allometric relationships in growth models to constrain
biomass allocation to the components of trees so that the resulting partitioning better mirrors that
observed in real stands. Obviously, this is an empirical as opposed to a mechanistic procedure, and lack
of knowledge about the factors affecting allometric relationships may result in models that have limited
scope to explain the effects of variables such as water supply or nutrition. Nevertheless, the use of
allometric relationships to estimate biomass allocation need not be entirely empirical and inflexible as
it is indeed possible to use them in flexible ways that allow dependence on variables that affect growth.
Canopies are formed by the crowns of trees. The architecture of a forest canopy is described by the
vertical and horizontal arrangement of foliage through the canopy space. This, and the leaf area in a
canopy per unit ground area determine how much photosynthetically active radiation (PAR) is
intercepted by the canopy, and hence the photosynthetic production by the canopy (Section 5.2).
The energy absorbed by canopies is also a primary determinant of their transpiration rate (Sec-
tion 2.5.3), and their roughness, in the aerodynamic sense, determines the effectiveness of the coupling
between canopies and the air (Section 2.5.1).
The leaf area in a canopy is an important variable affecting light interception, and hence photosyn-
thesis and carbohydrate production. Light interception is determined, in part, by the spatial distribu-
tion of foliage, and hence depends on the shape of tree crowns, the distribution of foliage in those
crowns and on the population (number) and distribution of trees in the stand. The amount of leaf area
in a canopy per unit ground area is known as the leaf area index, or LAI, of the canopy, and is of
fundamental importance to any estimation of forest productivity. How LAI can be determined is
82
Chapter 4

--- Page 97 ---

considered later in some detail (Section 4.4.2). The leaf area index attained when a canopy closes
reflects the site fertility, temperature and available water, and we present a simple empirical model for
leaf area index in eucalypt stands that illustrates how temperature, in particular, may affect the
development of canopy LAI.
Foliage dynamics – the emergence, growth, death and fall of leaves – determine the temporal
dynamics of canopies, and are clearly a major determinant of the state of deciduous canopies, where
the whole population of leaves on trees grows and is lost each growing season. Modelling this is
difficult, since it must involve stored carbohydrates; another area where our knowledge is uncertain.
However, foliage dynamics are also important in evergreen trees: if some leaves did not fall each year,
leaf area would rapidly reach very high values. Leaf loss – generally called litterfall, although litterfall
strictly includes twigs and dead branches – therefore has to be accounted for in attempts to describe the
growth patterns and carbon production of trees. Litterfall is also a factor in the overall carbon balance
of trees, although the amounts of carbohydrate involved are small in relation to the amounts consumed
by the growth of other organs.
Finally, we discuss coarse and fine roots and their distribution, and outline the distinction between
fine roots as active uptake organs and coarse roots as passive/structural anchors. Fine root turnover is
another significant consumer of carbohydrates, and has to be included in any biomass allocation
scheme. We discuss nutrient dynamics and uptake later (Section 6.1.1), which are heavily dependent on
root mass and root distribution.
4.1 STEM POPULATION DYNAMICS
For the commercial forester, stem number and size are the principal factors that determine yield.
Foresters often use the term ‘stocking’ to refer to the extent to which a site is occupied by trees. This
usually refers to the number of trees per hectare, although it is sometimes used in relation to basal area,
or wood volume per hectare in a stand, relative to some desired figure thought to be the optimum for
the health and productivity of the trees. In this respect, stocking is often used in a comparative context,
such as well-stocked, poorly stocked, or overstocked. In this book, we use the terms stem number, or
stem population density, to explicitly refer to the number of live trees per hectare of ground area
occupied by the trees. Stem population is an important determinant of canopy structure, and since this
determines radiation interception, also of the potential growth rate of the plants. Stem populations also
affect water and nutrient availability, primarily through competition between individual trees for
resources such as light, water and nutrients.
Competition increases as the stem numbers increase, and also as trees grow, so the demand for
resource increases as a stand develops. Consequently, particularly in plantations and even-aged stands
dominated by one or two species, the number of stems declines as a result of mortality induced by the
increased competition. Mortality may be simply random, or density independent and caused by
environmental stresses, or density dependent and induced by explicit competition for resources in
stands with a high population density. Catastrophic events such as fire or profound defoliation through
insect attacks are best modelled as discrete events in which a certain proportion of trees is removed, or
as intense defoliation events. In the following subsection, we outline some of the causes of tree
mortality and approaches to modelling it.
The situation in multi-species, uneven-aged forests is likely to be completely different. In these,
where old trees die and fall they are replaced by plants that may be of different species, which exploit the
consequent gaps. The situation is dynamic and the population of large old trees may be relatively stable.
The discussion on mortality in the following sections only applies to even-aged forests and plantations.
Stand Structure and Dynamics
83

--- Page 98 ---

4.1.1. Representing Mortality
Let stocking or population density be the number N (trees ha1) of live trees per unit ground area
occupied by those trees. In general, we would expect the number of trees that die in a time interval to be
proportional to the current population density, i.e.
dN

where the second form applies when gNDt is small. In general, gN varies with stand age, with general site
and stand conditions, and with the type of management imposed on a forest. The goal for modelling
mortality is to determine how gN varies with age, or with site conditions.
Typically, in a plantation or forest regenerating from clear-felling or fire, a proportion of seedlings
dies soon after planting, so mortality might initially be high. However, if seedlings that die are replaced
during the establishment phase, mortality is likely to be low in the period immediately following
planting. There is then an extended period during which the population is usually stable, possibly
followed by enhanced mortality as competition sets in, or diseases or environmental stresses manifest as
the stand ages. In this example, gN is strongly age dependent. If mortality has two phases, a suitable
function representing gN is
gN tð Þ ¼ gN0 þ gN1  gN0

where t is the stand age, gN0 and gN1 are the mortality rates for young and mature stands, respectively,
tgN0 is the stand age at which gN¼½(gN0þgN1), and ngN is a parameter that determines how rapidly
the transition of mortality rate from that of an immature stand to that of a mature stand occurs.
Traditional empirical mortality models variously quantify gN, or an alternative expression for
survival to that given by Eq. (4.2), in terms of variables such as age, the current population, and
stand basal area. Robinson and Ek (2003) used an equation that calculates the probability P of survival
in any given year of an individual in a stand:
Psurvival ¼ b1  1 þ en
ð

ð4:4Þ
where n and b1 are parameters. This looks simple enough, except that n is calculated from a complex
expression involving two variables and six species-specific parameters, which renders it of little value
for general use. They also use a linear equation, with three input parameters and crown coverage as the
independent variable, to calculate stand-based mortality.
4.1.2. Environmental Affects on Mortality
Foresters have devised a range of empirical models to describe how mortality depends on stand age,
density and environmental factors. These range from the statistical relationships for survival (e.g. as a
function of stand age, density and tree size) used in the purely empirical stand growth models
84
Chapter 4

--- Page 99 ---

conventionally employed by forest managers, to more mechanistic models (e.g. based on competition
for resources and the balance between photosynthetic production and respiration) used in process-
based growth models. Hawkes (2000) reviewed 61 mortality algorithms in woody plant growth models
and concluded that ‘‘modelling the mortality of woody plants . . . is difficult because of the complex
interactions between different environmental stresses, minimal understanding of whole-plant mortal-
ity processes, and a chronic shortage of data’’.
Process-based modelling of mortality in response to environmental factors is therefore a major
problem in plant growth modelling. The mortality rate gN is affected by environmental factors such as
prolonged frost, heat or drought, insect or fungal damage, and competition for resources. In principle,
data could be collected on tree mortality in response to a suitable measure of the intensity of any of
these factors. We would also need to quantify the intensity of each factor. In practice, this process is
quite difficult because mortality is rarely an instantaneous, well-defined event, and is usually only
evident some time after the event or events that precipitated tree death. Furthermore, some factors that
affect mortality, such as drought, may have different effects depending on the age of the trees and
of course on the severity of the drought. The complexity of environmental stresses and a general lack of
process information on tree mortality have led to the widespread adoption of empirical algorithms to
simulate mortality (Hawkes 2000). We will illustrate some of these complexities through references
to the physiological study of the effects frost and drought.
a) Frost Hardiness
The issue of frost hardiness, i.e. degree to which frost affects the physiology of leaves, is an important
determinant of the response of trees to frosts. This has been modelled by Leinonen (1995,1996) and by
King and Ball (1998). It involves the process of acclimation to frost, whereby low night-time tempera-
tures further the development of frost hardiness and high temperatures decrease hardiness. The greater
the hardiness, the better the tree can withstand an isolated heavy frost. King and Ball studied the time-
course of frost effects on seasonal photosynthesis of Eucalyptus pauciflora. The effect of the duration of
any particular frost event on subsequent performance was uncertain as both rapid recovery and chronic
effects occurred. The effects may be either on mature leaves, or on expanding shoots which may die and
then lead to a delay in the reinvestment in new leaves. King and Ball explicitly distinguished short-term
effects with a rapid recovery time from chronic, long-term effects that had a slow recovery.
If photosynthetic production is sufficiently reduced so that respiratory demands exceedes production,
then mortality would be enhanced. In this context, it would be the long-term effects that would induce
mortality, and some measure of these effects should be incorporated into the mortality rate gN. We are
not aware that this has been done.
b) Drought Induced Mortality
Drought-induced mortality also has a strong physiological basis, and ought to be amenable to
process-based modelling. Suarez et al. (2004) emphasise that drought-induced mortality (in
Nothofagus dombeyi) is not a random process, but is the outcome of numerous factors that predispose
a tree to drought stress: ontogeny, growth vigour, climatic sensitivity and physiology. For instance,
slower growing trees and trees with declining vigour were more susceptible to drought death, as were
those that showed a greater sensitivity to other climatic factors. Suarez et al. also believe that the effects
of drought and other factors on tree mortality are additive.
In their excellent review of the mechanisms of plant survival during drought, and the potential
effects of climate change on survival, McDowell et al. (2008) propose a hydraulic framework for
drought-induced mortality. They propose that mortality is induced by the duration and intensity of a
drought acting on three key mechanisms:
Stand Structure and Dynamics
85

--- Page 100 ---


carbon starvation, where the drought is long enough to reduce photosynthesis to the extent that
carbon reserves are insufficient to maintain respiration;

hydraulic failure, where the intensity of the drought pushes the plant into irreversible desiccation or
xylem cavitation before carbon starvation occurs; and

biotic agents, where the effects of biotic agents (insects and diseases) are amplified by carbon
starvation or hydraulic failure.
McDowell et al. base their analysis in part on a remarkable drought event in the south-western USA
during 2000–2002 in which mortality rates for pin˜on pine (Pinus edulis) were up to 95%, whereas for
juniper (Juniperus monosperma) they were only 25%. They distinguish between isohydric plants (such
as pin˜on) that maintain a relatively constant leaf water potential by reducing stomatal conductance,
regardless of drought condition, and anisohydric plants (such as juniper) that allow leaf water potential
to decline under drought conditions.
Under drought conditions isohydric plants have reduced photosynthetic production, and McDowell
et al. conclude that by avoiding the direct consequences of drought, they incur an enhanced risk of
mortality from other causes. On the other hand, although anisohydric plants are relatively drought
tolerant, they are more subject to hydraulic failure because their hydraulic safety margins are narrower.
McDowell et al. conclude that anisohydric versus isohydric regulation of water status may be a critical
factor in the regulation of survival and mortality during drought, and that stress from carbon
starvation enhances the susceptibility of trees to other factors.
4.1.3. Self-Thinning
Forest stands may be destroyed by fire, by logging or by insect or disease attack. Whatever the cause
of mass tree death or removal, most stands will regenerate if left to themselves. However, the regrowth
may be dominated by species different from those that dominated the original stand, depending on the
seeds stored in the soil (seed banks) and the conditions that affect the germination, survival and early-
stage growth of the seedlings. Kozlowski (2002) has provided an overview of the factors affecting stand
regeneration in natural forests.
The general pattern in regeneration following a major disturbance is the emergence of a high popula-
tion of young trees competing for light, water and nutrients. Some grow rapidly and become dominant;
others,suppressed by reduced lightorlimited access tosoil resources, grow more slowly and are more likely
to die from a variety of causes. If stem numbers are high in mono-specific stands – such as plantations – or
stands dominated by a single species, early-stage competition will be intense as all trees exploit the same
resources. In such stands, stem populations change in relatively predictable ways. In mixed forests, the
changes in stem population are complex and unpredictable, and depend on the mix of species and their
interactions with one another, the age of the forest and the dynamics of gaps in old forests.
Foresters and forest ecologists tend to regard attainment of canopy closure as the most important
event in the development of a stand. Canopy closure is when the branches of neighbouring trees meet
and overlap so that, viewed from above, the forest surface presents a complete cover. The canopy leaf
area index (L, m2 m2) at this point is likely to be at least 3, although the converse does not necessarily
hold, i.e. a canopy with L3 may not be closed because of spatial variation (Landsberg and Gower
1997). High populations of young trees may lead to rapid canopy closure within a few years of
establishment, whereas widely spaced stands may take many years to reach canopy closure.
Density-dependent mortality or self-thinning is likely after canopy closure. This is illustrated in two
ways in Figure 4.1, where observed stem numbers and stand basal area are plotted in (a) as functions of
the average stem mass (including branch and bark) of trees in the stand, and in (b) as functions of the
86
Chapter 4

--- Page 101 ---

stem diameter at breast height (DBH). Two patterns that typically characterise self-thinning are shown
in Figure 4.1: the first is that during self-thinning the stand basal area increases to a maximum that is
attained when self-thinning occurs; the second is that the stem population gradually declines and then
follows a well-defined line known as the self-thinning line.
Self-thinning has traditionally been expressed in terms of the dependence of the maximum average
mass wSx (kg tree1) of tree stems (including branch and bark) on the stem number N (trees ha1) of
the stand through the empirical equation
wSx N
ð

ð4:5Þ
where the so-called self-thinning power nm1.5, and wSx1 (kg tree1) is the maximum average stem mass
attained for a population of N¼1000 trees ha1. In Figure 4.1a, the self-thinning line is given by Eq. (4.5).
In a typical implementation of self-thinning in a model, once the average stem mass of the trees exceeds the
value of wSx at the current stem number, trees die so that Eq. (4.5) is satisfied. In this model, self-thinning
either occurs or it does not: the transition process clearly shown in Figure 4.1a is not modelled.
An alternative interpretation of self-thinning that does model a transition phase was provided by
Vanclay and Sands (2009), and is based on Figure 4.1b. They observed (a) that as a stand self-thins its
basal area AB (m2 ha1) increases to a maximum ABx, (b) the slope s of a plot of stem number N against
stem DBH dB on logarithmic axes was well approximated by a simple power function of AB, and (c)
that s tended to about 2 during self-thinning. Under these assumptions, stem number and DBH

Numerous authors (e.g. White 1981; Westoby 1984; Pretzsch 2002) have supported these observa-
tions about self-thinning with discussions of how various quantities scale as size changes. Their
arguments are simplistic, but nevertheless instructive, and use as an analogy for a stand of trees a set
of similar objects placed on a plane. Self-thinning occurs when closely packed trees compete for
resources following canopy closure. The projected ground area A of each object scales as the square
of some characteristic dimension d, its volume Vand mass W scale as the cube of d (assuming density is
independent of size), and if the number of (closely packed) objects is N, then A scales as 1/N. In the
analogy, it is assumed that the DBH of a tree can be used as the characteristic dimension d of the
objects, i.e. dB / d. Since stand basal area AB / NdB2,
AB / Nd2

ð4:11Þ
and N / dB  2. This is the analogy for the observation that during self-thinning, the stand basal area is
constant. The second analogy is based on the scaling of volume and mass:
W / V / d3 / A3=2 / N3=2:
ð4:12Þ
88
Chapter 4

--- Page 103 ---

This is the analogy of the self-thinning rule Eq. (4.5). However, these analogies say nothing about
what causes mortality; they are simply statements of geometry based on scaling.
Equation (4.5) was originally derived for herbaceous plants, for which the power n¼1.5 was
found to be extraordinarily stable across a wide range of species (see White 1981). Reviewing the self-
thinning rule, Westoby (1984) emphasised that variations in n are to be expected, and that both n and
wSx1 are affected by site conditions. He also noted that the self-thinning rule appears to apply across a
1010-fold range of population density, although the limiting biomass at any given pant density varies
only about 10-fold.
Givnish (1986) showed that wSx1 in Eq. (4.5) is proportional to the rate at which the stem mass
required to support unit area of canopy increases with canopy height, and inversely proportional to the
ratio of canopy radius to canopy height. He then took into account biomechanical constraints on the
stem and crown masses, which must be satisfied if the stem is to support the crown, to derive an
expression that shows wSx1 is proportional to the square root of canopy LAI and inversely proportional
to a low power (0.25) of stem height (H, m):
wSx1 / L0:5H0:25;
ð4:13Þ
where the proportionality depends on other quantities such as the elastic modulus of the stem, and the
wood and leaf densities. This implies that self-thinning occurs earlier in stands of low LAI and in taller
stands, although the effect of height is quite weak: where a 100-fold range in plant height (1–100 m)
results in only a threefold range in wSx1.
Typical values for wSx1 (i.e. the maximum stem mass at a population density of 1000 trees ha1) can be
obtained from the literature. For Pinus radiata Drew and Flewelling (1977) suggest wSx1380 kg tree1,
with similar values for Eucalyptus regnans and E. obliqua (P. West; personal communication). A rough
analysis of Pinus densiflora datapresentedas agraphby Drewand Flewelling suggests wSx1175–240 for that
species, and analysis of unpublished data of Lindsay (reported by Borough et al. 1978) gives wSx1205.
Further data analysed by us show wSx1280 kg tree1 for Pinus patula in South Africa (Dye 2001), and
wSx1460 kg tree1 for Eucalyptus globulus in Tasmania, Australia (Goodwin and Candy 1986).
Equation (4.5) is a useful tool for modelling stem populations in process-based models. Landsberg
and Waring (1997) used it in the 3-PG model where wSx1 is set to the best available value for the species
being modelled. At each time step new biomass is allocated to individual trees, so that mean single-tree
stem biomass wS increases. This is tested against the value of wSx1 for the current population density,
and if wSwSx1, N is reduced to satisfy this constraint. However, following an application of 3-PG to
data from 193 permanent sample plots in which almost 1000 observations were made over a period of
22 years, Pinjuv et al. (2006) emphasise that Eq. (4.5) only predicts mortality caused by overcrowding.
If mortality is caused by wind, insect attacks, drought stress, etc. i.e. under conditions of density-
independent mortality, random mortality predicted using a non-zero value of gN should be used.
4.2 HEIGHT AND DIAMETER RELATIONS AND DISTRIBUTIONS
Even within homogeneous stands, tree stems are not of uniform size; there will always be variability,
although the size range is likely to be much smaller in such populations than in naturally regenerating
and even-aged stands. The relationship between tree diameters and heights is one of the most
important elements of forest structure, and enormous effort has been devoted to establishing relation-
ships between these two variables for commercial forest species, as well as the statistics of their
distributions across the trees in a stand.
Stand Structure and Dynamics
89

--- Page 104 ---

4.2.1. Height and Diameter Relations
Tree height and diameter growth are strongly connected, and there are functional relationships between
them that tend to beconservative. Astrees increase in height, the forces of gravity acing on the stem and the
turning moment of wind acting on the canopy both increase. These forces can only be countered
by increasing the diameter of the stem, and for any given stem diameter dB (cm) there is a critical height
Hcrit (m) beyond which the stem is strongly susceptible to mechanical failure. This height is given by
Hcrit ¼ t E=rgw

1=3
d2=3
B ;
ð4:14Þ
where E (Pa) is the elastic modulus of the stem, rgw (kg m3) is the green wood density, and
the coefficient t0.037 (McMahon 1973, with dB now in cm). Equation (4.14) is a statement of the
principle of elastic similarity, and also applies to branches, where the coefficient t now depends on
the angle between the branch and the main stem. McMahon and Kronauer (1976) argued that trees
preserve elastic similarity in their stem and branching structure during growth, and that this maintains
a constancy of crown shape.
A typical elastic modulus for green stem wood is E¼10 GPa with a green wood density of
rgw¼800 kg m3 (e.g. Green et al. 1999). Equation (4.14) then gives Hcrit¼8.6dB2/3. McMahon
(1973) analysed data from 576 large trees representing nearly every species found in the USA and
showed that trees generally limit their overall height to about ¼ of this critical height. Thus the critical
height of a tree with DBH of 100 cm is about Hcrit¼185 m, suggesting an expected actual height of
about 45 m, which is reasonable, at least for Eucalypts.
Site factors, for example crowding and competition with weeds, or exposure to wind, affect stem shape.
Watt et al. (2009) studied the effect of such factors on the elasticity of P. radiata stems up to age 6 years
across an environmental gradient. They found that slender stems, i.e. those with a high height to diameter
ratio, had higher values of E. From Eq. (4.14) this implies that their critical height is greater. However, their
observed values for E (i.e. 2–6 GPa) were less thanvalues reported by Green et al. (1999). They also present
a simple regression model for E in P. radiata from various environmental and site factors.
A variety of relationships between stem height and diameter are employed in mensuration-based
forest growth models, e.g. Table 1 of Soares and Tome´ (2002). These relationships are empirical, and
not necessarily based on any biological principle. A particularly convenient relationship is an allometric
relationship inspired by Eq. (4.14), but including stem populations as an additional factor as it is
known that closely spaced trees tend to grow taller:
H ¼ aHdnHB
B
N=1000
ð
ÞnHN ;
ð4:15Þ
where aH is the scale factor, nHB and nHN are the allometric powers and the division of N by 1000 is for
numerical convenience.
Applying Eq. (4.15) to data from a spacing trial involving three clones of Eucalyptus grandis, up to
age 4.3 years of age, in rain-fed and irrigated plantations in Brazil (Auro Almeida, personal communi-
cation) gave aH¼0.830.09, nHB¼1.170.04 and nHN¼0.230.02 (r2¼0.95, n¼112). Applying
Eq. (4.15) with nHB¼1.17 to data from an E. grandis spacing trial involving trees of up to age 10
years at six sites in South Africa (Smith et al. 2005) gave aH¼0.770.003 and nHN¼0.260.01
(r2¼0.96, n¼285), but a higher value of nHB is preferred (1.4). In both cases, there is evidence
that aH varies with clonal material, and possibly with site.
The allometric power for dB in Eq. (4.15) is about 1.17, whereas it is only 0.67 in Eq. (4.14). Thus,
if Eq. (4.15) holds for all ages, the trees would eventually exceed the critical height for mechanical
90
Chapter 4

--- Page 105 ---

stability. With E and rgw as given above, this occurs when dB105 cm, which is significantly larger than
the plantation grown trees examined by Almeida and Smith, but comparable with large trees in native
forests. However, Niklas (1995) showed that the allometric power of dB was >1 for small and young
trees, and decreased to 2
/
3 and then to ½ as tree size and age increased. This indicates that trees change
their biomass allocation patterns as they age. Young trees are far from the stability limit and can safely
allocate more biomass to height growth. As trees increase in height, they then allocate more resources
to mechanical stability and less to height growth.
4.2.2. Predicting Stem Mass from Height and Diameter
The most comprehensive study of the relationships between tree biomass and stem height and
diameter is that of Cannell (1984) who reviewed data for 640 forest and woodland species from around
the world. He based his analysis on simple dimensional relationships that must exist between the total
stem biomass (i.e. stem boles and branches) in a stand; viz.
WS ¼ f rwABH;
ð4:16Þ
where WS is above-ground woody mass per unit land area (usually hectare), f is a form factor that
reflects the shape of the stems, rw is wood density, AB the stand basal area, and H is tree height. Cannell
presented a thorough analysis of the variation in form factors for different groups of trees, with
different proportions of WS accounted for by branches. Pooling all the data, he established regressions
(highly significant: r2>0.9), giving the equations:
WS ¼ 0:691 ABH
ð
Þ0:84
WB ¼ 0:406 ABH
ð
Þ0:9 ;
ð4:17Þ
where WB is stem bole biomass.
For stem boles only Cannell (1984) found that the form factors f for most trees were 0.50.05.
Peichl and Arain (2007) determined form (or biomass expansion) factors for white pine (Pinus strobus)
aged 2, 15, 30 and 65 years. For stem boles, only these were about 0.3; for total above-ground biomass
they were 0.95, 0.65, 0.62 and 0.46, respectively.
Experience with Eqs. (4.16) and (4.17) shows that the standing biomass of forests can generally be
estimated quite accurately from standard measurements of tree height and stand basal area, given
values for wood density. Mensuration-based forest growth and yield models commonly use sigmoid
equations to describe the time-course of the height of the dominant trees in stands, and a variety of
statistically complex models, usually based on height and stem diameter, to estimate the stem volumes
of trees in different size classes.
West et al. (1991) inverted this procedure and presented allometric relationships that can be used to
predict stem height and diameter from observed – or predicted – biomass data. Their starting point
was a generalisation of relationships such as Eqs. (4.15) and (4.17) – but ignoring stem numbers (West
et al. 1989) – that they inverted to express dB and H in terms of stem, branch and foliage biomass.
It is interesting that in the Pinjuv et al. (2006) study with the process-based model 3-PG, the use of
allometric relationships as the basis for calculating stand basal area performed as well as the statistical
models. This is one of the few studies in which a process-based model has been directly compared to
conventional statistical models developed for the area (South Island, New Zealand) and species
(P. radiata) under investigation. But, even if we use a process-based model to calculate biomass,
because trees are not all the same size, even in uniform plantations, we may need to use statistical
Stand Structure and Dynamics
91

--- Page 106 ---

descriptions of tree size distribution to provide the outputs required by forest managers. These
distributions may be applied to stem diameters, or tree height, or both.
4.2.3. Height and Diameter Distributions
Even in high-quality, clonal even-aged plantations there are variations in tree size. These variations
are partly caused by biological variation, and are partly caused by spatial variations across sites, soils
and by microclimatic conditions. In particular, there will be variations in tree height and diameter.
As stands develop, the distributions that describe size variations will change.
The equation most commonly used to describe the distribution of variables such as stem height or
diameter at any age is the three-parameter Weibull distribution (Weibull 1951):
f x
ð Þ ¼ c
a

ð
Þc;
ð4:18Þ
where x>x is the variable under consideration (height or diameter), a>0 is the scale parameter, c>0 is
the shape parameter, and the location parameter x gives the minimum value of the range. If c	1, the
value of f(x) decreases as x increases; if c<3.6, the distribution is skewed to the right; if c>3.6, the
distribution is skewed to the left; if c¼3.6, the distribution is approximately normal (Rennolls et al.
1985). The case when x¼0 is called the 2-parameter Weibull distribution, and when x¼0 and a¼1 it is
called the standard Weibull distribution. The equation for the standard Weibull distribution reduces to
f x
ð Þ ¼ cx c1
ð
Þexc;
ð4:19Þ
where x0 and c>0.
Landsberg et al. (2005) applied Eq. (4.18) to stem size distributions observed over more than
50 years in Pinus sylvestris plots in Finland, where maximum values of stem diameter ranged from 9 to
36 cm. and average stem diameters were simulated using the 3-PG model. In a different application,
McCarthy and Weetman (2007) used Eq. (4.18) with other structural indices to characterise the
structure and development of 50 stands of Abies balsamea, ranging in age from 11 to more than
100 years, in Newfoundland. The stands were subject to recurrent insect herbivory.
The temporal evolution of the diameter distribution was modelled by Nord-Larssen and Cao
(2006). They assumed the distribution was always characterised by the Weibull equation and estimated
the Weibull parameters for each of a large number of stands. These parameters were then fitted to
suitably chosen functions of stand-level variables such as mean dominant height, stem number and
stand basal area. Although this is not in itself a dynamic model, it provides the Weibull parameters, and
hence allows estimation of the diameter distributions to be made from stand-level variables that may be
predicted by a process-based growth model.
4.3 ALLOMETRIC SCALING AND ITS IMPLICATIONS
As a tree grows in response to the local climate, site conditions and management practice, its various
components change in size and shape in a coordinated manner. If an object grows in size so that its
shape is preserved and its overall proportions are maintained, it is said to scale isometrically: areas scale
as the square of its linear dimension, and volumes or mass as the cube of its linear dimension. However,
as an organism grows, its scaling often departs from perfect isometry and its shape is not preserved: it is
92
Chapter 4

--- Page 107 ---

more important, for biophysical reasons, that functional rather than geometrical relationships are
preserved. For example, we have already seen from Eq. (4.15) that as a tree grows its height does not
grow in proportion to its diameter. A departure from isometry is called allometry i.e. ‘different
measure’. Allometry is the study of how growth deviates from isometry.
In this section, we apply the basic equation of allometric scaling to describe the changes observed in
tree growth, but not how these changes are induced or coordinated. The results are equations that can
be used to derive the overall structural properties of trees and stands from knowledge of, for example,
stem biomass and stem numbers. Such equations are invaluable for deriving variables such as stem
height and volume that are of primary interest to forest managers (e.g. see Section 9.2.8) from the
outputs of process-based tree growth models (which focus on biomass production). They also provide
constraints that ensure biomass allocation generates trees with an overall structure consistent with
observations (e.g. Section 5.6.2).
4.3.1. Allometry: How Objects Scale
A common way of studying allometry is to examine how the biomass w of a plant component
scales with respect to the total biomass wT of the plant. The equation used is
w ¼ awn
T;
ð4:20Þ
where a is the allometric scale and n the allometric power. Isometric scaling has n¼1 since w and wT
have the same dimensions, e.g. kg tree1, but in general n6¼1. In this context, the term ‘allometric
ratio’ is sometimes used, probably because when n¼1 the ratio w/wT is constant. Allometric relation-
ships are often studied by plotting one variable against the other using logarithmic axes so that an
allometric relationship then appears as a linear plot. This can be seen from Eq. (4.20): taking
logarithms of both sides gives lnw¼ln aþn ln wT. However, it must be born in mind that log-plots
can be misleading as they appear to reduce scatter.
A second way of examining how plants scale as they grow is to examine relationships between some
attribute Q of the plant, e.g. its height or a biomass pool, and a measure d of the linear size of the plant.
In this case, the fundamental equation of allometry is
Q ¼ aQdnQ;
ð4:21Þ
where aQ is the allometric scale factor, and nQ is the allometric power. If Q is a length, an area or a
volume, then isometry would have nQ¼1, 2 or 3, respectively. In general, this is not the case, and if nQ
is higher or lower than this value, Q is said to show positive or negative allometry, respectively.
When equations like Eq. (4.20) or Eq. (4.21) are fitted to typical observed data there is inevitably a
high statistical correlation between the estimated values of a and n. This means that if the value of n is
changed slightly and a re-estimated, an almost equivalent fit to the data can be obtained. It also makes it
difficult to make a meaningful comparison of these parameters between related data sets, e.g. when fitting
data from different clones of the same species, or different treatments at the same site. Accordingly, we
suggest using a common value of n but allowing a to vary between clones or treatments. That way, the
variations in a are isolated from n and can be interpreted as a genotype or treatment response.
It is obvious that the relationships observed at any time between the biomass pools, or how any
biomass pool varies with stem diameter, are the end result (the integral) of rates of translocation of
assimilates to the various component parts. Therefore information about the allometric relationships
of trees can be used to estimate carbohydrate allocation rates, and can also be used in models to
constrain the form and structure of trees to ensure they conform to natural systems.
Stand Structure and Dynamics
93

--- Page 108 ---

There is a considerable body of literature that explores the physiological basis of the allometric
relationships found in living organisms in general, and not just plants. Of particular interest is the work
of Brian Enquist and his collaborators, and we refer the reader to West et al. (1997), Enquist and Niklas
(2001), and Enquist (2002).
4.3.2. Allometric Relationships Between Biomass Pools
Typical allometric relationships between the biomass pools of trees are illustrated in Figure 4.2 using
age-series data from stands of a conifer (P. sylvestris) in Sweden (Albrektson 1980), and time-series data
from a fast-growing hardwood plantation (E. grandis) in Brazil (Almeida 2003; Almeida et al. 2004a).
The biomass pools for the main stem, coarse roots and branches and foliage are plotted as a function of
total tree biomass. Since the axes are logarithmic, a straight line implies an allometric relationship of
the form of Eq. (4.20). It is clear that main stem biomass scales allometrically with total biomass, and
the allometric powers are similar: n¼1.17 for the pine and n ¼ 1.23 for the hardwood. Coarse root
biomass also scales allometrically, although the relationship is not as tight as for the stem, and the
allometric powers differ: n ¼ 1.20 for the pine and n¼0.85 for the hardwood. However, relationships
for branches and foliage are very weak or non-existent. This is quite typical: allometric relationships for
structural components are usually much stronger than those for functional components. This is
because the functional components vary dynamically in response to the environment, and are subject
to significant litterfall or turnover.
These two examples represent a wide range of age, spacing, growth conditions and genotypes. The
pine data are from stands ranging in age from 7 to 100 years and populations from 800 to
3000 trees ha1 growing in a boreal climate, while the eucalypt data are from trees up to only 7
years old and are from each of 5 different genotypes growing in sub-tropical Brazil. The difference in
growth rates represented is dramatic: the eucalypts attained a DBH of 15 cm at about 5 years, with a

height of about 23 m and stem mass of about 93 kg, while the pine did not reach a DBH of 15 cm until
age about 50 years, when height was only 14 m with a stem mass of around 64 kg.
An alternative way of viewing these data is to plot the partitioning of total tree biomass into each
biomass pool as a function of stand age. This has been done in Figure 4.3 for the data used in Figure 4.2.
This illustrates a quite general principle: once a stand has established itself, it attains a more-or-less steady
state in terms of biomass partitioning The emergence of patterns such as these is of course the outcome of
the generally time-varying allocation of new assimilate to the biomass pools. As such it is an emergent
property of the myriad processes and forces that govern allocation. How biomass allocation can be
constrained to ensure that predicted tree growth does indeed display these patterns will be considered in
Section 5.6.2. (Note that patterns may vary in coppiced stands, which are not considered here.)
4.3.3. Biomass as a Function of Diameter
A second way of exploring the allometry of tree growth is through the dependence of the biomass
pools on tree size, in particular through the use of Eq. (4.21) with d replaced by stem diameter at breast
height or DBH (dB, cm). In this context, isometric growth requires n¼3 since biomass is proportional
to volume which scales isometrically as the cube of size. However, in general this is not the case.
a) Allometry of Stem Biomass
A common application of Eq. (4.21) is to estimate stem biomass from stem DBH. Figure 4.4 illustrates
the relationship between total stem (i.e. main stem, branch and bark), coarse root, and foliage biomass
pools and DBH for the stands used as examples in the previous section. The allometric powers n are very
similar for both sets of stem data (i.e. n2.87), although the allometric scales a are different. We have
already commented on the wide ranges of age, spacing, growth conditions and genotypes in these two
examples. In fact, the robustness of relationships such as these is quite remarkable, and they have been
observed to hold across awide range of plant species, and awide range of orders of magnitude of plant size.

--- Page 111 ---

biomass could be predicted from single equations developed across the entire age sequence, using
diameter as the sole input variable. Ter-Mikaelian and Korzuhkin (1997) collated data for 65 evergreen
and deciduous American tree species and listed hundreds of values for a and n for stemwood with and
without bark, branches and foliage. Gower et al. (1994) collated data illustrating the allometric and
carbon partitioning patterns for pine forests. Snowdon et al. (2000) surveyed data from forests and
woodlands across Australia and provided a and n values for various species of eucalyptus (in planta-
tions as well forests and woodlands), and for P. radiata. The general allometric equations Snowdon
et al. produced for above-ground biomass had a values between 0.11 and 0.25, and a mean value for
n of 2.4.
Zianias and Mencuccini (2004) evaluated methods for estimating the allometric parameters for
above-ground biomass that avoided the expensive and time-consuming process of harvesting trees and
determining the oven dry weights of their component parts. They tested a small tree sampling scheme,
and predictions based on fractal geometry and tree size-shape relations, but concluded that neither is as
reliable as the standard method of destructive sampling. They also listed 279 sets of values for the
coefficients, covering deciduous and evergreen temperate trees, tropical trees and eucalypts. The mean
value of n was 2.370.016.
In view of the existence of the data bases mentioned above, anyone undertaking a modelling exercise
that requires allometric biomass relationships, and who cannot directly establish empirical values for a
and n for the species or situation under study, should be able to make appropriate estimates on the
basis of the published tables for different species and these general values should prove useful and
accurate enough in many situations.
b) Age Effects on Stem Allometry
In a typical application of Eq. (4.21), a number of trees spanning the diameter distribution in a
stand are harvested and the masses of their biomass components (stem, branches, foliage, etc.) are
determined. These data are then fitted to Eq. (4.21) to give relationships for the biomass as a function
of DBH. These allometric biomass equations are then applied to other trees in the stand to determine
their biomass components from a simple measurement of DBH. When the measurements are based on
trees of a common age, and then repeated for the same stand at a later age, the allometric parameters a
and n often differ. However, sound relationships for a and n as a function of stand age can usually be
developed if required (see Fontes et al. 2006). Another point to note is that the relationships
determined from trees of a single age differ from those determined from an age series of trees using
the stand-level mean biomass and diameter in Eq. (4.21). These are two very different relationships,
and are used for quite different purposes. Those based on data at a specific age are usually used to
determine tree biomass data at that age. Those based on age-series data are used to determine how
stand-level properties vary with stand age.
These comments are illustrated in Figure 4.5. This figure is based on the data used by Landsberg
et al. (2005) in an application of the growth model 3-PG to an age series of 24–87 year old P. sylvestris
stands growing in Finland. The results of fitting Eq. (4.21) to the stem biomass data for trees of age 24,
50 and 87 years are shown, along with the fit of Eq. (4.21) to the stand-level mean biomass and DBH
data. It is clear there is a systematic variation in the allometric parameters with age, and the allometric
parameters for the stand-level means differ from these. Which set of allometric relationships should be
used depends on the purpose of the exercise. For example, 3-PG (Landsberg and Waring 1997) uses
allometric relationships fitted to stand-level means from age- or time-series data to constrain biomass
allocation. To estimate the distribution of stand properties, such as stem volume from the distribution
of DBH, it is more appropriate to use age-specific allometric relationships.
Stand Structure and Dynamics
97

--- Page 112 ---

c) Allometry of Other Biomass Pools
The allometric Eq. (4.21) can be applied to other biomass pools, such as roots, branches and foliage;
see Figure 4.4. Strong relationships between biomass and DBH are regularly found for slowly varying
biomass pools, i.e. woody tissue such as stems, coarse roots and larger branches (see Figure 4.4a–d).
However, in the case of biomass pools that are responsive to environmental factors, such as foliage and
fine roots, often only a weak or no relationship is found (see Figure 4.4e and f).
The generally poor quality of the allometric relationships for foliage seen in Figure 4.4 is emphasised
by the relatively low r2 values for foliage allometric relationships as compared to woody biomass in data
bases such as those referenced in (a) above. However, quite good relationships between needle mass
and DBH are often found for conifers (e.g. Levia 2008), presumably because needles have a relatively
long retention period.
Almeida (2003) concluded that accumulated leaf litter should be added to current foliage biomass
when constructing allometric relationships for foliage. We have done this in Figure 4.4f by assuming no
significant leaf loss prior to age 2 years and subsequently a loss rate of about 0.08 per month, and used
linear interpolation to estimate leaf biomass between observations. However, the parameters for the
resulting relationship are very sensitive to the assumed litterfall pattern.
d) Clonal or Species Effects on Allometry
The above references to allometric studies of the distribution of biomass between the various pools in
trees show that there are definite species differences in allometry. A careful determination of biomass
based on good sampling procedures can also reveal useful information about genetic differences within a
species. We illustrate this using the data from the study of Almeida 2003 that we used in Figure 4.4.
Almeida (2003) sampled three trees in each of five clones in E. grandis plantations in Brazil every year up
to age 5 (now updated to 7 years). These clones were planted at the same date in one hectare plots at the same

site and with similar soils, climate conditions and fertilization. One tree was selected with the stand-mean
diameter and height, one was selected one standard deviation above, and the third one standard deviation
below the mean diameter. The relationships between DBH and stem biomass are shown in Figure 4.4b with
the clones differentiated, and similar data for coarse root biomass are shown in Figure 4.4d.
For both stem and root biomass, Eq. (4.21) was fitted to all clones simultaneously, but with the same
value of n used for each clone. The resulting allometric parameters are shown in Table 4.1, and the
differences between clones is significant. The relative order of the clones differs if stem or root biomass
is considered. These differences manifest in differing growth patterns for the clones, and Almeida et al.
(2004a) showed, using the 3-PG growth model, that they produced significantly different time-courses
of stand biomass, stem volume, and leaf area index. In particular, if the allometric scale a is considered,
clones C2 and C3 are quite similar for stem biomass, with C1 significantly different from these, whereas
for root biomass C2 and C5 are the extremes.
e) Effects of Stem Numbers, Fertility and Water Status on Allometric Relationships
Despite the apparent robustness of the allometric relationships for woody biomass in terms of stem
diameter, it is well known that stem number affects variables such as stem height – recall Eq. (4.15) and
the discussion following it. Accordingly, we need to consider the extent to which the allometric
relationships are affected by factors such as the number of stems per hectare, and even management
practices such as fertilization or soil water availability.
Almeida and Sands (personal communication) studied the effects of stem numbers and water
availability on allometric relationships for tree biomass components and height in terms of stem
diameter. Their data came from a spacing study involving irrigated and rain-fed E. grandis of three
genotypes grown in Brazil. Spacing ranged from 1111 to 10,000 trees ha1, and trees ranged in age from
1 to 4.3 years. An analysis of the correlation between treatment variables, stem DBH, height and the
biomass data showed that the variations in biomass and height were dominated by the effects of DBH and
stem number, but the effects of water availability were small, with the possible exception of its effects on
stem height and the fraction of above-ground woody biomass in the form of branches and bark. There
was considerably more variation in the functional biomass pools (foliage and fine roots) than in

where aQ is the allometric scale factor, and nQB and nQH are the allometric powers for stem DBH and
number, respectively, and the 1000 scales N to values appropriate for commercial forestry. Almeida and
Sands fitted Eq. (4.22) to the single-tree averages of the biomass pools, branch and bark fraction,
and stem height for the stands in their study. The estimates of aQ, nQB and nQH obtained when clonal
effects were separated, but treatment (rain-fed or irrigated) was ignored, are shown in Table 4.2.
If treatment effects were taken into account in addition to spacing and clonal material, the reduction in
the root-mean sum of squares was quite small.
It is clear from the standard errors of the estimates of nN that, with the exception of stem mass, for
which nN is not significantly different from 0, the effects of spacing on allometry are quite significant.
Another notable result is that the effects of stem diameter on foliage mass are very weak, with foliage
mass determined largely by spacing. It is of interest to consider the values of the allometric powers nN
shown in Table 4.2, and possible reasons for these values. However, we caution that these conclusions
are not necessarily general, as these stands are unusually fast growing and of young age.
Foliage biomass is essentially inversely proportional to stem number, irrespective of DBH. In the very
fast growing Brazilian eucalyptus plantations (Tables 4.1 and 4.2; Figures 4.2–4.4), where canopies were
closed at age 1 year, the space available for foliage was inversely proportional to N and proportional to
canopy depth. We expect canopy depth to be determined by radiation within the canopy so that at the
bottom of the canopy assimilation just balances respiration. If canopy depth were constant, with the
canopy simply moving up the growing stem, then we would expect foliage biomass to be independent of
stem DBH and inversely proportional to stem number, as is shown by the results in Table 4.2.

Chapter 4

In the case of root biomass, we note that there is usually a maximum depth to which roots penetrate the
soil profile, and the highest density of roots tends to be in the upper profile. Thus the volume of soil
available to roots is limited and we would again expect biomass to be inversely proportional to stem
number. Here nN¼0.67 for large roots, roughly consistent with this conclusion. However, in contrast to
foliage biomass, root biomass increases with stem size, as we would expect, because roots are a physical
continuation of the stem and also anchor the stem in the ground. Bigger trees need more roots for stability.
4.4 LEAF AREA OF TREES AND CANOPIES
Canopies are formed by the crowns of trees. The architecture of a forest canopy is described by the
vertical and horizontal arrangement of foliage through the canopy space. This, and the canopy LAI,
determine how much PAR is intercepted by a canopy, and hence the photosynthetic production by the
canopy. The energy absorbed by canopies is also a primary determinant of their transpiration rate
(Section 2.5.3), and their roughness, in the aerodynamic sense, determines the effectiveness of the
coupling between canopies and the air (Section 2.5.1). Accordingly, the characteristics of a forest
canopy are of great interest.
Landsberg and Gower (1997) provided a detailed discussion of canopy architecture, and the rest of this
section is based on their treatment. At the stand level the number, spacing, height and size distribution of
the trees are obviously major determinants of canopy architecture. At the tree level, environmental and
geneticfactors influencecanopyarchitecture.Treecrowns varybetween narrow,columnarshapes(i.e.large
height to crown radius ratio) associated with high latitude or relatively dry sites, and broad or spherical
crowns associated with low latitude, generally well-watered sites, and wide spacing.
Forest canopies tend to be characteristic of particular forest types, but there is generally considerable
variation in canopy architecture even within a given type, induced by spacing, stand age and species
composition, as well as differences in soil fertility, precipitation and the water balance. In deciduous
forests, the canopy changes through the growing season as foliage emerges, reaches maturity and falls.
The changes in evergreen forests are slower, depending to a large extent on the life span of the foliage;
species with long-lived foliage tend to support greater foliage mass.
4.4.1. Specific Leaf Area
Foliage mass at any time depends on the number of leaves and the specific leaf area (abbreviated as
SLA), which is the leaf area per unit foliage mass sF (m2 kg1). Leaf area is sometimes expressed as
the total surface area of leaves, but in this book the term refers to the single-sided or projected
foliage surface area – not the total surface area. The fundamental relationship linking leaf area AF
and foliage mass wF is
AF ¼ sFwF;
ð4:23Þ
where AF is in m2 and wF is in kg. If wF is in kg m2 ground area, then AF is the LAI of the tree, i.e. leaf
area per unit ground area. The corresponding relationship for a canopy is
L ¼ 0:1sFWF;
ð4:24Þ
where L is the canopy LAI and WF is the stand-level foliage mass, in t ha1, and the 0.1 arises through
conversion of units from kg to tonns and m2 to ha.
Stand Structure and Dynamics
101

--- Page 116 ---

Landsberg and Gower (1997) provide discussion on the conversion of sF to leaf area for different
leaf types, as well as guidelines for the measurement of sF. In particular, they recommend in the case of
studies on conifers that total needle surface area should be reported: if the projected area of needles is
calculated for randomly assorted needles a conversion of p should be used to convert to total leaf area,
but if the needles are oriented in a non-random pattern then a conversion factor based on the geometry
of the needles (commonly ranging from 2.2 to 2.6) should be used.
Specific leaf area varies with species, tree and leaf age, position in the canopy, and nutrition. Values
in many conifers range from about 4 to 5 m2 kg1. For mature eucalypts, sF is in the range 4–8 m2 kg1,
and 10–20 m2 kg1 for young eucalypts (e.g. Cromer et al. 1993). Values around 10 m2 kg1 appear to
be representative of broad-leaved deciduous trees. Ogaya and Pen˜uelas (2006), working with Quercus
ilex in Spain, found that sF (analysed in terms of its reciprocal, leaf mass per unit area) decreased with
increasing solar radiation, although the correlation was weak and with considerable scatter – values
ranged from about 4–12 m2 kg1. Cramer et al. (2000) also observed that SLA declined with increasing
solar radiation in seven deciduous species growing in fertilised and unfertilised plots of two early
successional northern hardwood stands in New Hampshire. Novellon et al. (2009) reported that SLA
declines with increasing height in the canopy of Eucalyptus plantations in the Republic of Congo. In fact,
this finding is a general one across a wide range of species, and implies that shade leaves have a higher
value of sF than do sun leaves.
In an enormous data collection and collation exercise which included more than 2500 species at 175
sites across the world, Wright et al. (2004) found all species followed the same trends and relationships.
The most marked relationships were those between sF and leaf photosynthetic capacity, and sF and leaf
N-concentration, both of which showed strong positive trends. Cromer and Jarvis (1990) found a
strong linear relationship between sF and plant nitrogen concentration in seedlings of E. grandis, while
Cramer et al. (2000) also observed that SLA increased with improved site nutrition in most of the
deciduous species they studied, and was hardly affected in others.
Some species have different foliage forms for young and mature trees. As each form has its own SLA,
and the proportion of immature and mature foliage varies gradually as the trees grow, through a
process of leaf shedding and renewal, SLA can vary with age. Accordingly, SLA averaged over a canopy
shows a smooth transition from high to low values as the stand ages. Figure 4.6 shows the variation of
