4.4.2. Estimating Leaf Areas
The direct measurement of the leaf area on a tree is not possible without destructive sampling.
However, as discussed below, indirect estimation based on simple measurements of stem or branch
diameter are possible. Similar indirect determination of canopy leaf area index is possible, although
modern instruments are available that give measures of LAI based on the transmission of light through
the canopy.
a) Tree Foliage Area
Equations relating foliage mass (wF, kg tree1) to stem or bole diameter at breast height (dB, cm) or
to sapwood cross-sectional area (AS, m2 tree1) allow the calculation of leaf area per tree (AF,
m2 tree1) using Eq. (4.23) if SLA is known. Reasonable statistical relationships between foliage
mass per tree and dB can usually be established at any age, giving equations of the form of Eq. (4.21):
wF ¼ aFdnF
B :
ð4:26Þ
However, the coefficients obviously vary seasonally in deciduous trees and with stand density, age
and nutrition in evergreens. We emphasised earlier (in Section 4.3.3) in the context of allometric ratios
and their implications, that relationships such as Eq. (4.26) do not necessarily hold for time series of wF
and dB data because of the high variability of litter fall.
A linear relationship between wF and sapwood cross-sectional area AS was established by Grier and
Waring (1974), and has since been shown by others for a number of species. So
wF ¼ mAS þ c:
ð4:27Þ
Equation (4.26) or Eq. (4.27) allow estimates of leaf area AF per tree from Eq. (4.23) if the specific
leaf area sF and the values of the parameters in Eqs. (4.26) and (4.27) are known. These values have to
be established, in most cases, by destructive sampling in the tree populations of interest, although we
note that non-destructive methods of estimating stand leaf area index LAI, using instruments that
measure the light intercepted by canopies, have improved considerably in recent years. The relationship
in Eq. (4.27) was confirmed by Whitehead (1978) and Kaufmann and Troendle (1981), and Waring
et al. (1982) proposed the biophysical explanation for it: unit mass of foliage is serviced by a specific
cross-sectional area of sapwood in the crown of the tree.
In general, trees in drier environments have smaller AF/AS ratios than trees in wetter environments.
Water stress tends to reduce leaf growth, with the result that the trees reduce transpiration losses and
Stand Structure and Dynamics
103

--- Page 118 ---

there is greater conducting capacity per unit leaf area. The relationship applies, strictly, to sapwood
cross-sectional area at the base of the crown, so for accurate estimates based on dB, knowledge of stem
taper and the height of the lowest branches is required. Using this kind of information, Waring and
Running (2007) provide a table (Table 2.3, p. 39 of their book) of the leaf area/sapwood cross-sectional
area (at breast height) ratios (AF/AS) for various conifers and hardwoods. The ratios (m2 m2) range
from 700 for Nothofagus solandri in New Zealand and 800 for P. sylvestris in England, to 7100 for
A. balsamea and 7500 for Abies lasiocarpa, in the Pacific North West of the United States.
b) Canopy Leaf Area Index
The vertical distribution and arrangement of foliage through the canopy may be formally described
in terms of the vertical distribution of (projected) foliage area density aF(z) (m2 m3) per unit volume
of canopy space at height z (m). We may then define the cumulative leaf surface area per unit ground
area L – i.e. the leaf area index – as
L ¼

aF zi
ð ÞDzi:
ð4:28Þ
where H is the height of the canopy and the summation is over n height zones of depth Dz. Leaf area
index is arguably the most important variable that indicates forest productivity and water use at all
scales from trees and small plots to large areas of forest at regional scales. We noted above that leaf mass
per stem can be estimated using allometric relationships, particularly in evergreen species where foliage
remains on the trees through the winter.
It follows from the above relationships that if we know the stand density (trees ha1) and the
distribution of tree size (i.e. the number of trees in each size class per unit land area, e.g. given by the
Weibull distribution – Eq. (4.18)), then we can calculate LAI for a stand. Given values for specific leaf area
sF and the parameters in Eq. (4.26) or Eq. (4.27) for the species under study, these relationships are applied
to determine the leaf area of a tree in each size class, and summation across the number of trees in all size
classes gives the total stand LAI. For the purposes of modelling light interception, if accurate, short-term
results are required, information about foliage distribution and clumping would also be valuable.
In non-forestry canopies the principles are the same but the practicalities are very different. In no other
system, except perhaps shrub lands, would LAI be estimated on the basis of individual stems, although it
remains a factor of primary importance as the determinant for PAR absorption and transpiration. In most
populations of small plants, LAI is determined by sampling, and the most common procedure involves
measuring biomass per unit area and determining how it is partitioned between various biomass pools (e.
g. foliage, stems, etc). As we noted in Chapter 2 (see Eq. (2.52)), forests are much more strongly coupled to
the atmosphere than smaller, usually smoother, canopies, so transpiration rates from forests are than
generally higher from shorter plant communities under equivalent conditions.
4.4.3. Modelling Closed Canopy LAI
For foresters and forest ecologists, the attainment of canopy closure is often regarded as the most
important event in the development of a stand. At canopy closure increases in leaf mass tend to be
matched by litterfall. After canopy closure, the radiation interception characteristics of the canopy are
unlikely to change much, unless there is massive defoliation by insects or disease or the stand is thinned
or subjected to wind-throw (Landsberg and Gower 1997). As a ‘rule of thumb’ canopies can be
considered as ‘closed’ when their LAI is about 3. Inserting L¼3 into Beers Law, i.e. Eq. (5.5), with
the extinction coefficient k taken as 0.5, indicates that 78% of incident radiation is likely to be
intercepted by a canopy with an LAI of 3.
104
Chapter 4

--- Page 119 ---

a) Closed Canopy LAI in Terms of Site Factors
When the canopy of a stand first closes, its leaf area index is determined by environmental and site
factors such as soil water availability and site nutrition. For instance, Battaglia and Sands (1997)
established empirical relationships between the closed canopy LAI and mean annual temperature, an
annual average of soil water availability, and site nutrition.
The closed canopy LAI of 15 heavily fertilised E. globulus research plantations at sites in south-
eastern Australia, Western Australia and Portugal is shown in Figure 4.7 as a function of (a) mean
annual temperature Tav (
C) and (b) the annual average eCav of the crop factor (ratio of actual to
potential evapotranspiration) calculated using a simple water balance model. This figure shows that
LAI is determined by temperature for sites that are not water stressed, and by water stress for sites
whose temperatures are outside the optimal range suggested by (a). The curves shown are ‘envelope
curves’ indicating the maximum LAI attained under those conditions. Since site fertility has a
pronounced effect on LAI (e.g. Bennett et al. 1997), LAI was assumed to be a function of a soil fertility
ranking FS, designed so FS¼0 for fully fertilised plots, 1 for the most fertile soils, and up to 4 for the
least fertile soils (see also Section 6.4.1).
Battaglia and Sands (1997) assumed that the actual closed canopy leaf area index Lcc was the
minimum of that determined assuming it was separately limited by only nutrition (LN), temperature
(LT) or water availability (LW):

b) Closed Canopy LAI and Physiological Parameters
A simple relationship for closed canopy LAI in terms of physiological parameters was derived by
Battaglia et al. (1998). Their starting point was an expression for net annual photosynthetic production
Pn with foliage maintenance respiration proportional to the tissue nitrogen content. They then
assumed that the closed canopy LAI is that value of L that maximises Pn, and derived the equation
L ¼ 1

ð4:30Þ
where k (¼0.5) is canopy light extinction coefficient, sF (¼3.5 m2 kg1) is specific leaf area, e (kg MJ1)
is light utilisation efficiency, Qa (MJ m2 yr1) is annual incident PAR, r0 (¼80 kg kg1 yr1)
is maintenance respiration per unit canopy nitrogen content, NF (¼0.011 kg kg1) is average
leaf nitrogen concentration, and gF (¼0.15 yr1) is the annual foliage litterfall rate. The parameter
values are those for E. globulus.
Battaglia et al. (1998) then applied this model to the E. globulus data used in the ProMod study of
Battaglia and Sands (1997). They first determined simple empirical relationships for the dependence of
e on mean annual temperature Tav and the water stress indicator eCav as used in ProMod, and then used
Eq. (4.30) to predict the closed canopy LAI of the stands in the ProMod study. They found that if a
constant gF¼0.15 is used, then the model over-estimates LAI on cold or dry sites, i.e. sites that were
most highly stressed. However, by having gF increase when Tav<13.4, or eCav<0.75, the model
performed well. The model was also successfully applied to E. nitens, a more frost tolerant species
than E. globulus, by decreasing the cold-sensitivity of gF.
These two examples illustrate that quite simple models can be used to estimate one of the most
important determinants of stand growth, namely the closed canopy LAI of a stand. Moreover,
Eq. (4.30) relates this to some basic physiological parameters and hence explores the effect of leaf
physiology on stand properties.
4.4.4. Foliage Distribution
The foliage of forest stands may be arranged in a wide variety of ways, so the same leaf area index may
result from diverse canopy structures. Closed canopies, in stands dominated by different species, in
different ecological locations, may be very dissimilar in terms of the spatial distribution of the foliage
and, consequently, the way the canopies intercept light. For example, we can envisage a horizontally
homogeneous canopy, composed of foliage (leaves or needles) randomly, and more or less uniformly,
distributed between some level Hbwhere the foliagestarts, i.e. the canopy base, and H, the average height of
the trees. Alternatively, and more commonly, particularly in broad-leaved deciduous stands, leaves are
bunched on twigs so that there are gaps through the canopy, even though there may be considerable
overlap between branches. These gaps may be large or small, depending on the stem population and the
vertical distribution of the foliage. The third alternative, which occurs particularly in conifer stands, arises
when foliage is grouped, or clumped, within individual tree crowns, leading to unequal distribution
through the canopy.
106
Chapter 4

--- Page 121 ---

From the point of view of radiation interception, the homogeneous canopy will intercept most
radiation in the upper layers. If foliage is unequally distributed through the canopy, interception will be
less effective and radiation will penetrate deeper into the canopy, with some direct beams passing
through gaps and reaching the ground without striking foliage (see also Sections 5.1.2 and 5.2.4b). For
the same LAI, absorption rates will be considerably lower in the upper part of canopies with clumped
foliage but are likely to become higher at lower levels (Cescatti 1998). If the spacing between trees is
such that, at full canopy, the crown bases are just touching, more light will pass through the upper part
of the canopy, while light conditions for foliage in the lowest part of the canopy may be considerably
improved. Oker-Blom and Kelloma¨ki (1983) provide detailed mathematical analysis of the effects of
foliage grouping on within-stand and within-crown light regimes, and Kelloma¨ki et al. (1985) provide
analysis and a useful diagram illustrating the alternative canopy structures.
There have been a number of studies on the distribution of foliage in forest canopies. Landsberg and
Gower (1997) collated some of these data to produce Figure 4.8. Kinerson and Fritschen (1971)
described the individual crowns of Douglas fir as triangular, although most studies on conifers have
indicated that foliage area density distribution with height can be described by a normal curve
(Whitehead 1978; Beadle et al. 1982; Halldin 1985). Wang et al. (1990) fitted a b-function to the
vertical and horizontal distribution of needle area density for the four needle-age classes of P. radiata
and Cescatti (1998) provided the vertical profile of needle area density for Norway spruce, for which he
illustrated the differences in canopy transmittance to direct and diffuse radiation.
4.4.5. Foliage Dynamics
The mass of foliage carried by trees, and consequently the leaf area index of forests, is never static – it
changes continuously, varying with tree age, size and weather conditions. In most forest stands, as we noted
earlier, leaf mass increases until canopy closure: new foliage is added as branch numbers and lengths increase
and (in conifers) new whorls are added and the rate of foliage growth exceeds rates of loss through litterfall.
After canopy closure, increases in foliage mass tend to be matched by litterfall, so the amount of
foliage carried by a canopy tends to be (more or less) stable. However, there is often a long-term decline
in foliage mass because allocation tends to shift away from foliage towards the stem due to the need to

Stand Structure and Dynamics
107

--- Page 122 ---

provide structural stability (Section 4.2.1) and to provide a greater stem hydraulic conductivity in taller
trees (Section 7.4). The rate at which older foliage falls, both before and after canopy closure, depends
on the tree type, forest structure – particularly stem population – and weather conditions. Sparse
stands may never reach full canopy cover.
Obviously, the foliage dynamics of deciduous and evergreen trees are completely different. Decidu-
ous trees produce the full complement of leaves, consistent with their size, each season; foliage mass
increases rapidly in the spring and early summer, reaching a maximum in mid-summer. Leaf fall in
autumn tends to be rapid. On the other hand, the life span of foliage on evergreen trees may vary from
2 to 10 years, depending on species and climate. In general, evergreen trees in colder areas hold their
leaves for longer, and species with greater leaf longevity tend to support greater foliage mass (Schulze
et al. 1977; Gower et al. 1993a; Reich et al. 1995). New foliage on coniferous evergreens, growing in
places where winter conditions restrict growth, is likely to be produced during spring and summer, but
leaf losses may occur at any time. Pook (1984) found that, in Eucalyptus maculata, patterns of leaf
production and leaf fall were variable: foliage production may occur at any time of the year, driven by
the availability of water, and leaf fall generally lagged behind production.
a) Environmental Effects on Foliage Dynamics
The effects of water supply on foliage dynamics of P. radiata were clearly illustrated by Linder et al.
(1987).They studied the litterfallpatterns in an experiment with five treatments: control (C);irrigation (I);
irrigationþhigh level of solid fertilization (IF); fertilization without irrigation (F), and weekly liquid
fertilization (IL; see also the paper by Landsberg 1986a). Litterfall was low during the wet growing season
(NovemberApril) of 1983–1984, and high in all treatments during the winter (June–August) period. The
summer of 1984–1985 was dry: needle fall remained low on the I and IL treatments but increased markedly
between December and March on the non-irrigated C and F treatments, where measurements of plant
water potential showed the development of high levels of stress. In the F treatment, where there had been
considerable canopy development during the 1983–1984 summer, available soil water was depleted much
more rapidly than in the C treatment so that in the summer drought of November to April foliage losses
were very heavy in the F treatment, and earlier than in the C treatment. The IF, IL and I treatments
experienced a sharp increase in needle fall in the winter of 1985, similar to the pattern of the previous year,
but this did not happen in the non-irrigated trees, which had shed large amounts of foliage during the
drought. Additional information about foliage dynamics in this study is given by Raison et al. (1992a,b).
Beadle etal. (1982) modelled annualchanges infoliage areain Scots pine in Scotland using a set of linear
regression equations based on measurements of litterfall and leaf biomass. Their analysis gives information
on the general response of the system, but no insights into the effects of changes in conditions on the
response times. Beadle et al. found that all new needles were produced between the beginning of June and
the middle of August (i.e. in summer), that some of the new needles were lost in the first year, losses
continued at an increased rate in the second year, and the remainder fell in the third year.
Work by Dougherty et al. (1995) showed that Loblolly pine (Pinus taeda) produces a new cohort of
needles each year but retains the previous age class until the fall of the following year, so phenology can
result in annual variations in leaf biomass of nearly 100%. However, both needle growth and loss can be
affected by weather conditions. Hennessey et al. (1992) showed that temperature, rainfall and evapo-
ration can alter foliage growth by almost 30%, or change the date of litterfall by up to 2 months. Using
results of an experiment running over seven growing seasons in Oklahoma, Dougherty et al. (1995)
fitted a logistic equation to needle mass accretion which increased smoothly from May to about August
across all their plots and treatments. Monthly needle fall was low in April, increasing rapidly through
August–September and generally reaching a peak in November, but the observed peaks varied from
September to November. Dougherty et al. found relationships between needle fall and the leaf mass
108
Chapter 4

--- Page 123 ---

from the previous year, as well as with several environmental factors of which water balance was the
most important (as in the Linder et al. 1987 study).
Itisobviousthatfoliagedynamicsplayanimportantroleindeterminingthegrowthrateandproductivity
of forest stands. To maximise photosynthesis, and hence net primary production, stands need to produce
and maintain enough foliage to ensure the capture of as much radiant energy as possible (see Section 5.1).
This incurs costs when high LAI results in a high rate of water use and depletion of available soil water which
may not be replaced in drought conditions, leading to the observations reported by Linder et al. (1987). The
result is both foliage loss and reduced growth, a point well-illustrated by Snowdon and Benson (1992),
reporting on the effects on tree growth of the treatments discussed in the paper by Linder et al. (1987).
b) Modelling Litterfall
In principle, foliar dynamics are described by the equation
dwF
dt ¼ rate of increase  rate of loss
¼ FPn  gFwF
;
ð4:31Þ
where wF is the current foliage mass, Pn is the net primary production and F is the fraction of Pn that is
allocated to new foliage, and gF is the fractional rate of litterfall and has units of time1. However, the
units of litterfall rate itself depend on the units of wF, and are usually kg m2 yr1 for individual trees,
or t ha1 yr1 on a stand-level basis.
There are various ways to deal with the litterfall term (gFwF) in Eq. (4.31) when modelling forest
productivity. For evergreen trees, the simplest procedure is to assume that litterfall rate is constant
through the year so that, for example, in a model with a monthly time step 1/12 of the average annual
litterfall, in terms of leaf mass, is removed from the trees each month. However, treating litterfall as a
once-per-year occurrence would, in most cases lead to large errors, with LAI increasing to high values
through the year, with a large step change when the annual correction is applied. Use of a time-
dependent litterfall rate, i.e. gF¼f(t) based on empirical observations, would lead to more accurate
simulation of the time-course of LAI. Sands and Landsberg (2002), assessing the performance of the
model 3-PG in simulating the growth of E. globulus plantations, used a logistic-shaped curve to
describe the increase in litterfall as trees developed from seedlings to full canopy (see Section 9.2.6).
Sands and Landsberg (2002) also recommended that ‘further development (of 3-PG) should take
into account environmental effects on litterfall’, a point that emerges from the review in the preceding
paragraphs. A mechanistic approach might modify a basic litter fall rate gF0 by a function of the water
stress integral (Sc) described by Myers (1988) and shown by Raison et al. (1992a) to account for more
than 50% of litterfall in the P. radiata experiment discussed above (and see Figure 2.3 for its effects on
stem growth). So we might write
gF ¼ gF0fgw Sc

where fgw(Sc) takes into account effects of seasonal water stress. Enhanced litter fall as a result of low

(1998). They also used simple multiplicative factors for the effects of low annual average temperature

These equations and their parameter values are unlikely to be general, but are presented to illustrate
how litter fall may be modelled in terms of environmental factors.
The 3-PG model, discussed in detail in Chapter 9, calculates net monthly biomass increments on an
area basis, but works on an individual tree basis to calculate carbon allocation with litterfall subtracted
from foliage mass at the end of each monthly time step. Landsberg et al. (2003) note that ‘‘the carbon
allocation procedure allows LAI to vary with growing conditions . . . Sensitivity analyses have shown
that appropriate litterfall rates must be used if the time-course of LAI is to be correctly simulated.’’
Clearly, if the values used for litterfall are unrealistic, leaf mass, and hence LAI, would be incorrect, with
all the implications that has for radiation interception, canopy photosynthesis and canopy water use.
4.5 ROOTS
Root systems support the plant and provide the means for absorbing water and nutrients from the
soil. For adequate support, trees need root systems that spread widely and deeply enough to hold the
tree in place in all normal situations; to be efficient absorbers of water and nutrients roots need the
large absorbing surfaces of fine roots with high surface area to mass ratios. Analyses of their effective-
ness in the performance of these functions cannot be made in terms of root mass only, but must take
into account the structure of the root system.
The structure that meets these requirements varies with soil type and condition, and both require-
ments may not be met simultaneously. For example, in wet soil and high winds the support provided by
the roots may be inadequate and trees blow over. However, if the soil in the root zone becomes dry,
a system of fine fibrous roots with large surface area would be a disadvantage. Such roots have to be
relatively short as there is high resistance to water flow, otherwise they cannot carry enough water to the
stem. Therefore the volume of soil explored would be relatively small, although it would be thoroughly
explored. Fine, fibrous root systems are characteristic of grasses, whose root systems enable them to
exploit very effectively the water and nutrients available during favourable growing periods.
The general form of root systems is characteristic of the species, and hence genetically controlled.
However, the size and structural characteristics of root systems – the way they are distributed through the
soil and the depth to which they penetrate – are significantly affected by environmental conditions such as
water and nutrientavailability. Rootstend toproliferate inregions inthe soilwith high fertilityand suitable
moisture status. In this way, they are more plastic and opportunistic than above-ground structures. The
roots of most forest species will not grow in water-logged soil, nor will they grow in dry soil.
Root systems are not static; there is constant adjustment. Trees do not simply produce a root system
of a particular type and conformation and sustain it under all conditions. When soil conditions are
non-limiting, assimilate is diverted to above-ground growth. But if the rate of water and/or nutrient
uptake is inadequate to sustain the potential growth rate of the tree, then assimilates will be diverted to
increase the absorbing root surface area, provided this is possible in the prevailing soil conditions. This
is an example of the functional balance of root and shot activity and implies that the specific activity of
roots varies (Section 3.5.1).
110
Chapter 4

--- Page 125 ---

We know very little about signals that elicit changes in the pattern of assimilate allocation to root
(e.g. changes in growth regulators). If there are no limitations to foliage production, or to cambial or
meristematic activity, and no nutrient shortage, then carbon allocation to above ground parts will be
favoured. The reverse also applies.
It follows from these arguments that the study of root systems must involve the study of their
dynamics. This is necessarily tedious, time consuming and difficult. However, the amount of good data
in the literature on tree root systems has increased. Some of this will be discussed here to illustrate the
points made and to provide some information on the amounts of assimilate involved in root
production and turnover. The experimental methods used to study roots and root systems are
discussed in the papers cited, and also by Bo¨hm (1979).
4.5.1. Estimation of Root Mass and Length
There are manyestimates ofthe root massoftrees atany particular timeintheirgrowthcycle.One ofthe
most comprehensive studies is that by Jackson and Chittenden (1981), who completely excavated the root
systems of a numberofP. radiatatrees thatwere from 3 to 8 years old. Theyseparated the rootsintofive size
classes. These are: Class 1 or very large, >20 mm diameter; Class 2 or large, 10–20 mm; Class 3 or medium,
5–10 mm; Class 4 or small, 2–5 mm; and Class 5 or fine <2 mm diameter. This classification is identical to
that advocated by Bo¨hm (1979), except that he had a further category of ‘very fine roots’ (<0.5 mm
diameter). Such consistency between classification systems is important to ensure comparability of results.
Jackson and Chittenden (1981) produced a series of regression equations from which the weight
wRi (kg) of roots in each size class i could be calculated from a measurement of another part of the tree.
A number of such independent variables were used. In the case of fine roots – arguably the most
important from the point of view of water and nutrient absorption – the best relationships were
obtained between fine root dry weight wR5 and either foliage weight wF or the weight wR4 of small roots:
wR5 ¼ 0:08 þ 3:6wR4
wR5 ¼ 0:12 þ 0:53wF ;
ð4:35Þ
where the weights are in kg. If the total mass of a root system varies over a relatively short time interval,
it will be the fine root component that varies most. Because of their small size, fine roots are more likely
to be transient than larger (support) roots. Also, relationships such as Eq. (4.35) would be expected
from the principles of functional balance and interdependence (Section 3.5.1a) because the small root
class provides the immediate support structure for fine roots, and foliage provides the assimilate
necessary to produce them.
The standard allometric Eq. (4.21) was also used by Jackson and Chittenden (1981) for the mass of
roots wR4 (kg tree1) in the small diameter class, in the medium diameter class (wR3), and for the total
root mass WR, in terms of stem diameter dB. Thus
wRi ¼ aRwidnRwi
B
;
ð4:36Þ
where the allometric scales and powers are given in Table 4.3. These results are analogous to those
found by Almeida et al. (2004a) for E. grandis and shown in Table 4.1. For each size class, root length LRi
(m) can be estimated from root dry weight, and Jackson and Chittenden again found that a standard
allometric equation provided the best results:
LRi ¼ aRLiwRi
nRLi:
ð4:37Þ
Stand Structure and Dynamics
111

--- Page 126 ---

The values they gave for aRLi and nRLi are also in Table 4.3. The values of the nRwi in Eq. (4.36) given
in Table 4.3 are sufficiently close to one another to assume that they are probably not significantly
different. If this is the case, it implies that the ratios between those root size groups were in fact
constant, which would be useful.
To analyse the effectiveness of root systems, investigators often use parameters such as root length
per unit soil volume (LRV) or root length per unit soil surface area (LRA; analogous to LAI). Equations
such as those given above for converting root biomass into root length are therefore essential.
In a comprehensive review of root biomass data for forests and plantations across Australia,
Snowdon et al. (2000) obtained root mass estimates for 118 species from 45 separate studies. They
found that the root:shoot (mass) ratio in coniferous and broad-leaved forests was about 0.2–0.25, while
in deciduous forests the ratio was about 0.45. They noted that root:shoot ratios are quite closely related
to plant height, and that stem diameter (dB) generally provides a good predictor of root biomass.
However, they also noted that for individual plants, allometric ratios involving root systems seldom
provide reliable results, probably because of the complex nature of root systems and the way their
growth is affected by soil conditions. Peichl and Arain (2007), in their very thorough study of biomass
and its distribution in four white pine (P. strobus) stands between 2 and 65 years old, found that the
data across all sites were well described by an equation of the form of Eq. (4.36), i.e. wR¼0.0078dB2.44.
4.5.2. Root Dynamics
Santantonio (1989) collated data from a number of studies on fine root mass and dynamics in
coniferous forests. He showed linear relationships between the biomass of fine roots and of foliage,
which were dependent on site quality: the slope of the line for poor sites was almost three times that on
good sites, i.e. fine root mass per unit foliage mass was about three times higher on the poor sites. But
Santantonio was not able to establish useful relationships between fine root dynamics and stand growth
from the published data. Information on root dynamics, and the distribution of roots in the soil
profile, has come from work by Ford and Deans (1977), Deans (1979, 1981), Persson (1978, l980a,b)
and others.
Ford and Deans (1977) found no net increase in the weight of fine roots (<0.5 mm diameter) of
Sitka spruce over a growing season, although the soil density of fine roots varied from 20 to 34 kg m3
and LRV varied from 18103 to 32103 m m3. Root carbohydrates increased in early summer in a
manner which suggested that the build-up was a result of export from the previous season’s shoots.

112
Chapter 4

--- Page 127 ---

The minimum soluble carbohydrate concentrations in the storage roots were reached one week after fine
root weight reached its maximum, suggesting that fine roots grew at the expense of labile carbohydrates.
Deans (1979) noted that root mortality occurred whenever the water potential of the soil fell
significantly below about 0.01 MPa. In a later study on coarse root production, Deans (1981)
found that the youngest and thinnest roots greater than 5 mm diameter grew fastest and also
contributed most to root biomass. Collating data from several studies, Deans concluded that for the
10-year-old spruce trees studied by him, and by Ford and Deans (1977), fine root production was
1.38 kg tree1 yr1, production of intermediate roots (2–5 mm diameter) was 0.01 kg tree1 yr1 and
of thick roots (greater than 5 mm diameter) was 0.83 kg tree1 yr1. On a land area basis, these values
indicated that total root production exceeded 8 t ha1 yr1, which was about 34% of mean root
biomass. Deans did not give data on total biomass production, but if we assume that it was roughly the
same as the annual production by P. sylvestris of similar age in Sweden – i.e. about 11 t ha1 yr1 in an
untreated plot (Linder and Axelsson 1982) – then total root production constituted about 70% of
annual production, and fine root production used 45% of the assimilates available for growth.
Grier et al. (1981) measured biomass distribution and above-and below-ground production in
young (23-year-old) and mature (180-year-old) ecosystems dominated by Abies amabilis in the
Cascade Mountains in Washington State, USA. Net primary (biomass) production by the young
stand was l8 tonne ha1 yr1, of which 65% was below ground. Production by the mature stand was
16.8 tonne ha1 yr1 of which 73% was below ground. Conifer fine root production was 36% of
primary production in the young and 66% in the mature stand. Grier et al. found that fine root
biomass in A. amabilis peaked in early spring and late autumn, with low values in summer and
winter. The increase in fine root biomass in spring occurred in both the young and mature stands
well before any sign of above-ground growth activity, and root growth in general appeared to be
independent of aerial growth. These data again illustrate the principle of allocation in the direction
of specific activity: activity in the roots commences in the spring and becomes the primary sink for
carbohydrates. As shoot growth commences, the total activity in the shoots becomes greater than in
the roots and available carbohydrates move towards the shoots. At other times in the growing season
the direction of carbohydrate flow (whether to roots or to tops) depends on the relative activity of
the plant parts.
Keyes and Grier (1981) tested the hypothesis that trees on sites short of water or nutrients expend
comparatively more energy on below-ground dry matter production than do trees on more productive
sites. They studied 40-year-old Douglas fir stands on high and low productivity sites, where the
standing stem biomass was 423 and 231 t ha1 yr1 respectively. Above-ground productivity was
estimated from direct increments of tree growth and below-ground productivity was derived from a
combination of core sampling and observation windows. Keyes and Grier did not find statistically
significant differences in the mass of small and fine roots on the two sites, but fine root turnover at the
low productivity site was approximately 2.5 times that on the high productivity site.
The study of Keyes and Grier (1981) supports the suggestion that a greater proportion of the
available assimilate will be diverted to fine root growth where soil conditions are limiting for growth.
The annual above-ground and coarse root biomass increment on the poor site was approximately half
(6.4 t ha1 yr1) that on the good site (12 t ha1 yr1), but primary productivity differed by only 13%
when all above-ground and below-ground components of the two stands were taken into account
(15 t ha1 yr1 on the low productivity site; 18 t ha1 yr1 on the high productivity site). However,
36% of the dry matter on the low productivity site went into fine root turnover with 13% going to
foliage. The corresponding figures for the high productivity site were 8% and 3.4%. The differences in
stem biomass at the two sites can therefore be attributed to the integrated differences in dry matter
partitioning over the life of the two stands.
Stand Structure and Dynamics
113

--- Page 128 ---

4.5.3. Fine Roots
The results of Keyes and Grier (1981) are supported by those of Persson (1980a), who studied fine
root dynamics in Scots pine stands with and without near-optimum nutrient and water regimes.
Persson’s data provide further evidence for the sensitivity of fine roots to water stress. He found that in
the irrigated and fertilised plots fine root production was slightly greater than in the control plots, while
above-ground production in the irrigated and fertilised plots was nearly double that in the control
plots. Therefore, as a proportion of total productivity, fine root turnover was much greater on the
control plots. Linder and Axelsson (1982) estimated from Persson’s data that fine root production in
the control plots used about 35% of net assimilate as opposed to about 11% in the irrigated and
fertilised plots.
Because of the relatively transient nature of fine roots, i.e. their rapid turnover, attempts to establish
allometric relationships between fine root mass and some stable and standard measure of tree size –
such as stem diameter – have generally been unsuccessful. Santantonio and Herrmann (1985) sampled
the fine roots of Douglas fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii) in several habitats over 30 months. They estimated
fine root production on the dry, moderate and wet sites to be 6.5, 6.3 and 4.8 t ha1 yr1, with
turnover 2.8, 2.0 and 1.7 times per year on those sites, i.e. their results provide yet further support for
the suggestion that a greater proportion of available assimilate will be used for root growth in less
favourable sites.
Helmisaari et al. (2007) and Landsberg and Gower (1997) review a number of studies that showed
an inverse relationship between fine root mass and nutrient availability in boreal, temperate and
tropical forests. In general, fertilization decreases the allocation of carbon to fine roots on a relative
basis for all forests, and on an absolute basis for red pine (P. resinosa). Data presented by Haynes and
Gower (1995) indicate fine root production between 1.5 and 2.84 t ha1 yr1, being significantly lower
for fertilised than for unfertilized plots. Coarse root production ranged from 6 to 9 t ha1 yr1.
All these studies indicate the great importance of fine root turnover in the carbon balance of trees,
and it is clear any productivity estimates that ignore roots may be grossly in error. It is also clear that the
rapid turnover of fine roots provides a flexible mechanism for responding to changing conditions.
If, for example, trees are on a site which is drying, the fine roots may die off in the upper soil layers,
since the soil will tend to dry from the top downwards (partly as a result of high root densities in those
layers), but they may proliferate in lower layers as the damp zone retreats. The amount of structural
root available in lower layers is less than in the upper layers, so that the potential for fine root
production is smaller in lower layers. But we should note that some species produce large taproots
that can reach remarkable depths: Carbon et al. (1980) found a typical exponential reduction with
depth of root length density – dominated by fine to very fine roots – under jarrah (Eucalyptus
marginata) in Western Australia, but large vertical roots penetrated to the 20-m limit of their sampling.
Presumably, these long roots develop over many years when there is water deep in the soil.
With less fine root, a tree’s ability to take up water is decreased, consequently the resistance to
transfer of water from soil to roots is increased, and water stress increases. The questions of root
resistance to water uptake and the importance of water potential in these matters are discussed in the
chapter on water relations (Chapter 7). Below-ground allocation of carbon is important not only
because of its implications in relation to patterns of tree growth but also because fine root turnover is a
major contributor to soil respiration and hence CO2 evolution from the forest floor.
Note also that Cairns et al. (1997) have provided a valuable collection of data relating to allocation
of biomass to roots in the world’s upland forests.

Radiation interception and biomass production by forest stands are the fundamental plant ecosys-
tem processes. Almost all the processes that we are concerned with in this book contribute, either
directly or indirectly, to biomass production. In particular, radiation interception depends on leaf area
index and canopy structure, while canopy photosynthesis depends on the photosynthetic character-
istics of the foliage and is modified by stomatal behaviour. Moreover, the photosynthetic characteristics
of leaves are determined by the nitrogen distribution in the canopy, and there is strong observational
and theoretical evidence that this distribution tends to optimise whole-canopy production. In this
Physiological Ecology of Forest Production
Joe Landsberg and Peter Sands
ISSN 1936-7961, DOI: 10.1016/S1936-7961(11)04005-X
Copyright # 2011 Elsevier Inc.
All rights reserved.

--- Page 130 ---

chapter, we consider how canopy structure, leaf area and photosynthetic properties can be coupled
using commonly used and useful radiation interception models to give estimates of tree and canopy
biomass production.
The basic relationship with which we are concerned is
Pn ¼ Pg  R;
ð5:1Þ
where Pg and Pn are gross and net whole-canopy photosynthetic production, and R is respiration. The
units used for these quantities vary with the context, e.g., kg tree1 for single trees, and kg m2 or
t ha1 for stands. In the latter case, 1 kg m2¼10 t ha1. Gross production is the result of two
fundamental processes: light interception by the canopy and the conversion of intercepted radiation
to labile carbon through photosynthesis. Net production is then used to form new biomass.
Canopy production is a complex process because foliage is distributed throughout a canopy, and the
main factors that influence photosynthetic production–radiation, temperature, vapour pressure defi-
cit, leaf nutrient status–vary temporally (Section 2.2) and spatially (Section 2.3). Predicting canopy
production thus requires integration of the equations describing photosynthesis over time and space,
and the determination of how these environmental factors vary within the canopy in response to
transpiration. Complete energy balance and photosynthesis modelling has been accomplished, and is
outlined later. However, more simplified models suffice for most practical purposes, and especially
when production is required for a stand over an extended period of time. These are the main subject of
this chapter.
An important simplifying concept in the study of biomass production is light-use efficiency, namely
some measure of production per unit of intercepted radiation. For example, various observed data and
theoretical studies suggest that net production DPn (kg m2) over some time interval is proportional to
the amount of photosynthetically active radiation DQint (J m2) intercepted by a canopy in that
interval. Thus,
DPn ¼ eDQint;
ð5:2Þ
where e is the light-use efficiency with units of kg J1. A wide range of studies has shown that over an
extended period of time, from days upwards, radiation Qint intercepted by a canopy of leaf area index L
is related to the radiation Q0 incident on a horizontal plane above the canopy by
Qint ¼ ð1  ekLÞQ0;
ð5:3Þ
where k is an effective light extinction coefficient. The concepts of light-use efficiency and intercepted
radiation are particularly useful when considering production by stands over extended time periods,
and we consider their application in detail below.
Various factors impose constraints on canopy productivity, operating through both light intercep-
tion and photosynthesis. In this chapter, we consider how these processes can be coupled through
commonly used and useful radiation interception models to give estimates of tree and canopy biomass
production. In particular, the extent and distribution of foliage in a canopy clearly affect the amount of
light intercepted by the canopy, with shading by the upper canopy and by neighbouring trees playing
an important role. But despite shading effects, some leaves deep in the canopy experience full sunlight
some of the time, and the difference between sun and shade leaves must be taken into account.
Calculating gross photosynthetic production is only the first step in the process of estimating forest
productivity and hence wood production. Not all carbon fixed in photosynthesis ends up as plant
biomass as there are respiratory losses in all growth processes, not just photosynthesis itself. These
116
Chapter 5

--- Page 131 ---

impose a cost in carbon, and hence reduce growth, and the term R in Eq. (5.1) represents the
respiratory losses. However, its precise definition depends on the context of the system considered,
or the objectives of the model. If the context is a single leaf or a shoot, then R commonly represents
dark respiration of the foliage, while in the context of a stand, R may represent whole-stand respiration.
The concept of carbon-use efficiency Y (kg kg1) is often used to relate net growth to gross production.
This concept is used in various ways, but most commonly to relate net photosynthetic production, and
hence new biomass production, to gross production through the relation
Pn ¼ YPg:
ð5:4Þ
There is active debate in the literature as to whether carbon-use efficiency Y is constant over time,
and how it varies between species and environments.
A further step in the determination of forest productivity is the allocation of net carbon production
Pn to the various biomass pools of the stand. This is an area that is still poorly understood from a
process point of view. However, we outline attempts to model biomass allocation in process-based
forest growth models, and provide the details of a simple allocation scheme that ensures that stand
growth mirrors the patterns observed in real stands.
5.1 RADIATION INTERCEPTION
Incident solar radiation reaching a stand consists of a beam of parallel rays from the sun–called the
direct beam–and diffuse radiation resulting from scattering of the direct beam in the earth’s atmo-
sphere and reaching the stand from all directions of the sky. The radiation traversing a canopy interacts
with plant organs by absorption, transmission and reflection, and part of this radiation further
contributes to diffuse radiation within the canopy. Direct radiation may penetrate through gaps in
the foliage, creating bright sun flecks, the frequency of which decreases with depth into a canopy.
The details required to calculate radiation interception by stands will depend, to a large extent, on
the purpose of the exercise. A thorough treatment is provided by Ross (1976), although all the
information needed to apply it is seldom available. If values of radiation absorption are required for
short periods (e.g. 30 min), and estimates are required of the radiation regime of foliage at various
levels in the canopy at particular times, the analysis will need to be based on real-time radiation data,
and include details about foliage distribution within the canopy, including the leaf-angle distribution.
However, the assumption that the leaf-angle distribution is spherical, which implies that the area of
foliage normal to the beam is independent of beam direction, will not introduce significant errors.
More detailed discussion can be found in Monteith and Unsworth (1990), and in Jones (1992).
Most forest canopies are not horizontally homogeneous, but are characterised by leaves bunched on
twigs or by clumped foliage. This leads to a non-uniform distribution of radiation in the canopy.
Oker-Blom and Kelloma¨ki (1983) provided the initial, detailed mathematical treatment of the effects of
foliage clumping on the radiation environment in canopies.
Radiation interception by stands is thus a complex process, affected by the size, orientation, optical
properties and distribution of leaves within the canopy, as well as by the type, intensity and angle of
incidence of radiation on the canopy surface. The most accurate treatment of interception subdivides
the canopy into thin layers, or even small volumes, each of which is assumed to be homogenous with
respect to leaf properties and the radiation environment. This level of detail is appropriate to modelling
short-term changes in the radiation environment and the associated photosynthesis. The required
models are very data intensive and not suited for the general modelling of forest growth, but they
The Carbon Balance of Trees and Stands
117

--- Page 132 ---

provide valuable research tools, and a vehicle for testing simpler models. A paper by Wang (2003)
discusses some of the intricacies and possible simplifications in radiation models.
We discuss some common simplifications in the following subsections. The simplest canopy
interception model is the big-leaf model, in which the canopy is treated as a single leaf with averaged
photosynthetic properties, and photosynthesis is driven by the average amount of intercepted radiation
determined using Beer’s law. In a sun-shade model, the foliage is divided into two components: foliage
illuminated by direct sunlight, and shaded foliage. Each component is then treated as a single leaf, but
with radiation interception and photosynthesis taking into account the radiation environment experi-
enced by that component. With suitable, and plausible, assumptions, both these simpler models can
incorporate the temporal variation of the main environmental factors affecting photosynthesis.
De Pury and Farquhar (1997) compared the performance of a multilayer with a big-leaf and a
sun-shade model. They found predictions by the big-leaf model were biased but concluded that a
single layered sun-shade model, also known as a two-leaf model (e.g. Wang and Leuning 1998; Dai et al.
2004), is sufficiently accurate and far simpler than a multilayer model.
5.1.1. Beer’s Law
Despite canopy heterogeneity, and variations in sun angle and radiation characteristics, many
empirical observations have shown that, when the profiles of radiation in a horizontal plane, and of
leaf area density, are measured at different levels in a canopy (e.g. as in Figure 4.8), the transmittance of
direct and diffuse radiation through the canopy can be adequately described by a simple exponential
relationship with accumulated leaf area. This is analogous to Beer’s law for attenuation along a single
beam, i.e.:
IðzÞ ¼ I0ekLðzÞ;
ð5:5Þ
where I0 and I(z) are respectively the irradiances in horizontal surfaces above the canopy and at some
level z within the canopy, L(z) is the accumulated leaf area index of the canopy at that level, and k is
called the extinction coefficient. The analysis that leads to Eq. (5.5) also shows that the fractions fsun
and fsha of a horizontal surface at the depth z that are illuminated by the sun or shaded are given by
fsun ¼ ekL;
fsha ¼ 1  ekL;
ð5:6Þ
respectively.
The procedure for determining k is to plot ln(I(z)/I0) against L(z), where L(z)¼0 at the canopy top
and is summed from the top down to the measurement level. Assuming sampling problems have been
overcome, this will usually yield a straight line with slope k. There are significant differences in the
values of k determined by measurements in different forest types (Jarvis and Leverenz 1983), but the
average value for both coniferous and deciduous forests is about 0.5. Equation (5.5) has been found to
provide good estimates of the radiation absorption profiles through forests. This is especially the case if
measurements are averaged over intervals long enough to smooth short-term fluctuations in the
radiation intensity, and within-canopy sampling is adequate to deal with spatial heterogeneity in
radiation distribution,
Monsi and Saeki (1953), quoted by Thornley and Johnson (1990), show that the irradiance Il on a
leaf (W m2, on a leaf area basis), and hence driving photosynthesis, is
IlðLÞ ¼
k
1  m IðLÞ ¼
k
1  m I0 ekL
ð5:7Þ
118
Chapter 5

--- Page 133 ---

where m is the average leaf transmittance. Thornley and Johnson quote typical values of m for
photosynthetically active radiation as up to 0.15 for thin leaves such as beech, and 0.02–0.05 for
conifers.
Equations (5.5) and (5.7) give estimates of the average irradiances at any level in the canopy, but in
fact the intensity of radiation within canopies may vary enormously because of sunflecks, especially
where there is a considerable component of direct-beam radiation. In these circumstances, the intensity
distributions under moderate leaf area indices tend to be bimodal with low-level background diffuse
radiation and bright sunflecks. This distribution may be of considerable importance for leaf processes,
such as photosynthesis, which depend non-linearly on radiation. The inclusion of sun and shade leaves
in the sun-shade model is considered in the next section.
Over short periods of time (e.g. 1 h) the errors involved in using the average irradiance in different
layers to calculate photosynthesis may be considerable, and any productivity model that purports to
provide good estimates of CO2 uptake by canopies over such periods must use a more complex model
such as the two-stream model described in the next section. However, if we are concerned only with
daily or longer time totals of CO2 uptake, then the use of Beer’s Law is unlikely to lead to large errors.
For these longer intervals we may use mean or daily total values of radiation, calculate the average
intensity and apply it to average values for leaf photosynthesis, or simply use total intercepted radiation
with a light-use efficiency value. This approach ignores the interaction between the non-linearity of
photosynthesis and the diurnal variability in radiation, and hence can lead to biased estimates of
whole-canopy photosynthesis. However, the light-use efficiency approach discussed in Section 5.3 and
applied over more extended time periods, as is done in the 3-PG model, can provide adequate estimates
of longer term canopy photosynthesis.
The advantage of using the simple Beer’s Law radiation interception model, with average values of
leaf photosynthesis, is that it can be applied with satellite-derived estimates of L and estimates of
canopy photosynthetic production can based on general information about the species of interest, so
covering large areas and applied over periods of months and years. For such applications there is little
justification in using complex and theoretically more accurate models, for which the data needed to
exploit the greater accuracy will, generally, not be available. For large-scale, long period applications
Occam’s Razor must apply: the simplest solution to the problem at hand is the one that should be
applied. The errors resulting from uncertainties in soil nutritional status and its effects on photosyn-
thesis, from uncertainties in the water balance because of uncertainties in available water in the root
zone, and from uncertainties in carbohydrate allocation, could all be more serious than those resulting
from inaccurate estimates of canopy NPP. Nevertheless, where data that permit the realistic use of more
accurate models are available, those models should be used. They can also be used as research tools to
test and improve those simpler models that are the tools of choice in practical applications.
5.1.2. Sun-Shade or Two-Stream Model
For research purposes or detailed applications, models that divide individual canopies into rela-
tively small volumes and calculate the radiation regime (and photosynthesis) for each volume over
periods as short as 30 min will often be used. An example of such a model is MAESTRA (see
Section 5.1.3a). Other options are the use of layered models of canopies, with the radiation interception
calculations carried out for each layer separately.
Sun-shade models divide the canopy into sunlit and shaded fractions, and model each fraction
separately using a single-layer model. This approach appears to have been pioneered by John Norman
(1982) and we present here the simple version of the sun-shade model, based on Norman’s work,
The Carbon Balance of Trees and Stands
119

--- Page 134 ---

largely as presented in the original edition of this book. More detailed treatments can be found in
de Pury and Farquhar (1997) and Chen et al. (1999). Clumping or grouping of foliage at any level
increases light penetration and leads to a smaller value for the extinction coefficient k. This is well
illustrated by the detailed study of Cescatti (1998), in which he investigated the effects of different levels
of leaf clumping on radiative regimes. This study used a detailed, three-dimensional model of crown
shape (Cescatti 1997) to generate different architectural scenarios and a radiative transfer model to
predict fluxes in the modelled canopies.
For canopies with a spherical leaf-angle distribution–which implies that there is always the same

Lsun ¼ 2ð1  e0:5OL=cosyzÞ cosyz

IdirC ¼ Idire0:5OL=cosyz

De Pury and Farquhar (1997), who combined the sun-shade model with the equations for leaf
photosynthesis (Section 3.1.4), demonstrated, for a wheat canopy for which experimental data were
available, that the results were as accurate as those obtained with a detailed multilayer radiation
interception model. Medlyn et al. (2000) derived an analytic expression for canopy photosynthesis,
using the sun-shade model and the assumption that nitrogen in the canopy–and hence photosynthetic
capacity–was optimally distributed. The derivation and equations are summarised by Medlyn et al.
(2003), who tested the performance of the model against the big-leaf model and eddy covariance
measurements made over a period of four years over a Sitka spruce plantation in Scotland. The sun-
shade/optimal nitrogen model performed best at daily, weekly and monthly timescales.
5.1.3. Single Tree and Rows
The heterogeneity of forest canopies, or of the distribution of individual trees within a stand, has led
to the use of detailed light extinction models that account for grouping of foliage between and within
trees. An example, MAESTRA, is described below. However, because such models require fine resolu-
tion inputs and detailed parameterization, they are impractical in larger scale applications such as
forest growth models. Accordingly, simple models that can be easily parameterized, and interception
models for individual canopies of simple shapes, are widely used. So-called summary models, that use
semi-empirical relationships to summarise the results from detailed models, comprise a third class of
interception model.
a) MAESTRA, a General Light Interception Model
The most commonly used detailed, general model is MAESTRA. Medlyn (2004) provides the
background on the genesis of this model: it was developed from a detailed array model called
MAESTRO developed by Wang and Jarvis (1990), which in turn was developed from the model of
Norman and Welles (1983). There were various changes to MAESTRO during the 1990s. In particular,
Medlyn made it easier to use, and set up a website to disseminate the code (Medlyn and Duursma
2009). At that time it was also renamed MAESTRA.
In MAESTRA, the positions of all the trees in a stand are specified by their x, y and z coordinates.
Each tree is individually described by the radius of its crown in the x and y directions, crown length
(i.e. the distance from the top of the tree to the lowest live whorl or branch), height from the ground to
The Carbon Balance of Trees and Stands
121

--- Page 136 ---

the crown base and total area of leaves within the tree crown. The positions of leaves in both the vertical
and radial directions are defined by functions describing the leaf area density distribution (Wang and
Jarvis 1990); the leaves within the crowns are classified with respect to their age and condition, and can
have different physical and physiological properties. MAESTRA includes sub-models to simulate the
radiation regime of the leaves, and their CO2 and water vapour exchanges. It takes into account both
direct solar radiation, and the diffuse radiation from the sky.
MAESTRA is essentially a research tool for exploring canopy processes. Its level of detail allows
examination of the influence of canopy architecture on radiation. It has been used to examine the
sensitivity of forest growth to stem number, foliar density or crown shape, to evaluate the importance
of changes in canopy structure following silvicultural treatments such as pruning and thinning, to
determine the consequences of defoliation by disease or insects, to examine the influence of tree
spacing and size in agroforestry systems (Medlyn 2004), and to test the accuracy of predicted canopy
light interception, photosynthesis and transpiration made with simpler models. But since MAESTRA
does not include routines for respiration, has no nutrient cycling or nutrient feedbacks and contains no
carbon allocation routines, it is not a model of forest growth. Nevertheless, it is a very useful and
important tool for exploring the detailed, short-term responses of forest canopies or individual trees.
The MAESTRA website provides a long list of publications describing various applications of the
model.
b) Single Tree and Hedgerow Light Interception Models
There have been a number of relatively simple treatments of radiation interception by discontinuous
and irregular tree canopies. Charles-Edwards and Thornley (1973) produced a model of light intercep-
tion by an isolated plant. The model assumes that individual tree canopies are ellipsoids truncated by a
horizontal plane and that the leaf area density within the canopy is uniform. Light traversing the
canopy is extinguished according to Beer’s law, based on the accumulated leaf area normal to the beam.
However, analytical expressions for intercepted radiation are not given. Later, Charles-Edwards and
Thorpe (1976) extended this model to predict the interception of direct-beam and diffuse radiation by
a hedgerow apple orchard, or plants in rows. They tested their model in an orchard, and found
excellent agreement with observed transmission of both diffuse and direct-beam radiation.
c) A Summary Model of Light Interception
Duursma and Ma¨kela¨ (2007) presented a summary model of light interception by non-homoge-
neous canopies. They support our earlier observation that Eqs. (5.2) and (5.3) provide a useful tool for
determining canopy photosynthesis over periods from months to years, especially for homogeneous
canopies. They modelled interception Qint (J tree1) by an individual unshaded tree in a homogeneous
canopy over some time interval using
Qint ¼ ð1  ekHLC=AT ÞATQ0
ð5:16Þ
where Q0 (J m2) is the accumulated radiation in the same time interval incident above the canopy,
kH is the extinction coefficient for a homogeneous canopy, LC (m2 tree1) is the leaf area in the tree
canopy and AT (m2 tree1) is the horizontal area occupied by each tree in the canopy. Note that LC/AT is
the leaf area index of the single tree, and ATQ0 is the radiation falling on the ground area occupied by
the tree. Equation (5.16) has also been used to model light interception by individual trees in a canopy
that has not closed.
Next, Duursma and Ma¨kela¨ noted that light interception by a crown is related to the crown area
projected in the direction of the sun, and that when averaged over all directions, this area is one-fourth
122
Chapter 5

--- Page 137 ---

of the crown surface area AC (m2 tree1) for convex crowns (Lang 1991). Accordingly, they rewrite
Eq. (5.16) as
Qint ¼ ð1  ekHLC=fACÞfACQ0;
ð5:17Þ
where f is an empirical parameter that depends on the mean solar angle for direct-beam radiation but
is constant for isotropic diffuse radiation. Consider a stand of N trees on a ground area AS; typically
AS¼1 ha. It follows from Eq. (5.17) that the fraction of light falling on AS but intercepted by one of
these trees is
fi ¼ f AC

They then considered stands in which the trees are Poisson distributed. This means that the probability

Qint ¼ ð1  eNfiÞQ0

Combining Eqs. (5.18) and (5.19) gives

A simplifying assumption involved in deriving Eq. (5.21) was that all trees were equal in size and

By comparing predictions from Eqs. (5.20) and (5.21) with the output of detailed light interception

latitude 10 to 0.73 at 65, but for diffuse light it was constant at 0.38. They also demonstrated that

into account important characteristics of the individual tree crowns. These are the crown surface area
AC, which is a good summary variable for crown size and shape independent of crown shape
(ellipsoids, cones, height/width ratios), and the leaf area LC in an individual crown. Also required is
the nominal light extinction coefficient kH of a homogeneous canopy.
Two useful formulae that may be needed when applying the summary model are the volume

VC ¼ p
