5.2 MODELLING CANOPY PHOTOSYNTHETIC PRODUCTION
It is not just the amount of radiation intercepted by a canopy that determines photosynthetic
production, and hence growth of a tree or stand, but also what happens to it following interception.
Once radiation has been intercepted and absorbed by chloroplasts in the foliage, it is used to convert
CO2 into carbohydrates for growth. Our understanding of photosynthesis at leaf level is largely derived
from gas-exchange experiments (see Chapter 3). Accordingly, it is necessary to consider how leaf level
photosynthesis can be integrated over space (the canopy) and over an extended time period to give the
canopy-level photosynthetic production by a stand. This is sometimes called ‘‘scaling-up’’. In this
section, we discuss the general issue of scaling from leaf to canopy, and how it has been accomplished in
the context of forest growth models.
A hierarchy of approaches has been used to scale leaf-level processes to the canopy-level. The most
complex subdivides the canopy into a large number of homogeneous volume elements and determines
the characteristics of radiation in each volume, infers leaf-level photosynthesis and transpiration, and
performs an energy balance to determine the leaf temperature and other meteorological factors in that
volume. Such models are often referred to as plant-environment models. Multilayer models subdivide the
canopy into horizontal homogeneous layers, and may or may not perform an energy balance. In two-leaf
models, the canopy is subdivided into sunlit and shaded foliage classes, while big-leaf models treat the
canopy as a single leaf. A useful review of these approaches can be found in Medlyn et al. (2003).
In this section, we expand on these approaches and review their performance and uses. First, we
make some general observations about the process of scaling in canopy models.
5.2.1. Scaling of Canopy Processes
An excellent overview of the options for scaling physiological processes from the leaf to the
canopy, and from minutes to months is provided by Norman (1993). ‘‘Scaling’’ refers to the use of
information on one temporal or spatial scale, to infer features or behaviour at another, usually
coarser, scale. Norman distinguishes between integrating and scaling: ‘‘integrating’’ (in a broad, non-
mathematical sense) is an orderly process of bringing disparate parts into a whole, whereas ‘‘scaling’’
often implies the use of intuition to bridge a gap of missing information. However, the scaling of
physiological processes may involve mathematical integration. Models based on scaling are
usually simpler than models that require detailed integration of diverse elements. This is illustrated
by the difference between the simple big-leaf or two-leaf models, which are scaled models, and the far
more complex multilayer or plant-environment models, which have a high degree of integration of
detail.
In forest growth models it is necessary to scale in two contexts: spatially from leaf-level to canopy-
level processes, and temporally from short-term processes to daily or longer timescales. In moving
from short-term, leaf-level processes to longer term, canopy-level processes there are many factors that
come into play: details of the canopy architecture such as leaf angles and distribution; short-term
temporal changes in the environment of the leaves due to leaf movement and longer term (diurnal or
seasonal) environmental changes; and gradients between the environment outside the canopy and the
124
Chapter 5

--- Page 139 ---

leaves within the canopy. The key to successful scaling from leaf to canopy is to be able to identify a few
of these factors as being dominant. If this cannot be done, then models of the disparate processes have
to be combined using an integrative approach (Norman 1993).
Norman (1993) reviewed various approaches to scaling instantaneous photosynthetic and transpi-
ration rates from the leaf to the canopy. He then compared predictions made with each of these
approaches to the results obtained from a comprehensive plant-environment model known as Cupid
(Norman and Campbell 1983) that had been validated against field observations. His results showed
that scaling based on a two-leaf model that distinguishes sunlit and shaded leaves provides a remark-
ably good fit to output from Cupid. This finding has been widely supported by other studies using a
range of models. The non-linear response of photosynthesis and stomatal conductance to meteorologi-
cal variables introduces considerable error if a big-leaf approximation is used to scale from the leaf- to
canopy-level.
If scaling is now extended from short-term processes to daily totals, the effects of the non-linearities
return because of the diurnal variation of the meteorological variables, e.g. radiation varies from zero to
values that saturate leaf-level photosynthesis. This is not an issue if a model is simply run repeatedly,
using observed metrological data. If these detailed data are not available, simple but realistic forms for
the diurnal variations of the meteorological data can be assumed (e.g. see Sections 2.2.1–2.2.3). In some
instances, these simple diurnal forms allow analytical solutions to be developed for the daily photo-
synthetic integrals.
5.2.2. Structure of Whole-Canopy Models
The structure for a whole-canopy model depends on the intended purpose of the model, in
particular the spatial and temporal scales at which it is to be applied, and on the available data.
Here, we summarise a hierarchy of structures used to create whole-canopy models; examples are given
in Section 5.2.3.
a) Plant-Environment Models
The instantaneous photosynthetic rate–and the associated transpiration rate–of a single leaf
depends on the direction and amount of light incident upon it, the temperature, vapour pressure
and CO2 concentration of the surrounding air, and on the photosynthetic parameters of the leaf. The
latter are in turn determined by the temperature and nitrogen concentration of the leaf. These factors
normally vary spatially within the canopy and over time. The variations are also often correlated
because the leaf and its environment are an interacting system, whose state is determined largely by the
energy balance of incoming solar radiation and outgoing latent heat through transpiration. Accord-
ingly, rigorous analysis of canopy processes requires quite complex models combining an energy
balance at each point within the canopy with the prediction of photosynthesis and transpiration in
response to the environment within the canopy, while taking into account the interactions between
these due to transpiration.
Models with this degree of complexity are known as plant-environment models, and were first
developed in the context of agricultural crops, e.g. maize and cotton. They are often linked to general
circulation climate models used to predict the regional–or global–environmental impacts of vegetation
and of changes in vegetation. Plant-environment models were also developed for forests, but applied
more as research tools than as forest growth models.
In general, plant-environment models apply a leaf-level model of photosynthesis (and transpiration,
as they should be determined jointly) at each of many points along parallel, narrow beams of light
The Carbon Balance of Trees and Stands
125

--- Page 140 ---

traversing the canopy to determine the contribution by foliage at those points to whole-canopy
photosynthesis. These contributions are summed to give whole-canopy photosynthesis and transpira-
tion. For direct radiation, all beams have the same direction, as determined by the sun, while for diffuse
radiation it is necessary to consider beams from a number of representative directions. This algorith-
mic approach is also used by MAESTRA.
It is instructive to consider how these models work because the principles apply to simpler models, even
those that attempt to find analytical solutions. Consider a single beam as it traverses the canopy. At each of
a number of points along it, the fluxof radiation intercepted by foliage at thatpoint is determined and local
conditions within the canopy used to determine the parameters in the leaf-level model.These are then used
to calculate the local photosynthetic rate, which is combined with the leaf area density to give the
contribution to whole-canopy photosynthesis by foliage at that point. The attenuation of radiation
along the beam is taken into account, usually using Beer’s law, and the process repeated at the next point
along the beam. Summing contributions by all points gives the contribution to canopy photosynthetic
production (and transpiration) by thatbeam. Thisprocess is repeated for all beams intersecting the canopy
to give total photosynthesis at that time. Finally, production and transpiration over an extended time
period are determined by repeating the procedure a number of times and summing the results.
Although systematic and straightforward, this process is numerically intensive. It is ideally suited to
situations were it is necessary to take into account the detailed variation of leaf area density and
environmental conditions within the canopy, or to determine canopy photosynthesis over short-time
intervals.
b) Multilayer Models
In a multilayer model the canopy is subdivided into homogenous horizontal layers. Each layer may
be further separated into sunlit and shaded fractions, and each fraction into leaf-angle classes. Each
layer may also be characterised by the foliage nitrogen concentration. Incoming direct-beam irradiance
is partially scattered by foliage to contribute to diffuse radiation within the canopy. Direct and diffuse
radiation is absorbed by sunlit leaves, but only diffuse radiation is absorbed by shaded leaves.
Photosynthesis by each leaf class in each layer is determined using a biochemical leaf-level model
(see Section 3.1.4a), and summed to give whole-canopy photosynthesis. Depending on the context, the
leaf-level model may or may not be coupled to a model of stomatal conductance (see Section 3.3), and
the model as a whole may or may not include a detailed energy balance to determine the distribution of
meteorological variables within the canopy. The whole process is repeated for a range of sun-angles and
the results combined to give daily production.
Various studies have shown that these detailed multilayer models adequately reproduce the observed
diurnal variation of canopy assimilation and transpiration. They are used primarily for research
purposes, and in particular as standards to which the performance of simpler models are compared.
c) Two-Leaf or Sun-Shade Models
Leaves exposed to direct sun have a very different environment to shaded leaves. Their irradiance is
predominantly direct and high, and often saturates photosynthesis, whereas irradiance on shaded
leaves is low and diffuse, and in the linear portion of the photosynthetic light response. Sunlit leaves
can also be several degrees warmer than shaded leaves. These differences in environment in turn lead to
differences in leaf structure between predominantly sunlit or shaded leaves.
These observations, especially on the radiation environment, suggest that it is essential to treat sunlit
and shaded leaves separately. Models that do this are referred to as two-leaf, or sun-shade models. It is a
126
Chapter 5

--- Page 141 ---

general finding that sun-shade models quite accurately reproduce the behaviour of similarly structured
multilayer or plant-environment models.
d) Big-Leaf Models
Big-leaf models treat the canopy as a single leaf and replace within-canopy variation of photosyn-
thetic parameters and climatic factors with fixed average values. Depending on the context of the
model, they may or may not calculate fluxes of energy, water and CO2 by coupled equations, and
represent photosynthesis with either a biochemical model or an empirical light response curve.
Big-leaf models are far easier to parameterise, and less demanding of computer time than multilayer
models. However, models that treat the canopy as a single leaf tend to overestimate fluxes of CO2 and
water vapour. This is because of the highly non-linear nature of the response of photosynthesis to
irradiance, and the wide range of irradiance experienced by leaves due to a combination of its diurnal
variation and decline with canopy depth.
5.2.3. Examples of Whole-Canopy Models
In principle, any plant-environment model developed for crop canopies can be adapted to forest
canopies. Examples are Cupid (Norman and Campbell 1983) and the model of Leuning et al. (1995),
but over the past two decades models have been developed specifically for forest canopies. It is generally
not necessary, when modelling forest production, to model the dynamics of canopy processes on short
timescales; furthermore, detailed data on leaf area distribution and within-canopy variation of
meteorological factors that affect photosynthesis and transpiration are seldom available. The various
approaches outlined in Section 5.2.2 have been applied to canopy processes in forests. In most cases
this results in models better suited to the longer timescales, e.g. days to months, used in practical forest
management, which can be applied with only basic, routinely available climatic data.
Although originally applied to wheat, and almost certainly too complex for use in forest growth
models, the Leuning et al. (1995) model is a good example of a comprehensive whole-canopy model.
It is a multilayer model that simultaneously solves for leaf-scale stomatal conductance, CO2 assimila-
tion and the energy balance as a function of leaf position within canopies. Sunlit and shaded leaves in
each layer are treated separately, and separate extinction coefficients used for diffuse and direct
radiation. Photosynthesis and stomatal conductance are determined for each foliage class using the
leaf level model of Leuning (1995). Leaf nitrogen content and light intensity decrease exponentially
within the canopy, and photosynthetic capacity depends linearly on foliage N concentration. The
model reproduced observed short-term variation of various climatic factors above a field of wheat at
Wagga Wagga, Australia. It showed that significant gains in daily canopy photosynthetic production
occur when leaf N is distributed according to the profile of absorbed radiation.
de Pury and Farquhar (1997) developed a series of whole-canopy models: a multilayer model with
separate sunlit and shaded leaf classes, a single-layer model that also distinguished sunlit and shaded
leaves, and a big-leaf model. All used the biochemical model of leaf photosynthesis with a constant
intercellular CO2 concentration (245 ppm) and no energy balance. Leaf nitrogen concentration
decreased exponentially with accumulated leaf area index in the canopy, and photosynthetic capacity
was proportional to leaf nitrogen above a threshold. Integration over the canopy in the sun-shade and
big-leaf models was based on this leaf nitrogen distribution. Irradiance was divided into direct, diffuse
and scattered components, and the multilayer model assumed a spherical leaf-angle distribution to
simplify the irradiance penetration model.
The Carbon Balance of Trees and Stands
127

--- Page 142 ---

The three models of de Pury and Farquhar (1997) were used to predict instantaneous and daily
canopy photosynthesis. Comparison of the results from these models showed unequivocally that the
single-layer sun-shade model accurately reproduced the outputs from the multilayer model under a
range of conditions. The big-leaf model could be calibrated to reproduce output from the multilayer
model, but the necessary adjustments depended on conditions. de Pury and Farquhar (1997) also
explored the consequences of leaf nitrogen distribution and argued that, although the distribution of
leaf nitrogen often mirrors the distribution of time-averaged absorbed irradiance, this distribution is
not the same as the instantaneous profile of irradiance and can lead to misleading conclusions about
the optimal distribution of nitrogen required to maximise total canopy photosynthesis.
Chen et al. (1999) developed a two-leaf sun-shade model based on the Farquhar biochemical model
of leaf photosynthesis, but with photosynthesis and transpiration coupled similarly to Leuning (1990),
i.e. ignoring boundary layer resistance. The effects of environmental factors on stomatal conductance
were taken into account by multiplicative functions (Section 3.3). Leaves were divided into sunlit and
shaded classes, and direct and diffuse radiation were explicitly recognised as described in Section 5.1.2.
Chen et al.’s emphasis was on the derivation of numerically efficient models of daily canopy photosyn-
thesis. Diurnal integration was aided by assuming that stomatal conductance varied linearly with time
in both directions about solar noon, and by simple assumptions for the diurnal variation of meteoro-
logical variables. They demonstrated the validity of this sun-shade model by comparing its output with
CO2 fluxes measured as part of BOREAS (Boreal Ecosystem-Atmosphere Study) in Manitoba and
Saskatchewan in 1994 and 1996. They also compared the big-leaf version of their model with the sun-
shade model and concluded that although the big-leaf model provided a reasonable simulation of the
general seasonal variation in canopy photosynthesis, it did not reproduce day-to-day variation
particularly well.
A more recent example is CLM 2L (Dai et al. 2004). This is a one-layered, two-leaf model that
distinguishes sun and shade foliage and uses the two-stream approximation for handling radiative
transfer (Section 5.1.2). Stomatal control links photosynthesis and transpiration for sunlit and shaded
leaves separately, and leaf temperatures are determined by an energy balance calculated for each foliage
class. Scaling of photosynthesis from the leaf to the canopy is based on the assumption that within a
canopy the maximum rates of carboxylation and electron transport (see Section 3.1.4) decline
exponentially with accumulated leaf area index (see also Section 5.2.4b). The model is solved numeri-
cally, using an iterative approach for determining leaf temperatures. Dai et al. applied CLM 2L to
broadleaf evergreen tropical and coniferous boreal forests; comparison with observed data showed that
the two-leaf model performed better than a similar big-leaf model (CLM 1L) that did not separate
sunlit and shaded foliage.
Models such as these synthesise knowledge of single-leaf physiology, and the physics of transpiration
and the radiation regime within a canopy. They are valuable research tools that provide a biophysical basis
for the responses of canopies to the various environmental and physiological factors that affect whole-
canopy photosynthetic production and transpiration. A general conclusion is that a single-layer sun-shade
model provides an excellent fit to outputs from far more comprehensive plant-environment models. They
also require simpler data than the plant-environment models, and are far less numerically intensive.
Further gains in numerical efficiency could be made if integration of the diurnal variation of
environmental factors were possible. Accordingly, we now examine models that attempt this.
128
Chapter 5

--- Page 143 ---

5.2.4. Analytical Models of Gross Canopy Photosynthesis
Scaling from the leaf to the canopy level can be achieved by making assumptions that allow the
general expression for canopy photosynthesis to be integrated analytically, leading to explicit formulae
for whole-canopy photosynthesis, either at a given time (e.g. Thornley 2002), or for a day (Sands
1995b). The basic mathematical problem is to perform the integrations in the expression for daily
whole-canopy assimilation AC (mmol m2 day1), i.e.
AC ¼
ðh

AðAmax; IlÞdLdt;
ð5:23Þ
where L is the canopy leaf area index, h (s day1) is the day length, A and Amax (mmol m2 s1) are the
instantaneous and light-saturated leaf-level photosynthetic rates, and Il (mmol m2 s1) is the irradi-
ance incident upon the leaf.
The leaf-level assimilation rate A is represented by a non-rectangular hyperbolic light response:

q
ð5:24Þ
where Amax (mmol m2 s1) is the light-saturated assimilation rate, a (mol mol1) is the quantum
efficiency, and y is the shape parameter. We have denoted A as a function of the light-saturated
photosynthetic rate Amax and the irradiance Il incident upon the leaf because it is primarily through
these quantities that A depends on the accumulated leaf area L. The use of Eq. (5.24) rather than a
detailed biochemical model is motivated by its simplicity, and is justified by the fact that it accurately
represents observed leaf-level assimilation rates. Its three parameters can be determined as a function of
leaf temperature and ambient CO2 concentration by gas-exchange experiments.
The irradiance Il incident upon a leaf at any level in the canopy is related (e.g. Thornley and Johnson
1990) to the irradiance I (mmol m2 s1) in a horizontal plane by
Il ¼
k
1  m I;
ð5:25Þ
where k is the light extinction coefficient in the canopy, and m is the leaf transmittance. It is also
assumed that at any level L in the canopy Il is determined by Beer’s law, in the form Eq. (5.7), and hence
Il is proportional to ekL.
a) The Basic Analytical Model
We consider first the model of Sands (1995b). This results in simple equations for total daily gross
canopy photosynthetic taking into account the decline of irradiance with canopy depth, and the
diurnal variation of irradiance and temperature. The model was designed to be used when only
standard meteorological data were available: daily insolation Q (MJ m2 day1) and daily maximum
and minimum temperatures. Although the original model considered only a single-leaf class, we
outline below how sunlit and shade foliage classes can be taken into account. Also, it was not coupled
to a model of stomatal conductance, but Battaglia et al. (2004) did this in the Cabala model, following
Leuning (1990).
The Carbon Balance of Trees and Stands
129

--- Page 144 ---

An important assumption that allows evaluation of the integral over L in Eq. (5.23) is that Amax
acclimates to the local mean irradiance. Mathematically, it is assumed that Amax is proportional to the
ratio of local PAR to full sun PAR, i.e.
AmaxðLÞ ¼ IðLÞ
I0
Ax ¼ AxekL;
ð5:26Þ
where Ax is the light-saturated assimilation rate at the top of the canopy, and I0 is the irradiance in a
horizontal plane at the top of the canopy. The second part of the equation follows from Eq. (5.7). It is
also assumed that the other photosynthetic parameters (a and y) do not change with position in the
canopy, which is likely as they appear to be independent of leaf N concentration.
The assumption embodied in Eq. (5.26) is based on work dating back to Field (1983) who showed
that total canopy assimilation was a maximum for a fixed total canopy leaf nitrogen provided the
change in assimilation rate per unit change in nitrogen concentration (i.e. @A/@N) was constant down
the canopy. Sands (1995a) derived this result on theoretical grounds, and showed that Eq. (5.26)
followed. Studies using multi-level plant-environment models have also shown that observed whole-
canopy assimilation is reproduced when leaf nitrogen concentration declines with canopy depth more
or less in direct correlation with time-averaged irradiance at that level. However, others have concluded
that nitrogen is not necessarily optimally distributed in this way. For example, in a study of Nothofagus
fica, Hollinger (1996) found that the observed nitrogen distribution showed less N in the brightest sites
in the canopy than would be expected under the assumption that @A/@N was optimum, which may
result in a 5–10% overestimation of canopy assimilation. Hollinger nonetheless suggested that
modellers may wish to take a pragmatic approach and assume an optimal distribution, which we do
here. Since Il is time-averaged irradiance, the de Pury and Farquhar (1997) caution against making this
assumption when using instantaneous irradiance should not apply.
By substituting Eq. (5.26) into Eq. (5.24), the integration over L in Eq. (5.23) gives
AC ¼ 1
k ð1  ekLÞ
ðh
0
AðAx; Il0Þdt
ð5:27Þ
where the integral over time is now the daily assimilation at the top of the canopy, i.e. in full sun. Sands
(1995b) assumed that the diurnal variation of irradiance and temperature is described by trig func-
tions, i.e. Eqs. (2.3) and (2.9). Explicit expressions for the daily integral in Eq. (5.27) for the two cases of
a rectangular hyperbola (y¼0) and a Blackman response (y¼1) were derived, and the general case was
determined from these two. The result is expressed in terms of the photosynthetic parameters, the light
extinction coefficient, the daily insolation, and the day length (see Sands 1995b). We do not give the
detailed derivation here, but discuss how the diurnal temperature variation was taken into account.
It was assumed that leaf temperature was the same as air temperature. Gas-exchange observations
from various studies show that Amax has a temperature optimum and declines in a parabolic fashion
either side of the optimum, a increases slightly with increasing temperature, while y appears to be
independent of temperature. Sands (1995b) then divided the day into sunrise-to-noon, and noon-to-
sunset periods, and inferred the mean temperatures for these two periods from the maximum and
minimum temperatures using Eq. (2.10). These temperatures were used to determine Amax and a for
the pre- and post-noon periods, and the integrated form of Eq. (5.27) used to determine canopy
photosynthesis separately for each period, and hence for the whole day. Sands (1995b) used simula-
tions for plantation grown Eucalyptus globulus in SE Tasmania, Australia to show that this simplified
treatment of temperature gave an excellent approximation to numerical integration of Eq. (5.27) with
diurnal variation of temperature and insolation explicitly taken into account.
130
Chapter 5

--- Page 145 ---

The observed effects of seasonal acclimation of photosynthesis to temperature (Battaglia et al. 1996)
were included in the model by Sands (1996). The model was then used to explore the sensitivity of
canopy light-use efficiency to site factors and to leaf-level photosynthetic parameters as discussed in
Section 5.3.2. The Sands (1995b, 1996) model is used in various site productivity or tree growth
models: e.g. ProMod (Battaglia and Sands 1997), CenW (Kirschbaum 1999), Cabala (Battaglia et al.
2004). These are reviewed in Chapter 8. A spreadsheet implementation of the Sands model is included
on the PHYSECOL web site.
The Sands (1995b) model was used to generate weekly averages of daily photosynthetic production
by the canopy of a stand of Eucalyptus globulus grown in SE Tasmania, using the observed variation of
solar insolation, air temperatures and daylength over a year. These results are compared in Fig. 5.1 with
the instantaneous single-leaf light response used for this species: a non-rectangular hyperbola with
parameters Amax = 15, a = 0.05 and y = 0.9. (For the purposes of the comparison, irradiance and
photosynthetic rate for the leaf response have been expressed as daily values.) The predicted canopy
photosynthetic data are also approximated by a non-rectangular hyperbola. The fitted parameters are
Amax = 19.1, a = 0.044 and y = 0.15, which differ from those of the original leaf response. Figure 5.1
illustrates how scaling up from a single leaf light response to daily production by a canopy reduces the
non-linearity of the light response. In this case, various factors have contributed to this scaling effect,
including the seasonal variation of air temperatures and daylength.
The fact that the response of daily canopy photosynthesis to daily intercepted radiation is also a
non-rectangular hyperbola was used by Ide et al. (2010) to determine canopy GPP from remotely
sensed spectral vegetation indices for a larch forest. They showed that the seasonal trends of Amax and
a for the canopy-level light response were related to these indices, while short-term variations were
related to meteorological factors. Their study demonstrated that by allowing parameters of the canopy
light-response function to fluctuate daily, high temporal resolution estimates of GPP in response to
short-term, diurnal and seasonal changes were improved. Their process predicted observed GPP for
4025 half-hourly measurements with an r2 of 0.83, with no significant bias but considerable scatter.
We suspect the quality of predictions for daily GPP would be considerably better.

b) Sun and Shade Leaves
Thornley (2002) also based leaf-level assimilation on Eq. (5.24), and obtained exact analytical
expressions for the instantaneous canopy assimilation when sunlit and shaded leaves were considered
separately.
Shaded leaves receive only diffuse radiation, which declines with canopy depth in accordance with
Beer’s law, but sunlit leaves receive both diffuse and direct radiation, where the latter is not affected by
canopy depth. Let Idir0 and Idif0 (mmol m2 s1) be the flux densities (irradiances) of direct and diffuse
radiation, respectively, in a horizontal plane above the canopy. The fraction fsun of sunlit foliage
declines with canopy depth,
fsun ¼ ekL;
ð5:28Þ
where k is the light extinction coefficient, and the fraction of shaded leaves is 1–fsun. The flux densities
Ilsun and Ilsha (mmol m2 s1) incident upon sunlit and shaded leaves are given by
IlsunðLÞ ¼
k
1  m ðIdir0 þ Idif 0ekLÞ
IlshaðLÞ ¼
k
1  m Idif 0ekL;
ð5:29Þ
respectively. These are then used in Eqs. (5.24) and (5.23) to separately determine whole-canopy
assimilation ACsun and ACsha by sunlit and shaded leaves, respectively:
ACsun ¼

ð5:30Þ
The integrations over L are more complicated than that leading to Eq. (5.27) and the final formulae can
be found in Thornley (2002). Numerical integration is required to determine daily canopy assimilation.
The original model of Sands (1995b) can also be adapted to a canopy comprising sunlit and shaded
leaves by separately determining photosynthesis from direct and diffuse radiation. The component of
daily canopy photosynthesis due to diffuse radiation falling on both sunlit and shaded foliage is
predicted using the original model with total insolation above the canopy (Q0) replaced by its diffuse
component. Canopy photosynthesis due to direct-beam radiation is obtained by integration of
Eq. (5.30) for ACsun but with the term for diffuse radiation in Eq. (5.29) for Ilsun omitted. This gives
the same equations as in the basic model, i.e. Eq. (5.27) and the subsequent integrations given in Sands
(1995b), but with Q0 replaced by its direct-beam component. Total canopy photosynthesis is now the
sum of the direct and diffuse components, and each is determined by a separate application of the
original Sands (1995b) model. This also allows the use of separate extinction coefficients for direct and
diffuse radiation. This procedure will lead to a slight underestimation of canopy photosynthesis on
partially cloudy days, but this is likely to be within the bounds of errors introduced by the other
assumptions in whole-canopy models.
c) Inclusion of Frost Effects
In a further development, King and Ball (1998) showed how the deleterious effects of frost could be
based on daily minimum temperatures and included in the Sands (1995b) model. They took into
account the seasonal variation of frost sensitivity due to hardening and de-hardening in response to the
132
Chapter 5

--- Page 147 ---

past history of night-time temperatures, and distinguished between the accumulation of short-term
effects of frost on Amax and a, which are offset by rapid recovery, and long-term chronic effects
accumulated over successive days of frost, which are offset by slow recovery.
King and Ball used the model to study the effects of frost on seasonal daily photosynthesis of
Eucalytpus pauciflora. The modelled effects of frost were highly non-linear and sensitive to the temporal
sequence of night-time temperatures, with considerable interaction with the degree of hardening.
Substantial declines in seasonal photosynthesis were predicted for locations where minimum tempera-
tures fall below 10 to 12 C, or where unseasonal frosts of lesser severity occur when the plants are
not hardened. Their results were consistent with observations that increases in frost severity following
land clearing were impeding the regeneration of eucalypts in frost prone areas.
The King and Ball (1998) approach can be applied in other models of photosynthesis; a similar
approach can be found in the CenW model of Kirschbaum (1999).
5.3 LIGHT-USE EFFICIENCY AND CANOPY PHOTOSYNTHETIC PRODUCTION
The instantaneous single-leaf photosynthetic rate is a highly non-linear function of incident irradi-
ance. However, as noted above, this non-linearity becomes less evident as photosynthesis is accumulated
over greater spatial scales, e.g. single canopies or whole stands, and especially over longer time frames,
e.g. days to years. In fact, for the longest time frames, the response to intercepted radiation is virtually
linear. This leads to a simple empirical model for canopy photosynthetic production as the product of
the light-use efficiency and the light intercepted by the canopy. As this approach is often used, and is the
foundation of models such as 3-PG discussed in Chapter 9, we discuss it in detail.
There is a large body of data that shows that photosynthetic production by a canopy, or the growth
of a whole plant, or of a stand of trees, is proportional to the amount of radiation intercepted by the
canopy. This is Eq. (5.2), which we reproduce here:
DPn ¼ eDQint;
ð5:31Þ
where DPn (kg m2) is the net primary production over some time interval, DQint (J m2) is the
radiation intercepted by the canopy in that interval, and e (g J1) is the light-use efficiency.
The concept of a light-use efficiency dates back at least to the 1960s when Landsberg (1967) derived
radiation use efficiencies for lucerne grown under irrigation in Africa, and found that the values
obtained declined with increasing water stress. However, the linear relationship (5.31) was first noted
by Monteith (1977)–in the context of agricultural and horticultural crops in Britain–although he had
conducted an earlier analysis of the various components that determine the efficiency of conversion of
solar radiation into useful biomass (Monteith 1972). Jarvis and Leverenz (1983) showed Eq. (5.31)
could be applied to forests.
The concept of light-use efficiency has been the subject of various experimental and theoretical
studies that seek to determine its generality, its underlying physiological basis, and the dependence of
e on environmental factors and leaf-level physiological parameters. Various authors (see Section 5.3.1)
have provided empirical determinations of e for forests, or applied the light-use efficiency concept as
the basis of simple models of stand production, e.g. the 3-PG model (Landsberg and Waring 1997)
discussed in detail in Chapter 9.
Alton and Bodin (2010) compared the performance of a model based on the light-use efficiency
concept and a multilayer model operating at a time step of 30 min. Both models were optimised to an
The Carbon Balance of Trees and Stands
133

--- Page 148 ---

extensive data set of observed fluxes of carbon, latent and sensible heat, and radiation from 30 sites and
71 site-years. They concluded that the multilayer model performed better than the light-use efficiency
model, but the difference in performance declined as the timescale increases.

5.3.1. Observations of
An important point to note when comparing values of light-use efficiency e is that the basis of their
calculation may differ. Variously, e is based on intercepted photosynthetically active (PAR) or total
radiation, and on gross or net primary production, total biomass production, above-ground biomass
production, or simply stem growth. To further confuse matters, the mass units may be grams of carbon,
or grams of dry matter, treated as CH2O. Values of e can be standardised by converting grams of dry
matter to grams of carbon (gC) by multiplying by 0.4 (¼12/30, i.e. the ratio of molecular weights), and
using 0.5 for the ratio of PAR to total solar radiation.
Light-use efficiency can also be expressed as mol C per mol PAR, where the conversion relationship is
1 mol mol1¼55 gC MJ1, based on the molecular weight of carbon being 12 g and 1 MJof PAR contain-
ing 4.6 mol of photons. This quantity is analogous to the quantum efficiency a of a single leaf, and in the
context of the 3-PG model we refer to it as canopy quantum efficiency, denoted by aC (see Section 9.2.1).
Data from studies that have provided reliable estimates of light-use efficiency e for a number of
species are summarised in Table 5.1. These values should be compared with those found by Monteith
(1977) in his original study of well-tended C3 field crops in Britain, namely about 1.4 gC MJ1.
Various observational studies suggest how e varies with external factors. Runyon et al. (1994)
compared light-use efficiency across a climatic gradient dominated by coniferous evergreen forests in
western Oregon, USA, and found significant climatic effects. Stape et al. (2008) showed for plantations
of Eucalyptus grandisurophylla in Brazil that e is higher in wet years or under irrigation than in dry
years, and Harrington and Fownes (1995) showed for a number of fast growing tropical species in
Hawaii that e declines with increasing water stress.
Saldarriaga and Luxmoore (1991) estimated e for trees spanning ages up to 140 years in a tropical
rainforest in Amazonia. They found that for NPP, e declined from about 0.5 gC MJ1 to 0.25 by age
15 years, and was then essentially constant up to age 140 years, while for above-ground biomass e
declined from 0.35 gC MJ1 to 0.1 by age 15 years, was then constant up to age 50 years, after which it
declined to essentially zero.
Leuning et al. (1991) used the model MAESTRO (Wang and Jarvis 1990) with observed data on the
growth of Eucalyptus grandis trees up to 16 months to examine radiation interception and use in
relation to foliage nutrient status. They found that for daily NPP, e increased from 1.3 gC MJ1 for the
unfertilised treatment to 2.4 for the fertilised treatment, whereas for annual above-ground growth, e
was 0.6 gC MJ1 for both treatments. Hammer and Wright (1994) found in peanut that e increased
linearly from 0.9 gC MJ1 to 2.07 while the specific leaf nitrogen increased from 1.39 gN m2 leaf area
to 2.07.
Ahl et al. (2004) examined the variation of e (based on annual NPP per unit intercepted PAR)
between species and between growing seasons of five major forest types in Wisconsin, USA. They show
that e varied significantly between forest types: from 0.18 gDM MJ1 for upland conifer to 0.45 for
deciduous broadleaf forests. The variation in e between two consecutive growing seasons was consistent
across forest types, but much smaller than the variation between types. For these forests this variation
was 0.03 and 0.05 gDM MJ1, respectively. As a result, Ahl et al. caution against using a single value of e
when modelling NPP across broad areas, e.g. using remotely sensed LAI data, and emphasise the
importance of taking into account both inter-species and inter-season variations. We show next how
this may be done.
The Carbon Balance of Trees and Stands
135

--- Page 150 ---

5.3.2. Dependence of
on Physiological and Environmental Factors
The studies just discussed consider the effects of external factors such as soil water stress and
nutrition on e, factors which would undoubtedly have affected the values quoted in Table 5.1. We now
examine various theoretical analyses that suggest how e can be determined from physiological para-
meters, and quantify environmental effects on e.
The whole-canopy daily photosynthetic production model of Section 5.2.4a was used by Sands
(1996) to study the annual light-use efficiency of Eucalypts globulus at a cool-temperate site in
Tasmania, Australia. It predicted values of e from 1.55–1.95 gC MJ1, depending on the seasonal
photosynthetic temperature acclimation, and was used to explore how e varies with site conditions
and single-leaf photosynthetic characteristics. Light-use efficiency declined with annual mean daily
irradiance Qa over the range 12–18 MJ m2 day1 although annual NPP nevertheless increased
with increasing Qa, and e was only weakly affected by annual mean daily temperature over the range
8–14 C. Also, e declined with increasing canopy extinction coefficient k, had a strongly increasing
response to increasing light-saturated photosynthetic rate Ax, increased with increasing curvature y of
the light response, and showed a saturating response to increased single-leaf quantum efficiency a that
resulted in only a slight increase in e for a>0.04 mol mol1.
Dewar (1996) used a simple model of canopy photosynthesis in which the photosynthetic rate had a
rectangular hyperbolic response to both irradiance and leaf nitrogen content, whereas respiration
increased linearly with N content. He showed that a relationship of the form of Eq. (5.31) is obtained if
nitrogen content maximises Pn. The light-use efficiency e was then proportional to each of the leaf-level
quantum efficiency a, the conversion efficiency of sugars to structural dry matter, and a combination of
other parameters. Haxeltine and Prentice (1996) based their analysis on the Farquhar leaf-level
photosynthesis model, and the light utilisation efficiency relationship also emerged when nitrogen
distribution was optimised. McMurtrie et al. (1994) used the BIOMASS model (McMurtrie et al. 1990)
to simulate carbon gain by Pinus radiata in response to rising atmospheric CO2 concentration, and
found that Eq. (5.31) applied over an annual timescale, with e increasing with increasing Ca and with
foliage N:C ratio. These studies collectively show that predicted measures of e compare well with
observed values, and e has a saturating increase to increased atmospheric CO2 concentration, increases
with increasing day length, and declines with increasing temperature.
5.3.3. Growth Modifiers
The response of light-use efficiency to environmental factors such as nutritional status and climatic
conditions such as temperature, vapour pressure deficit and soil water status is generally non-linear.
As a first approximation, interactions between factors are ignored, and e is modelled as a maximum
value ex attained under optimal conditions multiplied by a series of modifiers that express how e varies
with each of a single environmental factor xi:
e ¼ f1ðx1Þf2ðx2Þ  fnðxnÞex:
ð5:32Þ
The individual fi are called growth modifiers and are, usually, constrained between 0 and 1.
An exception is a modifier for atmospheric CO2 concentration (Ca) as this is normalised to 1 for a
nominal historic value of Ca, e.g. 350 ppm.
The growth modifiers have been applied in Eq. (5.32) multiplicatively. However, other approaches
are possible, e.g. the use of their arithmetic, geometric or harmonic mean. A related approach is the law
of the minimum:
136
Chapter 5

--- Page 151 ---

e ¼ minff1ðx1Þ; f2ðx2Þ; . . . ; fnðxnÞgex:
ð5:33Þ
This law was developed in the context of agricultural chemistry by Carl Sprengel in the 1820s who
formulated the theory that plant growth is limited by the lowest amount of an essential element. It was
subsequently popularised by Justus von Liebig and now commonly bears his name. The law of the
minimum was applied by Hackett (1991) in PlantGro, a simple approach to matching species and sites,
including both silvicultural and horticultural applications.
We call the use of an equation like (5.32) the ‘‘growth modifier approach’’. It was popularised in early
studies of bioclimatic indices, e.g. in the Fitzpatrick and Nix (1970) study of the productivity of
Australian grasslands. In the original version of this book the use of Eq. (5.32) was suggested as a means
of incorporating limiting factors into a top down model of forest growth. This suggestion was followed
by, e.g., McMurtrie et al. (1994), Landsberg and Waring (1997) in the 3-PG model discussed in detail
in Chapter 9, and Ma¨kela¨ et al. (2008) in an empirical model for conifers in Europe.
The concept of growth modifiers is also used in a wider context in basic studies in plant physiology,
e.g. the Jarvis (1976) analysis of environmental effects on stomatal conductance (see also Section 3.2.5).
5.4 NON-HOMOGENEOUS CANOPIES
Our discussion of light interception and canopy processes has focussed largely on horizontally
homogeneous canopies. What about canopies that are not horizontally homogeneous? Examples
include: block, row or single-tree plantings; mixed-species or mixed-age plantings; and natural forests.
An important aspect of irregular or mixed-species plantings is the difference in competition between
the trees for resources. Competition is often viewed as a negative process, but it is competition that
determines the structure of mixed-species or mixed-age stands, and it is definitely a positive process
where a nurse crop is used to enhance the establishment of a target species through its effects on the
environment of the target species.
We use Tasmanian blackwood (Acacia melanoxylon) plantations as an example of the use of nurse
crops. Blackwood is highly valued for furniture making and is native to southeastern Australia.
Plantations have been established in several countries, especially Australia, New Zealand and South
Africa. Plantation-grown blackwood is slow growing, and stem form is often poor due to low apical
dominance. In its natural habitat, blackwood establishes beneath an overstorey, where suppression of
light by neighbouring vegetation encourages apical dominance and discourages branch establishment.
To mimic these processes, blackwood plantations are established using a nurse crop that grows more
rapidly than the blackwood. However, the nurse crop does suppress growth of the blackwood, and so is
usually thinned and ultimately removed. A useful summary of these issues is provided by Pinkard and
Beadle (2002). Proper management of blackwood plantations requires understanding of how the light
environment of the blackwood changes as the nurse crop grows, and can be manipulated.
This illustrates an important issue with non-homogenous plantings: how to take account of
competition for light between different trees. In general, none of the models in Section 5.2.3 can
take horizontal inhomogeneity into account in a simple way. Both inter- and intra-specific competition
are important, and these models only account for intra-specific competition.
Various authors emphasise that to take account of both inter- and intra-specific competition it is
necessary to shift focus from the stand to a tree. For example, Porte´ and Bartelink (2002) provide an
extensive review of both empirical and process-based modelling of growth in mixed forests. They
The Carbon Balance of Trees and Stands
137

--- Page 152 ---

emphasise the need to distinguish between models for yield prediction and those for successional forest
dynamics, and suggest that single-tree based models offer the best opportunity to support decision
making in management of mixed forests.
An array model uses a single-tree growth model to predict the growth of each of many independently
specified trees. The geometry of an arbitrarily complex planting–the location and crown structure of each
tree–is specified, along with the distribution of photosynthetic and optical properties of the foliage.
Simulation of radiation interception and photosynthesis then takes into account the effects of shading
by the individual trees in a diverse canopy. Examples of array models are MAESTRA (Section 5.1.3a), and
the models COMMIX (Bartelink 2000) and MIXLIGHT (Stadt and Lieffers 2000). However, use of an
array model is a very numerically intensive approach and it is appropriate to consider alternatives.
5.4.1. Mixed-Species Stands
Consider first the simple case where the canopy is in fact layered, e.g. a nurse crop dominates the
target crop, and in essence there is an overstorey above a distinct understorey. In this case it suffices to
use Beer’s law and the LAI of the overstorey to determine the irradiance incident upon the upper level
of the understorey, and then apply Beer’s law to the understorey. This can be done separately for both
direct and diffuse radiation. The models of Section 5.2.3 can then be applied separately to both
components of the canopy.
Now consider the case where the two components intermingle. Rimmington (1984) and Sinoquet
and Bonhomme (1991) considered radiation interception in canopies with two species, taking into
account spatial relationships between the leaf elements, e.g. if foliage of the two species avoid mutual
shading, are randomly distributed, or clumped and tend to overlap. Sinoquet et al. (2000) review the
performance of several multi-species models, and include a generalisation of formulae in Rimmington,
and in Sinoquet and Bonhomme to canopies with many species. In particular, they show that the
fraction fi of radiation above the whole canopy i.e. intercepted by species i is given by
fi ¼
kiLi
P
jkjLj
1  exp 
X
jkjLj

h
i
ð5:34Þ
where the ki are the extinction coefficients and the Li the leaf area indices of the component species.
This is the generalisation of the Eq. (5.3) for a uniform, single-species canopy.
A simple model partitioning intercepted radiation and transpiration between two species in a mixed
canopy is ERIN (Wallace 1997). The light interception sub-model of ERIN estimates the radiation
intercepted by each component of a mixture from the canopy height, leaf area index and extinction
coefficient of each component, and the transpiration sub-model allows transpiration by each compo-
nent to be calculated. In the case of two competing species ERIN determines the fractions of irradiance
intercepted by each canopy, assuming in turn that one or the other is totally dominant, and then
weights these two fractions by a function of their relative heights. The equations, given below, follow
from a straightforward application of Beer’s law to each canopy.
Let fjd be the fraction of radiation intercepted by canopy j (j¼1 or 2) when it is totally dominant,
and fjs the fraction intercepted by canopy j when it is totally shaded. Then

--- Page 154 ---

spatially heterogeneous canopies, based on a knowledge of the canopy cover and the clumping of the
foliage of each species within the canopy.
Wallace showed that for a homogeneous two-species mixture ERIN produced very similar results to
those obtained from a complex, multilayer radiation-balance model. However, Sinoquet et al. (2000)
compared models for daily light partitioning in multi-species canopies, and found that ERIN gives
biased estimates of light partitioning when the difference in LAI between the two species is high, or
when scattering within the canopies is high. They then presented a more detailed model that avoids
these problems.
Specific mixed-species systems of ecological interest are savannah grasslands with widely spaced
trees, and agroforestry systems where trees and crops are planted together. Both are characterised by
dominant, possibly widely spaced trees, with a low understorey. Examples from a considerable body
of literature on studies of these systems are the use of TREEGRASS to analyse a savannah by Simioni
et al. (2000, 2003), an analysis of competition for light between hedgerows and maize in an alley
cropping system (Friday and Fownes 2002), a model for a coffee agroforestry system (van Oijen et al.
2010), and an overview of agroforestry as a provider of ecosystem services and environmental benefits
(Jose 2009).
5.4.2. Edge Effects for Block or Strip Plantings
Another case that needs special attention is where some of the trees in an otherwise homogeneous
stand are differently exposed to the environment. Examples are block or strip plantings where the trees
on the edge of the planting have reduced shading and greater access to soil water. Again, array models
could be applied, but we suggest for many purposes some simple rules of thumb may suffice. The trees
are divided into two classes: those interior to the plating and those on the edge. Growth of the interior
trees can be modelled as if they were an extensive, homogeneous planting. It is only the edge trees that
require special treatment. These edge effects are enhanced light interception, and greater access to soil
water, and these typically manifest as larger trees, possibly with fuller canopies and more branch
biomass.
The simplest way to treat the effect of enhanced light interception is to assume that a typical edge
tree intercepts fe times the radiation intercepted by a typical interior tree (fe>1), where fe depends on
the planting geometry. An array model such as MAESTRA could be used to determine the ratio fe for a
range of planting geometries. This would then be related to the within and between row spacing. If the
total number of trees in a block or strip planting is NT, and the number of edge trees is Ne, then the total
irradiance intercepted by the planting may be approximated by
IT ¼
1 þ ðfe  1Þ Ne
NT


Ihom
ð5:38Þ
where Ihom is the irradiance intercepted assuming the block were part of a homogeneous planting,
i.e. ignoring edge effects. If the light-use efficiency of edge trees is the same as that of interior trees, GPP
will be enhanced by the same ratio.
It is easy to estimate from Eq. (5.38) the uncertainty in production that arises from uncertainty in
the edge effect: an error Df in fe translates into an error (Ne/NT)Df in the ratio IT/Ihom. Suppose light
interception by edge trees is 50% higher than interior trees, i.e. fe¼1.5. Then for a square planting 10
trees on a side NT¼100, Ne¼36 and IT/Ihom¼1.18, i.e. the edge effect for the whole planting is an 18%
increase in light interception, and an error Df in fe translates into an error 0.31Df in IT/Ihom. Long strip
plantings 10 rows wide have an overall edge effect of 10% increase in light interception, with an
140
Chapter 5

--- Page 155 ---

uncertainty 0.18Df in IT/Ihom. It is clear that, for all but very small block or narrow strip plantings, the
error in the overall edge effect is much less than the error in the single-tree edge effect.
5.5 STAND RESPIRATION
New growth is derived from the assimilates left after respiration has been taken into account. This is
expressed by Eq. (5.1), and here we consider some of the issues needed to understand and model
respiration. An early view of respiration, expressed by McCree (1970) in the context of a crop (clover),
considered it as two separate components: one associated with growth, and the other with maintenance
of tissue. This approach is still used in practical forest growth models; we expand on it in Section 5.5.1.
However, recent studies have emphasised that respiration provides the energy to drive a wide range
of processes underpinning plant growth (Johnson 1990; Amthor 2000; Cannell and Thornley 2000;
Dewar 2000): photosynthesis, growth (synthesis of structures), tissue maintenance (production or
repair of enzymes), nutrient uptake, phloem loading and translocation (of water and nutrients). The
authors of these studies also argued that adequate modelling of respiration should take account of both
structural and substrate concentrations of carbon, nitrogen and possibly other nutrients, in various
plant tissues.
Cannell and Thornley (2000) outlined an approach to modelling plant respiration that explicitly
distinguishes these various aspects of respiration and their dependence on substrate concentrations.
Working with the Edinburgh Forest Model, Thornley and Cannell (2000) concluded that respiration
associated with mineral nutrition was small, phloem loading required 10–12% of total respiratory
energy, growth 36–42%, and maintenance required 46–48%. They also found that after the canopy was
established, total tree respiration was approximately 41% of gross canopy photosynthesis.
5.5.1. Growth and Maintenance Respiration
McCree (1970) expressed total respiration R of plant tissue as the sum of growth respiration RG,
being the energy used to synthesize new tissue, and maintenance respiration RM, being the energy
consumed in the turnover and synthesis of proteins. He expressed this as
R ¼ RG þ RM ¼ rG
dW
dt þ rMW;
ð5:39Þ
where W is the tissue biomass. The parameters rG and rM respectively characterise growth and
maintenance respiration, and have been determined for a number of herbaceous plants and tissues.
In general, rG is temperature independent while rM is temperature dependent.
Thornley and Cannell (2000) consider different demands and priorities for the carbohydrates from
the assimilate pool used for respiration. Maintenance respiration has priority over growth, and the
maintenance requirements of the entire plant are met before any assimilates are allocated to growth.
This is consistent with the now-classical scheme proposed by McCree (1970). After overall mainte-
nance requirements are met all new assimilates are allocated to growth and the maintenance require-
ment of each organ is met from a substrate pool local to that organ. Last in the chain of priorities, some
biomass is degraded and the carbon produced is added to the assimilate pool.
The Carbon Balance of Trees and Stands
141

--- Page 156 ---

5.5.2. Observations of Respiration
Growth respiration can be determined by using the known biochemical pathways to estimate the
amount of CO2 produced in the synthesis of the components of plant material (Penning de Vries et al.
1974). Penning de Vries (1972) calculated that 1.384 g of glucose is required to synthesise 1 g of plant
material, taking into account energy for nutrient uptake, with the production of 0.453 g of CO2. Thus,
rG¼0.45. This value is typical for whole plants, does not depend on temperature and is not expected to
change with changing atmospheric CO2 concentration. Given rG it is possible to determine growth
respiration from an observed increase in biomass.
In herbaceous plants, the maintenance component–and hence the value of rM–is usually determined
by monitoring CO2 efflux in the dark until respiration rates stabilize. This is best done at the start of a
dormant period as the contribution of growth to total respiration is then small. Alternatively, growth
respiration must be estimated and subtracted from the observed efflux. Gifford (2003) includes a
discussion of the pros and cons of various techniques for measuring respiration.
Maintenance respiration of living tissue is more strongly correlated with its nitrogen concentration
than with its biomass or surface area. This is because a high proportion of living plant material is in
proteins, and approximately 60% of maintenance respiration is concerned with enzyme maintenance
and replacement (Penning de Vries 1975). Ryan (1995) showed that CO2 efflux per unit foliage weight
was proportional to foliar nitrogen concentration in boreal and sub-alpine species. The slopes of the

142
Chapter 5

--- Page 157 ---

relationships were statistically similar at the two sites, but higher than those found for other species at
warm sites. In an analysis of an extensive database of respiration measurements from 287 species,
Reich et al. (2008) examined respiration rate versus nitrogen content for trees and herbs, and
contrasted leaf, stem and root respiration. Strong log–log relationships were found between mass-
based respiration rate and nitrogen concentration. The between-organ differences in these relationships
were greater than the between-species differences, and at a common nitrogen concentration, respiration
rates were consistently lower in leaves than in stems or roots. Accordingly, organ-specific relationships
should be used in models that simulate respiration based on tissue nitrogen concentrations.
Maintenance respiration differs between different tissues depending on their function. For example
it is highest in very active tissues such as foliage, sapwood and fine roots, and higher in sapwood than in
heart wood. This is because of their higher nitrogen concentration. Because of the largely peripheral
distribution of living tissue in trees (sapwood) with its high respiration rate compared to that of
internal non-living tissue (heartwood), the respiration rate per unit mass of woody tissue tends to
decline in inverse proportion to the radius of stems, branches and large roots. It is usual to express the
respiration rate of woody tissue in terms of its nitrogen content, surface area or sapwood volume.
Estimates of surface area or sapwood volume are made using relationships established between surface
area and stem or branch diameter and length, or between sapwood cross sectional area of a branch and
leaf area on that branch.
There have been numerous studies of tree respiration, and in Table 5.2 we summarise observed
respiration rates and Q10 values for tissues of various species. The units used to express respiration rates
varied from study to study and for some it was necessary to convert stem-area or sapwood-volume
based rates to sapwood-C content based rates, i.e. nmol mol1 s1. This required the density and
carbon content of the tissue; where these were unknown, a density of 450 kg dry matter m3 or 800 kg
fresh weight m3, and a carbon content of 50% was assumed. Further, the quoted rates have been
converted to a common reference temperature of 15 C using published values of Q10, or Q10¼2 if this
was not given.
In a comprehensive series of studies, Mike Ryan analysed respiration of different tissues (foliage,
stem, coarse and fine roots) of a range of pines, boreal and wet-forest tropical trees (Ryan 1990; Ryan
et al. 1994a,b, 1995, 1996, 1997b). Several of these studies also estimated annual respiration and its
effect on productivity (see Section 5.5.3). Lavigne and Ryan (1997) compared growth and maintenance
respiration for various boreal species across a latitudinal gradient. Edwards and Hanson (1996)
examined seasonal and annual stem respiration in oak and maple. Cernusak et al. (2006) examined
stem respiration of different eucalypt species in a northern Australian tropical savannah and its
response to fire.
Results from these studies are included in Table 5.2. Some general features are: foliage and fine roots
had similar respiration rates, which were higher for seedlings than for mature trees, while stem and
coarse root respiration rates were low, with branches and twigs intermediate. The values of rM for two
wet-tropical forest species were similar, and roughly twice those of temperate conifers. Respiration of
foliage and fine roots in P. radiata subject to different irrigation and fertilisation regimes was strongly
correlated with tissue nitrogen content, and significantly higher in fine roots than in foliage. Also,
foliage, fine-root and stem respiration were not affected by treatment. Fine-root Q10 is lower than stem
or foliage Q10, and there is evidence that in pines and spruce Q10 of foliage can be much higher in
autumn (2.3–3.3) than in summer (2.2). Refixation of respired CO2 is quite common in smooth-
barked species, especially eucalypts, and improves the overall carbon efficiency of a tree. It probably
reduces the carbon cost of transporting water up a stem because it reduces the carbon cost of
constructing or maintaining sapwood.
The Carbon Balance of Trees and Stands
143

--- Page 158 ---

Linder and Troeng (1981) made continuous measurements of stem and coarse root respiration of a
20-year-old Scots pine in Sweden from January to November. These data, together with data on
seasonal carbon balance from Linder and Axelsson (1982) and Benecke and Nordmeyer (1982),
indicate that total respiration losses from branches, stems and roots range from about 25% to 50%
of assimilated carbon.
Ryan et al. (1997b) provide a very useful discussion of the time course and seasonal variation of the
various components of autotrophic respiration. Up to half of this is generally attributed to fine-root
turnover, a factor i.e. clearly of major importance in the carbon balance of trees. When roots die their
carbon is lost to the tree, and ultimately respired by soil micro-organisms. There are two processes
involving respiration in fine-root turnover: growth of new roots requires carbon to form new biomass
and for maintenance, whereas existing roots only require carbon for maintenance respiration. Litton
et al. (2007) found, from a survey of reports of experiments that provided information about above-
and below-ground carbon fluxes, that total below-ground fluxes were tightly correlated with above-
ground net primary production.
We note here, however, new insights into soil respiration and the allocation of carbon to fine roots,
provided by the path-finding work of Ho¨gberg et al. (2001, 2008). An experiment (Ho¨gberg et al. 2008)
in which P. sylvestris trees were girdled by stripping the stem bark from the current xylem to halt the
flow of photosynthate to roots and mycorhizal fungi showed that girdling reduced soil respiration,
monitored with surface chambers, by more than 50% relative to controls. The results indicated clearly
that the flux of current assimilates to roots is a key driver of soil respiration. The implication of the
work is that soil respiratory losses are closely coupled to the flux of photosynthates from the canopy, i.e.
the seasonal pattern of below-ground CO2-allocation appears to be more important than soil temper-
ature in determining root respiration. Johnsen et al. (2007) were able to duplicate the effects of girdling
by the non-destructive procedure of phloem chilling. They included girdling treatments in their work,
which was on plots of Pinus taeda in the SETRES fertiliserirrigation experiment. Johnsen et al.
monitored soil respiration with open-system chambers and also measured photosynthesis. They
focussed more on comparison of techniques than on the relationship between below-ground allocation
and photosynthesis, but showed that chilling reduced the downward flux of CO2 by 8% in fertilised
plots and 26% in unfertilised, confirming the general view that below-ground allocation is greater in
low than in high fertility soil.
Models of the carbon balance of stands or of regions must accurately represent the balance between
photosynthesis and respiration (see Running 2008 for comment in relation to large-scale simulation).
Ho¨gberg et al. (2008) pulse-labelled Pinus sylvestris with 13CO2 and confirmed the close temporal
coupling between canopy activity and soil activity in forests. They commented that the widely held
view that most of the carbon allocated below-ground supports the growth of fine roots, assumed to
turn over several times per year, is not correct. Much of the below-ground C-flux is made available to
mycorhizal fungi and other soil biota closely associated with roots. This work has considerable
implications for stand modelling and carbon allocation; developments in these areas will be dependent
on studies on other species and locations.
5.5.3. Carbon-Use Efficiency
To model tree respiration using the methods proposed by Cannell and Thornley (2000), Dewar
(2000), and others, we must include substrate pools for both carbon and nitrogen. This leads to a level
144
Chapter 5

--- Page 159 ---

of complexity not usually supported by the kind of observations available. An alternative approach is to
use the concept of carbon-use efficiency Y to take respiration into account, where Y is the ratio of net
primary production Pn (the assimilate incorporated into plant biomass) to gross primary production
Pg. Thus,
dW
dt ¼ Pn ¼ YPg:
ð5:40Þ
As discussed below, various authors have reported that Y is similar for the same species at diverse
sites. This observation has been used in several forest growth models, and especially in 3-PG (Lands-
berg and Waring 1997; Chapter 9) as a means to convert GPP to NPP.
Ryan et al. (1997b) presented detailed data on autotrophic respiration for a number of forest species
in a boreal ecosystem and found it accounted for 54–71% of annual NPP, and 64–77% of annual GPP.
They also showed carbon-use efficiency was very similar for stands of the same species growing in
different study areas, but clearly differed between species: Y¼0.44 for aspen, 0.34 for black spruce, and
0.39 for old jack pine.
Waring et al. (1998) found Y¼0.470.04 from an analysis of annual carbon budgets at 12 sites
comprising evergreen and deciduous forests, and pine plantations in the USA, Australia and
New Zealand, in which Pn ranged from 120 to 1660 gC m2 yr1. However, Medlyn and Dewar
(1999) cautioned that the method Waring et al. (1998) used to estimate whole-plant respiration,
specifically the assumption that above-ground respiration is proportional to above-ground growth, has
a built-in bias towards a constant value of Y close to 0.5.
Litton et al. (2007) carried out a meta-analysis of existing literature and compiled annual carbon
budgets for forest ecosystems to test a series of hypotheses, among which was the proposition that
‘‘Partitioning to respiration is constant across a wide range of GPP in forest ecosystems and does not
vary with resource availability, competition, or stand age’’. They concluded that ‘‘partitioning to total
respiration is conservative across a wide range of GPP and does not change with resources, stand age or
competition within an individual site. However, sites do differ for unknown reasons’’. Litton et al.
found that 57% of carbon fixed (GPP) was allocated to respiration. Hence Y0.43, somewhat smaller
than the value found by Waring et al. (1998). They also found that Y varied between biomass pools.
The claim that Y is constant during forest growth has attracted considerable discussion in the
literature, both theoretical and based on observation, and we summarise this below. First, since the net
primary production of a plant is used to grow new tissue, growth respiration RG¼rGPn. It then follows
from Eqs. (5.1) and (5.39) that
Pn ¼
1
1 þ rG
ðPg  RMÞ;
ð5:41Þ
whence
Y ¼ Pn
Pg
¼
1
1 þ rG
1  RM
Pg


:
ð5:42Þ
Thus, carbon-use efficiency declines with increasing maintenance respiration as a fraction of GPP.
Following canopy closure, Pg is usually fairly stable because LAI tends to be stable, while the biomass of
the stand increases, hence so does its nitrogen content and maintenance respiration. As it is the mass of
active tissue, e.g. sapwood, that contributes most to maintenance respiration, Eq. (5.42) suggests that
carbon-use efficiency should decline as trees grow taller, or age.
The Carbon Balance of Trees and Stands
145

--- Page 160 ---

The question ‘‘Is respiration a constant fraction of gross primary production?’’ was specifically
addressed by DeLucia et al. (2007) through an extensive literature survey yielding 60 data points from
26 papers, covering a wide range of forest types and treatments, aged from 5 to 500 years. This survey
confirmed the substantial variation in carbon-use efficiency values reported in the literature. For all
forests examined, the slope of a plot of NPP against GPP was 0.53, with individual estimates of Y
varying from 0.23 to 0.83. If the data were grouped by forest type, scatter was substantially reduced. For
example for boreal forests Y ranged from 0.25 to 0.35, and for temperate deciduous forests, Y ranged
from 0.45 to 0.75. A substantial proportion of the variation of Y between forests was due to a consistent
decrease of Y with age, and Y increased with increasing ratio of leaf mass to total mass. However, the
way in which respiration had been determined varied across the studies, and may have contributed to
some of the variation.
Dewar et al. (1998) used a fairly detailed modelling analysis to examine, amongst other things, the
issue of approximate constancy of carbon-use efficiency. They argued that fast-turnover plant pools
(turnover-times days to weeks) tend towards an approximate steady state i.e. proportional to the time-
averaged canopy absorbed PAR, and this behaviour offers a mechanistic explanation for the observed
approximate constancy of both light- and carbon-use efficiency.
Evidence was presented by Ma¨kela¨ and Valentine (2001) that maintenance respiration of an
increasing amount of sapwood is a contributing factor to a downward trend in NPP following canopy
closure, and that GPP declines with increasing tree height. They argued on more rigorous theoretical
grounds than our Eq. (5.42) that Y declines with increasing height or age.
A recent theoretical analysis of carbon-use efficiency in self-thinning fir forests was based on power-
law relationships between tree respiration and mass, and GPP and mass, and the self-thinning
relationship between stand biomass and stem number (Ogawa, 2009). This study concluded that
Y0.5 in young forests but declines with age to Y0.3 at age 60 years, and increases with increasing
leaf mass to total mass ratio–as reported by DeLucia et al. (2007). (However, age and leaf-mass ratio
were highly correlated.)
5.6 ALLOCATION OF BIOMASS
We discussed in Section 3.5 some of the concepts and mechanisms concerned with carbon
allocation in trees, and concluded that our knowledge of the underlying physiological mechanisms is
not yet good enough to allow their use in practical forest growth models. Source–sink models using a
transport-resistance approach (Thornley 1991; and reviewed in Section 3.5.2) appear to hold out the
most promise, but until they have been more extensively tested against forest growth data, and made
more ‘‘user friendly’’, they are unlikely to be widely adopted.
We now show how the observed allometric relationships between the biomass components of trees
(Section 4.2.3) can be used to constrain biomass allocation in process-based growth models. To do this
we make use of a generic tree-growth model that provides the basic framework of all tree-growth
models, and then impose the observed allometric relationships in the form of Eq. (4.22).
5.6.1. A Generic Tree-Growth Model
In the generic tree-growth model, photosynthesis produces new biomass at a net rate Pn. This is
then allocated to various biomass pools, and here we consider the foliage, stem (including branch and
bark), and coarse- and fine-root biomass pools, denoted by wF, wS, wcR and wfR, respectively. The
equations for the rates of change of the biomass pools are simple:
146
Chapter 5

--- Page 161 ---

dwF
dt ¼ FPn  gFwF
dwS

dwcR

where t is time, the allocation ratio i is the fraction of net photosynthesis Pn i.e. allocated to the

F þ S þ cR þ fR ¼ 1:

Equations (5.43) have been written for a single tree. If they are applied to a stand, then we would
replace the w with W, and the loss rates would also take tree mortality into account (see Section 9.2.1).
The generic model was originally written in this form by McMurtrie and Wolf (1983), and has since
formed the basis of numerous models, each of which adds more detail by specifying how Pn, and the i
and gi are determined. We review some of these models in Chapter 8, and illustrate it in the case of
3-PG in Section 9.2.
5.6.2. Taking Allometry into Account
Early forest growth models treated the allocation ratios i as empirical constants. However,
although they are known to vary with age and with growth conditions, we can determine constraints
on the i using the allometric relationships between biomass components discussed in Section 4.3. We
write these relationships as

5.6.3. Determination of Allocation Ratios
In the original version of 3-PG, Landsberg and Waring (1997) combined the coarse and fine-roots
biomass pools, and based allocation R to the root pool on environmental and site conditions such as
available soil water and nutrient status. This, together with the identity FþSþR¼1 and Eq. (5.49)
for the foliage to shoot allocation ratio pFS (but with the factor involving N omitted), gave three
equations that uniquely determined the three allocation ratios in terms of DBH and site and environ-
mental factors. Numerous applications of this simple scheme have demonstrated its ability to repro-
duce observed time-series of the three biomass pools in response to seasonal or longer variations in
climatic and site factors.
This analysis can be extended to include allocation to coarse and fine roots, and to take into account
the dependence of allometry on stem number (Almeida and Sands, personal communication). First,
since the generic model (5.45) predicts the biomass pools, stem diameter can be determined by
inverting the allometric relationship (5.45) for the single-tree stem biomass wS:

Because of the functional balance between foliage and fine-root function (Section 3.5.1a), we impose as
the fourth constraint the requirement that the ratio pRF¼fR/F of fine-root and foliage allocation is
determined by soil water and nutrient availability. These four constraints provide equations for the
four i. Solving these gives the four allocation ratios in terms of site and environmental factors, and

Equations (5.52) were used to generate the data for Figure 5.3 to illustrate the variation in allocation
as a tree grows. We used the allometric parameters given in Table 4.2 for fast-growing Eucalyptus
grandis in Brazil. If the allocation ratios i are constant, the generic model, i.e. Eq. (5.43), predicts that
the foliage mass wF and canopy LAI would attain steady state values where growth of new foliage
balances litterfall. However, in the example used for Figure 5.3, F declines as the tree grows–because
the allometric power nSB for stem biomass exceeds the power nFB for foliage, and hence nFSB<0 and pFS
declines as the tree grows. Accordingly, following canopy closure, the LAI of these stands declines.
