Plants require a number of mineral nutrients for their growth. Inadequate supplies of any nutrient
will impose limits on the capacity of trees to utilize efficiently the radiant energy captured by their
foliage, or to convert photosynthates into new plant biomass. Nitrogen is ultimately derived from
atmospheric nitrogen, but all other nutrients have their origin in the parent minerals forming soil.
There are cases where foliar absorption occurs, and under some circumstances this can be a significant
contributor to the uptake of a particular nutrient by a plant. We do not deal with foliar uptake.
The so-called macro-nutrients – nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), sulphur (S), calcium (Ca), magnesium
(Mg) and potassium (K) – are required in relatively large quantities. The micro-nutrients, or trace
elements, are boron (B), chlorine (Cl), cobalt (Co), copper (Cu), iron (Fe), manganese (Mn), molybde-
num (Mo), nickel (Ni) and zinc (Zn). The latest element to be added to this list is nickel (Ni), which is
part of the enzyme urease. This is the only known function of Ni, and plants with a Ni deficiency
accumulate urea (Torgny Na¨sholm, personal communication). The typical concentrations of different
Physiological Ecology of Forest Production
Joe Landsberg and Peter Sands
ISSN 1936-7961, DOI: 10.1016/S1936-7961(11)04006-1
Copyright # 2011 Elsevier Inc.
All rights reserved.

--- Page 165 ---

nutrients in plants are summarized in Table 6.1. In trees these may differ markedly between active tissue
such as leaves, and structural tissue such as heartwood.
Among the macro-nutrients, nitrogen is used to build chlorophyll molecules and enzymes,
phosphorus to construct energy transfer compounds and potassium to regulate the charge balance
across plant membranes. Potassium is also thought to contribute significantly to the total ion
concentration in the cell, thereby adding to the (negative) osmotic potential and thus tending to
increase turgor. Most micro-nutrients are used to construct coenzymes in plants (Landsberg and
Gower 1997). We will not be attempting, in this book, to deal with all of these, or indeed with the
chemistry of any of them. The treatment we present here focuses on N and P, and we are generally
concerned more with the principles of nutrient uptake and utilization than with the complexities of soil
chemistry.
There is one overriding principle that applies to plant nutrition: all living tissues must contain some
minimum concentration of mineral nutrients to grow or at least to maintain themselves in a steady
(respiring) state. It is therefore axiomatic that the rate of supply of nutrients from the soil to the plants,
and the rate of uptake of those nutrients by the plants, must be high enough to satisfy the needs
generated by photosynthesis and carbon fixation. Unfortunately, the nutrient concentrations required,
and tolerated, by plant tissues vary considerably. So this principle, while useful, does not solve the
problem of calculating plant nutrient requirements; we can estimate the amounts of nutrients required
for a given amount of biomass, but the estimates will tend to be approximate. Nevertheless, they can be
used to assess probable constraints on growth, as has been done by Medlyn et al. (2000) and McMurtrie
et al. (2001). Both these groups demonstrated that soil processes – notably the mineralization of N (see
below) are likely to limit the responses of terrestrial ecosystems to rising global temperatures and
atmospheric CO2 concentrations (see also Johnson 2006).

152
Chapter 6

--- Page 166 ---

The nutrientreserves of aforestareinthefoliage,barkandbranches,sapwood andheartwoodof thetrees,
in the soil and in the understorey vegetation. A small fraction of these nutrients is in flux between the various
compartments of the system. An understanding of the dynamics of nutrients, or of nutrient cycling, is an
essential prerequisite for understanding and predicting the effects of nutrition on forest growth. Many
nutrients behave in quite contrasting ways in soils. Appreciation of this has led to a considerable amount of
research, in recent years, on nutrient contents, forest biomass and nutrient dynamics.
As in any complex biological situation, it is possible to consider the system at a number of levels.
Much nutrient cycling work is based on studies of litterfall and decomposition, where collection and
analysis of litter is at intervals of weeks or months (e.g. Miller et al. 1976a,b), or on biomass samples at
intervals of years (Forrest 1969, Attiwill 1979). However, these intervals do not reflect the turnover
times of nutrients in all components of the system.
Nutrient movement from soil to leaves takes place continuously, but the analytical techniques
commonly used cannot identify the short-term changes. In the laboratory, it is possible to measure
flows of nutrients into and out of root cells, but in the field translocation between organs is continuous
and mass changes can only be determined with any accuracy over intervals of days or weeks. For
example, Fife and Nambiar (1982) followed changes in nitrogen and phosphorus concentrations in
Pinus radiata needles over periods of weeks. They found that in spring, the initial accumulation rate of
nitrogen per unit tissue mass was about seven times that of phosphorus, but the rate of withdrawal of
nitrogen in summer was only twice that of phosphorus (0.011 mg mg1 day1 for P and 0.021 for N).
However, on a molar basis, which will be a better reflection of chemical activity, the ratio was about
three in spring and unity in summer (0.35 mmol mg1 day1 for P and 0.29 for N).
Leaves remain on trees for periods ranging from one season, in deciduous trees, to 10 or more years
in conifers. Litter on the ground may decompose and release most of the nutrients it contains within a
few months in a tropical forest, or it may require years in cold regions. Except in very young soils, the
weathering of soil minerals and the release of nutrients into the soil solution is generally an extremely
slow process, requiring many years to add a significant quantity to the available pool. The uptake of
nutrients, as ions from the soil solution by plant roots, is a continuous process. The rate at which
nutrients are required by trees depends on the growth rates, as determined by radiation interception
and other factors considered in Chapter 5. However, the rate at which nutrients are actually absorbed
by trees depends on the accessibility of nutrients to the root systems, and their chemical availability.
This does not imply that short-term growth rates are directly proportional to nutrient uptake rates; as
we have already noted, nutrients within trees may be quite rapidly re-mobilized and translocated
between tissues. Also, and particularly in the case of nitrogen, storage and re-mobilization are
important. Nevertheless, in the longer term, the amount of dry matter produced must be proportional
to the mass of nutrient absorbed.
Under steady state conditions, the rate of nutrient uptake by plants in stable ecosystems will balance
inputs from weathering of minerals and decomposition (oxidation) of organic matter. Steady state
conditions are unlikely to occur over short periods (up to seasons). In particular, total nutrient gains
and losses from forests intensively managed for wood production are unlikely to be equal. Management
of these systems must therefore have, as one of its main aims, achievement of a balance between
nutrient uptake and the processes that maintain nutrient availability. When these are out of balance,
both the chemical and mass equilibria of nutrients in the soil become disturbed, with possibly serious
consequences, such as aluminium toxicity and nutrient depletion.
There is an interesting anomaly in relation to plant – and particularly tree – nutrition and our ability
to predict the nutrient requirements of trees and the extent to which soil nutrient status influences, or
indeed controls, tree growth rates. The anomaly lies in the fact that there have probably been more
experiments aimed at identifying the nutritional requirements of plantation forests than there have
Nutrient Dynamics and Tree Growth
153

--- Page 167 ---

been on any other aspect of forest management (with the possible exception of spacing and thinning
trials). Despite this, our ability to predict the nutrient requirements of trees, or the effects of measured
amounts of nutrients in soils on tree growth, remains very poor. There has always been a major
problem in predicting the nutrient requirements of crops, including plantations, or the growth rates of
natural forests, because growth is not necessarily correlated with the usual measures of nutrient
availability in soils, as indicated by the various methods used to determine them.
Our inability to predict quantitatively the effects of nutrient losses during management, or responses to
fertilizer, results from boththe complexityof the soil–plant system and the approach thathas been adopted
in much of the research in this area. An enormous amount of work has been done onvarious aspects of soil
chemistry, aimed at characterizing soils in terms of their ability to supply nutrients. Much of this work has
been associated with studies on plant response, but these have usually been of the correlative or ‘relational’
type, i.e. total growth observed at the end of a period is related to soil properties, or to the quantity of
nutrients supplied to the soil or nutrient deficiencies are evaluated in relation to empirical plant response
curves  again in terms of total growth. This type of empirical experiment does not lead to results of
general application and, in the case of large, long-lived plants like trees, it is difficult to do. There have also
been many attempts to define critical foliage nutrient levels and to establish relationships between final
yield, or growth increment over a period, and foliage nutrient concentration at a particular time. These
lead to highly variable results, because tissue nutrient status at any time affects the growth rate at that time,
but nutrient status may vary significantly over the periods for which growth is measured.
Smethurst (2000) notes that a major limitation of current soil analyses is the need for extensive field
calibration by replication of fertilizer experiments through space and time. He also notes that
‘‘concentrations of P in various types of soil extracts have been regressed against relative yield, but
these calibrations have a high degree of specificity to type of extractant, the soil type, climate, genotype
of the crop species, and several other factors’’. Some of the difficulties associated with the use of soil
analyses to predict tree nutrient requirements are illustrated by Mendham et al. (2002), who made a
detailed study of the responses to P fertilization of young eucalypt plantations in 24 field experiments,
in relation to a range of soil P analyses that characterized various pools of soil ‘available’ P. Correlations
with the various measures of P were generally low, despite the fact that responses were assessed at a
stage (1-year-old) when the trees show the largest responses to P fertilization. Clearly, work in these
areas will continue, but we also need to consider different approaches.
In this chapter, we discuss some of the recent work on nutrient uptake and modelling, and try to
assess the extent to which the anomaly noted in an earlier paragraph has been, or is likely to be,
resolved. The next section provides a general discussion of nutrient cycling; the remainder of the
chapter deals in more detail with various components of the nutrient cycle and then with tree growth in
relation to nutrition.
6.1 NUTRIENT CYCLING
A simplified outline of nutrient cycling of a forest is presented in Figure 6.1. Switzer and Nelson
(1972) proposed that the circulation of nutrients in forests be defined in terms of three cycles:

geochemical cycles encompass the gains and losses of nutrients to the ecosystem by processes such
as weathering and leaching;

biogeochemical cycles encompass soil–plant relationships, including nutrient gains to the soil by
symbiotic fixation, organic matter decomposition and losses by plant uptake;
154
Chapter 6

--- Page 168 ---


‘biochemical’ cycles encompass internal transfer relationships or translocation of nutrients within
the vegetation. (This term should not be confused with the biochemical cycling of organic
compounds within the plant, for which we use the term ‘re-translocation’.)
These cycles are labelled in Figure 6.1, and the processes involved in them are discussed in sequence
below. The geochemical and biogeochemical cycles will be considered in relation to the volume of soil
exploited by roots so that addition of nutrients to that volume, or their removal from it, is taken as gains
or losses, respectively. We deal with the geochemical and biogeochemical cycles in the next sub-sections.
Consideration of re-translocation is deferred to the section on forest nutritional requirements.
6.1.1. The Geochemical Cycle
The geochemical cycle comprises the gains and losses of nutrients to the ecosystem by processes such as
weathering and leaching.Geochemicalprocesses are usually slow relative to thegrowth of trees.Nutrients are
added to the soil by the weathering of parent materials, and in rainfall. Charley (1981) collated data on
nutrient inputs in precipitation from a number of Australian sources. Mean value for K was
3.7 kg ha1 yr1, and in the same units, 6.4 for Ca, 5.2 for Mg and 5.3 for S. Baker and Attiwill (1981)
give 3 kg ha1 yr1 as an average value for N input by rainfall to Australian eucalyptus forests, while Miller
(1981) gives values between 5 and 22 kg ha1 yr1 for N inputs by rainfall at sites in Scotland and Europe.

FIGURE 6.1 Diagrammatic representation of nutrient cycling. Rectangles are nutrient pools and arrows are flows of nutrients between pools.
The valves are processes controlling these flows. Note that leaching may be an input or loss of nitrogen from the soil profile of any given tree.
Nutrient Dynamics and Tree Growth
155

--- Page 169 ---

Weathering refers to the release of nutrients from parent material or rock. Rates are difficult to
quantify, but are positively related to precipitation and temperature, so soils of tropical forests are
much more highly weathered than soils of boreal forests. Within a similar climate, parent material has a
strong influence on weathering rates, and on the nutrients released by weathering. An excellent example
of this occurs in the Blue Mountains of Australia, west of Sydney, where the predominant parent
material is sandstone which weathers to nutrient-poor soils with poor water-holding characteristics.
The dominant vegetation on these soils is relatively low productivity eucalypt forests, with heath land
on ridges. But in a number of locations, there are higher areas created by basaltic lava flows that
weather to deep relatively rich, soils which, in the same climate, support warm temperate rainforests.
Pritchett (1979) provided a useful summary of data for forest soils and noted also that the rate of
nutrient release depends on the nature and the amount of mineral reserves and on environmental
conditions such as temperature and wetting and drying cycles. Inputs of N and P from weathering are
usually small, especially in old, highly weathered soils. Wood et al. (1984) stated that, for a hardwood
forest in the northern USA, ‘‘more than 10 kg ha1 yr1 of organic phosphorus are mineralized
through decomposition processes in the soil’’. Values for the rates of release of K, Ca and Mg, collated
by Miller (1981), are 4–7, 12–21 and about 4 kg ha1 yr1, respectively.
Rainfall intercepted by foliage and branches, which then drips down through the canopy or runs
down stems, leaches nutrients from the trees and returns them to the soil. Miller (1984) reviewed data
on this process – which is, strictly, biogeochemical rather than geochemical – and concluded that
crown leaching is a significant process but that its importance varies between nutrients. Baker and
Attiwill (1981), from various sources, gave values around 5 kg ha1 yr1 for N. Data from Pastor and
Bockheim (1984) gave a ratio of 2.9 between nutrient concentration in rainfall and throughfall.
The main mechanisms of nutrient loss from the soil–plant system are leaching of soluble nutrients
out of the root zone, and losses through surface erosion, fire and harvesting. Surface erosion may
consist of movement of individual particles or of mass flow (i.e. downslope movement of large masses
of soil). The latter mechanism will obviously cause much greater nutrient losses. Miller (1984)
concluded that leaching losses are generally negligible in most undisturbed forest ecosystems as
nutrients are retained by microorganisms or absorbed by tree roots before they leave the system.
However, fertilizer N may leach out of many soils, and on sandy soils in particular leaching may be
responsible for significant loss of fertilizers.
6.1.2. The Biogeochemical Cycle
The biogeochemical cycle describes the transfer of nutrients between the soil and vegetation. It
includes gains of nutrients to the soil from decomposition of organic matter, and losses by uptake by
plants, burning and wood removal. Only a quarter to a third of the nutrients taken up by trees
accumulate in standing biomass over the life of a forest; the remainder are released in litter and root
turnover, and by crown leaching (Miller 1984). The principle here is that nutrient capital in a natural
ecosystem is not the main nutrient-related factor controlling productivity; rather, the rate of nutrient
cycling is the important process.
a) Nutrient Addition to Soil
The rate of nutrient addition to the soil by organic matter decomposition depends on the environmental
conditions, while the amount and type of nutrients released from organic material depend on the mass and
composition of the material. When living plant material dies, P and K may be leached from it before
microbial processes have become important (Smethurst, personal communication). Since decomposition is
carried out by microorganisms, nutrients in organic matter may not be released into the soil solution
156
Chapter 6

--- Page 170 ---

immediately but may be immobilized by the microorganisms themselves for a period. If the process of
decomposition is rapid – as in tropical forests – and essentially continuous, and the system is in equilibrium,
the relatively small amounts of nutrient retained in decomposing organic matter will not be important for
tree growth. Since rates of litter decomposition are much lower in temperate than in tropical forests, larger
amounts of litter accumulate under temperate than under tropical forests. O’Connell and Sankaram (1997)
gave figures of more than 100 t ha1 under forests in Switzerland, Germany and the northern United States;
the mean for temperate forests was about 43 t ha1. They presented a table of values for tropical plantations
which indicated that, in most cases, litter accumulation was less than 10 t ha1.
The rate of nutrient addition to the soil can be estimated from litter decomposition rates and the
nutrient content of litter. If WL (kg m2) is the amount of decaying residue (litter) present, then the
litter decomposition rate DL (kg m2 yr1) is assumed to be proportional to WL itself:
DL ¼ kWL:
ð6:1Þ
O’Connell and Sankaram (1997) give values of the rate constant k (yr1) in tropical forests that range
from about 0.5 to 2 (yr1), but this will depend on temperature and litter water content (see Paul et al.
2002; also Section 6.3.2). The turnover time (1/k) of organic residues on the forest floor can be
estimated using a mass balance approach (e.g. Olson 1963). If the annual litterfall rate La
(kg m2 yr1) is assumed to be constant, and decay is given by Eq. (6.1), then at any time t the litter
accumulated at the soil surface is given by
WL tð Þ ¼ WL0ekt þ 1
k 1  ekt


La;
ð6:2Þ
where WL0 is the amount of litter at time t¼0. The rate constant k can be obtained by fitting Eq. (6.2)
to observations of WL at various times. If La is not constant, but is known, a mass balance approach can
still be applied but a numerical approach is required in lieu of Eq. (6.2).
A major input of organic matter, such as occurs after logging, may lead to a massive increase in the
microbial population, with the result that significant amounts of nutrients are extracted from the soil and
become temporarily unavailable to plants. As decomposition proceeds and the organisms die off,
nutrients are released into the soil. In regions where microbial activity is inhibited by low temperatures
or dry periods, the period of nutrient unavailability could extend across several growing seasons,
becoming a significant factor in re-establishment. Ironically, the final result of logging may be a reduction
in soil organic matter (SOM), brought about by the stimulation of microbial activity by the higher
temperatures and water content of uncovered soil, and the better aeration resulting from disturbance.
Landsberg and Gower (1997) provide more detailed discussion of these issues than we do here. They
have more focus on the chemistry, on data pertaining to nutrient uptake rates from various forest
ecosystems, and on losses by leaching, surface drainage (erosion), fire and timber harvesting. Here, we
are more concerned to outline the principles involved than to provide detailed data.
b) Nitrogen
Nitrogen is the only nutrient that has no common soil mineral source. Accordingly, nitrogenous
fertilizers are an enormously important source of nitrogen. Although there are some deposits of NaNO3
in Chile, Peru and India, ultimately, the source of almost all plant nitrogen is the air through the
synthesis of ammonia (NH3). This is achieved industrially by the Haber–Bosch process, in which H2 and
N2 are combined to produce NH3, which is easily converted to nitrate NO
3 . Fritz Haber was a German
chemist who developed the process of producing ammonia from atmospheric N. Carl Bosch indus-
trialized the process and both Haber and Bosch received Nobel prizes for their work. Industrial-scale
Nutrient Dynamics and Tree Growth
157

--- Page 171 ---

production was achieved in 1913, and large-scale production was pioneered by Germany during the
First World War – ammonia being an important part of the manufacture of explosives. (Wikipedia has
an interesting discussion of the Haber–Bosch process.)
Nitrogen may enter the soil in rainfall, or through nitrogen fixation by microorganisms, either in
symbiosis with plants or free-living in the soil, or from plant residues or direct fertilization. Nitrogen
mineralization is the process where organic nitrogen is converted to ammonium (NHþ
4 ), which can
then be nitrified to nitrate (NO
3 ). Plants can take up both these forms of N, but some prefer one source
to the other. In the form of NO
3 it is easily lost by leaching, and by volatilization in the form of
emissions of nitrogen oxides (NO and NO2) when N-containing material is burned, as happens when
forests or logging ‘slash’ are burned. To maintain an adequate supply of N to plants, inorganic N in soil
needs to be replaced either by mineralization of the soil organic pool or by the addition of mineral N
from external sources, i.e. fertilizers or atmospheric inputs (Smethurst 2007).
Since nitrogen is the nutrient needed in greatest amounts by plants, and because of its importance as
a component of proteins, N supply is frequently used as the reference element in considering plant
nutrient uptake and dynamics. In the forms useful to plants, nitrogen is probably the nutrient most
universally limiting to plant growth. It is also most vulnerable to loss, and its biogeochemical cycle
warrants particular attention. Most models of plant nutrient dynamics and supply rates are concerned
with N (see Sections 6.3.1 and 6.3.2 for two examples).
Nitrogen is available to plants principally in three different forms: nitrate, ammonium and organic
nitrogen (excluding N-fixing plants, because they are a special case). Within the wide range of organic
nitrogen compounds, amino acids are the most prevalent group and a large number of recent studies
have shown that they are accessed by different plant species in a variety of ecosystems (Na¨sholm et al.
2009). In natural ecosystems, the different forms of nitrogen usually occur together in mixtures but the
contribution of each form to the total pool of plant available nitrogen varies. This variation goes from a
strong dominance of organic nitrogen forms in poor soils with a well-developed organic layer, to
ammonium dominated soils of intermediary productivity, all the way to nitrate dominated soils of
high productivity (e.g. see Kranabetter et al. 2007).
Information reviewed in Section 4.4 indicated that large amounts of carbohydrate are utilized to
form fine roots, which may decay after a short time. It follows that there must be a rapid turnover of
nutrients associated with these organs, unless the nutrients are re-translocated prior to senescence.
McClaugherty and Aber (1982) estimated the nitrogen requirements for fine root production in a
53-year-old stand of Pinus resinosa and in an 80-year-old mixed hardwood stand and found that these
approached the estimates of total N-mineralization and uptake in hardwood forest. They cite measured
mineralization rates in temperate forests ranging from 50 to 300 kg ha1 yr1, with an average of
about 100 kg ha1 yr1, while demand by above-ground vegetation in temperate forests ranges from
50 to 150 kg ha1 yr1. McClaugherty and Aber therefore conclude that, if the estimates of fine root
production are to be accepted, large amounts of nitrogen must be re-translocated from senescing fine
roots to above-ground parts of plants. This may well be the case (see Section 6.2.2 on re-translocation),
although these estimates of N-release rates cannot be taken as accurate.
The microbial biomass also takes up nitrogen, in a process known as immobilization. The balance
between mineralization and immobilization is net mineralization, which may be positive or negative.
Immobilization of soil N is likely to be a significant factor in the probable response of forest stands to
increasing atmospheric CO2 concentration, but this potential limitation might not exist where N
fertilizers are used. Anthropogenic enhancement of global N fixation is now about equivalent to
natural N fixation processes.
Most nitrogen in the soil was originally fixed by symbiotic and non-symbiotic organisms. However,
the rate of supply (i.e. addition of new N to the soil) from these sources is usually relatively slow in
158
Chapter 6

--- Page 172 ---

relation to rates of use by trees (Davey and Wollum 1979). It follows that the major source of the
nitrogen in the soil solution at any time is decomposing organic matter. Typical estimates of rates of
symbiotic fixation in eucalyptus forests are in the region of 10 kg ha1 yr1 (Baker and Attiwill 1981).
Pastor et al. (1984), in a thorough study of forest productivity in relation to N and P cycling, found
N-mineralization rates in mixed forests in Wisconsin, USA, from 26 to 84 kg ha1 yr1. Soil
N-mineralization was positively correlated with litter production, and with the N and P content of litter.
Symbiotic relationships occur in trees of the legume family, in which specialized nodules on the
roots provide favourable environments for N-fixing bacteria: the bacteria have access to carbohydrates
and the trees are provided with reduced nitrogen for use in the synthesis of amino acids and proteins.
When the roots, and the trees as a whole, die (if they are not harvested and removed from the land) the
organic nitrogen is recycled through the ecosystem by decomposition and transformation to the NHþ
4
or NO
3 forms utilized by plants.
The ‘classical’ example of symbiotic relationships between plants and microorganisms is N-fixation
by Rhizobia, although an important relationship between Frankia and Casuarina species was identified
by Bowen (1985). More recently Binkley and Giardina (1997) have provided a useful review of
N-fixation in tropical forests and plantations. (We provide some comment on the role of mycorrhizal
fungi in nutrient uptake in Section 6.2.1 and Table 6.5, below.) Anyone concerned with the establish-
ment of trees, particularly in areas where the species they are interested in has not been grown before,
should try to obtain any information available about microorganism associations with that species.
The least that can be done is to inoculate seedlings with soil from successful growing areas.
Nutrient uptake by trees and its transfer to their component parts (stems, branches, foliage, . . .) are
important facets of the biogeochemical cycle, but because uptake is the fundamental process underlying
tree nutrition we defer consideration of uptake until after the following brief discussion on nutrient losses.
c) Losses: Fire
Fire is an important factor in the biogeochemical cycle and may be a major cause of nutrient loss
from forest ecosystems (Walker et al. 1986). This is particularly so in countries like Australia and the
Mediterranean regions, where long dry periods may be followed by hot, low-humidity weather.
The impact of fire in these regions is also likely to be greater, from the nutritional point of view,
because the soils supporting the forests are often poor.
Fires in forests are either wild (uncontrolled), or prescribed to reduce fuel load and future fire risk, or
to assist site preparation and regeneration. The effects of hot fires are more serious than those of cool
fires. It is obvious that fire releases nutrients from the organic matter more rapidly than would otherwise
have been the case, but it must be emphasized that fires do not contribute any additional nutrients: they
simply change their form, hasten some cycling processes and also result in losses of some nutrients.
Nitrogen is volatilized (see above) during fire, and its loss parallels the loss in weight of burnt fuel (Raison
et al. 1985). Soil heating results in immediate release of significant amounts of NHþ
4 based N as a result of
oxidation of organic matter. Raison (1984) estimated that the quantity of N lost in burning forest floor
litter can equal or exceed the amount removed in harvesting. He gives figures of up to 1000 kg ha1.
After a fire there may be nutrient losses by water erosion as ash is easily carried by surface run-off, or
by removal of nutrient-rich ash from the site by wind. The nitrogen lost in fire must be replaced either
by rain or by symbiotic or non-symbiotic fixation, and since addition rates by these processes are so
slow (of order 0–10 kg ha1 yr1), frequent burning of a forest will result in nitrogen depletion and
probably reduction in the amounts of other nutrients available to the trees. Some phosphorus is also
lost in volatile forms in fire: Raison (1984) estimated that 50% of the phosphorus contained in litter
will be lost when the litter is burnt. Fo¨lster and Khanna (1997) provide a histogram showing the loss of
element content when harvest residue (slash) was burned in secondary forest in Brazil. At two sites
Nutrient Dynamics and Tree Growth
159

--- Page 173 ---

almost 100% of N was lost, with losses of other elements ranging from about 10% (Ca) to 60% (S),
while losses of P and K were in the 3–4% range. Although nutrient capital decreases due to fire,
availability of P and cations can be increased.
Reviews of the effects of fire on forest nutrition have been provided by O’Connell et al. (1981),
Raison (1979), Rundell (1981) and Walker et al. (1986).
d) Losses: Tree Harvesting
The other major mechanism of nutrient loss from forests is tree harvesting. If harvesting is selective
and only a few trees are removed, nutrient losses will be small and can largely be made up by weathering
and inputs from rain. However, when intensive plantation forestry culminates in clear-felling and the
removal of large masses of timber, the nutrient losses may be considerable. Various studies on the
removal of nutrients by harvesting eucalyptus forests indicate losses of 10–30 kg ha1 of P,
30–160 kg ha1 of K, 100–600 kg ha1 of Ca and 20–100 kg ha1 of Mg (Attiwill 1981). Data collated
by Turner (1981) also fall within these ranges. He showed N-losses to be in the range 70–250 kg ha1
for eucalyptus, and generally 200–250 kg ha1 for P. radiata. Values for the other nutrients are similar
in P. radiata and appear to be in the same ranges for other forest types (see Table 6.2). Fo¨lster and
Khanna (1997) provided a considerable amount of data on the quantities of nutrients in material
harvested from tropical plantations.
The nutrients removed by harvesting are in the stem and stem bark. Modern procedures sometimes
include the removal of foliage and small branches. This exacerbates nutrient losses, which also happens
when logging residue is burnt. Harvesting increases the rates of decomposition of remaining organic
matter because disturbance results in greater soil aeration and exposure, which may lead to higher soil
temperatures. There are also likely to be increased losses by leaching of nutrients from the profile
because there are fewer fine roots to absorb them in the period after clear-cutting  at least until herbs
become established to utilize the nutrients and release them when they die and decompose.
There are many data in the literature on nutrient budgets and cycling, although in recent years focus
has shifted to work on available pools (Philip Smethurst, personal communication). Landsberg and
Gower (1997) collated data from several forest types in the United States and Australia (their Table 7.7;

Chapter 6

--- Page 174 ---

p. 223) which show that the nitrogen balance across a rotation (i.e. gains from atmospheric deposition
minus losses from tree harvesting) was generally, but not always, negative. The range was from þ140 to
400 kg ha1. For P and Ca the balance was always negative, averaging about 20 kg ha1 for P and
between 300 and 900 kg ha1 for Ca. Older symposia devoted to this topic (‘Impact of Intensive
Harvesting on Forest Nutrient Cycling’ at the School of Forestry, Syracuse, NY (1979); ‘Australian
Forest Nutrition Workshop: Productivity in Perpetuity’, CSIRO, Canberra, 1981) contain many useful
data on the nutrient composition of the biomass of various forests and forest types, and on the nutrient
composition of the component parts (see Tables 6.2 and 6.3).
Management for sustained forest yield must include careful consideration of the need to leave the
largest possible amount of biomass on the ground and hence retain as much nutrient as possible. This
can be based on calculations of nutrient loss, addition and turnover rates. Such calculation may suggest
the desirability of de-barking harvested trees, as well as the need to add fertilizers. In other words,
management for sustained yield requires a good appreciation of the biogeochemical cycle.
6.2 FOREST NUTRITIONAL REQUIREMENTS
6.2.1. Nutrient Uptake
Nutrient uptake by plants is part of the biogeochemical cycle. At any time, nutrient uptake rate will
be a function of soil moisture, temperature, the effectiveness with which the soil is exploited by roots,
and  most difficult to quantify  the amount of ‘plant available’ nutrient in the soil solution. It may
also be an active process that uses energy released by respiration and includes a feedback in response to
nutrient concentrations in the roots. A model based on these ideas is presented later in this chapter, in
Section 6.3.3.
If mineral nutrients in the soil are to be available to plants they must be dissolved in the soil
solution. Absorption of ions by plant roots can only take place in the immediate vicinity of the roots
where ions arrive by movement in solution through the soil, or by the growth of roots into previously
unexplored soil regions. Transfer to roots is by mass flow in the transpiration stream, or diffusion down
a concentration gradient. The rate of diffusion varies between ions and may be affected by exchange
sites in the soil. The concentration of these ions is usually very low, and although experimental work
with nutrient solutions has shown that uptake rates increase with increasing solution concentrations,
roots can remove nutrients from very dilute solutions (see Ingestad 1982).
a) Movement of Nutrients Towards Roots
Research in this field shows that, at least for agricultural crops, mobile ions such as NO
3 move
towards root systems by mass flow in liquid water as well as by diffusion along concentration gradients
in the soil solution. Diffusion processes are generally slow, as illustrated in Table 6.4, which shows that
even nitrate (NO
3 , the most rapidly diffusing substance) typically diffuses only 7.5 mm in a day. It
follows that for immobile ions such as phosphate, uptake is limited by the volume of soil that can be
explored by roots, and by root abundance. This should strictly be described in terms of absorbing
surface area per unit volume of soil but, since this is almost impossible to measure, root length per unit
volume of soil (LRV) is a good substitute. The extent to which soil is exploited by roots determines the
length of the path along which nutrients must move to reach roots. In fact, it is widely believed that the
rate limiting step in nutrient acquisition by a plant is the flux of nutrients from the bulk soil to the
surface of the roots (Nye 1977). A thorough analytical treatment of water and nutrient movements to
roots and root systems is provided by Nye and Tinker (1978), but a simplified version of the theory
governing plant nutrient acquisition follows.

The flux from the soil to the surfaces of plant roots is governed by mass flow and diffusion. We will
express these fluxes on a unit root-area basis. The flux due to mass flow is the product of the soil solution
concentration [N] (mol m3) and the flow of water from the soil towards root surfaces caused by
transpiration (see Section 7.4.2), divided by the root surface area AR (m2). Since transpiration, i.e. the
flux of water out of the foliage, is the product of the transpiration rate ET (m3 m2) per unit leaf area times
total leaf area AF (m2) of the canopy, the flux JM (mol m2 s1) of nutrient to the root surface due to mass

Values of the diffusion coefficient for various ions and amino acids in water (Dw) and the
corresponding effective diffusion coefficient (DeN) in soil are included in Table 6.4. Because the solute
interacts with soil particles, slowing the rate of diffusion, diffusion in soil is several orders of magnitude
less than in water. Note too that the distance the solutes can diffuse in a day is really quite small:
even nitrate, the fastest, diffuses only 7.5 mm in a day, while the ammonium ion diffuses less than
1 mm in a day.
The depletion zone is difficult to estimate for intact trees, but Dr has been determined for plants in
pots. Bagshaw et al. (1972), for onion, and Gahoonia and Nielsen (1992), for rape, both report Dr of
3–5 mm, over which distance the concentration of extractable P declined to 50–75% of its bulk soil
value, depending on the extraction technique and the duration of the experiment. These values for
Dr are significantly larger than the daily diffusion distances shown in Table 6.4, with the exception of
nitrate. Syring and Claassen (1995) performed a theoretical analysis of the diffusion of nutrients
towards a root and show that Dr varies with the diffusion coefficient and duration:

Finally, application of Eq. (6.5) to data from Jackson and Chittenden (1981) for P. radiata assuming
transpiration of 5 mm m2 day1, and using the effective diffusion coefficient for nitrate from
Table 6.4, shows that the flow of nutrients towards the root surface due to diffusion is a small fraction
of the mass flow due to transpiration.
b) Factors Affecting Nutrient Uptake
Current conceptual and mathematical models recognize the relationship between soil solution
nitrogen concentration and biomass allocation, but do not include the potential effect of a change in
the form of nitrogen that occurs simultaneously with quantitative changes in nitrogen availability.
Na¨sholm et al. (2009) argue that these are likely to be important because:

as we see from Table 6.4, the diffusion coefficient varies for different N forms;

the carbon costs of assimilating these different N forms vary: it is high for nitrate, intermediate for
ammonium and low for amino acids (depending on the actual amino acid absorbed there might
even be a net gain);

the site of assimilation differs between N sources. Nitrate is usually (at least partly) assimilated in
(incorporated into) shoots while ammonium and amino acids are assimilated in roots. This means
that roots have first access to ammonium and amino acids and shoots have first access to nitrate.
This has implications for biomass partitioning and growth patterns.
Since most nutrient uptake takes place from soil solution, it follows that uptake will be greatly
reduced as soil dries. This may have important implications with regard to the effects of drought on
tree growth. Nutrient concentrations and organic matter are usually highest in the upper soil layers,
which dry most rapidly, so that reduction in nutrient uptake may be an early result of prolonged
periods without rain. In fact, reductions in nutrient uptake rates are likely to precede the development
of serious water stress by a significant interval because tree roots may exploit the soil to quite
considerable depth, where some nutrients are scarce but from which water can be extracted. This
will result in maintenance of turgor and continued growth of aerial tissues, which would be expected to
result in overall dilution of the mineral nutrient concentrations in trees.
Data in Fife and Nambiar (1984) support the suggestion that nutrient uptake is slowed by drought,
and also indicate that drought causes re-translocation from older to younger, more actively
164
Chapter 6

--- Page 178 ---

growing, tissues. However, water and nutrition also interact in complex ways to affect the physiology of
trees: Ewers et al. (2000) found that fertilization of Loblolly pine (Pinus taeda) resulted in a consider-
able reduction of root surface area relative to foliage area, with significant effects on tree water relations
and the hydraulic architecture of the trees. Phillips et al. (2001) found that compensatory changes in
stem hydraulic conductivity and stomatal conductance resulted in very similar transpiration rates from
fertilized, unfertilized and water-deprived plots in Norway spruce in northern Sweden. However, the
stands did not develop severe water stress. High levels of nutrition that cause an increase in leaf area
under favourable water conditions can lead to serious detrimental results in a subsequent drought, as
seen in the observations on litterfall from P. radiata made by Linder et al. (1987).
The role of fungal mycorrhizae in the absorption of nutrients has been recognized since early in the
twentieth century. The roots of virtually all tree species studied so far develop symbiotic associations with
mycorrhizal fungi, which may affect growth and morphology and the exudation of organic substances
from roots. Mycorrhizal stimulation of growth occurs mostfrequently in soils low inone or more nutrients
(Bowen 1985). In many associations between beneficial mycorrhizae and higher plants it has been found
that there are quite specific relationships between hosts and symbionts: particular strains of symbionts
often appear to have evolved in association with particular provenances of trees, although there are also a
number of examples of less specific relationships. Knowledge of these symbiotic relationships, and in fact
of the whole complex ecology of mycorrhizal fungi and their association with trees, is still scattered and
incomplete, but a good compendium of papers is provided by the book edited by Atkinson et al. (1983).
Results presented in Table 6.5 illustrate the effects on the growth of Sitka spruce seedlings of
inoculating two soils with several mycorrhizal isolates. All isolates increased growth, although the
effects were not consistent between soils. Similar results from other authors are given by Bowen (1985).
Increased uptake of nutrients occurs because ectomycorrhizal fungi act like extensions to the root
system, in effect increasing root length density. This is particularly important in trees, where root
length densities are low and, without mycorrhizae, exploitation of the soil volume is poor. The
enhanced exploitation by mycorrhizae is particularly important for the uptake of immobile or poorly
mobile nutrients, like P and some trace elements, partly because mycorrhizal hyphae extend beyond the
zone of nutrient depletion that develops around each root for these nutrients.

c) Calculating Nutrient Uptake
In general terms, the amounts of nutrient taken up over an interval such as a growing season must
be proportional to the dry matter increment. Obviously there are many caveats and qualifications to
that statement: for example, the relationship between nutrient uptake rate and growth of a young forest
is unlikely to be the same as that of an old, stable ecosystem. In particular, both retention in tissues and
transfers between tissues and in litterfall would be very different in these two systems. Some of these
factors will be discussed later, but the correlation between growth and nutrient uptake is strongly
supported by data collated by Miller (1984), and shown in Figure 6.2, and by the highly significant
correlation between net above-ground production by a mixed forest and rate of N-mineralization
obtained by Pastor et al. (1984). Figure 6.2 has logarithmic axes and the resulting linear relationships
show that uptake and biomass production are allometrically related, and in particular that N-uptake is
proportional to biomass production. Relationships such as these provide a means of analysing the
nutrient uptake component of the biogeochemical cycle: if we can estimate dry matter production, and
if we have values for the nutrient concentration in the dry matter, we can estimate uptake by trees.
To illustrate, denote the average mineral nutrient concentrations in trees as [M] (kg nu-

where [Mi] and Wi are the mineral nutrient concentration and biomass of plant tissue i. The growth
rate of single trees of the same age in closed-canopy stands may be vastly different in different stands, or
even within the same stand with a wide range of tree sizes. The rate of nutrient uptake by individual
trees will vary with these factors, as well as with age.
Equations (6.7) and (6.8) provide a basis for analysis of the nutrient (re)translocation between plant
parts. As a numerical example, assume that biomass sampling at time t shows the foliage mass WF of a
stand to be 3 t ha1 with N-concentration of 1.5% (by weight). The total mass of N in the foliage is
then 0.045 t, or 45 kg. A subsequent sampling shows that WF¼3.3 t ha1 but the N-concentration has
not changed. Total mass of the nutrient has therefore increased to 49.5 kg. The increase of 4.5 kg must
have come from either nutrient uptake, or from re-translocation from other parts of the tree.
It follows from this approach that, if we can calculate the growth rates that should be achieved by
canopies intercepting specified amounts of solar radiation, and we know the amounts of nutrient
associated with each biomass increment, it should be possible to estimate the rate of nutrient supply
that would be needed to support those biomass growth rates. This idea underlies some of the recent
research on forest nutrition, discussed in the next section.
6.2.2. Nutrient Re-Translocation
In the early stages of tree growth, most of the nutrients taken up from the soil will be retained in the
tree biomass, but as trees grow older the contribution of re-translocation to the nutrients required for
new growth increases. Before the formation of heartwood, and while the foliage mass of trees is
increasing, the rate of nutrient uptake and immobilization in tissue will be higher than the rate of
release in litterfall or supply by re-translocation. At equilibrium, when the tree or canopy leaf area is
stable, nutrients required for the formation of new tissues are matched by the quantity of nutrients
recycled, either through re-translocation or the biogeochemical cycle. At this stage, re-translocation
may be a major contributor of the nutrients needed for growth.
Re-translocation may be illustrated formally as follows: assume that at time t the organ i (where i is F
for foliage, etc.) has dry-weight biomass Wi and mineral nutrient content Mi; its nutrient concentration
is then [Mi]¼Mi/Wi. The total tree biomass is WT¼SiWi, and assume that the dry weight gain of a
stand or tree calculated or measured over a period Dt is DWT. This is allocated to foliage (DWF), stems
(DWS) and roots (DWR) according to the biomass allocation ratios i¼DWi/DWT (see Section 5.6.1).
We will assume for present purposes that heartwood has not yet formed, although it could easily be
incorporated into this analysis, as could litter. The nutrient concentrations in the tissues at the
beginning of the period of interest are [MF], [MS] and [MR], and average nutrient concentration
[M] is then given by Eq. (6.8). For example, using data from the same sources as Table 6.2, ignoring
branches, and assuming the nutrient involved is phosphorus, then

(which Linder and Rook 1984 suggest is the case), and roots? and so on.

from needles. Switzer and Nelson (1972) found that a ‘sizeable portion’ of the annual nutrient require-
mentsof 20-year-old loblolly pine(P. taeda) was met by transfers between the living fractions of the system,
and that the soil was a surprisingly small contributor to total annual nutrient requirements of N, P, K, Ca,
Mg and S. They also found that only about 19% of nutrients were retained by the trees, the remaining 81%
being recycled.Table6.3summarizes informationonthe concentration ofvariouselements ingreen foliage
and litterfall of various forest types and the withdrawal of elements from this foliage (Attiwill 1981).
Attiwill (1980) found the concentration of phosphorus in the tissues of mature Eucalyptus obliqua
was low by comparison with other species. Re-translocation accounted for 46% of the gross annual P
demand by the stand. A major contribution to re-translocation came from heartwood formation:
withdrawal of P prior to heartwood formation accounted for 31% of the total ‘biochemical’ cycle of
phosphorus and 17% of the ‘biochemical’ cycle of potassium, but he did not consider nitrogen. Attiwill
commented that the formation of heartwood may be regarded as a growth-regulating process in which
part of the annual net primary production is stored, rather than cycled as litter. The re-translocation of
nutrients from heartwood in E. obliqua is much higher than the recovery from tissues other than
needles identified by Miller (1984). It is clearly a matter of considerable importance.
Miller et al. (1976b) showed that nutrient concentrations in different foliage classes change
markedly during the growing season, and Fife and Nambiar (1982) demonstrated unequivocally that
the nutrient concentration of foliage is subject to continual change. They found that the phosphorus
content of P. radiata needles was 24.9 mg/needle after prolonged drought and 33.5 mg/needle 3 weeks
after rain. Their results indicate that phosphorus was being withdrawn from needles (of any age) in
favour of growing tissue when drought reduced or stopped uptake from the soil.
6.2.3. Growth in Relation to Nutrition
Managers of commercial plantations are, of necessity, much concerned with how to achieve the best
possible growth rates and yields. To this end, they must try to ensure the nutrient requirements of the
trees are met; they therefore require information about the nutritional requirements of trees that is as
accurate as possible. But, as we have already noted, despite thousands of field experiments aimed at
establishing optimum fertilization rates on various soils, prediction of nutrient requirements retains a
high element of uncertainty. Much of this research has been by necessity of a highly applied nature and
had to be repeated for every new combination of soil, climate, species, management, etc., All these have
only slowly built a generic understanding of nutrient management. The complexity of the issue still
thwarts the development of accurate, transferable, cost-effective methods for assessing nutrient status.
We do not intend to review the vast literature on field fertilization trails but, as a basis for predicting the
likely responses of plantation forests to fertilizer applications, we consider some of the principles
underlying tree responses to nutrition at the stand level.
Problems with field fertilizer experiments derive from the fact that the usual procedure is to apply
different amounts of fertilizer to stands, possibly using split applications. The stands may be on
different soils and of different ages. The amount and frequency of fertilizer application depends, to a
significant extent, on the expected responses in terms of commercial production, but it is interesting to
note that, until comparatively recently, the responses that could be obtained from high levels of
fertilization were frequently grossly underestimated. Where the rates of nutrient supply are sub-
optimal, apparent responses to additional nutrition may reflect the removal of (unidentified) limiting
factors other than nutrition, or, where the nutrient ratios within the plants were sub-optimal, apparent
responses may reflect unidentified interactions rather than an unequivocal move up some clearly
defined nutrient response curve. Consequently, results are frequently highly variable in terms of growth
responses and the duration of those responses. Most attempts to explain the variability and to
Nutrient Dynamics and Tree Growth
169

--- Page 183 ---

generalize the results have focussed on differences in soil types, climatic conditions – particularly water
supply – species and management practices such as spacing. It is not surprising that this plethora of
uncontrolled variables virtually ensures confusion and inhibits understanding.
a) Growth and Relative Nutrient Addition Rate
A breakthrough in thinking about forest responses to applied nutrients came from the work of
Tamm (1968) and Ingestad (1982) in Sweden. Ingestad explicitly recognized a point previously noted
by various authors: that plants can extract nutrients from very dilute solutions, and if a balanced
nutrient solution is supplied to roots fast enough for the plants to maintain optimum concentrations
of nutrients in their tissues, growth rates will not be limited by nutrition but will be determined by
other factors (temperature, light). Ingestad proposed the idea of relative nutrient addition rate, which
specifies that nutrients must be supplied at rates that will maintain plant growth rates – exponential in
the case of seedlings – and consistent with the highest obtainable growth rates as plants mature.
He showed that adding a limiting nutrient at an exponential rate led to the same exponential growth
rate while maintaining a stable internal concentration of that nutrient in young plant tissues. This
system has become very useful for generating plants of defined, stable nutrient status.
The relative growth rate (RGR) of a plant depends on its internal nitrogen concentration [N] e.g. see
Figure 6.4. RGR increases up to a limit over the nitrogen response range, it is then independent of [N]
over the sufficiency range, and declines when nitrogen toxicity occurs with even higher internal [N].
For the RGR of a plant to remain constant, nutrients must be taken up fast enough to maintain a stable
nutrient concentration in the tissues. If growth is limited by one nutrient – say P – and N is relatively
freely available/accessible, there is likely to be excess uptake of nitrogen.
Sands and Smethurst (1995) developed a simple model for nitrogen-limited plant growth using
Michaelis–Menten kinetics to control nutrient uptake by roots. Application of this model to data from
experiments based on the concept of relative nutrient addition rate reproduced key results from these
experiments: plant RGR equals nitrogen relative addition rate up to a limit; when nitrogen uptake
Deficiency
range
Response
range
Tissue nitrogen concentration [N]
Relative growth rate (RGR)
Sufficiency
range
Toxic range
FIGURE 6.4 A schematic illustration of the dependence of plant relative growth rate (RGR) on the internal plant nitrogen concentration [N ].
Four regions are shown: the non-response or deficiency range for very low [N], the response range over which RGR varies approximately
linearly with [N], the sufficiency range where RGR has its maximum value and a toxic range where [N] is in excess and inhibits plant growth.
170
Chapter 6

--- Page 184 ---

kinetic parameters are within the range reported in the literature, this limiting growth rate agrees with
that observed and the nitrogen concentrations in the external solution are consistent with those
published. Hence, consistency was demonstrated between the nutrient flux density approach of
Ingestad for growing plants at a defined RGR and internal nutritional status, and the known mechan-
isms of active nutrient uptake at the membrane level of root cells.
However, we now know (Na¨sholm, personal communication) that, at the membrane level, there are
two types of uptake mechanisms for many nutrients and in particular for N: the Low Affinity Transport
System and the High Affinity Uptake System (LATS and HATS, respectively). When plants take up N
from very dilute solutions they use the HATS. But plants are also well equipped to take up N from
solutions of higher concentrations using the LATS. Uptake via LATS is faster but the diffusion coefficient
Km is obviously lower, and vice versa for HATS. In nature, it is possible that soil heterogeneity of N
availability is met through differential use of these two systems. In his experiments, Ingestad used a
technique based on low concentrations of nutrients, which allowed for better control and tuning of
nutrient additions. But (referring back to the discussion on processes governing transport of nutrients to
root surfaces), if there is a strong, transpirationally induced mass flow towards root surfaces we may
expect concentrations around roots to increase if, or when, transport systems such as HATS cannot keep
pace with transport towards roots. In this situation, plants can and will use the LATS instead.
In Ingestad’s simple set up, plants would not experience such variations, as they do in nature.
A˚ gren (2004) pointed out that the relationships between N in the plant and RGR are different from
those between P concentration and RGR. The relation for N is linear whereas the relation for P is
quadratic. This is consistent with theoretical predictions based on the assumption that autotroph
growth requires proteins (which are N-based), and proteins presuppose rRNAs (which are P-based).
A˚ gren shows that this has the consequence that the N:P ratio in growing tissue increases at low RGRs,
passes a maximum and then decreases at high RGRs.
There are other implications of the relations between nutrient supply rates and plant growth, some
of which were outlined by Knecht and Go¨ransson (2004). They noted that, for a plant to achieve a
particular RGR, the minimum amount of all essential nutrient elements required to maintain this rate
must be available for uptake. Because availability of all elements corresponding exactly to the plant’s
requirements is unlikely, RGR is likely to be limited by the availability of the nutrient element least
available relative to the plant’s requirement (recall the comment about Liebig’s law of the minimum
and Carl Sprengel in Section 5.3.3).
When plants experience a shortage of a particular nutrient they may respond by inducting a specific
uptake system with high affinity for that nutrient. This causes them to become less efficient in
absorbing other nutrients, hence if one nutrient is limiting it will affect the capacity of a plant to
acquire others. When the limiting nutrient is supplied, the plants then become more efficient in
absorbing other, non-limiting nutrients. For many plant species, but not for all species or for all
nutrients, the concentration of the limiting nutrient in the plant can be considered a measure of the
availability of that nutrient. A certain minimum concentration of each nutrient is required before
growth can occur, and there may be uptake beyond some optimum concentration, but this will not
further increase (relative) growth rate (Figure 6.4). At optimum nutrient ratios – the ratios when all
nutrients are limiting growth simultaneously – the RGR will be determined by the internal concentra-
tions of all the nutrients.
All elements can be taken up in excess of requirements for growth, but plants can also down-regulate
uptake to avoid toxic concentrations. Knecht and Go¨ransson (2004) surveyed all available data relating
to nutrient concentrations of plants growing under terrestrial conditions. They found that excessive
uptake of nitrogen or phosphorus generally occurred in small amounts, and that there is a relatively
strong down-regulation which restricts excessive uptake of these two elements when they are not
Nutrient Dynamics and Tree Growth
171

--- Page 185 ---

limiting growth. Na¨sholm (personal communication) commented: ‘‘I do not agree with this. Conifers
that receive excess N accumulate it in needles in the form of the amino acid arginine, which can account
for 20% or more of needle N. In trees that do not experience N excess, arginine concentrations are very
low but increase dramatically when N is added in larger amounts than needed for growth’’ (see also
Na¨sholm and Ericsson 1990). Knecht and Go¨ransson found that the concentrations of potassium,
calcium and magnesium were, in most cases, higher than the optimum ratios, confirming that plant
growth is generally limited by either N or P.
b) Nitrogen Productivity and Growth
To describe growth as a function of nutrition, we would expect to have to write models starting with
the general relationship
dW
dt ¼ f N
ð
Þ;
ð6:12Þ
where W is the amount of biomass and f is a function of some measure N of the nutritional state of the
plant or tree. This would lead immediately to requirements for information (about functional relation-
ships) that we do not have, and information which would possibly be very difficult to obtain. However,
progress is possible using a different approach.
A˚ gren (1983) proposed a model based on the concept of nitrogen productivity  the amount of
biomass produced per unit of nitrogen taken up by a stand. He dealt only with nitrogen because it is
commonly one of the most growth-limiting macro-nutrients and relevant data are therefore readily
available. A˚ gren also restricted his analysis to foliage, recognizing that growth of other tissues can be
estimated from knowledge of foliage mass, distribution and photosynthetic properties. The basic
premise of his model is that the amount of new biomass produced is proportional to the total mass
NF (kg) of nitrogen in the foliage, and the amount lost (either through litterfall or to meet the needs of
respiration) is proportional to the total foliage biomass WF (kg). The basic equation of this model is
then
dWF
dt
¼ eNNF  gFWF;
ð6:13Þ
where eN (kg foliage kg1 N yr1) is the nitrogen productivity, i.e. the mass of new foliage produced
per unit mass of nitrogen in the foliage, and gF is the rate of foliage loss (yr1), which is assumed to be
constant.
A˚ gren argued that because plants do not grow in size indefinitely as they take up nitrogen, then eN
must decline with increasing plant mass. He accordingly assumed that
eN ¼ aN  bNWF;
ð6:14Þ
where aN and bN are species-specific parameters. For conifers with relatively stable, long-lived foliage
biomass, eN can be approximated as (current year’s foliage biomass)/(total nitrogen in all foliage).
A˚ gren estimated eN in this way from a number of data sets available in the literature, fitted Eq. (6.14) to
this data, and obtained the results presented in Table 6.6.
If additional increments of nitrogen produce decreasing foliage dry matter increments, a point must
be reached where foliage mass is at its maximum (WFx) and the foliage contains the maximum possible
amount of nitrogen (NFx). Setting the rate of growth dWF /dt¼0 in Eq. (6.13), substituting for eN from
Eq. (6.14) and solving for NFx gives

Given estimates of aN, bN, [NFx] and gF, Eq. (6.17) allows estimation of the maximum mass of foliage

agreed quite well with observed values.
A˚ gren’s (1983) analysis has been discussed in some detail because it represented a significant step
towards the essential goal of a quantitative description of the growth of trees in relation to nutrition. The
model is written in terms of amount of the nutrient, rather than its concentration, but if we substitute
Eq. (6.14) for eN into Eq. (6.13) and divide through by WF we obtain an expression for the leaf RGR:

This indicates that leaf RGR depends on tissue nutrient concentration and content. However, our

the plant. As a plant grows, its internal nitrogen status will change, and this is affected by nitrogen
uptake. More detailed discussions of nitrogen productivity, including the effects of uptake, are
provided in A˚ gren and Bossata (1996).
Ingestad (1982), and the series of papers he refers to, used nutrient solutions in the laboratory to
demonstrate that constant seedling RGRs and constant internal nutrient concentrations can only be
