Nutrient Dynamics and Tree Growth
173

--- Page 187 ---

maintained if the rate of nutrient supply to the roots (nutrient flux density) increases in the same
manner as growth; i.e. if the relative nutrient addition rate is constant. Equation (6.18) suggests that
even if the nitrogen uptake (addition) rate is fast enough to maintain [NF] constant as WF increases,
RGR will decrease. However, examination of the data in A˚ gren (1983) suggests that the second term on
the right in Eq. (6.18) is much smaller than the first and hence constant foliage nutrient concentration
is not inconsistent with constant foliage RGR.
Equations (6.13)–(6.18) are valuable for analytical purposes, and for the insights they provide into
growth in relation to nutrition. However, they are of limited value for predictive purposes unless the
form of A˚ gren’s model can be confirmed, and reliable parameter values established for many other
species. (The analysis should also be tried for nutrients other than nitrogen.) If dW/dt is predicted by a
growth model, the nutrient requirement of the stand can be evaluated. To do this, we will need to know
how leaf growth and photosynthetic characteristics are affected by nutrition.
In practice, to predict growth, we only need to know the amount of nutrient taken up and the
concentration in new growth, which is the practical application of the nutrient productivity concept
without requiring the complexity of A˚ gren’s analysis. Smethurst et al. (2004) used a model called
PCATS to calculate the supply and uptake of P and base cations to young plantation eucalypts. The
model calculates nutrient uptake using esimates of average root surface and the principles of mass flow
and diffusion outlined earlier. It works on a daily time step and uses an analytical solution to calculate
the concentration of nutrient at an average root surface. PCATS, combined with CABALA (Battaglia
et al. 2004), provided ‘reasonable’ simulations of nutrient uptake and of biomass accumulation.
Linder and Rook (1984) reviewed the effects of mineral nutrition on carbon dioxide exchange and
the partitioning of carbon in trees. They state categorically: ‘‘When a tree’s growth is stimulated by
fertilization a significant part, if not most, of the result is due to an increase in the total area of the
photosynthetic surface’’, i.e. in leaf surface area and number of leaves produced. There are also likely to
be effects on leaf longevity, although these may vary. In situations of serious nutrient deficiency, leaf
numbers may be reduced by early shedding caused by nutrient withdrawal. This, of course, will cause
increased litter production and may lead to immobilization on the forest floor. These conclusions
indicate that the leaf biomass allocation coefficient (F) is the factor most likely to be affected by
nutrition, and Linder and Rook conclude that fertilization of non-closed coniferous stands, where
nutrient supply is limiting, leads to increased growth by increasing needle biomass. This leads to more
effective energy interception, as well as possibly higher photosynthetic rates at given photon flux
densities. The effects of nitrogen on photosynthesis seem to be much more pronounced in hardwoods
than in conifer seedlings but the limited information available on the overall effects of fertilization on
hardwood stands did not allow Linder and Rook to draw conclusions about the effects on annual
carbon balance.
c) Comprehensive Forest Nutrition Trials
Ingestad’s ideas (see above) were developed from work with seedlings in controlled environments,
where the nutrients were provided by continuous flow of very dilute solutions of more or less constant
concentration. The ideas were transferred to the field in the Swedish Coniferous Forest Experiment
(SWECON), which started in 1974. Pines on a poor sandy soil were supplied daily with a complete
fertilizer by means of an irrigation system with the dosage adjusted to the estimated uptake capacity of
the vegetation. The amounts of nutrients accumulated by fertilization followed sigmoid curves during
each growing season over a number of years. The growth rates achieved in this experiment were
considerably higher than could have been predicted from knowledge obtained from conventional
fertilizer experiments, demonstrating that nutrient availability was a more important limiting factor
for forest growth in Sweden than previously thought. Sub-soil sampling throughout the experiment
established that negligible amounts of the applied nutrients (particularly N) were leached out of the
174
Chapter 6

--- Page 188 ---

root zone into the water table beneath the trees (Sune Linder, personal communication). This is an
important point: applications of nitrogen fertilizer in excess of the amounts that crops can use are
currently causing serious pollution problems in rivers in various parts of the world. The experiment
demonstrated, among other things, the possibilities of matching nutrient supplies with demand by
crops, while increasing productivity and reducing leaching losses.
The SWECON experiment gave rise to others of the same type in Australia, Portugal, North Carolina
(the SETRES experiment), Hawaii, Brazil and northern Sweden. We review two of these. The first is the
Biology of Forest Growth (BFG) experiment with P. radiata plantations near Canberra, Australia
(Raison and Myers 1992). The second is the Flakaliden experiment with Norway spruce (Picea abies)
in northern Sweden (Linder 1995). Both were intensively studied, and a large number of papers have
been based on the results of those studies, some of which we cite below and elsewhere in this book.
In Australia, Sune Linder, who had been a leader of the SWECON experiment, set up the BFG
experiment (mentioned in Section 4.4.5a) with P. radiata on relatively poor yellow podsolic soil near
Canberra (Raison and Myers 1992). The area has average monthly summer temperatures of about
22 C, average winter temperatures are about 7 C with relatively frequent frost, rainfall is around
700 mm yr1, and air humidity is generally low. The BFG experiment used seven treatments: C, a
control; F, two applications of solid fertilizer containing 200 kg N ha1 applied in September and
November, 1983; I, irrigated to maintain ‘field capacity’ from 1984 to 2003; IFþ, irrigation as in I plus
fertilization as in F; IF, as for IFþ but irrigation stopped in 1988; ILþ, irrigated to maintain soil field
capacity during 1984–2003 and with macro- and micro-nutrients applied in irrigation water at
approximately 1800 kg N ha1 during 1984–1990; and IL, as for ILþ but with approximately
1200 kg N ha1 applied over 6 years until treatments stopped in 1988.
Linder later set up the Flakaliden experiment on Norway spruce (P. abies) on podsolic sandy glacial
till in northern Sweden. It was laid out in 1986, with four treatments: I, irrigation; IL, irrigation with a
complete nutrient solution during the growing season; F, fertilization with solid fertilizer mix in early
June each year; and C, the no-irrigation, no-fertilization control. Linder (1995) said, in relation to the
Flakaliden experiment and others like it established in Sweden, that the principal aim of these
experiments was to eliminate water and mineral nutrients as growth-limiting factors, while avoiding
leaching of nutrients groundwater. The supply of mineral nutrients was adjusted annually to the
nutrient status of the trees and soil and, to optimize biomass production in a given climate, all essential
mineral nutrients were supplied at a rate adjusted to the current mineralization and fixation rates and
nutrient demand of the crop. The optimal proportion between nutrient elements was assumed to be
similar for all higher plants and defined in relation to nitrogen. Linder used the ratios of macro-
nutrients found in plants with free access to those nutrients as target values for foliage concentrations
in a long-term nutrition experiment in the Flakaliden experiment. These target values were changed as
new research gave better estimates of optimum nutrient ratios.
The special issue of the journal Forest Ecology and Management edited by Raison and Myers (1992)
published 14 papers dealing with results from the first 4 years of the BFG experiment, while Waterworth
et al. (2007) report on 20 years’ data, including the effects of thinning. The salient point about these
results is that, 4 years after the application of treatments to the BFG experiment, the IFþ treatment had a
stand volume increment of 55 m3 ha1 yr1, more than 2.5 times that of the control treatment
(20 m3 ha1 yr1). The irrigated (I) treatment reached about 33 m3 ha1 yr1 4 years after treatment
application, but then dropped to between 20 and 30 m3 ha1 yr1 for the next 15 years. The response to
nutrition illustrates the extent to which growth in that environment was limited by nutrients, once the
water limitation was alleviated. Waterworth et al. commented: ‘‘The continued positive interaction
between water and N in the IFþ stand 19 years after application of the N differs from the results of the
majority of fertilizer trials (unirrigated) in which the effect of single applications of N on stem growth is
usually lost after about 5 years (Snowdon 2002). This indicates ongoing enhancement of the N-cycle in
Nutrient Dynamics and Tree Growth
175

--- Page 189 ---

the IFþ stand. Greater mineralization of needle and root litter N through increased water availability is
likely, but continued internal recycling of N may also contribute to the sustained response’’.
In relation to the Flakaliden experiment in northern Sweden, Bergh et al. (1999) presented data
showing that, over a 10-year period, stand volume growth in the liquid fertilizer irrigated (IL) treatment
was higher than in the control (C) by a factor of 3.4. In a similar experiment in southern Sweden, stand
volume growth was higher by a factor of 2.2. On the basis of analysis of the concentrations of all nutrients
in the foliage of varying age at Flakaliden, sampled at different seasons over 7 years, Linder (1995)
concluded that the growth rates of stands (in that experiment) were not limited by carbon but by nutrient
availability, and that nutrient imbalances can be detected by calculating the ratios between elements.
The results outlined above provide dramatic illustrations of the extent to which nutrients can limit the
growth of trees in unfertilized soils, particularly where the water limitation is also removed. It is clear that
potential growth, as determined by environmental conditions such as available water, radiation and
temperature, can only be achieved when the supply rates of all the nutrients required by trees are high
enough to maintain the nutrient concentrations and ratios in foliage. However, the experiments that
demonstrated this, while they provided an immense amount of information about all aspects of tree
physiology, included treatments that were well outside the range likely to be used in practical forest
management and therefore could not provide fertilizer management guidelines for operational forestry.
The data provide information about the upper limits to tree growth rates – the rates that can be attained
under given radiation and temperature conditions, unconstrained by nutrients or water.
6.3 MODELLING SOIL NUTRIENT DYNAMICS
Chemical analyses of soils have been used for decades to provide information about soil fertility. Soils
may be characterized in terms of the nutrient concentrations per unit mass of soil, or the total mass of N
and P and exchangeable cations (e.g. Kþ, Mgþþ, Caþþ) per unit ground area. Such data, which may
include information about topsoil and sub-soil layers, as well as soil physical characteristics such as bulk
density and water-holding characteristics, are conventionally used to determine soil fertility. In the case
of Pand the exchangeable cations, there is little alternative to chemical analysis as a measure of soil fertility.
The main problem is to determine the relationship between some measure of the amount P in the soil, the
uptake of P by trees, and the likely response of trees to additions of given amounts of phosphorus in the
form of fertilizer. Smethurst (2000) provides a useful discussion of these issues.
In the case of nitrogen, mineralization - the process by which N is released into the soil by
decomposing organic matter - is the primary mechanism by which N is made available to forests.
Net primary production is often highly correlated with the net rate of nitrogen mineralization (see
Figure 6.5), which can be estimated from soil litter and humus decomposition rates. For this reason,
and because nitrogen is frequently the element that is most limiting for tree growth, N mineralization
models are the most advanced and useful tools for making quantitative estimates of soil fertility and
probable responses to fertilization. We describe here two of the models used for this purpose:
CENTURY is well established as a mechanistic, long-term predictor of C turnover, and to some extent
is useful for long-term predictions of soil total N status (e.g. Chilcott et al. 2007); SNAP was developed
as an empirical model based on mineralization rates determined from laboratory incubation adjusted
by soil temperature and moisture modifiers. Several decades of N mineralization research by numerous
researchers in Australia have been built into the SNAP model. Both models are used by modellers, but
neither is used for operational N fertilizer management decisions because the error of prediction of
specific responses to a fertilizer application is still too large. We also describe a commonly used
relationship for the uptake rate of a specific nutrient: nitrogen.
176
Chapter 6

--- Page 190 ---

6.3.1. CENTURY
CENTURY was originally developed by Parton and colleagues (Parton et al. 1987, 1988) to simulate
the water, carbon and nutrient budgets of grasslands. Its current version (Metherell et al. 1993) includes
plant production sub-models for grasslands, crops and forests, linked to a common SOM sub-model. It
is the SOM sub-model that is of interest to us here, and especially its handling of carbon and nitrogen
fluxes.
The SOM sub-model simulates the flow of C, N, P and S through plant litter, and organic and
inorganic pools in the soil. It divides the soil carbon into three pools: active, slow and passive. These
pools, respectively, correspond roughly to microbes and microbial products with a turnover time of up
to 4 years, plant material such as lignin and lignin-like components resistant to decomposition with a
turnover time of 20–50 years, and physically and chemically stabilized organic matter with a long
turnover time – probably in excess of 800 years.
Kirschbaum and Paul (2002) found CENTURY gave relatively poor results for forest soils. To improve
its performance for these soils they re-structured it by incorporating additional fine and coarse woody
litter pools, allowing the N content of SOM to vary, constraining N mineralization and immobilization to
the active SOM pool, incorporating a small flux of N to the resistant SOM pool, allowing mycorrhizal
uptake of N, and reformulating the effects of temperature and moisture on decomposition. The following
discussion of CENTURY pertains to the Kirschbaum and Paul (2002) version.
The decomposability of litter is classified on the basis of its lignin:N ratio. Lignin is highly resistant
to decomposition, and is technically termed ‘recalcitrant’. The ratios between metabolic and structural
litter are described by simple functions:
fm ¼ 0:85  0:018 NfL

Nutrient Dynamics and Tree Growth
177

--- Page 191 ---

As these materials decompose, there are flows of both C and N between pools, as well as respiratory
losses of CO2. Carbon flow between pools is controlled by an inherent maximum turnover coefficient,
modified by functions of water and temperature. Turnover coefficients are based on empirical monthly
constants. Leaf and fine root detritus are transferred into surface and fine root residue pools, and
allocated to structural and metabolic residues based on the lignin:N ratio of the residue material.
Woody components have specific decay rates, and the lignin and non-lignin components are trans-
ferred to the slow and active SOM pools, respectively. For full details see the diagram in Figure 2 in
Kirschbaum and Paul (2002).
Kirschbaum and Paul (2002) allowed the structural and metabolic C:N ratios to vary, provided the
proportionality r between these ratios is constant; they assumed r¼5. Hence, variations in the N
concentration of fresh litter were reflected in variation in the C:N ratio of both structural and metabolic
litter, i.e.
Nmet ¼
Ntot

litter pool, Ntot is the total N flux to litter, Cmet and Cstr are corresponding C fluxes, and r is the ratio of
the C:N ratio of the structural pool to that of the metabolic pool.
The decomposition rate Ds of structural litter is calculated as
Ds ¼ kfDTfDWebLsL;

decomposition rate. There is a critical C:N ratio RCN in the active pool that determines whether
nitrogen is mineralized or immobilized. The rates of N mineralization (Nmin) and immobilization
(Nimm) are given by

where PaN and PaC are amounts of N and C in active SOM pools, respectively. The flux Nmin is the flux
of N entering the mineral N pool, while the flux Nimm is the flux of N from the mineral pool to SOM.
There is also a relationship that describes active mycorrhizal N-uptake.
Mineralization or immobilization of N occurs to the extent necessary to maintain the C:N ratio in
the active pool at its critical value. Immobilization occurs when excess C is received from litter pools
with high C:N ratios. When the C:N ratio falls below the critical value (RCN) as a result of C losses, e.g.
as respiratory CO2, excess N is mineralized so that the C:N ratio of the active pool remains at the critical
value.
Kirschbaum and Paul (2002) parameterized their version of CENTURY using data from a number of
forest experiments. These data included information about litter types and properties, experimental
178
Chapter 6

--- Page 192 ---

measurements of litter decomposition and N-mineralization rates, and soil temperatures and water content.
The authors provided detailed assessment of the model’s performance, which was satisfactory, overall.
However, it is clear that CENTURY cannot be applied without a very considerable amount of
information. This includes the litter types on the forest floor and information about litter quality, as
well as appropriate values for the critical C:N ratio, for the parameter b in Eq. (6.21), for estimates of
mycorrhizal uptake and parameters in the temperature and water modifiers (see Section 6.3.2). Clearly
therefore, although CENTURY has been widely used and tested, it remains essentially a research tool.
However, examination of the equations given above, and the structure of this model, provides a good
illustration of the current state of knowledge about N-mineralization in forest soils.
6.3.2. SNAP
SNAP – Soil Nitrogen Availability Predictor – is an empirical model for nitrogen mineralization rate
developed by Paul et al. (2002) with the aim of producing ‘a simple and accurate predictive model that
can be used by both researchers and forest managers to predict nitrogen availability across a range of
forest sites’. SNAP was developed using data from 17 forest sites in Australia, encompassing various
eucalyptus species and P. radiata, and predicts seasonal and annual rates of net mineralization.
The mineralization rate Nbase of wet soil at a reference temperature of T0¼40 C was determined in
the laboratory as a basal reference. The effects of soil temperature Ts (C) and water content ys (g g1)
on mineralization rate were then taken into account by modifying the basal rate using multiplicative
modifiers fDT(Ts) and fDW(ry), respectively. Thus

SNAP ranged up to 100 kg N ha1 yr1, with a mean error of around 12 kg N ha1 yr1.
Nutrient Dynamics and Tree Growth
179

--- Page 193 ---

The model performed well against data independent of those from which it was derived. Paul et al.
(2002) verified the model using data from 9 native forests, 12 radiata pine plantations, and 12 eucalypt
plantations across southern Australia encompassing a wide range of soil types, climatic conditions and
management systems. Predicted annual nitrogen mineralization rates agreed closely (r20.76,
P<0.001) with the observed rates, irrespective of whether observed or predicted daily values of soil
temperature and water content were used. Paul et al. (2002) concluded that ‘‘SNAP was demonstrated
to be an easily implemented and cost-effective method to estimate daily, seasonal and annual field rates
of mineralization across a range of soils using a simple laboratory-based measurement of N-
mineralisation’’.
6.3.3. Modelling Nitrogen Uptake Rate
A relationship that has been applied in various forest growth models to calculate the uptake of
nitrogen from the soil solution assumes that N uptake is an active respiratory process which requires a
carbon substrate, and is inhibited by high levels of N in the roots. The uptake rate by a stand on a root-
surface area basis is assumed to be proportional to an effective soil nitrogen content that takes the
different species of soil nitrogen into account, and to some measure of root surface area, e.g. the fine
root dry matter content. Uptake is also affected by soil temperature. Figure 6.6 is a causal loop diagram
summarizing the main factors that influence nitrogen uptake by roots – but not factors that influence
root growth. If uptake of nitrogen increases the root N content such that [N] increases, then this
decreases subsequent uptake, i.e. there is a negative feedback loop controlling nitrogen uptake acting
through the nitrogen concentration in the root tissue. Whether this feedback acts depends on the
balance between uptake and translocation of N from the root to other parts of the plant, and on the
growth of new root biomass.
On the basis of these assumptions, Thornley and Johnson (1990) describe the nitrogen uptake rate
UN (kg N m2 day1) by

ratio (since KN	KC). If the response curves are plotted for a series of constant C substrate concentrations,
the nitrogen response declines to 0 with increasing N concentration.

6.4 A PRAGMATIC FERTILITY INDEX
The discussion in this chapter shows that the concept of site fertility is complex. It is the outcome of
many factors rooted in soil chemistry; it varies both seasonally and throughout the lifetime of a forest
stand as a consequence of changing climatic conditions; and it is affected by the withdrawal of nutrients
as a result of stand growth, and the subsequent recycling of nutrients following litterfall. Models that
could fully account for these various factors would be extremely complex. However, attempts have been
made to avoid these complexities by devising pragmatic site fertility indices, and we outline two of
these here.
6.4.1. A Fertility Index Based on Closed Canopy Leaf Area Index
The site productivity model ProMod (Battaglia and Sands 1997; and see Section 8.2.2f) predicts the
closed-canopy leaf area index of a stand from site factors such as mean annual temperature, a measure of
annual site water stress and a site nutrient availability index FS (see Section 4.4.3). The latter is a ranking
of sites on a scale of FS¼0 for sites with no fertility limitation to FS¼4 for very bad sites. In an
application of ProMod to plantations of Eucalyptus globulus in Tasmania, this FS was assigned on the basis
of a site classification scheme (Grant et al. 1995) that uses a soil classification based on the geology of the
site and mean annual rainfall as inputs. For example, on the basis of this classification, the index of FS¼4
was assigned to poor sites with sandy soils supporting vegetation dominated by heaths. Such schemes are
rather subjective as the effects of recent land use, e.g. improved pastures, have to be included.
An alternative, objective derivation of FS can be based on specific data obtained from an analysis of the
soil chemistry at sites. For instance, Rumley (1999) examined the closed-canopy leaf area index (L) of 14
Eucalyptus nitens plantations, some of which were probably severely limited by nutrient availability, and
compared their observed L with a number of soil factors such as nitrate, ammonium and phosphate
concentrations and SOM content. If the site temperature and soil water availability are taken into account
using the approach described in Section 4.3.3 the variation of L cannot be explained. However, a potential
nutrient leaf area index modifier LN can be calculated as the ratio of observed L to the L predicted on the
basis of temperature and water availability alone. Examination of the dependence of LN on the soil
chemistry data then suggested a relationship between LN and site [NO3] of the form
LN NO3

where NSc is the concentration at which nitrate ceases to be limiting, and b is a parameter. This is shown
in Figure 6.8, where NSc¼2 and b¼0.25, and 84% of the site variation in relative LAI is now explained.
This example illustrates how empirical site nutrient indices could, in principle, be based on observed
data. However, a large number of observations are required, and care must be taken to ensure that
correlations between observed soil chemistry data are taken into account. Typical soil analysis calibra-
tions are of the same form, e.g. relative yield in relation to the concentration of nutrients in soil or
foliage. Relationships of this type could, in principle, be incorporated into the 3-PG model (Landsberg
and Waring 1997; also Chapter 9).
182
Chapter 6

--- Page 196 ---

6.4.2. The 3-PG Fertility Rating
The 3-PG model described by Landsberg and Waring (1997), and discussed later in this book
(Chapter 9), characterizes site fertility through a simple fertility rating FR that varies from 0 for the
most nutrient limited sites up to 1 for sites at which nutrients are not limiting growth. The effects of site
fertility on net primary production are taken into account by assuming that light use efficiency declines
with declining FR, while biomass allocation to roots is assumed to increase with declining FR. Although
these simple assumptions lead to realistic simulations of stand growth under a wide range of conditions,
no simple objective means for assigning FR on the basis of soil chemistry has been available.
The problem of how to assign site fertility indices for use with 3-PG has been a widely raised issue.
Generally, FR has been assigned using more or less subjective means, based in part on soil chemistry
and in part on knowledge of growth on similar sites, or of previous crops or stands on the site in
question. If a reliable set of parameters for the species in question is available for sites that are not
nutrient limited, then we can in principle use the model to determine FR for other sites simply by
varying it so as to obtain a best fit of predicted stand growth to that observed at the site. However, we
emphasize that this cannot in any way constitute a validation of the model: it is simply an application of
a model that is already assumed to be valid. In principle, 3-PG (or any otherwise validated model that
requires some simple site fertility index) could be applied in this way to determine the fertility indices
of a wide range of sites. These indices could then be examined in relation to the detailed soil chemistry
of each site, and from this, hopefully, some useful empirical relationship would emerge between readily
observed characteristics of soil chemistry and FR. This was done by Fontes et al. (2006).
There are two important issues related to this pragmatic approach to the characterization of site
fertility in models such as 3-PG. The first is whether the fertility index determined by applying the
model with one species at a site, can be used at the same site but for a different species or management
regime. The second is whether the manner in which FR is used in the model does indeed reflect the
response of sites to changes in site nutritional status.
With respect to the first issue, we emphasize that FR is intended to be a property of a site and hence
must be the same for all species. If applications of the model at a single site for different species suggest
different FR, then there must be species-specific differences in those parameters in the model that
characterize its response to site fertility that have not yet been taken into account.

Nutrient Dynamics and Tree Growth
183

--- Page 197 ---

The second issue has been examined by various authors for various species. A critical analysis was
made by Stape et al. (2004) using paired plots in Eucalyptus plantations across 40 sites that captured
regional differences in soil and productivity. Each member of each pair had the same age and similar
initial woody biomass, but one was fertilized to eliminate any nutrient limitations on growth. 3-PG was
first parameterized using complete carbon balance data from irrigated plots, and then validated against
growth data from the paired plots. Of the two growing seasons of this study, one was very wet, the other
normal. A fertilizer response (FER, t ha1) that measures the response of a site to fertilization was
determined from the observed growth data for the wet years. 3-PG was also applied to the paired sites,
and the value of FR adjusted so the predicted wood increment matched the observed increment in the
wet years. When these FR values were compared with the observed FER, it was found that FR1 for
sites with no fertilizer response (FER0), and FR declined with increasing FER.
This study demonstrated that the 3-PG fertility index FR, and the manner in which site fertility
effects are included in 3-PG, enable it to predict observed responses to site fertility. A plot of observed
versus predicted wood biomass increments gave high r2 values (0.83 for the fertilized plots, 0.71 for
control plots) but the points did not fall on a 1:1 line, suggesting possible errors in parameters.
Stape et al. (2004) also examined the relationship between FER and various attributes of soil
chemistry. They constructed a soil fertilizer response index (SFRI) from a linear combination of
extractable potassium, phosphorous and cation exchange capacity in the upper soil layer that explained
56% of the observed variation in FER. However, it is not known if this SFRI relationship is general.

7.5 Models Including Storage
206
7.5.1. Tissue Water Storage
208
7.5.2. Models Based on Pools and Resistances
209
7.5.3. Stem Hydraulic Conductivity and Its Implications
213
7.6 Water Relations of Stands
214
7.6.1. Quantitative Measures of Water Stress
215
7.6.2. Consequences of Water Stress
216
7.7 Concluding Remarks
218
Water is a controlling factor in the growth of forests; indeed, forests do not occur in low rainfall
regions of the world. The water balance of stands depends on precipitation, interception, run-off,
evaporation and drainage; with the exception of precipitation all these processes are strongly influ-
enced by tree populations, stand structure and canopy architecture (Landsberg and Gower 1997).
Physiological Ecology of Forest Production
Joe Landsberg and Peter Sands
ISSN 1936-7961, DOI: 10.1016/S1936-7961(11)04007-3
Copyright # 2011 Elsevier Inc.
All rights reserved.

--- Page 199 ---

The availability of water in the soil at any time, interacting with the evaporative demand of the
atmosphere and the hydraulic capacity of the trees, determines canopy conductance and the ability
of the trees to absorb CO2 for photosynthesis.
Tree–water relations are an excellent and well-documented example of processes at different levels
with different response times. We discussed in Chapter 3 leaf energy balance and its interaction
with stomata. Response times to changes in leaf energy balance are of the order of seconds, leading
to rapid changes in the transpiration rate of individual leaves. When water is lost by transpiration
from the mesophyll cells adjacent to the sub-stomatal cavities of a leaf it is replaced by water moving
from surrounding tissue, in turn replaced by water moving into the leaf tissue from the plant’s
conducting system. Changes in the water content of tissue cause changes in tissue water potential,
resulting in potential gradients through the plant and from the soil to the roots. These gradients
provide the driving force for water uptake from the soil and movement through plants. The relations
between rates of transpiration and rates of water uptake and movement through the soil–plant system,
across the frictional resistances to flow through the system, therefore determine the water status
of plants.
The major advances in understanding and predicting tree–water relations in recent years have
come from the recognition, driven largely by the work of John Sperry, that the hydraulic conduc-
tivity of plants declines as plant water potential declines. As the availability of water in the soil to
the root system declines, there is an increasing risk that the xylem conduits in the stems will
cavitate (become air-filled), so that the flow of water is stopped. It follows from this that, to avoid
catastrophic failure of the hydraulic system, there must be a limit to the transpiration rate
determined by a minimum leaf water potential at which stomata will close to prevent further
water loss (see, inter alia, Tyree and Sperry 1988; Sperry 1995, 2000; Sperry et al. 1998, 2002). A
considerable literature has been generated by this work, and we cannot hope to review it here nor
would it be useful to try to do so. However, in the water relations section of this chapter
(Section 7.6) we summarise the main points and indicate the implications for forest modelling
of the hydraulic limitation hypothesis.
The hydrology of forest ecosystems is important not only because of the interactions between the
soil water balance and tree growth but also because of the importance of catchments as water supply
systems. In many countries important water supply catchments are forested. With increasing pressure
on the world’s fresh water supplies for direct human consumption, irrigation and industry, the need
to improve our ability to maximize the amount of rainfall collected in catchments is becoming ever
more urgent. As a consequence, there have been a number of studies on the hydrology of forest
catchments and comparisons of the water yield from forested and non-forested catchments (see
review by Bosch and Hewlett 1982). Calder (2007) examined the impact and implications of forests –
particularly plantations – on the water yield of catchments in a wide range of countries, for which he
provided quite detailed analysis in terms of the yields provided, or reduced, by forests. No clear
picture emerged, but the widespread perception that forests are always beneficial and add value to the
land (they may contribute to flood and erosion control as well as providing timber) is not always
supported by the evidence. Water yields from catchments may be reduced, and while forests are
generally considered to improve water quality, this is not always the case. Calder recommends the use
of geographical information system (GIS) based models to analyse the situation in any area of
interest. Brown et al. (2007) used such a model to analyse the likely impact of plantations on the
water yield of a large (regional scale) catchment in south-east Australia, and Dunin et al. (2007)
presented an interesting assessment of the hydrological consequences of land use change in Australia.
They focussed on the different rates of water use by plant communities of different leaf area and
physical structure.
186
Chapter 7

--- Page 200 ---

We are not concerned, in this book, with the water yield of catchments – forested or otherwise – but
with the water balance of a volume of soil supporting trees. Any attempt to model or explain
the growth of forests must include calculations of the soil water balance, which must take account
of the soil moisture holding characteristics and the capacity of soils to supply the water needed for
tree growth. Therefore, in the first part of this chapter we provide an outline of the hydrological
balance and its components, including consideration of water in root zones and water movement in
soils. We then consider tree–water relations and conclude with a brief discussion of the consequences of
water stress.
7.1 THE HYDROLOGICAL BALANCE
The amount of water in a given depth of soil at any given time depends on the balance between water
input by rainfall and losses through run-off, drainage, evaporation of intercepted radiation, soil
evaporation, transpiration by trees and the understory integrated over previous times. In addition,
the net hydrological balance of a catchment requires consideration of the lateral movement of water.
These various processes and their interplay are illustrated in Figure 7.1 and summarised in the equation
of hydraulic balance. This equation is given below as Eq. (7.1) and expresses, essentially, that water is a
conserved quantity: the change in the content of water in a given mass of soil is the difference between
the amount of water added and the amount loss.
7.1.1. Equation of the Hydrological Balance
Two measures are commonly used for specifying the amount of water in soil. The first is the actual
amount of water Ws beneath a unit area of ground down to a specified depth, which can be root depth,
the depth to some impermeable layer, or simply some nominal depth. The amount Ws is usually
expressed as the mass per unit ground area (kg m2), or as the equivalent depth (mm) of water; these
two units are numerically equivalent. The second measure is the volumetric soil water content ys.
This is the volume of water contained in unit volume of soil and has units m3 m3 or mm m1,
where 1 m3 m3¼1000 mm m1. If the depth of soil associated with an amount Ws (mm) of water is
zs (mm), then ys¼Ws /zs where ys has units m3 m3.
This basic equation of the hydrological balance describes the conservation of the amount Ws (mm)
of water in the soil profile over some time interval Dt:
Ws t þ Dt
ð
Þ ¼ Ws tð Þ þ R  IR  ET  ES þ qL  qD  qR;
ð7:1Þ
where R is the amount of rainfall in the time interval of concern, IR is the rainfall intercepted by the
canopy and subsequently evaporated, ET is the transpiration by trees and understory, ES is the
evaporation from the soil, while qL is the net lateral flow of water into the soil volume, qD the amount
of water which drains out of the root zone, and qR the amount of surface run-off. The units of all
quantities in Eq. (7.1) are the same, i.e. depth of water (mm) or mass of water per unit ground area
(kg m2); these are numerically equivalent. How we quantify the various components of the hydro-
logical or soil water balance appearing in Figure 7.1 and Eq. (7.1) is discussed in more detail in
Section 7.2.
If zs (mm) is the depth of the soil profile, then ys¼Ws/zs and we may re-arrange Eq. (7.1) to estimate
changes in soil moisture content:

ð
Þ ¼ ys tð Þ þ R  IR  ET  ES þ qL  qD  qR
zs

ð7:2Þ
From Eq. (7.2) we see that, given a starting value for ys(t), knowledge of rooting depth and rainfall,
interception loss, soil evaporation and transpiration data we could, at least conceptually, calculate ys for
any subsequent time. To give a simple example, if ys(t)¼0.25 m3 m3 and zs¼1.5 m, then
Ws¼0.251500¼375 mm and if R¼15, IR¼4, ET¼15, ES¼8 (all mm) and qL, qD and qR are all 0,
then ys(tþDt)¼0.25þ(154158)/1500¼0.242 mm m1, and the corresponding Ws¼363 mm.
Calculations of the soil water balance, using rainfall data, evaporation and transpiration rates, and
estimates of run-off and drainage, can easily be made over hourly intervals. However, although
calculations of transpiration rates can be checked by physiological and micrometeorological

FIGURE 7.1 Illustration of the various processes that determine the hydrological balance of a stand or catchment. The amount of water in a
given depth of soil at any location, at any given time, depends on the balance between water input by rainfall and losses through run-off,
drainage, evaporation of intercepted rainfall, soil evaporation and transpiration by trees and understory. Water in the root zone is removed by
uptake through transpiration and replenished through flow into the root zone from wetter soil. If a water table is present, it can supply water
to the soil above it via capillary rise, and drainage may in turn replenish the water table. Lateral flow represents the horizontal movement of
water between different points in the landscape.
188
Chapter 7

--- Page 202 ---

measurements, soil–water balance calculations cannot be checked by direct measurement over periods
of less than 1–2 weeks because of the variability in forest ecosystems. This is probably the realistic
interval to use for evaluating the effects of water status on growth patterns and productivity at the stand
level.
7.1.2. Quantifying Soil Water Content
A soil profile cannot hold water beyond a certain limit. The saturated water content Wsat (mm) is
the value of Ws at which the soil is saturated and any water added at the surface simply runs off. If the
water content is below saturation and above what is called field capacity (Wfc, mm), water in the profile
drains freely under the influence of gravity. At water contents below field capacity drainage is
negligible, but water can be lost by plant uptake through transpiration. Another important character-
istic of a soil profile is the wilting point Wwp (mm). This somewhat old-fashioned but still useful,
concept is defined as the soil water content below which plants can no longer withdraw water from the
soil. However, the soil can dry further through other processes such as evaporation from the surface.
If plants experience a prolonged period where the available water is below the wilting point, they lose
their turgor and die. The critical soil water contents Wsat, Wfc and Wwp depend on the depth and
stoniness of the profile and on physical properties of the soil such as its texture and porosity, which
depend on the proportions of sand, clay and loam and organic matter content.
The volumetric soil water contents corresponding to saturation, field capacity and wilting point are
denoted by ysat, yfc and ywp, respectively. Field capacity is often defined to occur when the average soil
water potential (see Section 7.3.1) in the root zone is about 33 kPa (0.03 MPa), while an estimate of
the wilting point is provided by the value of ys at which soil water potential in the root zone is
1500 kPa (1.5 MPa). This is only an approximation to the lower limit of available water, but in view
of the errors involved there is little point in making too many complex calculations to try to estimate
ywp. The amount of water available to the plant community is WsWwp, or (ysywp)zs where zs (mm)
is the depth of the profile. If the soil contains stones, these amounts must of course be reduced by the
volume of the profile occupied by stones.
The so-called pedotransfer functions are used to determine water contents at saturation, field
capacity and wilting point (i.e. ysat, yfc and ywp) and other important but sometimes hard-to-measure
properties of soils in terms of more readily available characteristics of soil texture. Authors such as
McKenzie and Cresswell (2002) and Saxton and Rawls (2006) express a range of soil properties as
empirical relationships in terms of measures such as the percentage of clay, silt and sand in the soil.
Examples of the values of ysat, yfc and ywp, and other properties introduced later, were derived from
software made available by Saxton (2009) and are shown in Table 7.1 for a range of soil types.
7.2 COMPONENTS OF THE HYDROLOGICAL BALANCE
We now consider in turn each of the component processes in the hydrological balance as expressed in
Figure 7.1 and Eq. (7.1). Some processes will be considered in detail, while we only touch on others.
Further detail is available in texts such as Hillel (1998). Our emphasis is on those processes that directly
involve or affect the plant or stand.
The lateral flow, drainage and run-off terms in Eq. (7.1), i.e. qL, qD and qR, will not be considered in
any detail, although they may be of considerable importance in the water balance of a particular site.
Between rain events, if the run-off and drainage terms in the hydrological equation can be taken to be

zero, the water balance of a site is determined by transpiration through plants, the loss of intercepted

In many cases transpiration and soil evaporation are estimated jointly and called evapotranspiration.
7.2.1. Transpiration
We discussed transpiration in some detail in Chapter 2 and canopy transpiration in particular in
Section 2.5.3. Here, we simply summarise the salient results for determining the transpiration ET of a
stand for use in the hydrological balance. The Penman–Monteith equation for the rate of canopy

7.2.2. Rainfall Interception
There have been quite extensive studies of rainfall interception by canopies and the subsequent
reduction in effective rainfall. As we will see, there is large variation in the fraction of rainfall that is
lost due to the evaporation of intercepted rainfall. We also discuss simple ways in which this can be
modelled.
Hydrology and Plant Water Relations
191

--- Page 205 ---

a) Observations of Rainfall Interception
Not all rainfall is effective in replacing soil water because some is intercepted by the foliage and
subsequently evaporated. Doley (1981) reviewed many empirical studies of interception loss (IR) in
relation to the amount of rainfall (R) and discussed the considerable differences observed in various
forest types: IR was anywhere between 5 and 60% of R. McNaughton and Jarvis (1983) also reviewed
available data and gave IR as 10–55% of total annual R, depending on canopy LAI, crown structure,
species and climate. More recently, Granier et al. (2000) found that interception in beech forests, with
LAI of about 5, was about 25% of the amount of rain falling in each event. Myers and Talsma (1992)
measured interception in a Pinus radiata plantation and found average interception decreased from
55% of R during events of less than 5 mm to less than 10% during events greater than 40 mm. Almeida
et al. (2007) measured average interception losses of about 10% of R, with no significant variation over
the 6 years of their study although LAI varied from less than 2 to more than 4.
For dense canopies throughfall may be very low until the canopy saturates. In most cases some water
will reach the ground after the start of a rain event and before the canopy is completely saturated
because of drip and (possibly) stemflow. The amount will depend on the nature of the canopy,
branches and stems and of the precipitation. When raindrops are large, rain splash from rigid surfaces
(e.g. branches or leathery leaves) can contribute to throughfall. The direct throughfall component
of light, fine rain is likely to be smaller – perhaps much smaller – than that of heavy rain with
most of the water in large drops. Furthermore, light rain will wet canopy surfaces more evenly, and
in low wind speeds there will be less mechanical displacement of drops and hence less drip than in
heavy rain.
Interception losses will be much larger when rainfall is intermittent than when it falls continuously
for relatively long periods. This is illustrated by the hypothetical situation where each of a series of
rainfall events provides enough water to almost saturate the canopy, while the interval between events
is long enough for all the water on the canopy to evaporate. In this case effective rainfall is much less
than if the same amount of rain fell continuously.
The rate of evaporation of water from wet canopies depends mainly on the atmospheric vapour
pressure deficit, and on wind speed, and is not controlled by stomatal conductance. The effectiveness of
wind depends on the aerodynamic properties of the canopy – which depend on its height, openness
and foliage density (see Section 2.5.1). Evaporation rates from wet canopies are roughly the same as
transpiration rates (McNaughton and Jarvis 1983). Evaporation from the upper layers of multi-layered
canopies will be comparable to that of single-layered canopies but, because of the high humidity and
low wind speeds in the understory (see Section 2.3), evaporation from lower layers may be negligible.
All the above considerations indicate why water losses by canopy interception vary greatly in
different situations and also indicate that, in plant ecological work, detailed study will be necessary
to characterize water balance of particular habitats. Interception loss has often been described (e.g.
Gash 1979) by linear empirical relationships with storm size (total rain per storm). Such studies may
provide useful estimates only for ‘‘broad-scale’’ calculations, but for detailed hydrological work, or
studies on plant growth patterns and distribution, interception must be analysed in terms of the
basic processes affecting interception losses. A fraction of the incident rain is transmitted directly
through the canopy, and of that intercepted by the canopy only some is actually detained on the leaves
because leaves can only detain a certain amount of water (e.g. equivalent to a liquid layer up to
0.25 mm thick), with any additional water immediately shed. Water detained on leaves evaporates
essentially at the prevailing rate for a wet surface. However, leaves may not be uniformly wet, and if
a portion of the leaf area is dry, this reduces the overall evaporation rate from the canopy. Finally,
the frequency, intensity and duration of rainfall events will be key factors influencing interception
losses.
192
Chapter 7

--- Page 206 ---

b) Models of Rainfall Interception
Various authors have proposed or reviewed rainfall interceptions models; for examples see Aston
(1979), Shuttleworth (1989), Jiagang (1988) and Anzhi et al. (2005). The simplest approach is to treat
the canopy as a single moisture store filled by incoming rainfall and draining at a rate expressed as a
function of its proportional fill, and from which water is lost through evaporation back into the
atmosphere. Such a model was implemented by Rutter et al. (1971, 1975) and simplified by Gash
(1979). Landsberg and Gower (1997, pp. 94–96) provide a summary of this model. Accurate modelling
of rainfall interception can require considerable detail in the form of the canopy and the nature of
rainfall events.
Fortunately, simple models suffice for long-term water balance studies and, when modelling
interception aggregated over extended periods, very simple models of interception have been used.
For instance, in the original 3-PG model (Landsberg and Waring 1997), interception IR increases
linearly with canopy leaf area index (L) up to a maximum and is subsequently independent of L. This is
expressed by
IR ¼ iRx minf1; L=LRxgR;
ð7:6Þ
where R is the total above-canopy rainfall over some extended period, e.g. a month, iRx is the maximum
interception fraction, and LRx is the canopy L at which interception is maximised. Typical parameter
values are iRx0.15 and LRx3.
A second simple model is based on discrete rainfall events and takes into account some of the
processes discussed above. It assumes a canopy stores water on its leaves as a layer of moisture of
uniform thickness up to a maximum thickness of twx (mm) and any water remaining at the end of a
rainfall event evaporates. The fraction fR of rainfall R that is direct throughfall is assumed to decline
with increasing canopy leaf area index L. By analogy with Beer’s law for the transmission of light
fR L

ð7:7Þ
where kR is the rainfall transmission coefficient. For example, if kR¼0.1 throughfall is 75% for a canopy
with L¼3. It can then be shown that the amount of rainfall lost by evaporation from the foliage during
a rain fall event of amount R (mm) lasting a time TR (h) is
IR ¼ min

These equations can be readily generalised to situations where the canopy does not completely dry
between events. The model represented by Eq. (7.8) predicts that the fraction of rainfall (i.e. IR/R) lost
through canopy interception and subsequent evaporation is constant (i.e. 1fR) for low rainfall and
then declines as 1/R for higher rainfall. This is illustrated in Figure 7.2 where the dependence of the
fraction of rain lost through canopy interception is shown as a function of total rainfall for each of a
number of canopy leaf areas, and for each of a number of rainfall event lengths, i.e. intensities.
Hydrology and Plant Water Relations
193

--- Page 207 ---

7.2.3. Redistribution of Rainfall
Rainfall may be redistributed by vegetation so that, although the hydrological equation may describe
the average situation over relatively large, homogenous areas, it will not be accurate on the microscale.
As a result, soil heterogeneity and the variable distribution of vegetation in a stand may result in
considerable variation in soil moisture content over small areas.
The distribution of the unintercepted component of rainfall obviously depends on canopy homoge-
neity. Water intercepted by the canopy and not evaporated either coalesces into large droplets which drip
off a multitude of low points (leaf tips, twigs, etc.) in the canopy, or it reaches the ground as stemflow.
Drips may or may not be evenly distributed, depending on canopy type. Stemflow will inevitably cause
gross distortion of the soil wetting patterns that would have occurred from uninterrupted rainfall. For
example, if rainfall is heavy enough to cause significant stemflow, the soil at the base of the dominant
trees of a canopy may be considerably wetter than that around the base of subdominants.
Stemflow may begin early in a rain event but does not commence until the streaks of water collecting
on the trunks of trees from the lower side of branches are established as continuous flow lines. Doley
(1981) gives a table of data characterizing the partitioning of precipitation of tropical forests and
woodlands. Stemflow has been observed to vary between 0% and 39% of the total rain in a storm. As in
the case of interception losses, such data are of limited value as a guide to what might be observed in
any particular situation because stemflow amounts depend so strongly on the physical characteristics of
canopies and on rainfall characteristics. However, many of the studies cited by Doley suggest that
stemflow may be treated as a constant fraction of total precipitation, and a mean value of about 1%
would be representative for many subtropical forest types.
7.2.4. Soil Evaporation
Many observations show that evaporation of water from wet soil initially proceeds at a high rate
approximating that of open water and determined by the energy available to vaporise water in soil the
surface layer. As the surface layer dries the evaporation rate decreases due to the inability of capillary rise to

maintain a wet surface layer and an increase in the resistance to the movement of liquid or gaseous water
towards the surface. This is the basis for the so-called two-phase Ritchie model (Ritchie 1972). In fact, this
empirical model can be derived from physical principles after some simple approximations (see below).
Soil evaporation could also be modelled using the Penman–Monteith equation with surface
conductance declining strongly as the surface layer dries. Equation (7.3) can be adapted for this
purpose provided gC is the conductance gCs for water vapour through the soil surface, and boundary
layer conductance gb is that for the boundary layer of air at the soil surface. When the soil surface is
saturated, the conductance gCs is large, often assumed to be infinite, but gCs declines and may become
quite small as the soil surface dries. Also, the radiation term ’na is replaced by the net radiation ’ns
incident on the soil surface, taking into account the possible shading by any canopy above the soil.
In the first phase of the Ritchie model (Ritchie 1972) (i.e. immediately after a wetting event) the
daily rate eS of soil evaporation (kg m2 d1) is constant and assumed to be given by the potential rate
of evaporation. The second phase is assumed to commence once accumulated evaporation ES (kg m2)
exceeds an amount ES1, and the rate of evaporation is then assumed to decline with increasing
evaporation accumulated above that in phase 1.
If the rate eS is required to be a continuous function of ES, it can be written as

To implement this model eS1 is set equal to wet surface evaporation, either from observed meteoro-
logical data or calculated using the Eq. (7.3) applied to a wet surface. In the latter case, surface
conductance gC is infinite and attenuation of radiation by any canopy above the soil is taken into
account. The parameters ES1 and ES2 are properties of the soil and hence determined by soil structure.
Choudhury and Monteith (1988) performed a detailed theoretical study of the heat budget of land
surfaces. A similar approachwas taken by Zhang etal.(1996).Choudhury and Monteith used the Penman–
Monteith equation to predict soil evaporation and assumed that as the soil dries surface evaporation is
limited by diffusion through the upper, dry layer. They also assumed that until the depth dS of the dry layer
exceeds a critical value dS1, the surface conductance gCs (m s1) in the Penman–Monteith equation is
infinite. This is phase 1 evaporation as given by Eq. (7.10). They expressed ES1 in terms of the depth dS1 and

7.2.5. Run-Off and Drainage
Run-off from a non-saturated soil occurs when the rate of water input (application) exceeds the
infiltration capacity. A rough measure of infiltration capacity is given by the saturated hydraulic
conductivity of the surface layers, and depends on the soil texture and structure, and preceding soil
moisture contents, e.g. whether or not an impervious crust has formed. Infiltration is highest in loose
sandy soils, whereas it is low in clay or loam soils. Run-off will always occur when water is applied to
saturated soil, regardless of the application rate. Run-off from forests is usually relatively slow and is
generally only significant when the upper layers are truly saturated. The run-off characteristics of soil
under trees and covered with litter may be very different from those of soil exposed to the sun in open
canopies. With litter on the ground, and countless barriers to the movement of water across the surface,
the capacity of tree-covered areas to accept water is usually high. This is one of the reasons why water
supplies from forested catchments are usually stable.
Drainage includes both vertical and lateral flow in the soil and is likely to be significant when the
root zone is saturated and soil there cannot hold more water. Such losses are difficult to measure and
will not be discussed further, but we note a paper by Almeida et al. (2007) which reports a thorough
study of the water balance of a Eucalyptus plantation in Brazil, including measurements of ys and qR.
Over a 6-year period run-off was only about 3% of total precipitation. Almeida et al. found that values
of ys estimated using a detailed, daily time step model (Soares and Almeida 2001) which included
calculations of water movement between layers in the soil were (perhaps not surprisingly) more
accurate than estimates calculated using the 3-PG model (Landsberg and Waring 1997). 3-PG uses a
monthly time step and only a single soil root zone layer, although in Section 9.5.5 we discuss how this
simple water balance model can be improved while still using monthly data. Note that the choice of
model for this type of calculation depends on the accuracy required and the input data available.
7.3 WATER IN SOILS AND THE ROOT ZONE
Most soils are extremely variable spatially, and the depth of layers that can be considered homogeneous
also tends to vary within quite short distances (say, a few metres). Obtaining accurate estimates of soil
water content at specified levels is therefore difficult, particularly in forest soils where there are root
196
Chapter 7

--- Page 210 ---

channels of varying size and irregular distribution, which complicate throughflow and drainage
patterns. This adds to the complexity introduced by the patchy distribution of rainfall coming through
canopies where some of it is intercepted.
A further complication to studying, let alone predicting, water movement in soils and water
uptake by plants, is the relationship between water in soils and uptake by roots following
transpiration by plants. Roots themselves are highly variable in their extent and distribution
within the soil profile, and their distribution is in a feedback relationship with water distribution in
the soil.
For these reasons the best that can be done when studying water in the root zone, in most situations,
is to deal with properties averaged over relatively large areas. In this section we make the assumption
that the soil profile and root zone can be treated as more or less homogenous entities. However, it is
common practice, and relatively easy, to regard the soil profile as a series of horizontally homogeneous
layers and take into account movement of water between layers.
7.3.1. The Soil Water Potential
We begin this exploration of water in soils, and in particular in the root zone, with a discussion of an
important quantity characterising the movement of water in soils and plants: the water potential.
Further information is provided in texts such as Hillel (1998).
As we have seen, the flux of some substance is usually expressed in terms of the gradient of the
concentration of that substance. A possible candidate for water in soil is the volumetric soil water
content ys, as it has the dimensions of a concentration (amount of water per unit volume of soil).
However, consider two adjacent parcels with identical ys. If these are on the same horizontal level we
would expect no net flow of water between them, but if they are arranged vertically, water drains from
one to the other under the force of gravity. Clearly, the difference in volumetric soil water content alone
does not determine the flux of water.
The appropriate variable to use is the so-called water potential. This is defined as the energy per unit
volume and is a measure of the capacity of water at a point to do work, compared to the work capacity
of pure free water (Slatyer 1967). Since water potential gradients indicate different energy states, it is
fundamental to the analysis of water movement through the soil–plant system. The water potential is
usually denoted by c, and expressed as a negative value relative to pure free water, for which c¼0.
Based on its definition as energy per unit volume, the units of c are dimensionally equivalent to
pressure (J m3¼N m2¼Pa), and plant and soil water potentials are conventionally expressed in kPa
or MPa.
Total water potential includes contributions from various sources. In the case of plant water
potential these are osmotic (i.e. chemical) and turgor components, and soil water potential includes
osmotic and matric components (i.e. forces such as surface tension at liquid–air interfaces and
adsorption of water on surfaces within the matrix of soil particles). If water at different heights is
involved, e.g. when we consider the water potential within columns of conducting (xylem) tissue, the
gravitational potential energy of the water is included.
Differences in the water potential of plant tissues, or between points in the soil or between soil and
roots, determine the direction in which water will move and the amount of work that must be done to
move water from points with higher to points with lower potentials. Movement will be from regions or
tissues of higher (less negative) to lower potentials.
Hydrology and Plant Water Relations
197

--- Page 211 ---

7.3.2. Root Distribution and Soil–Root Resistance
The effectiveness of water uptake by roots depends on the extent to which the soil is explored by roots
(defined by the root length per unit volume, LRV; see Section 4.5.1), contact between roots and soil and
the water potential gradient between roots and soil. This gradient depends on the rate of water loss
from the plant through transpiration, and the moisture content and hydraulic characteristics of the
soil. Estimation of the depth of soil effectively exploited by roots is a matter of paramount importance
in relation to modelling plant water relations and their effects on tree or stand growth; we discussed in
Section 4.5 factors affecting the mass of roots present under forest communities.
Fine roots will, to a significant extent, determine the effective soil hydraulic conductivity because a
high density of fine roots ensures that the path length for water flow from the soil to the nearest root is
always short. In that case, when bulk soil hydraulic conductivity falls to very low values water can still
move to the roots along the potential gradient between the root surfaces and the soil, fast enough to
provide a useful contribution to the transpiration stream even if roots have extracted most of the water
in their immediate vicinity–thus the effective conductivity is higher than bulk conductivity.
The resistance to flow from soil to roots depends on root length, soil wetness and hydraulic
properties, and the contact between roots and soil. At one time it was considered that the main source
of resistance was the reduction in soil hydraulic conductivity caused by water extraction and the
consequent dry zones formed around the roots, as conductivity declines in dry soils. However, the
consensus that has emerged from theoretical and experimental studies is that resistance to flow through
roots themselves may be the limiting factor when soil is wet, while low soil hydraulic conductivity
becomes increasingly important as soil dries.
A further factor which may be important is root–soil contact – the length of root in close contact
with the soil may decrease as soil dries (for an experiment with trees see Dosskey and Ballard 1980).
Root–soil contact may be poor simply because of the heterogeneous nature of soils and the fact that
roots tend to grow preferentially down paths of least resistance – such as cracks and holes – where root–
soil contact may be intermittent. In their analysis of water movement through plant roots and the
source of resistances to flow Landsberg and Fowkes (1978) identified ‘‘effective root length’’ as a factor
in root resistance, and Herkelrath et al. (1977) incorporated this into a comprehensive uptake model.
Sperry et al. (1998) showed that the rhizosphere is the limiting point for the flux of water through the
plant–soil system in species resistant to cavitation in coarse-textured soils when the root surface to leaf
area ratio is low, but the evidence suggests that most plants are limited by the conducting capacity of
xylem.
On the macro-scale of the root systems of plant communities in the field, when the soil is wet water
uptake is most rapid where root systems are most dense (cf. the point made earlier in relation to fine
root density). However, in the absence of rain, the usual higher density of roots near the surface of the
soil leads to rapid drying of the surface layers where root concentrations are highest, so that water
content falls, and hence so do the water potential and hydraulic conductance (see Eqs. (7.15) and
(7.16)) and the potential difference needed to extract water from those layers increases. The extraction
zones then tend to move downwards to regions where the soil is wetter, although root density in those
regions may be lower.
7.3.3. Movement of Water in Soil
Water in the soil moves down water potential gradients, just as in plants. Soil water potential cs (Pa) is
the sum of gravitational, matric and osmotic potentials. The matric potential cm of soil, a very
important component of cs, arises through adhesive molecular forces (e.g. capillary and electrostatic)
198
Chapter 7

--- Page 212 ---

between water molecules and the solid constituents of soil, and is a function of particle size and
arrangement. The gravitational potential cg is the gravitational potential energy of the water per unit
volume. Thus cg¼grwh, where g¼9.8 m s2 is the acceleration due to gravity, rw¼1000 kg m3 is
the density of water and h (m) is the height, or gravitational head, of the water relative to some
reference height.
Volumetric soil water content ys (m3 m3) and water potential cs (Pa) are related by soil moisture
characteristic curves such as those shown in Figure 7.3a. These curves can be described over much of
the range of cs by the empirical relation (Gardner et al. 1970)

the product of the conductivity and the potential gradient. The conductivity Ks depends on soil texture

Chapter 7

A much more detailed treatment of these relationships can be found in any good soil physics text,
such as that of Hillel (1998). The numerical values of the parameters in many of these physical soil–
water relationships are conventionally determined in homogeneous, sieved samples of soil. They may
provide very inaccurate indications of the real situation in the highly variable, heterogeneous environ-
ment in which roots function in the field. However, the equations do provide a guide to the type of
relationships that can be expected.
7.4 WATER MOVEMENT THROUGH TREES
To understand the interactions between transpiration rates, leaf water potential and the key variable of
stomatal conductance and to improve our ability to calculate the soil water balance under forests, we
also need to understand water movement through trees. The most significant developments in this area
within the last 20 years have come about as a result of technological advances that allow the rate at
which water moves up stems (the sap flow rate) to be measured accurately and over extended periods.
Granier (1985, 1987) was a pioneer of this procedure, which is based on measuring the heat transferred
by the flow of sap between two probes inserted into the xylem, one of which is heated. Since then the
technique has been refined and calibrated by various people, and commercial versions of the apparatus
are available. An improved (heat ratio) method (Burgess et al. 2001) provides increased accuracy at low
flow rates. This has been an important factor in the identification and measurement of night-time
transpiration, with its implication that stomata may be open in the dark.
Sap flow measurements have provided a large body of data on the hydraulic conductivity of tree
stems, new data on water storage in stems and improved estimates of transpiration by forest stands.
With improved data on the hydraulic conductivity of stems has come increasing recognition of the
extent to which stomatal opening – and hence stomatal conductance – is controlled by resistances to
flow through the whole soil–root–stem–branch–leaf system. Sperry and his collaborators (cited earlier)
have provided seminal contributions in this area.
Almost all studies on water stored in trees have been carried out on conifers, in which the xylem
water conducting elements are in the sapwood – the youngest and outermost wood layers. There are
considerable anatomical differences between tree species in their conducting tissue: angiosperms (the
so-called ring porous) have vessels and tracheids, while conifers (gymnosperms) have diffuse porous
wood structure. For precise comparisons between trees of different ages and species we would need a
great deal of detailed information about wood structure, and the hydraulic characteristics would have
to be compared under strictly comparable conditions. In this book, we are not attempting such
comparisons (for which, in any case, the data generally do not exist), but will restrict ourselves
to the general, usually approximate, descriptions and situations dealt with in most of the literature
in this area.
7.4.1. Water Movement and Water Potential
Water movement along roots is governed by the water potential gradients along the roots and the
resistances in the flow pathways; these are (approximately) inversely proportional to the reciprocal of
the fourth power of the xylem element radius (Poiseuille’s law). For a given mass of assimilate available
for root formation, there is an optimum root-length/root-diameter relationship. This was analysed by
Landsberg and Fowkes (1978), who were able to provide a mathematical definition of this optimum for
a single root with laterals. They later (Fowkes and Landsberg 1981) extended their analysis to examine
root systems as a whole and tried to determine whether they conformed to some ‘‘optimal’’ criteria.
Hydrology and Plant Water Relations
201

--- Page 215 ---

It emerged from the analysis that the best root system for a plant in a given environment – and
therefore, presumably, the one most likely to have evolved in that environment – will depend on the
transpiration rates likely to occur there and on the water potential that the plant can tolerate.
We have already noted that water moves from soil to roots and through plants along water
potential gradients, which are caused primarily by changes in leaf water potential as a result of
transpiration. Water uptake by plant roots and movement through plants has been widely described
by an analogue of Ohm’s law: the mass flow or volume flow rate of water (J, m3 s1) through a tree–soil
system can be represented by flow through a series of hydraulic resistances and described by the equation
J ¼
cs  cl
rs þ rr þ rx þ rl
;
ð7:23Þ
where cs and cl are the water potentials of the soil and leaves, respectively, and the various r are the
series resistances to flow of water from the soil to the atmosphere: rs is the resistance to water flow
through the soil to the root, rr is the resistance through the root to the xylem, rx is resistance to flow
through the xylem, and rl from the xylem to the evaporating surfaces in the leaves. In any soil, rs will
vary with ys and with root length density LRV, as discussed in Section 7.3.2. Much early work assumed
that the resistances to flow within the tree, particularly rx, were constant, but it has become clear that
this is not the case.
If the resistances ri are constant, Eq. (7.23) can be rewritten as
cl ¼ cs  J

ð7:24Þ
This is essentially an equation for a tree where the leaves are assumed to be at an average potential cl;
however, it is now more usual to substitute a conductance for the resistance terms. In their review
of hydraulic limits to leaf water supply, Sperry et al. (2002) distinguish conductivity (K) from conduc-
tance (k): conductivity is defined by inverting Eq. (7.17) to give
K ¼ grw

while conductance k (s1) is defined as
k ¼ grw

Sperry et al. emphasised that conductance applies to the whole flow pathway, whereas conductivity is a
property of the conducting tissue (and may vary along that tissue or spatially in the soil). As defined
above, the units of k are m2 s-1, while those of K are m3 s-1. However, if J in Eq. (7.23) is a volume flux,
the units of K are m s-1, the same as hydraulic conductivity Ks.
It is also convenient to analyse flows in terms of transpiration per unit leaf area (EL). For trees, where
height (H) is significant, the gravitational potential cg¼rwgH has to be taken into account, and the
flow equation is written in the form
EL ¼ ksl cs  cl  Hrwg

ð7:27Þ
where ksl is the conductance of the soil to leaf pathway. The corresponding conductivity Ksl
is not constant but is a function of tissue (stem) water potential, and in terms of this Eq. (7.27)
