ð7:28Þ
Sperry et al. (1998) used a detailed model to show that declining hydraulic conductivity in the soil and
xylem, brought about by progressive loss of conducting xylem through cavitation of vessels, results in a
minimum rate of steady-state transpiration (Ecrit) and corresponding minimum cl(ccrit) at which Ksl
approached zero. They showed that, below a threshold value of the ratio of root surface area to leaf area
AR/AL, the loss of rhizosphere conductivity limited Ecrit and ccrit. Since conductivity in coarse-textured
soils falls faster as their water content declines (see Figure 7.3b), the coarser the soil type the more
limiting soil will be to the flux of water to roots, and the higher the AR/AL threshold for plants in those
soils.
7.4.2. The Hydraulic Hypothesis
The hydraulic hypothesis (Ryan and Yoder 1997) posits that that tree height is limited by the supply of
water to the leaves. Because water is dragged up trees under tension, and every metre of height growth
is equivalent to an increment in gravitational water potential cg of 0.01 MPa, the taller the tree the
higher the tension has to be. Air bubbles are therefore more likely to form in the xylem vessels, causing
cavitation and loss of conducting ability. To prevent this, taller trees have to close their stomata earlier
in the day or earlier in a drought; this limits photosynthesis and so slows tree growth.
Sperry (2000) later showed that interaction between hydraulic conductivity and cl creates unam-
biguous limits on the range of water potential over which gas exchange can occur, i.e. on stomatal
conductance. Decreases in plant Ksl have the effect of amplifying the water stress and increasing the
sensitivity of the stomatal response to drought. Sperry et al. (2002) formulated a series of testable
hypotheses deriving from the hydraulic hypothesis.
A noteworthy application of the hydraulic hypothesis was described by McDowell et al. (2008) who
carried out a meta-analysis of published data and found that the essence of the hypothesis – that
reduced soil water supply coupled with high evaporative demand causes xylem conduits and the
rhizosphere to cavitate, stopping the flow of water and leading to desiccation of plant tissues – provides
a good explanation of drought susceptibility and death as a consequence of drought. They provided
interesting information about the contrasting behaviour of isohydric and anisohydric plants. Isohydric
plants reduce stomatal conductance as soil water potential decreases or VPD increases to maintain a
relatively constant midday leaf water potential, regardless of drought conditions. Anisohydric species,
by contrast, allow midday water potential to decline as the soil dries. The differences are well illustrated
by analysis of pinˇon pine (isohydric) and juniper (anisohydric). Isohydric plants are less likely to
experience hydraulic failure because they close their stomata rather than risk cavitation, while aniso-
hydric plants maintain higher gas exchange rates during drought at greater risk of cavitation. McDowell
et al. noted that the hydraulic hypothesis allows quantitatively informed analyses of the mechanisms of
mortality. Their analysis was extended to include the consequences of carbon starvation, and hence
greater susceptibility to biotic attack.
7.4.3. Representation of Effects on Conductance
The implications of the hydraulic hypothesis, for forest water relations as a whole, are conceptually
clear, but their application as predictive tools to the calculation of tree water status is more problem-
atic. We have, as yet, relatively little information about the changes in hydraulic conductivity among
Hydrology and Plant Water Relations
203

--- Page 217 ---

species, and the interaction with water movement in soil introduces additional complexity in different
soil types. A promising advance has been made by Novick et al. (2009), who analysed combinations of
published conductance measurements obtained across boreal to tropical climates to explore the
relationships between canopy conductance (see Section 2.5.3) and hydraulic constraints. Their objec-
tive was to determine whether generic relationships exist for diverse species between measurable
features of hydraulic architecture and canopy conductance, and whether these are simple enough for
incorporation into coarse-scale models. They wrote Eq. (7.27) in the form
EL ¼ Ksl
AS
AFH
cs  cf  rwgH
	

;
ð7:29Þ
where EL (mmol m2 s1) is transpiration rate per unit leaf area, AS (m2) is sapwood cross-sectional
area, AF (m2) is foliage area, Ksl (mmol m1 s1 Pa1) is the tissue-specific hydraulic conductivity per
unit sapwood cross-sectional area and tree height H (m) approximates the path length from soil at
water potential cs (Pa) to foliage at potential cf (Pa). Novick et al. then replaced EL with the product of
canopy conductance (gC) and vapour pressure deficit (D), and restricted their analysis to a reference
conductance (gCref) defined for D¼1 kPa under conditions where both light and soil moisture are not
limiting. This allows gCref to be expressed as a function of AS/AF, Ksl, cf and H and leads to
gCref ¼ Ksl
AS
AFH
cf  rwgH
	

:
ð7:30Þ
They found that, across 29 sites for which all variables were available,
gCref ¼ 98:2 AS
AFH þ 37:3
ð7:31Þ
with r2¼0.75 and P<0.0001. Equation (7.31) was presented earlier as Eq. (2.47).
Equation (7.31) was the primary result from the Novick et al. (2009) study. In contrast, there was
only a weak general relationship between reference conductance gCref and height alone among 42
forested ecosystems representing a large number of species from a wide range of climates, although
there was a strong relationship when data from temperate sites were treated alone. A general expression
for gCref allows the application of Eq. (2.46) to estimate canopy conductance as a function of D (see
Ewers et al. 2000).
We note that the 3-PG model (Landsberg and Waring 1997) uses reference conductance rates that
are modified by D, but for Eq. (7.31) to have predictive utility at coarse spatial scales, AS/AF must be
specified. This varies between species, and may also vary as a result of growing conditions, but Novick
et al. (2009) commented that ‘‘A mechanistic model for this variation would greatly enhance the
generality of the derived relationship between gCref, H and AS/AF. . .’’ and then concluded ‘‘. . .we do not
know of a generic, prognostic model for AS/AF that would facilitate the application of Eq. (7.31) over
coarse spatial scales. . .’’.
The results obtained by Novick et al. suggest ‘‘differences among species in leaf physiology and the
anatomy of the transport tissue, and differences in soil properties among sites, may exert a smaller
effect on canopy conductance relative to the direct effects of canopy architecture, and that height and
sapwood-to-leaf area ratio explain most (75%) of the variation’’ Clearly, more research is needed in this
area, but these results are encouraging.
Support for the indication that it is possible to establish general relationships between evaporative
demand, the physical properties of trees, critical values of leaf water potential and stomatal conduc-
tance comes from Mencuccini (2002), who carried out a meta-analysis across 50 species of several
204
Chapter 7

--- Page 218 ---

different life forms to evaluate the main factors affecting the relationship between whole-plant
hydraulic conductance and leaf area, gas exchange at the leaf-level and leaf-specific xylem hydraulic
conductance. He found that a large fraction of the variability in stomatal aperture could be accounted
for by species-specific hydraulic properties, and that whole-plant leaf area was highly significantly
related to the subtending hydraulic conductance across species of several life forms. Barbour and
Whitehead (2003) tested a theory developed by Roderick and Berry (2001) and found that, for
Dacrydium cupressinum trees in exposed canopy positions, sap velocity was directly proportional to
(1rb)2, where rb is wood density. Barbour and Whitehead commented that ‘‘This reveals a new
linkage between structural and functional properties of the water-conducting pathway and is likely to
advance the understanding of hydraulic processes in trees’’.
7.4.4. Stem Water Storage
A complication in the analysis of flow through trees may be introduced by the fact that trees store
significant quantities of water in tissue such as sapwood (Landsberg et al. 1976; Waring and Running
1978; Cˇ erma´k et al. 2007). The effects of this can be seen by testing Eq. (7.24) by plotting Dc¼clcs
against J (either measured by stem flow or calculated as ELD). If conductance k is constant this should
yield a straight line. If the soil is wet, cs can be taken as approximately zero, so the plot reduces to cf
against J.
Equation (7.24) has on occasion been adequate for this type of analysis (Landsberg et al. 1975), but
in most cases, and particularly if the diurnal course of transpiration is plotted against (say) hourly
average values of cl, such plots yield hysteresis loops, such as those shown in Figure 7.4. This is a
consequence of either the movement of water in and out of storage tissues or of changes in hydraulic
Dry soil
Wet soil
Transpiration rate as % of maximum
Intermediate
soil moisture
−3
Leaf water potential (yl, MPa)
−2
−1
FIGURE 7.4 Diurnal course of leaf water potential cl as a function of (normalized) transpiration rates for trees in varying soil moisture
conditions (redrawn from Hinckley et al. 1978). The arrows in the curves denote their direction in time (falling in the morning, rising in the
afternoon). As soil water potentials fall, falling potentials in the flow pathway result in increasing flows out of tissue storage. As transpiration
rates decline there is replacement. Under the driest soil moisture regime transpiration is greatly restricted. The broken lines represent the
slopes (resistances) that would result from fitting Eq. (7.24) (see also Landsberg et al. 1976), so the intercept on the cl-axis provides an
estimate of the ‘‘effective’’ soil water potential.
Hydrology and Plant Water Relations
205

--- Page 219 ---

conductivity. Figure 7.4 shows relationships between transpiration and leaf water potential at different
values of cs. It can be interpreted as follows: in wet soil, as the transpiration rate increases during the
first part of the day, water is withdrawn from storage in the tissues nearest to the foliage, then
progressively from lower parts of the tree, creating gradients from roots to the soil (Cˇ erma´k et al.
2007). If soil–root resistance to flow is lower than the resistances to flow out of storage, the system will
behave in a linear manner, consistent with Eq. (7.22). In non-saturated soils, water will have moved,
during the night, to re-wet those regions around roots that were dried out during the previous day, but
flows early in the day will cause rapid increases in Rs, leading to extraction of water from storage tissues
and hence the hysteresis patterns seen in Figure 7.4. As water is withdrawn from the roots and from
storage tissue at high transpiration rates, potentials in both these regions fall so that to sustain a
particular flow rate later in the day leaf water potentials must fall further than earlier in the day. The
extent to which the soil around roots dries out during the day, the distance the water has to move, the
wetness of the soil and its hydraulic properties all influence the extent to which the soil surrounding the
roots is recharged. When transpiration rates fall later in the day – and hence leaf water potentials rise –
water moves from the transpiration stream back into storage tissues.
Alternatively, according to the hydraulic limitation hypothesis, as transpiration rate increases and
the soil–root resistance increases, particularly in drier soil, cl falls towards the critical point where
stomatal closure occurs. We have to postulate reversible cavitation, so that as transpiration rates fall
later in the day cl does not return to the same values, for a given transpiration rate, but to lower values
as a result of the lower hydraulic conductivity – as yet unrestored – of the stems.
Cˇ erma´k et al. (2007) found that refilling of cavitated elements occurs daily when soil moisture
increases during winter, as suggested by Waring and Running (1978). This is important since it means
water stored in trees need not be taken into account when the time step in a model is significantly
longer than a day. This is supported by Hunt et al. (1991), who reviewed plant water flow models,
including those that deal with stored water, and concluded that steady-state models which include only
resistances are sufficient to predict total daily water uptake by roots. Non-steady-state models, which
use both water flow resistances and capacitances, are necessary for predicting both cf and the
instantaneous water uptake rates required for calculating diurnal variation of transpiration rate in
individual plant models.
Sap flow measurements made at 1.5 m on larch trees in Siberia in a study by Arneth et al. (1996)
were normalised on the basis of projected crown area to give a normalised flow J0. Total stem
conductance (Gt, expressed as mm s1) was calculated as k(J0/D), cf. Eq. (7.26), where k was a
temperature-dependent conversion factor and D the vapour pressure deficit. Plotting half-hourly
values of Gt against visible irradiance (see Chapter 2) and against D produced massive hysteresis
loops, indicating a considerable decrease in Gt later in the day. The authors dismiss the possibility that
the hysteresis was caused by movement of water out of storage, but it is possible that this may have been
happening.
7.5 MODELS INCLUDING STORAGE
In the context of Figure 7.4 we discussed hysteresis loops in the diurnal time course of transpiration as a
function of leaf water potential. It was, at one time, argued that these phase lags between transpiration
rate and cl were a consequence only of the differences in rate of water movement to the roots through
the soil and through the stem. However, numerous studies on lags between cl and stem shrinkage, and
206
Chapter 7

--- Page 220 ---

patterns of swelling and shrinkage in cambium confirm that Eqs. (7.24) and (7.26) are inadequate to
describe the patterns of water movement through trees (and probably through all plants), at least in the
short term. These are well reviewed by Whitehead and Jarvis (1981) and Jones (1978).
More recent work on trees demonstrate clear linkages between changes in stem diameter and
measures of physiological activity in the stem, such as xylem water potential and sap flow. For example,
Offenthaler et al. (2001) monitored simultaneous changes in stem diameter, xylem water potential and
sap flow in Norway spruce and found strong correlations such that increased sap flow reduced both
stem diameter and xylem water potential. Changes in wood diameter were linearly related to change in
xylem water potential, except at the ends of the diurnal cycle of transpiration. This is because cells, and
hence wood, are elastic structures, and Offenthaler et al. determined the modulus of elasticity to be
0.83 GPa.
In a comparison of the drought response of Pubescent oak and Scots pine in Switzerland, Zweifel
et al. (2007) demonstrated that the stomatal behaviour of these two species differed because of a
difference in the degree to which stomatal opening was controlled by the rapidly changing crown water
deficit or the slowly varying tree storage water deficit. They found that the oak avoided serious
cavitation damage by withdrawing more water from storage pools while reducing leaf water potentials
to lower levels than did the pine.
Therefore, to simulate diurnal variations in transpiration rates, rates of water uptake and the time
course of leaf and other tissue water potentials, it may be necessary to include the fluxes in and out of
storage (but see Hunt et al. 1991). Models that do this are discussed in the following section. It is
important to understand these processes, but we note that, over periods longer than days, they
probably do not significantly affect the amount of water extracted from the soil and transpired by
forest canopies.
Two approaches to modeling within-tree transport are resistance–capacitance models and porous
media models. In a resistance–capacitance model the key elements are a resistance to flow from soil to
foliage, in parallel to a capacitor representing storage in stem tissue with an associated resistance for
recharge and discharge of the capacitor. The flow through this system is driven by the water potential
gradient between soil and foliage. Phillips et al. (2004) used a bulk resistance–capacitance model to
describe the hydraulic characteristics of Pinus abies and Pinus taeda trees in which flow rates were
measured with sap flux sensors, together with a coupled hydraulic-stomatal physiology model to
evaluate the role of stomatal conductance in regulating the observed sap flow responses. Using a
combination of misting and shading they imposed short-period perturbations on transpiration rates
and were able to estimate values for the bulk resistance and capacitance.
In a porous medium model the conducting tissue is treated as a porous medium with flow described
by Darcy’s law modified to include sinks due to transpiration. Chuang et al. (2006) proposed a porous
media model that leads to a mass-conserving partial differential equation which describes the spatial–
temporal dynamics of a tree hydraulic system and connects sap flux to transpiration. This approach
leads to complex mathematics and can only be applied when sap flux data are available. The results
presented by Chuang et al. were ‘‘noisy’’ and provided little insight into the physiology and behaviour
of the Norway spruce trees on which their measurements were made.
The papers by Phillips et al. and by Chuang et al. are interesting reports of research with the
potential to provide parameter values that will improve our ability to model water movement through
trees that are large enough to store significant amounts of water in their stems and branches, leading to
hysteresis in diurnal values of cf.
Hydrology and Plant Water Relations
207

--- Page 221 ---

7.5.1. Tissue Water Storage
Whitehead and Jarvis (1981) provided a detailed discussion of the relationships between the various
parameters describing water in tissues. They emphasised that because living cells are elastic and hence
can change their volume there is a difference in the water storage behaviour of living cells in leaves,
phloem and cambium and dead cells in sap and heart wood. Also, not all water in a tissue is freely
available as part of the water is held in small capillaries or bound to cellulose in cell walls. The pressures
required to extract this water exceed 30 MPa, considerably higher than those generated under normal
physiological conditions.
The volume of water in living tissue is usefully expressed by the relative water content wt of the
tissue by
wt ¼ Vw

given by

where 1530 kg m3 is the density of the solid material in wood.
The contribution of stored water to transpiration in 40-year old Scots pine (Pinus sylvestris) stands
of four different stem populations (610–3280 trees ha1) was analysed by Waring et al. (1979). Plots of
lowest population stored the equivalent of 12 mm of water in sapwood, while those with the highest
population stored 20 mm in sapwood. In all stands, storage in foliage and phloem was 5–6 mm. Waring
et al. estimated that in rapidly changing weather conditions 1–1.5 mm day1 of stored water could be
removed from sapwood, compared to transpiration rates that rarely exceeded 3 mm day1. These
values are similar to those reported for 50–60 m tall Douglas fir in the Cascade Mountains (Oregon,
USA): 27 mm stored in sapwood, with withdrawal from storage of up to 1.7 mm on clear days
following cloudy or rainy weather.
The change in water content of a tissue per unit change in water potential (i.e. the slope of the
pressure/volume curve) is called the capacitance C of the tissue (Landsberg et al. 1976). Thus
C ¼ Vt

ð7:38Þ
where yt is the volumetric water content of the tissue, i.e. volume of water per unit volume of tissue (Vt)
and ct is the water potential of the tissue. It follows from Eq. (7.38) that the volume flux J of water into
the tissue in question is given by
J ¼ Vt

As defined by Eq. (7.38), capacitance has units of m3 Pa1, but units of kg Pa1 are sometimes used;
the two values are related by the density of water. Because capacitance is an extensive property (i.e. is
size dependent) the specific capacitance (kg kg1 Pa1) is more often quoted. Landsberg et al. (1976)
reported specific capacitances of 2108 kg kg1 Pa1 for the stems of large and small apple trees,
and 3.7108 kg kg1 Pa1 for leaves.
7.5.2. Models Based on Pools and Resistances
The following treatment was presented in the first edition of this book; we have retained it because it is
still relevant. Equation (7.23) can be broken into separate linear equations describing flux from soil to
roots, through the roots to the xylem, through the xylem and from the xylem to the evaporating surfaces
in the leaves. The various pools and fluxes are illustrated in Figure 7.5. The storage compartment
comprises both leaf and sapwood storage, and although flows into or out of storage could be expected to
cause changes in the volume of the store, these are neglected. The resulting simplification leads to
expressions for the time course of leaf water potential and the water content of the storage tissue, i.e.
Eqs. (7.46). Although developed for a single tree, they can be applied to a stand, in which case variables
and parameters are ‘‘bulked’’ and refer to unit land area rather than to individual plants.
The flux Js from the soil into the xylem conducting tissue of the roots and stem is given by
JsRs ¼ cs  cx
ð7:40Þ

Equations (7.43)–(7.45), or (7.46), provide the basis for numerical simulation of the time course of leaf
water potential and storage tissue water content. Estimates of all the resistances are available and can be
obtained by the methods used by Landsberg et al. (1976), Powell and Thorpe (1977) and Running
(1980). Landsberg (1986b) determined relationships between c and y of the form c¼ayn on the
basis of data for Pseudotsuga menziesii (Waring and Running 1978) and Pinus sylvestris (Waring et al.
1979), and give a¼3.6610–5 MPa and n¼20 for foliage, and a¼0.025 MPa and n¼4 for storage.
The elegant and detailed study by Cˇ erma´k et al. (2007) provides a very clear illustration of the
processes where water is stored in stems and moved out of storage in response to transpiration losses
from the foliage, and back into storage when transpiration falls to very low levels at night. They studied
flow patterns in three very large (57 m tall) Douglas fir trees, placing sap flow sensors in the stems at 4
and 51 m, and in branches at 46, 51 and 56 m. Electronic dendrometers were used to measure changes
in stem volume at 4 and 51 m. They gave flow rates into and out of the various parts of the trees (stem,
lower crown, upper crown, branches) and estimates of volumes and amounts of free water in those
tissues. Their results are illustrated in Figure 7.6, taken from the paper by Cˇ erma´k et al.
The main points emerging from the Cˇ erma´k et al. (2007) paper are that the diurnal course of sap
flow closely followed foliar transpiration, beginning at branch tips, followed by branches, then the stem
near branches with the most pronounced delay in the stem furthest from the foliage. There was a
pronounced time shift in sap flow between the stem at 51 m and the stem base at 4 m, where sap flow
lagged behind the top of the canopy by 1–2 h, i.e. for up to 2 h outflow from the upper stem was greater
than inflow from the lower stem, but the balance became positive again at about 1000 h when input to
the stem at 4 m was greater than output at 51 m. Water withdrawn from storage came from both the
upper and lower stem, but the amount coming from the lower stem was 3–5 times that from the upper
stem. Most of the stored water came from the zone between 4 and 51 m. Diurnal change in stem
volume paralleled the cumulative difference in sap flow during the day and volume increases at night,
when no stored water was extracted from tissues. The relationship between volume change and changes
in water storage was generally linear during the daytime.
Water withdrawn from storage may provide 20–30% of daily sap flow in the large Douglas fir
trees studied by Cˇ erma´k et al. 2007, but their data show that this water is likely to be replaced
by inflows at night. They argued that tree transpiration can be maintained with stored water for about
a week. However, it can be maintained by stored water from the upper crown alone for only a few hours.

Flows from storage may be important in refilling cavitated xylem elements (those in which hydraulic
continuity has been lost as a result of increasing water stress, causing water columns to break with
consequent loss of hydraulic continuity) and hence ensuring that the overall hydraulic conductivity of
the stems is not reduced. This is an important factor determining stomatal conductance: hydraulic
connections are continuous from the soil to the evaporating surfaces in the foliage, hence Eq. (7.23),
and a reduction in the hydraulic conductivity (an increase in resistance) of the flow pathways is likely to
lead to large drops in leaf water potential and stomatal closure. If the stomata do not close, the foliage
will desiccate. We return to this point later in this chapter.
7.5.3. Stem Hydraulic Conductivity and Its Implications
The discussion in the previous sections has been concerned with the interactions between stem
hydraulic conductivity, leaf water potential and stomatal conductance. The effects of these interactions
on the longer term growth of trees are not yet unequivocally established, but they are (thought to be)
implicated in the commonly observed decline with age of growth rates and the productivity of forests
(Ryan et al. 1997a). Measurements, as well as yield tables developed on the basis of conventional
mensuration data, normally indicate that the mean annual increment (t ha1 year–1) of forest stands
rises to a maximum at about the time of canopy closure, then declines. The reasons for this decline in
productivity have been the subject of numerous investigations, but until recently, there was no
consensus on the cause (or causes).
Yoder et al. (1994) observed a reduction in photosynthetic rate and stomatal conductance, and an
increased effect of stomatal limitation, in old trees of both P. contorta and P. ponderosa, and speculated
that reduced hydraulic conductance could account for the decline in growth efficiency (carbohydrate
production per unit leaf area) with tree age. Mencuccini and Grace (1996) examined light interception,
production and xylem conductance in Scots pine trees ranging in age from 7 to 60 years. They showed
that stem hydraulic conductance and net primary production had similar trends with stand age: both
increased up to a maximum at a stand age of 15–20 years, and then declined markedly with age (and
stem diameter). They concluded that decreased hydraulic conductance, and increased maintenance
costs in big trees, may be the main causes of the observed reduction in productivity in mature Scots
pine. Landsberg and Waring (1997) used the results presented by Mencuccini and Grace to justify their
use of an empirical expression in 3-PG, in terms of relative stand age, to account explicitly for the
reduction in maximum stomatal conductance as stands age.
In 1997, Ryan and Yoder suggested that as trees grew taller stomatal conductance tends to be
reduced as a result of increased resistance to water flow through the stems, increased gravitational
potential and maintenance of a species-specific minimum water potential. This hypothesis generated a
great deal of work to test it, and Ryan et al. (2006) found more than 305 papers that addressed the
question in various ways. They used an equation based on Eq. (7.26) as the framework for their meta-
analysis:
gS ¼ kslDc=D;
ð7:47Þ
where gS is average stomatal conductance of the tree’s foliage, ksl is called average hydraulic conductance
of the whole tree from soil to leaf (per unit leaf area), D is the leaf to-air vapour pressure deficit, taken
as a surrogate for transpiration rate, and Dc is the soil-to-leaf water potential difference. A reduction in
ksl as trees increase in height would result in a proportional reduction in gS if D and Dc remain
constant. Ryan et al. focussed on leaf gas exchange as the variable likely to provide the definitive test of
the hypothesis, since if the commonly observed decline in productivity after canopy closure is
attributable to reduced gs as a result of increased hydraulic resistance to water movement to leaves,
Hydrology and Plant Water Relations
213

--- Page 227 ---

this should be reflected in reduced CO2 uptake per unit leaf area as well as reduced transpiration. Ryan
et al. (2006) found that increased hydraulic resistance accounted for reductions in CO2 assimilation
rates at the leaf level in many species, but there were exceptions, notably Eucalyptus saligna, studied in
useful detail by Barnard and Ryan (2003).
In the Barnard and Ryan study, measurements of the carbon budget of the whole forest were
evaluated in relation to the hydraulic hypothesis. The data did not support the hypothesis that the
conductance for liquid water, acting through lowered canopy conductance, is responsible for reduced
growth after canopy closure. The water conducting system in E. saligna changed significantly as the
trees grew taller – the ratio of sapwood conducting area per unit leaf area AS/AL increased considerably
in the older, taller trees, compensating for increased path length and gravitational potential, so that
canopy conductance was maintained although growth declined. Barnard and Ryan (2003) found no
significant influence of capacitance in their study. Whole tree photosynthesis for the taller trees was
74% of that in the shorter trees, similar to the ratio of growth for the 5-year (taller) plot to growth for
the 1-year plot (0.69).
However, Ryan et al. (2006) noted that ‘‘the reductions in A (i.e. CO2 assimilation) seen at the leaf
level ... are unlikely to account for reductions in wood production measured in many studies (30–90%)
unless other sinks also decline with tree height growth, and reduced A only reduced wood production.
That strategy for carbon allocation seems unlikely, because fluxes to respiration, below-ground foliage
and wood production appear to change in concert with changes in canopy A’’. Their review led to the
conclusion that hydraulic limitation appears to operate in many trees (but not all), and where it occurs
it lowers assimilation rate, stomatal conductance and tree water use in older, taller trees.
We should note here that much of the previous discussion focussed on trees as single entities,
whereas their hydraulic architecture is, in fact, complicated. There are resistances to flow from soil to
roots, from roots up through stems and from stems to branches and from branches to petioles to the
evaporating surfaces in sub-stomatal cavities. Clearly, a complete description of the network of
resistances, with a view (for example) to accounting for the influence of pathway resistances on
stomatal opening, would be immensely complicated and, for most purposes, impractical. So we
normally treat the system as a ‘‘lumped’’ whole, particularly when calculating canopy conductance
(see Section 2.5.3). Nevertheless, the variable resistances in flow pathways must exert major influence
on stomatal behaviour and may lie behind the considerable variation frequently observed in field
studies of stomatal behaviour (e.g. Watts et al. 1976; Waring and Silvester 1994; Ewers and Oren 2000).
7.6 WATER RELATIONS OF STANDS
Although the treatment in this chapter has so far been in terms of single trees, the concepts discussed
can equally be applied to stands. In the case of stands, the flux J through the average tree is (flux density
per unit land area)/(tree population per unit area). If, as is usually the case (see McNaughton and Jarvis
1983; Whitehead et al. 1984), transpiration from a forest is dominated by the aerodynamic term
(Eq. (2.49)), then increases in L as canopies develop will result in proportional increases in gC through
Eq. (2.46), and increasing flow of water through individual trees. If transpiration were dominated by
the energy term (Eq. 2.48), so that increases in L beyond L3 result in little further increase in
absorbed energy, then the flow rate through individual trees will not change as L increases. The
consequences of changing the populations in terms of exploitation of the soil by roots, and effective-
ness of utilization of available soil moisture, are areas which require both experimental and theoretical
research (see Landsberg and McMurtrie 1984).
214
Chapter 7

--- Page 228 ---

Flow resistances through trees, relationships between sapwood cross-sectional area and foliage mass
(see Section 4.4.2) and sapwood cross-sectional area and flow resistances (Rx) are all areas where
additional information is needed. Whitehead et al. (1984) provided valuable data for Pinus sylvestris.
They found that, on plots with 608 and 3280 stems ha1 (leaf area per tree¼39.5 and 9.5 m2,
respectively; L¼2.4 and 3.1, respectively) transpiration rates on the low population plot were about
0.7 times those on the high population plot, implying much higher fluxes through the trees on the plot
with the lower population. Hydraulic resistance to flow through these trees was 7.5105 MPa s m3,
compared to 16105 MPa s m3 for the trees on the high population plot.
7.6.1. Quantitative Measures of Water Stress
It will be clear from the discussion in this chapter that defining water stress is not simple. There has been
much discussion about this at various times, some of it concerned with the definition of
stress by analogy with the engineering concept. However, it is clear that there is no cut-off point,
in terms of some measure of tissue water status, on one side of which plants are stressed, and on the other
unstressed. Rather than attempting to define stress we should, perhaps, be more concerned to explain
the effects of tissue water status on growth. This leads to the question of which water relations variable
should be used to define status and to analyse growth, i.e. which quantity should be used as the
independent variable in any attempt to relate growth to plant water status. The answer to this question
is clearly strongly dependent upon the growth process under study (see Hsiao 1973).
Turgor pressure cp is probably the most important factor as it affects processes such as cell division
and elongation, and hence shoot and root extension. It is also the operational factor in stomatal
opening and closing. One of the mechanisms by which plants adjust to water deficits over periods of
days or weeks is turgor maintenance by osmotic adjustment (reviewed by Turner and Jones 1980).
Pre-dawn water potential, measured regularly, may be used to provide a measure of water stress over
periods such as a season, or of the differences in ‘‘effective’’ plant water potential at different sites, but
we note the work by Kavanagh et al. (2007) (Section 3.2.4), showing that night-time transpiration is, in
many cases, not zero, and that pre-dawn water potential measurements should be corrected for the
effects of D on conductance (Eq. (3.21)). The use of cpd in this way is illustrated by Collatz et al. (1976)
and by Parker et al. (1982).
Myers (1988) produced a measure of water stress – the water stress integral Sc – which is the
integrated value of pre-dawn water potential cpd over some specified period such as a growing season.
The equation for Sc is
Sc ¼

made at intervals of Dt. Myers examined the relationships between Sc, the length of P. radiata needles
(Ln, mm) at the end of a season’s growth, and annual stand basal area increment (DAS). In each case he
found highly significant non-linear relationships that explained a large proportion of the variation in
the dependent variables. They were

215

--- Page 229 ---

An interesting point about these relationships is that they included data from the five different
irrigation and fertilisation treatments (see Section 2.1, Figures 2.2 and 2.3, and section 6.2.3.c).
White et al. (1999) showed that stomatal conductance declined with increasing values of Sc summed
over a number of days preceding the day when conductance was determined.
7.6.2. Consequences of Water Stress
It is clear from Figure 7.4 that the leaf water potential of trees on a particular site and at a particular
time depends on the diurnal course of transpiration and water movement through the plants. The
resultant drop in leaf water potential is relieved as transpiration rates fall later in the day. If the soil is
dry and average cs in the root zone is low, the pre-dawn water potential will be relatively low and
midday values of cf will be low, particularly if the evaporative demand is high. Conversely, even if the
soil is dry, cf may remain relatively high on cool, damp days.
The consequences of water stress may vary in relation to the length of time spent at low potentials,
and on the severity of the stress. In the short term (hours) one of the best documented consequences of
stress is stomatal closure as a result of loss of turgor in leaf cells: this leads to reductions in photosyn-
thesis and hence reduced growth. Other relatively short-term results (hours to days) may be changes in
the concentrations of growth regulators such as abscisic acid, and in cambial and shoot growth.
However, short term water stress can be ameliorated, or delayed, by water stored in tissues
(Section 7.5.1).
Some of the effects of water stress over longer periods are illustrated by an experiment reported by
Larsson and Bengston (1980). They used a plastic cover to exclude water from the roots of a Scots pine
throughout a summer growing season in Sweden. Photosynthetic rates in the stressed tree declined
through the season, but recovered fully within a week of re-watering. Cambial activity – as reflected in
the growth rings – was affected in the year of treatment and for 2 years afterwards. A year after the
stress, shoot growth was reduced, but needle growth was not affected either in the stress year or in the
following year. It is not possible, over such long periods, to identify the component of water potential
responsible for the stress and its effects.
a) Effects of Water Stress on Foliage
In a review of physiological responses of plants to moderate stress, Bradford and Hsaio (1982) noted that
‘‘the first sign of (longer-term) water stress is usually a restriction in foliage growth’’. Using data from
crop plants they developed a simple analysis demonstrating a strong feedback effect – a reduction in the
amount of foliage results in less photosynthesis and assimilation, hence less foliage production and so
on. Of course, reductions in foliage area also result in reduced transpiration. The study by McDowell
et al. (2008) showed that tree mortality during drought may be a result of hydraulic failure per se for
isohydric seedlings or trees near their maximum height, or carbon starvation caused by prolonged
stomatal closure in response to hydraulic failure. Biotic agents may exacerbate drought-induced stress.
Severe or prolonged stress not only results in reduction in leaf emergence and foliage expansion, but
also causes leaf senescence. This was clearly demonstrated by Linder et al. (1987) (discussed in
Section 4.4.5), who showed that, after a period of adequate water followed by drought there was massive
foliage loss in non-irrigated P. radiata trees, and stem diameter decreased. Raison et al. (1992a,b),
reporting on the same experiment, found that needle length, weight, specific leaf area and rates of
production were all strongly influenced by the duration and severity of water stress. Borchert (1980) and
Reich and Borchert (1982) demonstrated that leaf shedding, and the consequent improvement in the
water balance of some tropical trees, triggered flowering. Thus water stress affects phenology.
216
Chapter 7

--- Page 230 ---

Brix (1972) found that irrigation caused an increase in leaf size in Douglas fir – and hence in leaf
area. In the year following irrigation, leaf size was not different between irrigated, fertilized, and
irrigatedþN-fertilizer treatments, but leaf number was greatly increased by treatments. Stem growth
was increased by irrigation and fertilizer combined in the treatment year and somewhat less by
irrigation alone. There was a strong irrigation–fertilizer interaction: the response to the combined
factors was about twice as much as the sum of the responses to each separately. Brix concluded that
water stress had a more adverse effect on cambial growth than on photosynthesis.
b) Water Use Efficiency
Many agricultural water use/irrigation/water stress experiments are assessed in terms of water use
efficiency (WUE). This may be expressed over short periods at the leaf level as the ratio of carbon fixed
per unit water transpired, or over long periods at the level of trees or stands as biomass produced
per unit of water transpired. Ku¨ppers (1984) gave leaf-level values ranging from 0.88 to 2.95 g kg1
(i.e. dry mass equivalent per kg water transpired) for a selection of woody species in Europe, while
Pereira et al. (1986) found that WUE of E. globulus leaves was highest in spring (13 g kg1) falling to
low values in autumn. Pereira et al. cite a WUE range from 4.3 to about 7 g kg1 for other woody
species. Lindroth and Cienciala (1996) found, in short-rotation Salix viminalis, that instantaneous
WUE showed a dependence on VPD very similar to that commonly observed in gs, with values for
leaves, trees and a stand (determined by flux measurements) declining from about 35 g kg1 to about
10. Integrating over time, stand level WUE was constant through the summer (in Sweden) at about
10 g kg1. On the basis of above-ground biomass values only, data for E. globulus from Western
Australia (Hingston et al. 1995) had WUE values of about 5 g kg1. If roots and respiration were
included in the biomass component, these values would be very similar to other values reported for
woody plants. Almeida et al. (2007) gave similar values for fast-growing E. grandis plantations in Brazil.
They showed that WUE and closed canopy L declined precipitously with stand productivity from the
third year of a 6-year rotation. Landsberg (1999) reviewed relationships between water use and tree
production and found no evidence of differences between species. Although WUE is strongly affected
by environmental conditions, it is not a particularly useful variable in relation to tree growth but may
be a useful indicator of responses to water stress, and of the ability of plants to produce in spite of it.
c) Acclimation to Drought
The effects of stress on trees will vary depending on whether the stresses are applied suddenly or
develop gradually. When discussing responses to stress, the concepts of acclimation and adaptation are
important. Acclimation describes the physical and physiological changes that occur during the life of a
plant as a consequence of drought. Adaptation reflects the genetic or evolved properties or character-
istics that make a particular species better able to tolerate drought or cold, heat or other stresses.
Trees growing in well-watered regions subjected to sudden drought are likely to suffer more severely
than trees acclimated to drought. The sudden onset of drought may result in wilting and leaf shedding.
In trees acclimated to drought osmotic adjustment may occur, resulting in turgor maintenance and
higher leaf conductance at low cf values. Sustained periods of water stress may also lead to a shift in
root: shoot ratios in favour of roots – in effect, an increase in the carbohydrate partitioning coefficient
for roots. The fertilisationþirrigation trial in Australia – the BFG trial discussed in Section 6.2.3 –
included two directly comparable treatments: irrigation with no fertiliser and the control. These were
maintained over 28 years and the results were presented by Waterworth et al. (2007). Figure 7.7, based
on Fig. 8 in Waterworth et al., shows that despite the poor sandy soil on which that experiment was
conducted, trees in the irrigated treatment grew consistently faster than those in the control. After 26
years the basal areas and stand volumes in the control and irrigated treatment were, 35.3 m2 and
340 m3 ha1 and 43.7 m2 and 438 m3 ha1, respectively.
Hydrology and Plant Water Relations
217

--- Page 231 ---

Adaptation to drought is likely to be reflected in properties such as specialized leaves or leaf
properties, phenological responses and inherent root characteristics. Leaves may be narrow, with
high reflectivity, so that they are efficient heat exchangers. The leaves of plants adapted to drought
are often relatively rigid with waxy surfaces. Some, such as many of the eucalyptus species, orient their
leaves away from the sun to reduce absorbed energy. Phenological responses include the capacity to
produce flowers when drought is broken. Some trees (again eucalyptus species provide examples) have
very large, deep root systems, enabling them to exploit very effectively water stored deep in the soil.
That this is an adaptive trait is indicated both by empirical evidence (e.g. Carbon et al. 1980) and by the
evidence that structural differences in tree root systems are highly heritable (Nambiar et al. 1982).
7.7 CONCLUDING REMARKS
In our view the final objective of all studies in plant–water relations is to arrive at the ability to predict
tissue water status and to use functional relationships between the appropriate parameters and growth
to evaluate the effects of water status on growth. We should be able to specify soil type, canopy
structure, leaf area and stomatal responses and weather conditions, and from this information calculate
the time course of water status over any period of interest. Soil type would be specified in terms of soil
water-holding characteristics and probable rooting depth. The first step, using the physical models in
this chapter, is to calculate the soil water balance, and the average water potential in the root zone,
e.g. Paul et al. (2003). Information on the resistances in the flow pathways is needed so that the

218
Chapter 7

--- Page 232 ---

equations presented in this chapter can be used to calculate the plant water potential. The work
reviewed by Ryan et al. (2006) and the results obtained by Novick et al. (2009) indicate that calculations
of forest transpiration rates should include a term to account for the effects of tree height of canopy
conductance.
Myers’ (1988) water stress integral (Sc) appears to be a useful, and so far underutilised, measure of
the effects of water stress on tree growth. It can be relatively easily calculated over any required period
through water balance and tree water use simulations. However, it is important that there should be
more investigation of the relationships between Sc and carbohydrate allocation, leading to varying
growth patterns in trees. It may be that these (longer term) relationships are relatively simple, being
largely determined by the effects of water stress on leaf growth and hence on the leaf area index of a
canopy at any time (see Almeida et al. 2007), with consequent feedback to stand productivity through
radiation interception and photosynthesis. The most important short-term effect of water stress, acting
through the hydraulic system of the tree, is on stomatal conductance.

CHAPTER 8
Modelling Tree Growth: Concepts
and Review

and Hybrid Models

The earlier Chapters of this book discussed significant processes that determine tree growth and its
response to environmental factors and management interventions. We also considered how these
processes can be described mathematically, and hence incorporated into predictive models. Reference
was made to models, or sub-models, representing these process, but it is now time to consider how
these various components can or have been integrated into working models of forest growth. This is the
subject of this Chapter, while a more detailed discussion of a specific model, 3-PG, will be presented in
Chapter 9.
It is not our purpose to present a treatise on the art and philosophy of modelling per se, nor a
detailed exposition of the principles of simulation modelling. Rather, we first discuss a few important
aspects of the modelling process, then distinguish between three broad classes of forest growth model,
namely process-based or mechanistic, hybrid, and empirical, and provide an overview of the structure
and use of a number of examples of each. Finally, we consider the all-important topic of model
evaluation, and the principles of testing and validation as they apply to the different model types.
Physiological Ecology of Forest Production
Joe Landsberg and Peter Sands
ISSN 1936-7961, DOI: 10.1016/S1936-7961(11)04008-5
Copyright # 2011 Elsevier Inc.
All rights reserved.

--- Page 234 ---

8.1 CONCEPTS AND PRINCIPLES
Models are essentially abstractions that should encapsulate the essential features of the system being
modelled. They may serve any of a number of purposes. They may be designed as practical tools with
which to simulate the behaviour of a system in response to change or stimuli, so that managers or
decision-makers can assess the probable consequences of those changes or stimuli. They may be
developed primarily as research tools designed to provide a framework within which current know-
ledge and information can be set and the relative importance of different parts of the system evaluated.
Or models may be formulated as statements, or sets of statements, embodying current knowledge or
hypotheses about the way systems work. In one sense, all models are hypotheses so they should be
testable; i.e. it should be possible to design experiments, or make measurements which, at least in
principle, could falsify the hypotheses and show the model to be wrong.
Models can be verbal, diagrammatic, graphical or mathematical. To make quantitative predictions
about the behaviour and responses of systems, models usually have to be written in mathematical
terms, which is the form primarily used throughout this book. Much of the mathematics we use is
deterministic, which implies that in relationships of the form y¼f(x) particular values of x will always
lead to the same value of the dependent variable y. But there is uncertainty associated with all the
knowledge we have. This is particularly the case with the relationships we establish between biological
variables, where there is often significant uncertainty in our understanding of the fundamental
processes, and natural biological variability. Biological relationships do not have the precision of
laws in physics, and the uncertainty tends to increase as we move from lower level (physiological)
processes to more aggregated systems (organs – whole plants – plant communities – ecosystems).
The preceding paragraphs could open a Pandora’s box of definitional problems, semantics, quasi-
philosophical discussion about types of model, problems of uncertainty, levels at which systems can
reasonably be modelled, criteria for falsifiability and so on. We have to touch on some of this but try to
adhere to the principle of Occam’s Razor, i.e. that any hypothesis or explanation should be as simple as
possible. According to this principle, a model should not contain more components or sub-models
than are necessary to make predictions at the level of interest, commensurate with the aims of the
model (see also Sands 1988a).
Some obvious preliminary points need to be made. First, the systems we are dealing with, as
throughout this book, are trees and forest stands. Each can be considered as an assemblage of
interconnected processes at different levels interacting with one another and with their environment.
As we discussed in Chapter 2, plant growth can be treated as a hierarchical set of processes and, in
principle, it should be possible to describe or model systems at higher levels in terms of processes one
level down. As we move up the hierarchy from lower to more aggregated levels, processes at a lower
level can usually be described by simpler relationships at a higher level. In the preceding chapters, we
have presented various equations describing physiological processes involved in the growth of trees and
stands, or relationships between processes or variables. These too can be regarded as models. However,
in this chapter we are concerned, for the most part, with models at the stand level.
Second, the purpose for which a model is built must be clear. The form of the product and, to some
extent, the way the equations of the model are written, will be determined by the use for which it is
designed. This, in turn, is likely to be affected by the probable users of the model – the clients. Most
models are developed as research tools, are seldom used by other scientists and even less frequently by
non-scientists such as managers and policy makers (Sands 1988b) – although there is progress in this
area (Sands 2003). The question of model uses and users has been discussed at some length by Battaglia
and Sands (1998b), Landsberg (2003) and Sands (2003), while Peng (2000) discusses the use of
empirical and mechanistic models in relation to sustainable forest management in Canada. We will
222
Chapter 8

--- Page 235 ---

not consider this issue further, but emphasise that those who develop models describing aspects of tree
physiology and the growth of trees should be very clear about their objectives and target ‘audience’.
8.2 TYPES OF MODEL IN FOREST ECOPHYSIOLOGY
This book is concerned with the physiological processes that underlie and drive forest growth. There
are three main types of forest growth model: empirical, process-based or mechanistic and hybrid. Note
that, in some cases, process-based and hybrid models overlap – the differences are not always clear-cut.
We are not primarily concerned with conventional, empirical forest growth and yield models, but argue
that they can either be supplanted, or at least significantly improved upon, by models that are based on
or incorporate physiological processes. Furthermore, hybrid models, which combine process-based
models (PBMs) with statistically based empirical models, can improve on the predictions of both
process-based and empirical models by building on the strengths of each. In this section, we provide an
overview of these three classes of models.
The characteristics of each are outlined in relation to its purpose, and we review a small sample of
the models of each type that have been developed in forest ecophysiology in recent years, focusing on
those of their properties that are of particular interest in relation to their purpose. The study of Miehle
et al. (2009) is of interest because it illustrates how several models, including an empirical model,
perform on independent sites relative to the sites used in their development.
8.2.1. Empirical Models
Traditional empirical models of stand growth are derived by fitting standardised functions to extensive
bodies of often quite noisy observed data from stands. The goal of empirical models is to predict the data
necessary for economic management of the stand, rather than to understand how and why the stand is
what it is. No attempt is made to model the actual mechanisms underlying growth. However, they usually
predict growth at the sites and under the conditions for which they were developed with considerable
accuracy. These statistically based models are long-established as the standard tools of forest managers.
No explicit recognition is made of variations in environmental conditions during growth of the stand,
nor of differences in management applied to these stands (except possibly of thinning). It is therefore
difficult to extrapolate to localities with radically different climatic conditions or management regimes.
For instance, in a review of the development of growth models for Pinus radiata in New Zealand, Goulding
(1994) stated that because of the intrinsic site-specificity of their empirical growth models it was found
necessary to develop distinct models foreach of seven general regions in the country. He also noted that the
regional models differed from each other in both structure and parameterisation.
Variations in site productivity are taken into account through the concept of site index. The
definition of site index is commonly a measure of the height of the dominant or largest full-crowned
trees on the site at a specific age. For example, site index might be defined to be the mean height, in
metres, of the 20 largest trees in the stand at age 20 years. Site index is itself determined by a host of site-
specific factors, such as soil quality, climatic conditions, and establishment and post-establishment
management regimes. Empirical models do not provide a means to predict site index, rather it is
usually a necessary input.
Empirical models are usually stand-based and predict stand-averaged values, although some are tree-
based and predict values for single trees. Stand-level models typically predict height growth, stand volume,
basal area and mortality. They often also include assumptions about the distribution of stem diameters,
Modelling Tree Growth: Concepts and Review
223

--- Page 236 ---

and use this to disaggregate the basic outputs into stem diameter classes. Further, algorithms for managing
thinning, or predicting future growth following thinning, often form part of an empirical growth model.
There is a multitude of empirical models in the forestry literature; here we outline the properties of
two that take different approaches. The first, called NITGRO was developed by Candy (1997) for stands of
Eucalyptus nitens grown in Tasmania, Australia and New Zealand. The second model is CanSPBL, which
constitutes a stand level growth and yield model for P. radiata in New Zealand (Pinjuv et al. 2006).
a) NitGro – Empirical Growth Model for Eucalyptus nitens
The state variables of NITGRO are the mean dominant height H (m), the basal area B (m2 ha1), and
the stocking N (trees ha1) of live trees. Site index HS (m) is the mean dominant height at age tS¼15
years. The model has 11 equations and 14 parameters, values of which have to be determined by fitting
to inventory data. It requires as inputs values of mean dominant tree height, basal area and stem
population from inventory measurements, uses an asymptotic value of height and calculates changes in
mean dominant tree height over time. If site index is not provided from inventory, it can be inferred
from the height growth equations. Stand basal area is modelled as an asymptotic function of time,
starting from the basal area provided by an inventory. Changes in stem population (stocking) are
modelled as a simple exponential decline. Finally, stand volume at any time is calculated in terms of
height and basal area. Given inventory data NITGRO can be used to predict the expected time course of
stand volume, basal area and height (see Figure 8.1), or it can be used to predict the growth of stands
for which a site index is assumed but for which there are no inventory data.
A basic version of NitGro has been implemented as a spreadsheet application on the PHYSECOL
website.
b) CanSPBL, a Stand-Level Growth and Yield Model for Pinus radiata
CanSPBL is a system of non-linear least-squares regression equations which constitutes a stand-level
growth and yield model (Pinjuv et al. 2006). It is not a general model and is not transportable. It was
developed for P. radiata on South Island, New Zealand, where the trees are grown across a wide range of
geographical elevations. The equations of the model are complex and specific to the plantations for
which they were developed – the normal situation with such models. It is an example of a model
tailored to local requirements using non-linear least-square statistical procedures, with minimisation
