of errors and residuals in relation to the inventory measurements used to obtain the values of constants
and coefficients. The end result is specific to the plantations and data sets considered.
CanSPBL requires as inputs initial and final stand age, initial height, basal area and stem population,
and elevation (Pinjuv 2006). It uses 15 separate equations for the projection of stand tables and stand
level predictions, with six additional equations for tree level projections. Each of these equations has a
number of parameters and fitted constants (Pinjuv 2006). CanSPBL makes stand level predictions of
mean top height, basal area, stocking, volume, and diameter distribution at specified stand ages.
Diameter class distributions are described using a reverse Weibull function. Pinjuv expanded CanSPBL
by including in it a sub-model for water balance, taken from the 3-PG model (Landsberg and Waring
1997), and made detailed comparisons of the performance of both CanSPBL with and without the
water balance, against another statistically based model (CANTY) and 3-PG, using more than 960
observations to test the results. We discuss some of these comparisons in Chapter 9.
8.2.2. Process-Based or Mechanistic Models
A PBM can be defined as a description of the behaviour of a system in terms of a set of functional
relationships (e.g. plant growth processes) and their interactions with each other and the system
environment. These functional relationships are usually chosen at a level, in the hierarchy of processes
that make up the system, one below the entire system. The central problem when formulating a PBM is
to capture the essential features of the processes at levels and with response times that are useful in
explaining variation and the influence of external conditions, so that integration over time leads to
values for the measurable variables in which we are interested.
There is a multitude of processes, at a range of levels, involved in the growth of trees. The first
decision to be made in developing a PBM is which are the dominant, or first order, processes that we
know enough about to describe in quantitative terms, and which we can safely assume are not
important in determining tree growth. There is now almost universal agreement about these matters,
and the processes we have focussed on through this book reflect that consensus.
PBMs are also classed as mechanistic models. This implies that the behaviour of the entire system is
simulated in terms of a series of sub-models describing the causal processes (mechanisms) that
determine that behaviour, and the responses of those processes to changes in environmental inputs.
Most of the measurements and observations made on plants in the field describe the state of the plant
in terms of attributes such as mass (if harvested), height, size, foliage area, tissue nutrient concentra-
tions, or stage of development at some point in time. The values obtained from such measurements or
observations are, therefore, usually plotted against time. There is a fundamental flaw in this procedure
because time is not a driving variable, i.e. the change in state of a plant is not dependent upon time per
se, it is the end result, or integral, of the rates of and interactions between those processes that affect the
observed state. For example, changes in mass are caused by the absorption of CO2 and the formation of
carbohydrates, or by losses due to respiration. Carbohydrates are transported to different parts of the
plants and alter the mass of roots, branches or foliage. Bud development is the precursor to the
formation of flowers and fruiting bodies. The aim of a PBM is to describe changes in the state of plants
in terms of these processes.
The driving variables – the independent factors that determine the rates of particular processes – are
invariably environmental. Photosynthesis, often regarded as the primary plant process, is driven by
radiant energy; respiration rate is affected by temperature; the rate of nutrient supply to root systems is
affected by water movement to the roots and by rates of nutrient diffusion. The change of state of plants
with time in terms of these factors may be formally stated as a differential equation
Modelling Tree Growth: Concepts and Review
225

--- Page 238 ---

dy
dt ¼ f x
ð Þ;
ð8:1Þ
where t is time, y is a state variable and f(x) is a function of various quantities x such as exogenous
environmental factors. The state of the plant at time t can, at least in principle, be determined by
integration:
y tð Þ ¼
ð
f tð Þdt:
ð8:2Þ
In fact the situation is seldom so simple, and we often deal with cases where there are many state
variables, and the functions f also depend on the state variables themselves. One of the basic problems in
biological research is to determine the forms of the f(x), i.e. to establish the relationships between the
rate of change of each state variable and the state variables themselves and the amount, intensity or
concentration of the external variables or factors. It is these relationships that are used in models, and
often form sub-models of the overall model. The relationships can become extremely complex, and
unmanageable using analytical mathematical techniques unless some drastic simplifications can be
made; numerical techniques frequently have to be used to perform the integrations implicit in Eq. (8.2).
At the level of the sub-models, descriptions of process behaviour are usually empirical. In many
cases, it may be possible to model the processes involved in terms of even lower level processes. For
example, we could model stomatal responses in terms of guard cell water potential and hydraulic
feedback loops between foliage and stem conducting systems (see Franks 2004, and Chapter 7), or we
could model respiration in terms of its biochemistry and the costs of tissue synthesis (see Penning de
Vries 1975). But de Wit (1970), many years ago, warned against modelling systems across more than
two levels of organisation, and even the vast increase in computing power since then does not justify
the procedure. Processes two levels below that which provides the result of final interest almost
certainly require numerous additional parameters and are likely to have response times that differ
from the overall processes (tree and stand growth) by several orders of magnitude (see Table 2.1). It will
be difficult to estimate parameter values, and the complexity of the model will lead to increased
uncertainty in the outputs.
a) Light-Use Models
The simplest PBM of forest growth in terms of carbon balance is the radiation (light) utilisation model,
described by the equation
Pn ¼ e y; N; T
ð
Þ’abs  R;
ð8:3Þ
where Pn is net primary production (NPP), the carbohydrate that forms biomass, and R is total
respiration. The first term on the right-hand side is gross primary production, where e is the light-
use efficiency describing the conversion of absorbed PAR (APAR, ’abs) to carbohydrates, and y, N and
T denote the effects of soil water, nutrition and temperature, respectively. These factors modify the
efficiency e with which ’abs is converted to carbohydrates. Implementation of Eq. (8.3) clearly involves
a high degree of empiricism.
An early example of the use of a light-use model to examine forest growth is the Byrne et al. (1986)
analysis of monthly biomass increments in P. radiata grown at three climatically distinct sites. The
light-use efficiency e was found to be a function of soil water status only.
226
Chapter 8

--- Page 239 ---

Ruimy et al. (1994) showed that this model could be used to estimate above-ground NPP at global
scales using remote sensing data; they surveyed all the available literature and provided values of a for a
number of the major terrestrial biomes. They also provided a table of ratios of below-ground to above-
ground NPP found in the literature. Landsberg et al. (1997) provided a detailed analysis of the use of
Eq. (8.3) and of the values of e in the literature, with particular reference to forests.
Gower et al. (1999) reviewed direct and indirect methods of estimating leaf area index (L), APAR
and NPP. They summarised the literature, corrected for inconsistencies in the units of radiation, dry
matter and carbon allocation, and provided a more detailed and comprehensive set of values of e. They
also provided additional analysis of the ratios of above-ground to total biomass production. None of
these studies could offer values for R but, given an estimate of L and calculating APAR from Beers Law,
the information they provide is sufficient to allow Eq. (8.3) to be applied to obtain practical estimates
of forest productivity across large areas and over intervals of, say, a month or more.
Most PBMs are significantly more complex than Eq. (8.3). The increased generality and predictive
power is gained by describing the processes involved in growth in more detail. This comes at the price
of many more parameters and state variables, for which values have to be found. The processes that
have received most attention are radiation interception and canopy photosynthesis: all PBMs
concerned with carbon balance must calculate the assimilation of carbon, and its subsequent distribu-
tion between the different pools in the stand. However, this distribution – or allocation – of carbon, is
much less well understood than radiation interception and canopy photosynthesis. Consequently, a
variety of allocation mechanisms are used in complete forest carbon balance models.
Many of the better known process-based forest growth models are for homogeneous canopies or
stands. The characteristics of some of these are outlined below.
b) FOREST-BGC and BIOME-BGC
Of the detailed canopy/stand forest models, one of the earliest was FOREST-BGC, developed by
Running and Coughlan (1988). It required as inputs L and daily maximum and minimum tempera-
tures, daily average relative humidity, daily total precipitation and shortwave radiation. FOREST-BGC
in its original version has 20 state variables and requires 41 parameter values. The model simulates the
carbon cycle by calculating daily carbon and hydrological balances and transferring the net annual
balances to a yearly compartment where allocation of available carbon to leaves, stems and fine roots
takes place with the allocation patterns controlled by leaf water status and nitrogen. The state of the
trees is updated and this information is transferred back to the daily compartment. The soil carbon and
nitrogen subroutine was based on CENTURY (Parton et al. 1987, 1988; see Section 6.3.1).
FOREST-BGC has been widely used and further developed. Running and Gower (1991) replaced the
entire yearly half of the original model with dynamic and interacting carbon and nitrogen budgets.
They also made it possible to change the yearly time step, e.g. to a weekly step. Running and Hunt
(1993) generalised FOREST-BGC to a more general ecosystem process framework called BIOME-BGC,
with the primary goal of calculating water, carbon and nitrogen cycles through an ecosystem. A recent
example of a study using BIOME-BGC is reported by Chiesi et al. (2007). They describe the application
of the model across the Tuscany region of Italy, and discuss the meteorological and remote sensing
input data required and the calibration of the model.
Both FOREST-BGC and BIOME-BGC require large numbers of parameter values and inputs, not
least of which is daily weather data. Running (1994) commented, in relation to the use of FOREST-
BGC: ‘‘It is extremely difficult and costly to obtain rigorous parameterization and complete validation
data for complex ecosystem models. . .’’ But, despite these difficulties and complexities, FOREST-BGC
and its successor, BIOME-BGC, are important models.
Modelling Tree Growth: Concepts and Review
227

--- Page 240 ---

FOREST-BGC also provided the basis for a hybrid model, TREE-BGC (Korol et al. 1996), which
incorporated some modifications of the physiological routines and allocated carbon on the basis of
stand volume, diameter and height measurements made in forest plots. STAND-BGC (Milner and
Coble 1995) was also derived from FOREST-BGC, using essentially the same physiological processes
and climate drivers, but simulating the physiology for shrubs and grasses within the stand, as well as
trees. Milner et al. (2003) linked STAND-BGC to a Forest Vegetation Simulator to produce a ‘hybrid
biometrical–physiological model that could potentially be used in forest planning analyses. . . The
validation results were encouraging’.
c) BIOMASS
Another important PBM of the growth of forest stands was BIOMASS (McMurtrie et al. 1990).
BIOMASS uses a daily time step and includes a detailed photosynthesis model based on dividing the
canopy into three horizontal layers of equal depth, using a two-stream (direct and diffuse) radiation
interception model. Photosynthesis in each layer is calculated from a hyperbolic light–response rela-
tionship, with temperature corrections and allowance for foliage nitrogen concentrations. BIOMASS
includes water balance, respiration and carbohydrate allocation functions based on empirically deter-
mined fractions of dry matter allocated to leaves, stems branches, bark and litterfall. Transpiration
is calculated using the Penman–Monteith equation (see Chapter 2) with stomatal conductance in
each layer corrected for soil water content, atmospheric vapour pressure deficit and photon flux density.
In 1992, McMurtrie et al. introduced the Farquhar and von Caemmerer (1982) model of leaf photosyn-
thesis and the Ball et al. (1987) stomatal model (see Chapter 3 for both of these) into BIOMASS.
BIOMASS, like FOREST-BGC, requires a large number of input and parameter values. It has been
extensively used as a research tool; McMurtrie et al. (1992b), and McMurtrie and Wang (1993)
compared results for MAESTRA (Chapter 5) and BIOMASS as tools for modelling canopy photosyn-
thetic productivity in relation to rising CO2 concentrations and temperatures. Bergh et al. (1998)
modified BIOMASS to incorporate low-temperature effects – identified from field and laboratory
work – on photosynthetic production. They parameterised the model for Norway spruce (Picea abies)
and used it to explore the climatic factors constraining growth in northern Sweden. In a later paper,
Bergh et al. (2003) used BIOMASS to quantify the likely effects of increased temperatures and
atmospheric CO2 concentrations on coniferous and broad-leaved tree species growing in different
Nordic countries. BIOMASS has generally performed well where it has been parameterised for
particular species and regions and comparisons have been made between observed and modelled forest
growth values. For examples see: McMurtrie and Landsberg (1992) for a study on P. radiata; Hingston
et al. (1998), who used the model to assess water use by Eucalyptus globulus trees in Western Australia;
and Hingston and Galbraith (1998), who used it to calculate stemwood production and seasonal tree
growth, with empirical relationships obtained from test plots to allocate carbohydrates to stems,
branches, leaves and roots.
d) CenW
Kirschbaum (1999) developed a complete forest stand growth model (CenW) which includes
carbon allocation, plant respiration, senescence and loss terms, water balance and nitrogen uptake
and dynamics, and runs on a daily time step. Available N is calculated with a variant of CENTURY
(Parton et al. 1987). Kirschbaum used the results obtained by Sands (1995b; see Section 5.2.4a) to
calculate daily canopy photosynthesis. The effects of CO2 and temperature on photosynthesis are
described by functions affecting parameters of the leaf-level light-response used by Sands. The model
incorporates the Ball et al. (1987) stomatal conductance model and uses the Penman–Monteith
equation to calculate transpiration. CenW is primarily a PBM, but includes conventional equations
for tree size.
228
Chapter 8

--- Page 241 ---

CenW was carefully calibrated and tuned using data from an experiment on P. radiata which
included fertility and water treatments (the Biology of Forest Growth experiment, described in
Section 6.2.3c) so that it realistically simulated water use, foliage production and turnover, nutrient
dynamics, and wood production. Kirschbaum said ‘‘the model can be used to simulate the effects of
silvicultural treatments, such as stand thinning, or to assess the sustainability of long-term site
productivity based on simulation of the nutrient budget of forest stands’’. In other studies with this
model (e.g. Kirschbaum et al. 2007 and references cited therein) CenW was used to investigate the
factors affecting the net exchange of CO2 between forest stands and the atmosphere, and various
possible effects of climate change on forest growth, including the effects of soil nutrients and their
feedback to forest productivity.
e) The ITE Edinburgh Model
The ITE Edinburgh model (Thornley 1991) is model of growth of a homogeneous forest that is
fundamentally different from virtually all other process-based forest models. It includes structural and
labile pools of carbon and nitrogen. The light incident on foliage at any depth in the canopy is
calculated by Beers law taking into account foliage transmissivity; leaf photosynthesis is represented
by a non-rectangular hyperbolic light–response, and an analytical expression is used for the integral of
leaf photosynthesis through the canopy. An effective soil nitrogen concentration is calculated, and
nitrogen uptake rate depends on the carbon substrate in the fine roots. The growth of foliage, branches,
stems, coarse and fine roots is determined by carbon and nitrogen substrate concentrations. The fluxes
of both substrates are calculated using a conductance multiplied by concentration differences,
with conductance scaled according to tissue sizes, which are constrained to specified maxima. Thornley
and Cannell (2004) described some modifications to the model, including allowing photosynthesis to
acclimate to environmental conditions (Thornley 1998, 2004), separation of components of respiration
(Thornley and Cannell 2000), and calculation of photosynthesis for sun and shade leaves (Thornley
2002).
Thornley’s aim was essentially heuristic, and he used the model as a tool to assess the value and
applicability of the transport-resistance approach to dry matter partitioning. However, it was cali-
brated to mimic the growth of a pine plantation in Scotland, and used to investigate the implications of
various forest harvesting regimes. Thornley and Cannell (1996) used it to examine the responses of the
pools and fluxes of carbon, nitrogen and water in a fully coupled temperate forest-soil system in the
uplands of Northern Britain to potential changes in atmospheric CO2 and temperature. More recently,
Thornley and Cannell (2004) examined the long-term effects of fire frequency on carbon storage and
productivity of boreal forests.
Despite these useful applications, the ITE Edinburgh model is very much a research tool, and
requires a multitude of parameter values. It is unlikely that a model with this structure will be widely
adopted or used as a forest management tool.
f) ProMod
PROMOD was developed by Battaglia and Sands (1997) to predict the quality of sites for the
establishment of plantations, as expressed by their mean annual volume increment (MAI). The
minimum inputs required are of a quality and quantity that forest managers can readily and cheaply
obtain for screening prospective sites: latitude, monthly means of standard meteorological data, and a
classification of the soil with respect to its depth, texture, stoniness, drainage, and fertility. However,
PROMOD can be run using daily weather data and hence predict the seasonal variation of production. It
was initially parameterised for E. globulus using physiological measurements, and tested against
E. globulus plantations grown in Tasmania and Western Australia. It has also been parameterised for
and applied to E. nitens and P. radiata.
Modelling Tree Growth: Concepts and Review
229

--- Page 242 ---

The primary outputs from PROMOD are the peak MAI following canopy closure, the peak canopy
LAI, and the seasonal variation of NPP, evapotranspiration, water-use efficiency and soil water content
(or average or total values of these). An indication of biomass partitioning around the time of peak
MAI and the relative effects of different environmental factors in limiting production can also be
obtained. The model can be used on a plot-level basis, is ideally suited for spatial applications, and can
be driven by externally observed canopy LAI.
Closed-canopy leaf area index is calculated from the mean annual rainfall and temperature at the
site, and a simple rating of site fertility (see Section 4.4.3a). The light-limited GPP is then calculated
using the Sands (1995b, 1996) daily canopy photosynthesis model, taking account of diurnal
temperature variation and seasonal photosynthetic acclimation. Canopy respiration is temperature
dependent and based on data for E. globulus in Pereira et al. (1986). A simple soil water balance
model determines evapotranspiration from observed pan evaporation and a crop factor, which is a
function of soil water content. Water-limited NPP is then determined from evapotranspiration and
water-use efficiency, which is a function of VPD. Actual NPP is taken as the minimum of the light-
and water-limited values.
The observed peak MAI from nine plots of E. globulus in south-eastern Tasmania and in Western
Australia was fitted to an empirical function of the predicted NPP to provide a means for converting
NPP to a variable of direct interest to a forest manager. PROMOD has also been combined with the
empirical growth model NITGRO to provide a hybrid management tool (see below).
PROMOD has been used as a research tool, and for decision making or forest management in
Australia (Sands et al. 2000). It is also currently the basis of industrial plantation management in
parts of Portugal. Battaglia and Sands (1998a) analysed the sensitivity of PROMOD’s outputs to the
structure of the model, and to its species-specific parameters. Their results show which parameters
need to be determined to adopt the model for new species, and suggest how seedlings could be selected
for optimising productivity. Sands et al. (1999) used PROMOD to study the effects of the amount,
frequency and timing of irrigation on the growth and economics of plantation grown E. globulus in the
Murray-Darling basin, Australia. Mummery and Battaglia (2001) applied PROMOD spatially to screen
for prospective E. globulus plantation sites in Tasmania and Battaglia et al. (2002) examined the cost
effectiveness of using tools such as PROMOD to predict the spatial variation of productivity across a site
prior to planting. It was shown, on the basis of two plantations that were established without any prior
site analysis, that even a low intensity soil survey would have prevented the worst of the land being
selected for plantation development. The cost of the survey was more than recouped in savings on stock
and planting.
PROMOD was also used extensively by Daryl Mummery in his PhD thesis (Mummery 2009) to
explore a range of general issues associated with the spatial application of models such as PROMOD in
forest management. First, PROMOD was used as a tool to enhance the interpretation of results from a
large-scale forestry experiment in Tasmania (Mummery et al. 1999). It was found that if terrain-derived
indices of waterlogging and between-plot variation in soil depth were used as inputs to PROMOD, it
successfully accounted for variations in tree growth. Second, a general method was developed (Mum-
mery and Battaglia 2001) to enable spatial estimates of uncertainty in predicted site quality to be made
based on variations of uncertain variables such as soil depth and fertility. Another study (Mummery
and Battaglia 2002) examined the effects that the spatial resolution of soil and climate data have on the
ability of a model such as PROMOD to adequately predict the spatial variation of productivity. The
results indicated that PROMOD is not scale dependent and, even with the use of broad-scale input data,
it provided an unbiased estimate of mean productivity for studies covering large areas. However, since
sub-grid variance at broad scales can be very large, particularly for soil variables that can change
abruptly over short distances, specific applications may require higher resolution inputs.
230
Chapter 8

--- Page 243 ---

g) Cabala
The PBM CABALA (Battaglia et al. 2004) evolved from attempts to make PROMOD a fully dynamic
forest growth model, and is considerably more complex than PROMOD. CABALA has been extensively
tested using data from a wide range of E. globulus plantations across Australia. It predicts stand
development, the changing patterns of biomass allocation, water use, soil and stand nutrient dynamics
of plantations in response to climatic factors, soil water and nutrient availability. It also predicts
responses to silvicultural options such as fertilisation, thinning or pruning, and responses to disease or
pest damage affecting the foliage.
CABALA is a complex model with a daily time step. It has pools for the functional foliage, sapwood
and fine-root biomass, and for the structural stem, branch and coarse-root biomass. Litterfall and fine-
root turnover are determined by environmental stresses such as waterlogging, water stress and frost,
where frost affects only the young, outer leaves, whereas other stresses affect older leaves. Gross canopy
photosynthesis is calculated using the Sands (1995b) daily canopy production model, fully linked to
stomatal conductance via the Ball–Berry stomatal model (Ball et al. 1987; Leuning 1990). Production is
affected by environmental factors such as temperature, VPD and soil water status, and by foliage
nitrogen concentration. Respiration accounts for both construction and maintenance costs, where the
latter are temperature dependent functions of tissue nitrogen content. Allocation of NPP is on a daily
basis so as to minimise any functional imbalance between carbon capture by leaves and water and
nutrient uptake by fine roots, while ensuring there are sufficient transport organs and that various
structural constraints are satisfied.
Water use is based on the Penman–Monteith equation, and soil water balance uses a detailed
multilayer soil water model with standard equations for water movement through soils (e.g. see
Chapter 7). Water stress is based on the relative water content of the zone occupied by roots, and
water uptake is controlled by soil water potential and fine-root mass. Nitrogen comes from various
sources: atmospheric deposition, fertiliser application and mineralisation of organic nitrogen from the
soil, humus or litter. Supply to the plant is ultimately determined by net mineralisation of soil nitrogen,
and decomposition of organic matter derived from litter and turnover, using the CERES-N model of
Goodwin and Jones (1991). Uptake is determined by supply rate and fine-root biomass. Thus, CABALA
is a fully linked plant–soil model, accounting for the supply and demand of carbon, nitrogen and water.
CABALA was initially parameterised for E. globulus largely from the results of physiological experi-
ments. Agreement between its predictions and observed stand growth was excellent under a wide range
of conditions and silvicultural regimes (Battaglia et al. 2004). CABALA appears to capture the observed
temporal response of stands to both climatic and site factors, and to silvicultural interventions across a
wide range of sites. In particular, it provides reliable predictions of thinning responses, and of the
responses to extreme conditions under which other models fail. The model has been used to study
issues of soil water management of plantation grown E. globulus in southwest Western Australia,
including the development of management regimes to minimise risk of drought death and to
ameliorate the effects of soil–water drawdown on growth of the next rotation as explored in White
et al. (2009).
CABALA has also been used as a research tool to highlight the pitfalls of using long-term mean
climatic data as input to PBMs. This is especially important when the focus is on a study of risk, e.g.
drought death due to water stress, because the manner in which daily rainfall is distributed can
critically affect the predicted yields or measures of stress (Mummery and Battaglia 2004). CABALA is
also being used to predict the consequences of insect damage or disease affecting foliage extent and
function under present and future climates (Pinkard et al. 2010). Recently, output from CABALA has
been used as input to a model called CAMBIUM that predicts wood formation at the xylem level, and
hence wood properties (Downes et al. 2009; Drew et al. 2010).
Modelling Tree Growth: Concepts and Review
231

--- Page 244 ---

In common with all the other PBMs discussed here, CABALA requires a large number of state
variables, input variables and parameter values. However, it is being applied with some success to
matters of practical interest to forest management, and although its use has focussed on applications to
E. globulus, it is being applied to other species, and plantations outside Australia (M. Battaglia, personal
communication).
h) Modular-Hierarchical Forest Growth Models
An important group at the Department of Forest Ecology of the University of Helsinki is following a
modular-hierarchical approach to modelling forest growth. This views the forest as a collection of
individual trees, which means that the forest ecosystem must be considered as a hierarchical entity
consisting of different levels of organisation across temporal and spatial scales. The following summary
is based on Annikki Ma¨kela¨’s (2003) paper (see also the references cited therein).
The modular-hierarchical approach implies a relative isolation between the hierarchical levels. The
components at a particular level, are themselves hierarchical systems that are internally coherent but
only interact loosely with other components at the same level. For example, a stand is modelled as a
population of trees and each tree is modelled as an autonomous unit, with the behaviour of the stand
emerging from the behaviour of the trees and their interactions. The approach taken by the Helsinki
team is based on a carbon balance, including competition and differentiation among trees due to
resource depletion. At that scale, some important aspects of the performance of their models include (i)
realistic dynamic properties of trees and stands in the long term; (ii) realistic responses of the relative
growth rates and mortality of individuals in different positions, and (iii) realistic responses to changes
in ecophysiologically defined inputs.
Light interception and photosynthesis models provide carbon inputs, while allocation is based on
functional relationships, specifically on the pipe model (Valentine 1985; Ma¨kela¨ 1986), that induce
feedbacks so that increasing height increases the growth requirement of wood relative to foliage and
fine roots. This means that the partitioning pattern results in gradually diminishing growth potential as
the woody organs grow in length, and that there is a maximum height of trees, related to metabolic
parameters. As a result of the pipe model allocation pattern, the ratio of the live woody tissue to foliage
biomass increases with tree size. This produces a feedback effect, so that if specific maintenance
respiration rates are relatively constant the ratio of net to gross production will gradually decline.
The hydraulic limitation hypothesis (see Section 7.4) implies that the rate of photosynthesis declines as
a function of tree height, which has been included in the Helsinki models. These modular-hierarchical
models are being evaluated and applied to some management problems.
8.2.3. Hybrid Models
Models that contain both causal and empirical elements at the same hierarchical level are often
termed hybrid models (Ma¨kela¨ et al. 2000). Although many PBMs contain empirical relationships, we
use the term ‘hybrid model’ to mean a model that is essentially a combination of process-based and
empirical models, each in their own right. This implies that the flexibility, analytical and predictive
power, are provided by the PBM component, while the practical constraints and results of specific
interest to forest managers derive from the empirical model.
We
have already
noted
that both
FOREST-BGC
and
BIOMASS
have
been
combined
with mensuration data to become hybrid models. In this section, we consider three examples of
hybrid models – PROMOD–NITGRO (Battaglia et al. 1999), Forest 5 (Robinson and Ek 2003),
and TRIPLEX (Peng et al. 2002). Each of these illustrates the approach of hybridisation and
its potential value. The 3-PG model (Landsberg and Waring 1997) is also a hybrid model (and
232
Chapter 8

--- Page 245 ---

see Almeida et al. 2003), but since we deal with it in detail in Chapter 9 as an illustration and
summary of many points made through the book, we will not consider it here.
a) PROMOD–NITGRO Hybrid Model
We described NITGRO (Candy 1997) and PROMOD (Battaglia and Sands 1997) earlier in this Chapter.
PROMOD itself does not grow a stand, but Battaglia et al. (1999) combined them to form a hybrid model
that does grow a stand, and in a way more familiar to practicing foresters than do PBMs with an
emphasis on biomass rather than wood products. The hybrid model can be used to introduce
environmental variation and basic physiology into a traditional forest management system.
The primary output from PROMOD is the seasonal variation of NPP, and hence total annual NPP is also
predicted. We explained earlier that an empirical relationship between annual NPP and observed peak
MAI was established as part of the development of PROMOD. This allowed NPP, a physiological output, to
be re-interpreted as a variable of immediate interest to traditional forest management systems, in this case
peak MAI. The critical step in the development of the hybrid model was to use NITGRO to derive an
empirical relationship between the peak MAI of a stand and its site index (Battaglia et al. 1999). This
relationship is the link between the two models: PROMOD is used to determine total annual NPP at a site,
and hence its peak MAI. The aforementioned relationship then gives the site index, and NITGRO is used
precisely as it would be if the site index had been determined from a plot survey.
Battaglia et al. (1999) emphasise that errors in the prediction of height or volume using the hybrid
model are the result of cumulative errors from PROMOD when predicting NPP, and from NITGRO in not
accurately representing the growth trajectory of height and volume. They also emphasise that NITGRO
explicitly assumes tree form is a function of site index alone, which is not true. More complex purely
process-based growth models that predict biomass allocation dynamically avoid this problem. Alterna-
tively, the empirical model should be modified so that stem taper does respond to environmental factors.
b) Forest 5
Forest 5 (Robinson and Ek 2003) is an individual tree growth and dynamics model, developed and
parameterised for the Great Lakes region of the United States. It includes reasonably detailed descrip-
tions of physiology, soil and climate, and incorporates both empirical and physiologically based
modules derived from other models. It is complex and involves many sub-models, stand variables
and parameters, but is a notable attempt to use the information from measurements of forest growth
and knowledge of ecophysiological processes to produce a model that could, in principle, be used as a
management tool. We outline it very briefly here.
Forest 5 uses Ma¨kela¨’s (1997) individual tree-growth model, based on the pipe theory and functional
balance, on the basis that this is one of the few individual tree growth models that is both mechanisti-
cally based (most are entirely empirical), predicts tree dimensions, and is ‘amenable to management
questions’. At the start of a simulation, the model initialises the trees, climate, soil and the litter profile.
The litter profile is persistent, i.e. it is not reset annually, being added to by senescent foliage and fine
roots, and coarse woody material as trees die. Available soil nitrogen is linked to litter decomposition.
The climatic data are calculated for each month, and the climate growth multiplier for the year is
calculated from the monthly data at the start of the year. Soil water is set to field capacity at the
beginning of the year and evapotranspiration is calculated using a temperature-based relationship. Leaf
area index is proportional to crown mass, which is a non-linear function of crown surface area.
To calculate canopy photosynthesis, maximum canopy-level instantaneous photosynthetic rate is
estimated, based on nitrogen concentrations and projected specific leaf area, and scaled to an average
monthly rate. Carbon is allocated to individual trees, respiration is at individual tree level, and the
amount of carbon per tree is reduced by a linear function of height. Volume growth is simply the
Modelling Tree Growth: Concepts and Review
233

--- Page 246 ---

accretion of carbon mass per stem, converted to volume using wood density. Stem mortality is
calculated using empirical survival equations developed for various species in the forests of interest.
Robinson and Ek (2003) said that they evaluated Forest 5 as a scientific statement of knowledge.
They noted that the model simulates the growth and dynamics of individual trees, taking into account
the effects of nutrient balance (at least in terms of N) and climate on tree growth and stand dynamics.
This differentiates it from Ma¨kela¨’s (1997) model, which simulates the average tree, and from models
that only simulate whole stands, and permits more accurate depiction of inter-tree competition and
activities such as thinning.
c) TRIPLEX
TRIPLEX (Peng et al. 2002) combines three models developed separately by others, and therefore
follows the procedure advocated by Robinson and Ek (2003). TRIPLEX combines a canopy radiation
interception sub-model based on TREEDYN3 (Bossel 1996), the carbon production and partitioning
models from 3-PG (Landsberg and Waring 1997); a tree growth model (diameter, height allometry and
form factors from TREEDYN3); and the soil carbon decomposition model (CENTURY) of Parton et al.
(1988). It uses monthly climate data and works on a monthly time step.
Like the 3-PG model, TRIPLEX is intended to bridge the gap between empirical forest growth and
yield models and process-based C balance models. It is comprehensive without being complex, and
minimises the number of input parameters required, while capturing well-understood key processes
determining forest growth and important interactions among the C, N, and water cycles of complex
forest ecosystems (Peng et al. 2002). TRIPLEX simulates average stand characteristics rather than those
of individual trees, and can be parameterised for even or uneven-aged coniferous or broad-leaved
species. It produces outputs of direct interest to managers, in particular tree diameter, height, basal area
and volume, as well as outputs of interest to ecophysiologists, such as leaf area index, NPP, biomass
pools, and the soil C, N and water dynamics. Zhou et al. (2004) calibrated TRIPLEX for two study sites
in central Canada and assessed the feasibility of generalising the parameter values. Their study
‘‘presented a reasonable and balanced parameter generalisation procedure that did not lead to a
significant reduction of model accuracy, but did increase the model practicability. The comparison
of observations and simulations produced a good agreement for tree density, mean tree height, DBH,
soil carbon, above-ground and total biomass, net primary productivity (above-ground) and soil
nitrogen in both short- and long-term simulation’’. Zhang et al. (2008) adapted and applied TRIPLEX
to subtropical forests regions in Zhejiang Province, south-eastern China.
d) General Comments
Of the hybrid models discussed briefly here, the PROMOD–NITGRO model is the simplest and
probably the most flexible. It requires fewer input data and can be more easily calibrated and
parameterised. PROMOD–NITGRO is a stand model and lacks the individual tree component, but if
this product information is needed it can be incorporated by applying stem size distribution equations
to the outputs (see, for example, Landsberg et al. 2005).
Forest 5 is a complex model and, at this stage, appears more valuable as an illustration of what can be
done using and adapting a number of sub-models from different sources. The model provides no new
insights into the effects of physiological processes on tree growth. Robinson and Ek’s (2003) paper includes
very useful discussion of model performance in terms of (what they call) mensurational and ecological
diagnostics, which provide insights into various aspects of model performance. They also discuss a topic
generally neglected in evaluation of forest models: the question of emergent properties – phenomena
that emerge at one level of organisation, for example the stand, as a result of the interaction of units at a
lower level, for example the trees.
234
Chapter 8

--- Page 247 ---

TRIPLEX, designed expressly as a practical tool, can clearly produce results that correspond closely
to a number of variables describing the state and growth of forests for which it is calibrated (Zhou et al.
2004; Zhang et al. 2008).
8.3 DISCUSSION ARISING FROM EMPIRICAL, PROCESS-BASED
AND HYBRID MODELS
It is useful to gather together and discuss some points arising from the overview of the various models
presented earlier in this section.
8.3.1. Reinventing the Wheel
An important characteristic of Forest 5 (Robinson and Ek 2003; and discussed above) is that it was
constructed using established sub-models of the key growth processes identified as important for forest
growth and dynamics. The authors argue thatthis procedure saves a great deal of time by avoiding the need
to construct theirown sub-models, and say that‘‘a great deal of excellent work may go towaste every time a
research team constructs a new way of simulating processes rather than borrowing from the strength of the
past. Furthermore, by taking the modules out of theirown context and applying them in a new framework,
we were able to identify short-comings that may not have been obvious before’’. Robinson and Ek conclude
that it is reasonable to extract components from disparate forest growth and dynamics modelling
traditions, join them together in some way, and expect to be able to make defensible predictions.
We support this argument: most of the key growth processes have been modelled a number of
times – in some cases a great many times – and new models frequently do not add anything in terms of
understanding or simulation accuracy. In many cases, it is highly likely that modelling exercises are
restricted by the availability of high quality data to test and parameterise new sub-models. However,
care must be taken when combining sub-models from different sources to ensure that they are mutually
compatible in terms of temporal or spatial resolution, modelling time step, etc.
An area where there has been a great deal of work, and where there may be little justification for
‘re-inventing the wheel’ is light interception by canopies, canopy photosynthesis and the calculation of
GPP (see Chapter 5). The models available range from the simple use of Beers Law, through the Sands
(1995b) analytical treatment of light interception and canopy photosynthesis, to Norman’s (1982) two-
stream model and its use by De Pury and Farquhar (1997) in association with the Farquhar et al. (1980)
model of leaf photosynthesis, and by Chen et al. (1999). If detailed analyses of photosynthesis and
transpiration are to be carried out for canopies for which correspondingly detailed information on
foliage amounts and distribution is available, the model MAESTRA (see Medlyn 2004) is the ‘state of
the art tool’ and is available on the web (Medlyn and Duursma 2009). The recent treatment by
Duursma and Ma¨kela¨ (2007) led to a simple model to estimate mean annual light interception by
single unshaded trees, and by stands with Poisson-distributed trees. The model is useful in larger scale
applications because it is parameterised by available data without the need for additional empirical
parameters. It can also be used to study the effect of stand structure on mean annual light interception
and productivity. Useful analyses and comparisons of canopy radiation models, and conversion of
canopy intercepted radiation to photosynthate, are provided by Wang (2003) and Medlyn et al. (2003).
There are other areas where similar arguments apply, particularly, perhaps, in water relations.
Modelling Tree Growth: Concepts and Review
235

--- Page 248 ---

8.3.2. Parameterisation and Calibration
All models contain possibly many parameters, to which numerical values must be assigned, that
determine their detailed, quantitative behaviour. Different sets of values for these parameters lead to
different behaviours, generally subtly different, but sometimes so radically different that a casual
examination of the output suggests it is from a different model. A major role of the parameters of a
model is often to distinguish one species (of tree) from another. These are referred to as species-specific
parameters. An important part of the modelling process is that of assigning appropriate values to the
parameters of a model; these are may be subject to considerable uncertainty.
The terms ‘calibration’ and ‘parameterisation’ are often used synonymously. The difference is subtle,
but important. Calibration is the process of assigning values to all or some of the parameters of a
model. Ideally, this is achieved by directly assigning values measured in independent experiments, e.g.
gas-exchange analyses provided the photosynthetic parameters for use in PROMOD. Failing this, the
parameters are assigned values by the process of parameterisation: the parameters are systematically
varied, by hand or using a computer program, so as to minimise the sum of squares of the residuals
between observed and predicted data, i.e. so that model output best fits a set of observed data.
(Calibration is also the process of taking an already parameterised model and making a few basic
adjustments so that its output fits some other set of data.)
The processes of calibration and (especially) parameterisation are greatly aided by a sound
understanding of the model in question, and in particular of its structure. For example, the
maximum light-use efficiency ex and carbon use efficiency Yappear multiplicatively in the expression
for NPP in 3-PG (see Chapter 9). Thus, these two parameters cannot be distinguished unless
NPP and GPP are separately observed. Ideally, parameter estimation should also provide the
standard errors of the estimated parameters, and the correlations between the estimates. A more
in-depth discussion of parameter estimation, with particular reference to 3-PG, is available in Sands
(2004b).
As a general rule, a model should be calibrated at the level of its sub-models, preferably using
direct observation of the parameters in question (Passioura 1973). For example, for a model of
stomatal behaviour or leaf photosynthesis, foliage gas-exchange measurements may be used to
establish the parameter values appropriate to the species and conditions under study. Or nitrogen
mineralisation measurements in the soil may be made to establish the relationships between the
components of soil organic matter and nitrogen release rates, or to determine the coefficients of
uptake equations (see Section 6.2.1). However, lack of data, poor understanding of processes, or
problems with scaling-up, may make it necessary to estimate the parameters of sub-models using
empirical data relating to the whole system. This is frequently achieved by adjusting parameter
values to improve the fit between model output and observations at the level of the whole system.
Ma¨kela¨ et al. (2000), in a paper dealing with PBMs as tools for forest management, discussed the
issue of calibration and noted that in many cases parameters have to be assigned by adjusting their
values until model output best fits a set of observations. In conclusion they commented that ‘‘for the
development of operational models, it is necessary to give up belief in a dichotomy between process-
based and empirical modelling, and accept that all models can have both empirical and causal
components at the system level’’.
A general point emerged from our brief summary of the PBMs that we have considered: all of them
have been parameterised so that they reproduced as accurately as possible the growth of the stands
from which the parameter and input values were obtained. However, there are acknowledged difficul-
ties in generalising these models so that they can be applied with confidence to forest stands for which
236
Chapter 8

--- Page 249 ---

they have not been specifically calibrated. Nevertheless, progress is being made in this direction
(see Landsberg et al. 2003; Fontes et al. 2006), and many PBMs do very well when tested against data
from independent stands or experiments. Contrast this with conventional, empirical forest models
which are, virtually without exception, specific to the forest types, areas and general management
protocols for which they were developed, i.e. they are not generally applicable.
8.3.3. Why Use Process-Based Models?
The rationale for PBMs is almost always that, because they are written in terms of the mechanisms that
govern tree growth and respond to environmental variables, they allow exploration of the consequences
of a range of conditions outside that for which empirical data and experience are available. So, having
confidence in the performance of the model in observed situations, we can use it to extrapolate to
others. We argue that PBMs contribute to improved knowledge and understanding of how trees grow,
taking it for granted that such improved understanding must lead, sooner or later, to an improved
ability to predict responses to conditions we have not observed. This argument has particular
resonance in relation to rising atmospheric concentrations of CO2, and likely changes in temperature
and the probability of drought resulting from climate change.
We are not alone in our bias towards PBMs. Korzuhkin et al. (1996) compared empirical and PBMs
and also concluded that the latter offer significant advantages when managing forest ecosystems.
Johnsen et al. (2001) discussed the use of PBMs as tools in both research and management in forestry,
and also favoured their use, but noted that their ability to usurp empirical models as the primary forest
management tool requires that relatively simple standards of nutrition will have to be developed (recall
Section 6.4).
The argument about increased understanding is not universally accepted. A notable sceptic (Zeide
2003), commenting on ‘‘the acknowledged fact that PBMs may provide mainly qualitative descriptions
of stand growth, and that their aim may be to improve knowledge rather than accurately or precisely
predict outcomes’’, said: ‘‘The separation of understanding and prediction is false. Only meaningful
models can be consistently accurate. There is a simple reason for inaccuracy: failure to understand the
studied object . . .The (analyzed) models do not use as predicting variables the readily available
inventory data and the most valuable heritage of forest science – long-term observations on permanent
plots’’. Zeide goes on to develop a concept of modelling labelled ‘the U-approach’, which is essentially
empirical, utilising some simple measure of environmental drivers or constraining factors – such as an
estimate of the light absorbed by the crowns of a stand incorporated as an independent variable in an
empirical model of stem diameter increments. His approach is similar to that of (some of) the hybrid
models discussed in the next section but, while it adheres to the practicality of empirical models, it does
not exploit the power and flexibility of PBMs.
Zeide’s assertion that PBMs do not use ‘the most valuable heritage of forest science’ is contradicted
by a large number of recent studies in which PBMs have been calibrated and tested against mensuration
data, including long-term measurements in permanent plots. It has been demonstrated, again and
again, that they can reproduce and explain both spatial and temporal variations in forest growth,
reflected by such data (see Mummery et al. 1999; Waring 2000; Coops et al. 2001, 2007; Tickle et al.
2001a,b; Mummery and Battaglia 2001 2002; Landsberg et al. 2005; Fontes et al. 2006; Waring et al.
2008). We also note the increasingly recognised value and potential of PBMs, linked to satellite
measurements, for predicting forest productivity over wide areas using PBMs (see Nightingale et al.
2007; Smith et al. 2008; Waring et al. 2010).
Modelling Tree Growth: Concepts and Review
237

--- Page 250 ---

8.4 MODEL EVALUATION: TESTING AND SENSITIVITY ANALYSES
Models are tested in various ways during their construction, and the terms verification, validation
and testing are variously used. Detailed discussion of model testing procedures and sensitivity
analysis would take us too far from the subject matter of this book, so we provide here only some rather
cursory remarks to outline the main points that need to be considered in relation to modelling the growth
of trees and forests. For a provocative discussion of the issue of model testing see Passioura (1973).
8.4.1. Model Testing
Empirical, mensuration-based growth and yield models are essentially statistical descriptions of
particular stands. Testing them is likely to consist of statistical evaluation of the extent to which
modelled values correspond to the observed values obtained from measurements not used in the
development of the model. The essence of testing (and this applies to all models) lies in the
requirement that model performance should be evaluated in relation to data that were not used in
the development and calibration of the model.
Four methods of testing the performance of empirical models are Average Model Bias (AMB),
Model Efficiency (ME), residual analysis and regression analysis (Pinjuv et al. 2006). The equations for
AMB and ME are:
AMB ¼ 1
n

2
P Yi  Yi
ð
Þ2 ;
ð8:5Þ
where the summations are over all n observations, Yi are the observations, ^Y i are modelled values, and
Yi is the average observed value. Residual analysis consists of evaluating the statistical significance of
differences between all individual observed and modelled values. These may also be plotted against, say,
the modelled values of the variables and tested for statistical distributions, bias and goodness of fit.
Regression of ^Y i on Yi and assessing the fit in terms of the variance accounted for by the regression is a
useful and frequently used test. The slope of the relationship should be approximately unity.
The terms verification and validation are sometimes used to describe separate processes. (Whether
this is helpful is, perhaps, debatable.) Verification is a process that checks that the formal structure of a
model is correct, and that its qualitative, dynamic behaviour is as expected. Validation, on the other
hand, demonstrates that the model provides good quantitative agreement with observed data, consis-
tent with the purpose for which the model is intended. To validate a model therefore involves testing its
performance against specified criteria, which in turn implies that the purpose for which the model has
been developed is precisely specified. Rykiel (1996) discussed the concept of validation at some length.
He argued, among other things, that: models can be validated as acceptable for pragmatic purposes,
whereas theoretical validity is always provisional; validation can be a useful activity regardless of
whether the model is declared validated or invalidated; and validation is not an essential activity for
evaluating research models, but is important for building model credibility in the user community.
The outputs of both process-based and hybrid models may be tested using the same methods as for
empirical models. Testing the output of a forest growth model as a whole may be done using direct
measurements of state variables, such as biomass pools or a soil water balance. Where such data are not
238
Chapter 8

--- Page 251 ---

available, biomass surrogates such as stem diameter or shoot length measurements, at intervals of days
or weeks, can be used. The important point is that there is a strong correlation between the surrogate
and the state variable, e.g. between DBH and stem biomass.
Individual relationships, be they purely empirical or mechanistic, are tested in some sense when they
are postulated. When combined in a model, it should be verified that their behaviour remains as
expected. This is especially the case where an empirical relationship, that may be statistically sound but
not necessarily based on biological understanding, is used. If such a relationship is included in a model,
and the inputs move outside the range of values for which the relationship was derived, the result may
be catastrophic behaviour. For example, a response may be observed to be linear or weakly quadratic,
whereas if pushed with an extreme input it may saturate. This is also important when combining sub-
models from different models, as suggested above.
All models should also be tested at the level of their sub-models, if this is possible, across intervals
appropriate to the response times of those processes. As an example, radiation interception models can
obviously be tested by measuring the radiation regime above, within and below canopies and compar-
ing the results with profiles modelled using only above-canopy radiation data and the leaf area
distribution (see Chapter 4). A leaf photosynthesis model, formulated using, for example, parameter
values from the literature, may be tested by measuring photosynthesis and the environmental variables
that affect it. If it is found necessary to alter the parameter values for subsequent modelling exercises,
then the measurements are being used for calibration, not testing.
Bernier et al. (2002) provided a good example of testing the transpiration sub-model of a canopy
gas-exchange model. They measured hourly values of air temperature, humidity, wind speed, net
radiation and precipitation, as well as in situ measurements of leaf gas-exchange, canopy leaf area index
and leaf mass distribution of sugar maple through a growing season. They also measured tree sap flux
and soil water content at a range of depths. These measurements provided them with data to test at
hourly intervals their detailed canopy gas-exchange model and the functions modifying stomatal
behaviour in response to soil water content, and to integrate the results over days and the season to
evaluate the longer term accuracy of their model.
Measuring the integrated output of a process-based growth model provides only a crude test of the
sub-models. It is quite possible that a model may produce the right answers for the wrong reasons: it
may contain compensating errors, particularly in relation to processes such as photosynthesis and
respiration (if both photosynthesis and respiration are too high), so the end result may be nearly
correct. Or incorrect allocation of carbohydrates may lead to apparently correct stem growth, although
root growth might be found to be very poorly simulated, if it could be measured directly. It is for this
sort of reason that PBMs should be tested both at the level of their sub-models, and at the level of the
model as a whole. If sub-models are parameterised using measurements in a particular stand, simula-
tions of the performance of that stand evaluated using the complete (whole-system) model may be
regarded as a legitimate – if not the most rigorous – test of a PBM.
8.4.2. Sensitivity Analysis
Sensitivity analysis is a powerful tool for examining issues relating to uncertainties in model
structure, or in input or parameter values. Inputs may be defined as the proposed values of any
parameter or state variable whose true value is uncertain. The objective of sensitivity analysis is three-
fold: to estimate the uncertainty in the model’s predictions caused by uncertainty in the values of
inputs; to examine the consequences of varying the model’s structure on its generality and predictive
power; and to determine the degree to which inaccuracies in their assumed values could lead to serious
errors in prediction.
Modelling Tree Growth: Concepts and Review
239

--- Page 252 ---

There are various approaches to sensitivity analysis. Green et al. (2000) outline and discuss the use of
Bayesian synthesis, which ‘‘may provide refined estimates of parameters and input values of a model,
particularly when the original values are obtained by indirect means’’. However, the procedure is complex
and time-consuming, and only rarely likely to be of value in the case of forest models. Simpler methods
are more usual and, in most cases, probably adequate. For example, with a few simple statements in a
model program, it is possible to cause the input and/or parameter values to cycle randomly between
maximum and minimum probable values. This process is known as a Monte Carlo sensitivity analysis
(e.g. see Ma¨kela¨ 1988). Different output values will be produced by each of many model runs, and the
influence of variations in the parameter and input values can be assessed. Obviously, in some runs, some
values can be held constant so that the influence of variation in other parameters can be assessed.
An alternative procedure was described in some detail by Battaglia and Sands (1998a). They carried
out structural and parameter sensitivity analyses on PROMOD, described earlier, assessing the effects of
simplifications in model structure, and variations in parameter values against model predictions for a
number of sites. The relative sensitivity s(X; p) of an output variable X with respect to a parameter p is
the change DX in X produced by a change Dp in p relative to the original values of X and p; i.e.
s X; p
ð
Þ ¼ DX
X
p
Dp :
ð8:6Þ
Relative sensitivity of a variable to a parameter is positive or negative depending on whether an increase
in the parameter leads to an increase or a decrease, respectively, in that variable. A relative sensitivity of
unity indicates that a given percentage change in the value of a parameter will result in the same
percentage change in the variable.
A structural sensitivity analysis of PROMOD was carried out by simplifying six sub-models and
evaluating the effects. For example, respiration in the model is a function of temperature, so it was
made independent of temperature; photosynthetic rates are influenced by changes in diurnal temperature,
so they were made independent of temperature; leaf area index varies with site index, so it was made
constant. Predicted mean annual increment was plotted against the MAI observed at a number of research
sites at widely different locations. The full model provided unbiased estimates of MAI across three
geographic regions with a high degree of correlation (r2¼0.88). Comparisons with simplified versions
indicated that, in general, simplification of the sub-models resulted in a loss of generality in PROMOD.
The parameter sensitivity analysis was based on predictions of annual net biomass production by
PROMOD, and was achieved by running the model for each of nine sites with the parameters set to their
standard values and then varied by 10%, 20% and 30% of the standard value. Analysis of the
effects of varying the parameter values revealed that these may differ between sites, as a result of the
responses of non-linear physiological responses to driving variables. Battaglia and Sands (1998a)
provided a means for quantifying these environmental variations.
The specific results obtained from the sensitivity analyses of this particular model are not the focus
of concern here. Rather, the exercise and approach provide a useful illustration of sensitivity analysis
and the contributions it can make to assessing the performance of complex models, and improving
those models. The procedure described by Battaglia and Sands has also been used by Almeida et al.
(2004a) and Esprey et al. (2004).
