In this chapter, we present and discuss in detail the model known as 3-PG (Landsberg and Waring
1997), which has been mentioned at various points throughout this book. The acronym is an
abbreviation for Physiological Processes Predicting Growth. We focus on this particular model because
it is an archetypal, but simplified, canopy carbon balance model which has been, and is, widely
used around the world. There are a number of reasons for this widespread adoption. They
include the model’s structure, its focus, its data requirements, and the fact that the code is freely
available.
3-PG embodies the basic processes that underlie the growth of forests in a relatively simple
structure. It was written as a deliberate attempt to bridge the gap between conventional empirical
(mensuration-based) growth and yield models and process-based carbon balance models. It was also
written with the objective of producing a model that could be used in association with remotely sensed
data to scale estimates of stand growth to regions. 3-PG is a hybrid model in the sense of Section 8.2.3
because it combines elements of conservation-of-mass process-based models, with allometric con-
straints to ensure that biomass allocation reproduces typical observed patterns of biomass partitioning
in the stand. It uses empirical relationships based on measurements of trees to produce output of
immediate relevance to foresters. The time step is a month, and the data required to apply 3-PG are
basic and readily obtained.
Besides its simple, general structure, a significant factor in the widespread adoption of 3-PG has
been that implementations of the model have been made freely available to all who wanted to use it. For
a number of years now the basic stand-level model has been available as a spreadsheet-based interface
(Sands 2004a), originally named 3PGPJS but now known as 3PGXL. Implementation in a standard
spreadsheet environment provides the user with considerable flexibility with no need to modify
computer code (although the code can be accessed and edited if required). Normal spreadsheet
operations can be used to transform output, generate additional data, graph stand development and
examine how stand characteristics depend on 3-PG parameters. The code is supported by technical
reports and a willingness on the part of the original authors of both the original 3-PG model and the
3PGPJS software to provide help with their use and discuss problems that may arise. This open
approach to the software has resulted in widespread testing, improvement and use of the 3-PG
model and its interface. Our experience with 3-PG provides a lesson for those who argue that software
should be protected and licensed: in this case, at least, the benefits of freeware have far outweighed any
benefits that might have resulted from restrictions on the use of the model.
In this chapter, we provide an overview (Section 9.1) of why 3-PG has the structure it does, describe
that structure and summarise the various data and species-specific parameters required to run the
model. Section 9.2 discusses the assumptions that underlie the sub-models and functional relationships
used in it, and we discuss in Section 9.3 the manner in which species-specific parameter sets can be
established. Only basic equations are presented in the description of the model, but a full treatment is
available on the PHYSECOL web site.
Any model must consist of a set of statements that constitute hypotheses about the way the
system being modelled works. Wherever possible these should be in a form that is testable, either by
direct measurements designed to test particular sub-models, or indirectly by measurements that
evaluate the model as a whole at the level of its outputs. Accordingly, we provide in Section 9.3.3 a
description of how a species-specific parameter set, and 3-PG as a whole, can be tested. Applications
of 3-PG across a wide range of environments and species are summarised in Section 9.4. This
allows assessment of the extent to which it fulfils the criteria for evaluation, whether it provides
a framework within which we can set and evaluate current knowledge and information about
tree physiology and the factors that affect and determine stand growth, and whether it is a useful
practical tool.
242
Chapter 9

--- Page 255 ---

Finally, we consider in Section 9.5 changes that could be made to various parts of the model and
assess the implications of these changes in terms of the number and availability of the parameter values
that would be required in relation to possible gains in the accuracy and precision of predictions.
A fully functional implementation of 3-PG as an Excel application, and various subsidiary software
and additional documentation are freely available for download from the PHYSECOL web site.
9.1 AN OVERVIEW OF 3-PG
3-PG deals primarily with the biology and physiology of growth, but includes a module that converts
its biological output, especially the biomass pools, into data of immediate interest to forest managers.
The biological core of 3-PG is based on the generic model of Section 5.6.1, and includes sub-models for
NPP, biomass allocation, stem population dynamics and soil water balance. The manner in which the
details of the generic model are fleshed out to form 3-PG is the main topic of this section, together with
the basics of the conversion module.
9.1.1. The Basis for the Structure of 3-PG
Various decisions have to be made leading to the development of a carbon balance model of forest
growth. Amongst these are the temporal resolution of the output, the input variables, and the
likelihood that these will be readily available. These decisions will be influenced by the objectives for
the model: is it to be a research tool, intended to provide a framework within which to set current
knowledge and hypotheses, and to evaluate the implications of various assumptions; is it intended to be
a tool for analysing the responses of trees to particular stimuli or inputs; or is it to be a practical tool to
be used by forest managers?
In the case of 3-PG, the major objective was to develop a practical tool that is accessible to
forest managers, as well as to scientists. For this reason it was decided to use simplified
descriptions of biophysical processes, combined with empirical relationships derived from experiments
and measurements made over long periods in forests and plantations (Landsberg and Waring
1997). 3-PG was explicitly intended to bridge the gap between realistic but possibly complex
process-based forest growth models, and the empirical growth and yield models familiar to all forest
managers.
The level of simplification to be used suggested a time step of a month. This is long enough to allow
simulations to be run for long periods but short enough to reflect the effects of factors such as water
stress, or periods of low temperature, which would be masked by yearly time steps. However, a month is
too long to use a detailed radiation interception and canopy photosynthesis model that may require
hourly values of radiation and detailed information about canopy structure.
9.1.2. Input Data
Because of the intended use of 3-PG, it was clear that close attention had to be paid to the data required
to run the model. There are four classes of input data: weather data, site description, stand initialisation
and 3-PG parameters. The nature of these data is determined by the intended use and level of
simplification of the model.
The 3-PG Process-Based Model
243

--- Page 256 ---

a) Weather Data
The weather data used as inputs to 3-PG are monthly averages of maximum and minimum tempera-
ture and solar insolation, total monthly rainfall, and the number of rain and frost days in a month.
Daily insolation is a critical input; not all meteorological services routinely provide this but various
empirical relationships can be used to determine insolation from temperature data (see Section 2.2.1d
and Appendix 1). Other variables such as VPD can also be derived from temperature data. Whether
these are historical data or long-term averages depends on the context of the application.
Using monthly average weather data in 3-PG is an adequate procedure unless there is particular
interest in specific events. For example, fast-growing eucalypts in South America can reach harvestable
biomass in 6–7 years, but over such short rotations 1 or 2 months of drought can make significant
differences to annual increments and yield at harvest (see Almeida et al. 2004b; Mummery and
Battaglia 2004). Accordingly, and especially following the widespread adoption of 3-PG as a practical
management tool by plantation growers in South America, considerable emphasis is now placed on the
use of actual historical weather measurements in their applications, rather than long-term average data.
For species for which the growth periods to be simulated run into many years (possibly hundreds, in
the case of the long-lived conifers of the Pacific North West of the USA), average monthly weather data
for the sites of interest are generally used.
Atmospheric CO2 concentration can be provided as an optional factor, where the default is
350 ppm. Almeida et al. (2009) introduced into 3-PG a realistic, though admittedly simplistic, way
of taking changes in atmospheric CO2 concentration into account through its potential effects on light-
use efficiency and stomatal conductance. This is discussed further when we consider environmental
effects on growth (see Section 9.2.3).
b) Site-Specific Factors
The minimum site-specific data required as input to 3-PG are site latitude, a simple site fertility rating,
and the maximum amount of plant-available soil water (MaxASW) that can be stored in the soil
profile. The fertility rating is used because it is difficult to adequately characterise the fertility of a site,
and it is clearly unreasonable to expect managers to have spatially explicit descriptions of the soil
profiles in their stands. Both fertility and plant-available soil water affect the light-use efficiency of the
canopy, canopy conductance, and the balance between above- and below-ground biomass allocation.
The fertility rating is expressed on a scale from 0 on the poorest sites up to 1 for sites where nutrients
are not expected to limit growth. We discussed this in detail in Section 6.4.2 and noted that it is
generally difficult to establish quantitative relationships between soil chemical characteristics and tree
growth. Accordingly, 3-PG users usually depend on expert knowledge about sites and regions to
establish values for the fertility rating. Fontes et al. (2006) tested this procedure and found that the
ratings determined from conventional soil chemical analysis, and expert assessment of the data, gave
good results when 3-PG was used for the analysis of data from eucalyptus trials in Portugal.
The relationship between plant-available soil water and characteristics of the soil profile such as
depth, stoniness and texture was discussed in Section 7.1.2. Some implementations of 3-PG may
require these as input data and derive MaxASW from them.
An optional site-specific input is a measure of the salinity of the soil. This is specified by the
electrical conductivity of the soil, which increases as salinity increases. Growth is possibly unaffected by
low salinity up to some threshold, but then declines with increasing salinity. Beyond a second threshold
growth ceases and plant death occurs (e.g. see van der Moezel et al. 1989; Fung et al. 1998; Tattini et al.
2002).
244
Chapter 9

--- Page 257 ---

c) Stand Initialisation Data
Simulations can be started at any point in the growth cycle of a forest, and can run for as many years as
needed. The data required to initialise a stand for analysis using 3-PG include stocking (stem popula-
tion), stem, root and foliage biomass of the stand, and the available soil water in the root zone, all at
some initial stand age. The initial biomass data can be specified on a per hectare or a per tree basis.
If the stand is initialised at planting it is appropriate to use a standard weight for a seedling, and divide
the biomass of the seedling between the biomass pools in some standard way because the model has a
strong tendency to self-correct for errors in initial data (Sands and Landsberg 2002).
d) Species-Specific Parameters
The 3-PG parameters are primarily species-specific and serve to distinguish between different species.
These will be discussed during our description of the various sub-models of 3-PG and are summarised
in the 3-PG documentation on the PHYSECOL web site. There are also some unit conversion factors that
are generic.
9.1.3. Structure of 3-PG
The biological core of 3-PG is a model comprising sub-models for net primary production, allocation
of assimilates to the various biomass pools, stem population dynamics and the soil water balance. The
state variables are: biomass pools for foliage, stems and roots; stem number; and plant-available soil
water. Sub-models allow calculation of light-use efficiency e, the allocation ratios i, the loss rates gi,
and stem mortality gN (see the generic model in Section 5.6.1). We discuss these sub-models, and a fifth
sub-model – the conversion module that develops outputs specifically for forest management – in
detail in Section 9.2.
Implementations of 3-PG combine this core with other elements, usually for pre-processing
weather, site-specific and stand initialisation data, and post-processing outputs into other, and
generally management-oriented, outputs. This is illustrated in Figure 9.1.
The way weather, site and stand initialisation data are pre-processed depends on the available data
and the context of the implementation. The weather module may convert daily observations into
monthly averages, or determine the VPD and solar insolation from other data, or sum the rainfall and
count the number of rainy days, or other actions as needed. The site factor module uses available site
data to assign a fertility rating and determine the maximum plant-available soil water, e.g. from soil
texture and depth. The stand initialisation module assigns values for the state variables of the model at
some initial stand age, i.e. each biomass component, stem number and plant-available soil water. The
initial biomass values may be observed, obtained from empirical relationships, or derived from a
standard seedling at planting. Finally, the 3-PG parameter set for a species is determined by a detailed
process of parameter estimation, or a default set of parameters is calibrated for a specific circumstance.
9.1.4. Typical 3-PG Output Data
The primary outputs from 3-PG are its state variables: the biomass pools, stem number and plant-
available soil water. The model also provides stand-level outputs that will be familiar to forest
managers: e.g. stand basal area, main-stem volume, mean annual volume increment and mean
diameter at breast height (DBH). Depending on how 3-PG is parameterised, DBH can be either the
arithmetic or quadratic mean of single tree diameters. The latter is preferred. Outputs from 3-PG can
be either monthly or annually accumulated or averaged values.
The 3-PG Process-Based Model
245

--- Page 258 ---

Many other variables, of interest to the physiologist and researcher, are determined as part of the
process of updating the state variables. Probably the more important are gross primary production
(GPP), net primary production (NPP), stand evapotranspiration (ET) and canopy leaf area index
(LAI). Specific leaf area, the growth modifiers that take into account the effects of environmental
factors on NPP, canopy quantum efficiency and light-use efficiency, the individual allocation ratios and
biomass turnover rates, and various details in the soil water balance such as canopy rainfall interception
and runoff losses, all of which vary in time through model runs, are also available.
9.1.5. Spatially Explicit Versions of 3-PG
Coops et al. (1998a,b) modified the original 3-PG model so that remotely sensed observations can be
utilised as inputs to it. They called the modified model 3-PGS, the ‘‘S’’ symbolising the use of satellite
data in the model framework. 3-PGS is widely used in spatial applications, some of which are discussed
in Section 9.4.3.
The essential features of the modifications are that the model is driven by estimates of the fraction of
light intercepted by green canopies (fPAR) derived from the normalised difference vegetation index
(NDVI). This spectral vegetation index is calculated from the reflectance of radiation by the canopy in

models for estimating the growth modifiers and NPP, biomass allocation, stem number and stem mortality, and the soil water balance. The
management module converts the 3-PG state variables into outputs of importance to the forest manager, e.g. stem volume, height and MAI.
The details of the modules for pre-processing weather, site and initialisation data, and for processing 3-PG outputs depend on the data
available for the application and the focus of the implementation.
246
Chapter 9

--- Page 259 ---

the near infra-red and red wavelength bands, as measured by satellites. NDVI has been shown, both
empirically and theoretically (Kumar and Monteith 1982; Sellers 1985, 1987), to be related to the
fraction of PAR absorbed in vegetation canopies. NDVI may also be used to estimate the canopy LAI, in
which case Beer’s law is applied in the usual way. Monthly weather data for any pixel are obtained by
extrapolation of local temperature and rainfall records. Photosynthetically active radiation (PAR) is
estimated from global solar radiation, derived from established empirical relationships (e.g. see
Section 2.2.1b). Monthly constraints on productivity are calculated from the standard 3-PG growth
modifiers. 3-PGS partitions NPP into root and above-ground foliage and stem mass, but eliminates the
stem number and size calculations.
In a recent paper, Waring et al. (2010) review the information needed by 3-PGS that can be obtained
from various sensors on satellites, and discuss how these data can be used to delineate forests with
similar structural properties, assess nutrient limitations on canopy photosynthetic capacity, and
evaluate interactions between tree and soil properties that affect above-ground NPP. Waring et al.
outline the progress being made through the use of remote sensing technology to improve land cover
classification, and to get better estimates of climatic drivers, leaf area index, canopy nitrogen and
chlorophyll concentrations, quantum efficiency (by monitoring changes in xanthophyll pigments), and
biomass and above-ground growth. They comment that ‘‘we see advantages in using process-based
models, such as 3-PGS, in concert with remotely sensed measurements of biomass to provide
explanation for seasonal and inter-annual variation in growth and water use’’.
Another spatial version of 3-PG, called 3PG-Spatial, was developed specifically for implementation in
Geographical Information Systems, without the use of satellite-derived input data. It generates spatially
and temporallyexplicitoutputs atthe scaleofthe inputGIS datasurfaces.Itsapplicationisalsodiscussedin
Section 9.4.2. 3PG-Spatial is more a specific implementation of the plot-level 3-PG than a distinct model;
if 3PG-Spatial is applied to a single plot, it gives the same results as obtained with 3PGXL.
9.2 BIOLOGICAL SUB-MODELS OF 3-PG
The state variables of the basic 3-PG carbon balance model are the biomass pools for foliage (WF), stem
(WS) and roots (WR), all in units of t ha1, the stem number (NS, trees ha1) of the stand and the
volumetric soil water content in the root zone. (Note that 1 kg m2 is 10 t ha1.) The biomass pools and
stem number are linked through a stand-level carbon balance model based directly on the generic tree
growth model of Section 5.6.1. Canopy photosynthesis is modelled by a light-use efficiency model
(Section 5.3). The effects of the various environmental factors (site and weather) on light-use efficiency
and canopy conductance are taken into account using a series of multiplicative growth modifiers, while
respiration is modelled by a constant carbon-use efficiency (Section 5.5.3). NPP is then allocated to the
biomass pools with the root allocation ratio determined by site fertility and current soil water status,
along with allometric structural constraints as in Section 5.6.3. Stand population dynamics are modelled
as a combination of a constant or age-related mortality rate together with self-thinning mediated
through stand basal area (Section 4.1.3). Finally, a simple, single-layer soil water balance model takes
into account canopy interception losses, evapotranspiration, and losses due to runoff or drainage.
9.2.1. The Basic Stand-Level Model
A change in a stand-level biomass pool occurs through growth of individual trees, but also through
mortality, which reduces the number of trees contributing to the pool. The equations for the rates of
change of the stand-level biomass pools are therefore obtained from the tree-level equations, i.e.
Eqs. (5.43), by including tree mortality:

The determination of the i and of gN is discussed below (see Sections 9.2.4 and 9.2.5), but the gi for the
biomass pools are species-specific parameters, and possibly age dependent in the case of gF (see
Section 9.2.6). The units and typical values of all 3-PG parameters are available with the 3-PG material
on the PHYSECOL web site.
The terms involving gN in Eq. (9.1) take into account the loss of biomass from the stand-level pools
when trees die or the stand is thinned, and are derived from the number of trees dying (gNNS), times
the average biomass per tree (Wi/NS), times the fraction (mi) of this average associated with each tree
that dies. In general, trees that die are suppressed in some way, and hence we would expect each mi<1.
Default values for the mi are probably adequate, e.g. mi¼0.5. How thinning is included in Eqs. (9.1)
and (9.2) is considered in Section 9.2.8.
9.2.2. Determination of NPP
NPP in 3-PG is determined using a light-use efficiency approach, with respiration taken into account
by a constant carbon use efficiency. The light-use efficiency is affected by site and environmental factors
through a series of growth modifiers. A causal loop diagram for this sub-model is shown in Figure 9.2.
The primary outputs are the assimilates formed from the input of CO2, which are inputs to the
biomass allocation sub-model, and respiration which is lost from the system. Climatic and site factors
affect assimilation through their effects on the light-use efficiency, and the soil water content and
canopy leaf area index are provided by other sub-models. Mathematical details of the NPP sub-model
follow.
Monthly gross primary production Pg (t ha1 month1) by the canopy on a dry matter basis is

eg ¼ MDMqMJaC;

where MDM¼24 gDMmol1 converts moles of plant matter into grams of dry matter (assuming dry

9.2.3. Growth Modifiers for Site and Environmental Effects
The effects of site and environmental factors on stand-level GPP are taken into account using a series of
growth modifiers, in the sense of Section 5.3.3. These are applied to the canopy quantum efficiency aC
as in Landsberg and Waring (1997) but with the addition of modifiers for the effects of soil salinity and
atmospheric CO2 concentration. Thus
aC ¼ fTfFfFRfSfCa’aCx;
ð9:8Þ
where the species-specific parameter aCx (mol mol1) is the canopy quantum efficiency when no
factors are limiting growth and atmospheric CO2 is 350 ppm, and ’ is the so-called physiological
modifier given by
’ ¼ fage minffD; fyg:
ð9:9Þ
The individual growth modifiers are as follows: fT is a function of the (monthly) average daily
temperature Tav (C), fF is a function of the number of days dF of frost per month, fFR is a function
of the site fertility rating FR, fS is a function of the salinity of a site as measured by the soil electrical
conductivity CS, fCa is a function of the atmospheric CO2 concentration Ca (ppm), fD is a function of
daytime average vapour pressure deficit D (mba), fy is a function of the relative plant-available soil
water content yr in the root zone (i.e. the ratio of the current plant-available soil water to the maximum
plant-available soil water for that soil), and fage is a function of stand age t (years).
These growth modifiers are discussed in more detail below, and Figure 9.3 illustrates the dependence
of each modifier on its associated factor. Their equations are given in Appendix 2, and parameter values
are included with the 3-PG documentation on PHYSECOL.
The temperature-dependent growth modifier fT (Figure 9.3a) takes into account the well-established
observation that production increases with increasing temperature up to some optimum temperature,
and then declines. The three species-specific parameters that characterise this growth modifier
are collectively called the cardinal temperatures, and are the minimum (Tmin), optimum (Topt) and
maximum (Tmax) temperatures for growth. The temperature dependence of single-leaf photosynthesis
can be used as a guide for assigning the cardinal temperatures. They can also be assigned by
examining the dependence of observed or fitted values of canopy quantum efficiency aC on average
daily temperature.
The frost-dependent growth modifier fF (not shown) is characterised by a single parameter: the
fraction kF of a days worth of growth lost for each day of frost. By default kF is assumed to be 1, but for
highly frost sensitive species, kF may be greater than 1.

account the number of frost days.

--- Page 264 ---

The VPD-dependent growth modifier fD (Figure 9.3b) declines exponentially with increasing D and
is characterised by a single species-specific parameter kD (mbar1) that determines the strength of the
effect of VPD. This modifier is also a determinant of canopy conductance, and kD can be assigned a
value on the basis of observed effects of VPD on stomatal conductance.
The soil water-dependent growth modifier fy is shown for contrasting soils in Figure 9.3c. Its two
parameters cy and ny are determined by the soil texture, and are listed for different soil texture classes in
Table 7.1.
The fertility-dependent growth modifier fFR (Figure 9.3d) declines with declining FR. It has two
parameters: fN0 is its value when FR¼0, and nfN is a power that determines its shape. Based on
published information reviewed by A˚ gren and Ingestad (1987), Landsberg and Gower (1997) and
Linder and Murray (1998), fFR is by default a linear function of FR and its value for low fertility is 0.5,
i.e. nfN¼1, fN0¼0.5.
The age-related growth modifier fage is a function of the stand age t (years). It is close to 1 for young
stands, and only differs significantly from 1 when the stand age approaches a species-specific maximum
age (Figure 9.3e). The parameters are the maximum age tx expected for a stand of this species, the value
rage of t/tx at which fage¼1/2, and a power nage that determines the strength of the response. Whereas tx
is a species-specific parameter, generic values of rage¼0.95 and nage¼4 probably suffice.
The salinity-dependent growth modifier fS is a function of soil electrical conductivity CS (dS m1).
It is 1 for CS less than a lower threshold CS0 and then decreases to 0 at an upper threshold CS1
(Figure 9.3f ). To the best of our knowledge no one has yet attempted to parameterise salinity effects
for 3-PG.
Separate modifiers are required for the effects of atmospheric CO2 concentration Ca (ppm) on
canopy quantum efficiency aC and conductance gC. The modifier fCa is applied to aC (Figure 9.3g) and
increases hyperbolically from 0 with increasing Ca, has the value 1 when Ca¼350 ppm and is
parameterised by its value fCa700 when Ca¼700 ppm. The modifier fCg is applied to gC (Figure 9.3h)
and decreases hyperbolically to 0 with increasing Ca, has the value 1 when Ca¼350 ppm and is
parameterised by its value fCg700 when Ca¼700 ppm. This formulation of the effects of atmospheric
CO2 on growth and water use was introduced into 3-PG by Almeida et al. (2009), and is consistent with
observations of the response of single-leaf gas-exchange data to changes in ambient CO2 concentration,
and with results from various FACE experiments.
It is important to note that it is the product of Y and aC that determines NPP, therefore only the
product YaCx can be estimated by fitting output from 3-PG to observed growth data. The only way Y
and aCx can be estimated independently is if both GPP and NPP have been measured. In most
applications Y is fixed at the value 0.47 found by Waring et al. (1998). It is often useful to fit observed
growth data from a range of different sites to 3-PG output by varying aC . This then gives values of aC as
a function of site and environmental factors that can be used to determine parameters in the growth
modifiers discussed in the next section. However, there will in general be a high degree of correlation
between the estimated values of aC and estimated parameters in the growth modifiers.
Sands and Landsberg (2002) estimated aCx¼0.06 mol mol1 for Eucalyptus globulus, while for
Eucalyptus grandis Almeida et al. (2004a), Esprey et al. (2004) and Stape et al. (2004) estimated values
of 0.068, 0.06 and 0.08, respectively. Such high values are surprising as the typical quantum efficiency a
for single leaves is of the order of 0.05 mol mol1 at normal temperatures. Sands (1996) found the
upper limit to canopy quantum efficiency is a/(1m), where m is the leaf transmission coefficient.
Since m0.1, this suggests aCx¼a/(1m)0.055.
252
Chapter 9

--- Page 265 ---

9.2.4. Biomass Allocation and Turnover
The assimilates from the NPP sub-model are allocated to the foliage, stem and root biomass
pools using an approach that allows root biomass and canopy LAI to vary with growing
conditions, but forces the allocation of carbon to follow patterns that lead to realistic structures. The
causal relationships in the allocation sub-model are shown in Figure 9.4. The primary input is the
assimilate pool from the NPP sub-model, and the primary outputs are the biomass pools (and canopy
LAI), and the losses of dry matter through litterfall and root turnover. Site fertility and plant-available
soil water affect the balance between above- and below-ground growth, with favourable conditions
enhancing above-ground growth. The measure of tree-size used in the structural relationships is the
mean DBH, and is determined from the average single-tree stem mass through an allometric
relationship.
Assuming that the allometric equation (Eq. (5.45)) has been fitted to observed stem biomass data to
give the species-specific allometric parameters aS, nSB and nSN, DBH is determined from Eq. (5.50),

and foliage mass are affected by losses, i.e. root turnover and litterfall, respectively, that have not been shown.

9.2.5. Stem Numbers and Mortality
The change in stem number per unit area caused by mortality is calculated using a combination of an
external mortality rate accounting for the effects of stress or environmental factors, as well as the well-
established process of self-thinning. These processes are modelled in the population sub-model shown
in Figure 9.5. The right-hand loop shows that self-thinning reduces stem numbers, but it also shows
that as basal area increases, the intensity of self-thinning increases. If stem number is reduced following
an intentional thinning, basal area is reduced and hence the intensity of self-thinning is also reduced.
Age- or stress-related mortality is an external input, and acts in addition to self-thinning. Stem mass is
an input from the biomass sub-model, and is used to determine the stand DBH and hence its basal
area. Self-thinning is then modelled using the Vanclay and Sands (2009) interpretation of self-thinning
(see Section 4.1.3) in which self-thinning is a continuous process during stand development whose
intensity increases as the stand basal area approaches a maximum.
The stress-related mortality rate gN (month1) can be age dependent, or constant, and Eq. (4.3) can
represent typical patterns of mortality in stand rotations. By setting gN0¼gN1 it can also represent a
constant background mortality, and if needed it can be augmented with known mortality events. Self-
thinning is taken into account by rewriting Eq. (4.6) as

9.2.6. Age-Dependent Variables
In addition to the age-dependent growth modifier fage , 3-PG includes several variables that are
explicitly age dependent. This means 3-PG is not a strictly state-determined model. However, in the
interests of simplicity, we choose conservative empirical relationships for these variables in lieu of
complex and uncertain mechanistic relationships. They are also easy to parameterise from observed
data. The relationships in question are for the specific leaf area sF (m2 kg1), leaf litterfall rate gF
(month1), the environmental or stress-related mortality rate gN (month1), the proportion pBB of
stem biomass that is in the form of branches and bark and the basic density rw (kg m3) of stem wood.
The latter two have no effect on the predictions of growth by 3-PG but provide one way to convert stem
biomass into stem volume (see Section 9.2.8). The equations for these variables are given in Appendix
A2.2, and their variation with stand age is shown in Figure 9.6 for typical parameter values.
9.2.7. Soil Water Balance
A single-layer soil water balance model is used in 3-PG. The month is divided into dR equal periods
with an amount R/dR rain falling as a single event at the beginning of each period, where R is the total
monthly rainfall and dR the number of rainy days, and the water balance is performed separately over
each period. This approach is more realistic than the whole-month time step used in the original
version of 3-PG.
The causal relationships in the water balance sub-model are shown in Figure 9.7. The primary input
is rainfall, some of which is intercepted by the canopy and lost through evaporation. The remainder,

i.e. throughfall, is added to the soil water pool. If the resulting volumetric soil water content exceeds the
capacity of the soil, this excess is deemed to be lost as runoff or drainage. Evapotranspiration, i.e.
canopy transpiration and soil evaporation, is then taken into account to give the new soil water
content. Increasing canopy LAI increases both interception loss and canopy conductance, and higher
conductance increases ET. Evapotranspiration is directly affected by solar radiation and VPD, while it is
indirectly affected by VPD and soil water through their effects on the conductance.

Chapter 9

--- Page 269 ---

Rainfall interception is modelled using the interception model of Eq. (7.8). Water is stored on the
foliage as a uniform layer up to some maximum thickness, and is evaporated at the wet-surface
evaporation rate determined from the Penman–Monteith equation for a wet surface, i.e. Eq. (2.32).
The canopy rainfall transmission coefficient kR and the maximum thickness twx (mm) of moisture on
foliage are species-specific parameters, but generic values may suffice.
The Penman–Monteith equation for canopy transpiration (i.e. Eq. (2.48) or (7.3)) is adapted to
model evapotranspiration by the stand, i.e. soil evaporation plus canopy and understory transpiration.
To do this, we interpret the net radiation term ’na (W m2) as net radiation above the stand, i.e. the
radiation which falls on the canopy and any bare soil due to incomplete canopy closure, and interpret
the canopy conductance gC (m s1) as a bulked vegetation and surface conductance. Kelliher et al.
(1995) show that the ratio of bulked conductance to stomatal conductance at the top of the canopy
varies from about 2 for an open canopy to about 3.5 for an LAI of 5, and is constant for higher LAI.
They also show that the limitations imposed by environmental factors can be taken into account by
modifiers in a way similar to Eq. (9.8) for the effects on light-use efficiency.
Accordingly, we model the bulked canopy conductance gC as a function of environment and canopy
leaf area index L by the equation
gC ¼ fCg’gCopt L

and soil water stress on conductance, fCg is a modifier taking into account the effects of atmospheric
CO2 on stomatal conductance and gCopt (m s1) is the unconstrained canopy conductance. This is

9.2.8. Stand Management Outputs and Interventions
Three stand-level characteristics of interest to forest managers are determined as part of the biological
sub-model of 3-PG: DBH, basal area and stem number. Other characteristics such as mean dominant
height, stem volume and mean annual increment (MAI) can be computed from the DBH, stem
biomass and stem number. We consider two approaches to determining stem volume: the use of
allometric equations, and the use of stem biomass along with the fraction pBB of stem biomass in the
form of branches and bark, and the basic density rw of stem wood. In general, the allometric approach
is recommended because of uncertainties due to unaccounted for age- and site-related effects on pBB
and rw.
We have already seen (Section 4.2.1) that stem height H (m) can be related to DBH and stem
number through an allometric relationship. A similar relationship can be used for stem volume
VS (m3), e.g. volume under bark, or some other measure of merchantable volume. Thus we have
H ¼ aHdnHB

where the allometric coefficients (a) and powers (n) are species specific and must be determined by
fitting observed height and volume data to observed DBH and stem number. The required data are
usually available from spacing trials, e.g. Smith et al. (2005). MAI is simply VS divided by stand age.
An alternative approach to determining stem volume under bark is based directly on the predicted
stem biomass by subtracting the biomass in branches and bark and converting mass to volume using
the basic density. This approach requires information on the branch and bark fraction pBB and the basic
density rw (kg m3), both of which are explicitly age-related. The volume under bark VS (m3 ha1) is
then given by
VS ¼ 1000 1  pBB
ð
ÞWS

;
ð9:19Þ
where the 1000 converts tonnes to kg.
Two stand-level management options that can be imposed in 3-PG simulations of stand develop-
ment are pruning and thinning. Pruning entails the removal of a certain fraction of the foliage biomass,
and in 3-PG this is achieved by decreasing the foliage biomass by the required amount. Thinning entails
the removal of whole trees, and in 3-PG this is done by decreasing the stem number and removing
biomass from all biomass pools. If thinning is treated as a mortality event in the context of Eq. (9.1),
gN is the fraction of trees removed, and thinning from above would have mi>1 since the larger trees are
being removed.
258
Chapter 9

--- Page 271 ---

In stands where growth is slow and there are long intervals between measurements, 3-PG accurately
simulates the growth of thinned stands (e.g. Landsberg et al. 2005), but this may not be the case with
fast-growing plantations, where these approaches to pruning and thinning may be too simplistic, and
fail to capture physiological responses. These issues are discussed further in Section 9.5.
9.3 CALIBRATION, PERFORMANCE AND VALIDATION
From the above description of 3-PG, it is clear there are numerous parameters, many of which are
species specific, and all of which have to be provided with values before the model can be used. In this
section, we outline how these parameter values can be assigned, then illustrate the performance of the
model and discuss aspects of its validation.
9.3.1. Calibration and Parameter Estimation
Three different approaches are used to assign values to parameters in a model: direct measurement,
analogy with other species, and parameter estimation. Parameter estimation involves fitting model
output to observed data by systematically varying one or more parameters so as to give an optimal fit.
Together, these constitute the process of model calibration.
Some basic principles guiding the process of model calibration based on our experience with 3-PG
and other models can be stated, in no particular order:
1.
Parameters should whenever possible be measured directly, and assigned or estimated at the level
of the sub-model that they characterise.
2.
A sound understanding of the structure of the model, and of the relationships in it, can guide the
process of calibration, especially when parameters are being estimated. This includes knowledge of
the likely range of the parameters being assigned, and of all output variables.
3.
A prior sensitivity analysis provides valuable information to guide the calibration. Examples of
sensitivity analyses applied to 3-PG are the work of Almeida et al. (2004a), Esprey et al. (2004) and
Xenakis et al. (2008).
4.
The standard error of all parameter estimates and their correlation matrix should be examined.
If estimates are highly correlated, consideration should be given to finding alternative and
independent ways to assign their values.
5.
Parameter estimation should be based on observations of as many distinct but independent output
variables as possible, and from sites covering a wide range of conditions. There is no point in
basing a fit on observed data that are correlated (e.g. stem mass and DBH).
6.
It is advantageous if time-series observations are used for calibration, especially if parameters in
the growth modifiers are estimated as it is these that determine the short-term response to
environmental factors. However, the model can be calibrated using single measurements from a
wide range of sites.
7.
It is important to verify that the behaviour of all outputs is reasonable, especially when parameter
estimation is employed. We are dealing with a complex system of interacting components that
must function correctly as a whole. This is the subject of the next subsection.
These principles are illustrated or enlarged up on in the following discussion. They were also
consciously followed in various calibrations of 3-PG, e.g. Sands and Landsberg (2002), Almeida et al.
(2004a) and Esprey et al. (2004).
The 3-PG Process-Based Model
259

--- Page 272 ---

Wherever possible, calibration should be through the direct measurement of parameter values.
However, ‘‘direct measurement’’ may also include the fitting of a simple model to well-determined
data. The important point is that a parameter whose value is being assigned directly characterises the
observed data. Examples for 3-PG are the use of biomass harvest data to determine the allometric
parameters for stem mass as a function of DBH, or the use of gas-exchange data to determine the
dependence of stomatal conductance on VPD for use as a growth modifier. In some cases a parameter
might be calculated using another model, e.g. the canopy production model of Sands (Sands 1995b,
1996; see Section 5.2.4) could be used to calculate canopy quantum efficiency from photosynthetic
light-response data and typical weather data.
Many parameters appear to be generic, and can be assigned values by analogy with other species.
Some examples are the carbon-use efficiency Y¼0.47, the canopy light extinction coefficient k¼0.5,
and the parameters nage¼4 and rage¼0.95 in the age-related growth modifier fage. If 3-PG output is
insensitive to the value of a particular parameter, then it is appropriate to treat that parameter as
generic and use values obtained for another species.
Failing these two options, parameter values can be assigned by a process of systematic estimation.
This may be manual or automated by using software to adjust parameter values to optimise the fit of
predicted outputs to observed data. However, irrespective of the model, these powerful tools must be
used with discretion as seemingly very good fits to specific data sets can be obtained for entirely wrong
reasons. For this reason it is essential to carefully examine the behaviour of all output variables, not just
those to which data were fitted in the estimation. The PHYSECOL web site includes a software package,
NONLINXL, that can be used for parameter estimation based on the implementation of 3-PG also
included on PHYSECOL.
Because 3-PG is a dynamic model, it is advantageous if time-series observations are used for
calibration. This is especially the case if parameters in the growth modifiers are estimated as it is
these that determine the short-term response to environmental factors. NPP or GPP, LAI and soil water
content are inherently dynamic, so time-series observations of these are valuable. However, stem and
(total) root biomass, and DBH, are essentially monotonic in behaviour, and slowly varying, so useful
progress can be made with few values of these variables. In fact, reasonable calibrations of the model
can be based on single measurements of stem mass or DBH from a large number of sites with a wide
range of conditions, although this situation is far from ideal.
In a number of cases, surrogates may reasonably be used in place of directly measured variables. For
example, biomass data are not routinely measured in forestry trials so common surrogates for stem
biomass are diameter at breast height (dB) or stem volume. Because dB is calculated in 3-PG by
inverting the allometric relationship between stem mass and dB, it is a suitable surrogate for stem
biomass. So, fitting predicted values of dB to observations of DBH is an acceptable way to estimate the
parameters characterising biomass production and allocation. However, it is desirable that the coeffi-
cients in the allometric equation, Eq. (5.45), are assigned directly from measured values of stem mass as
a function of dB, and not estimated as part of the fitting process. Getting a good fit to both stem
biomass data and DBH (or stem volume) data does not increase confidence in the model over and
above that suggested by good fits to biomass alone. However, good fits to both stem biomass and
canopy LAI do improve confidence in the model – and a good fit to all three biomass pools is of course
even better!
At all stages in a calibration, and especially when parameter estimation is employed, it is important
to verify that the behaviour of all outputs is reasonable. This is particularly important for those
variables not used in the calibration process. As an example, consider estimations where only stem
biomass (or DBH, or stem volume) is fitted to observed data. LAI is very important because of its
influence on, and interaction with, radiation interception and hence biomass production, but once it
260
Chapter 9

--- Page 273 ---

exceeds a value of about 8, further increases have only a minor effect on NPP (<2%) and hence on
growth. In that case, similar values of stem biomass can be obtained by large values of canopy quantum
efficiency and low allocation to stem biomass (and high allocation to foliage, i.e. high pFS), or vice
versa. In fact, the lack of feedback from LAI to production can lead to unreasonably high predictions of
LAI. This situation can only be prevented by including canopy LAI amongst the data to which model
output is being fitted, or by imposing some reasonable upper limit on LAI when observed LAI data are
not available.
It is often the case that several parameters can be varied to alter the same outputs of the model. For
example, foliage biomass is directly affected by canopy quantum efficiency, the allocation ratio pFS and
the litterfall rate. It is also indirectly affected by the parameters controlling allocation to roots because
these affect above-ground allocation. This is where a good understanding of the model is important; an
inappropriate choice of parameters makes it difficult or impossible to determine a valid set of values for
these parameters because their estimates exhibit a high degree of correlation. As a general rule, it
advisable to determine the standard errors of the parameter estimates, and the matrix of correlations
between these estimates, and most parameter estimation software provide these data as part of their
output. If a high degree of correlation is encountered, convergence of the estimation process will be
slow, and the standard errors of the estimates large – and the results may well be meaningless.
9.3.2. Performance
When 3-PG, or any model, has been calibrated and a parameter set established, basic checks must be
performed on both the parameters and the subsequent outputs from the model: all parameter values
must be biophysically or biologically reasonable; a basic sensitivity analysis of the parameter values
should be performed and attention paid to the values assigned to sensitive parameters; and it must be
checked that the behaviour of all outputs is reasonable.
It is particularly important that those output variables not used in the calibration process be
checked for reasonableness. The lack of feedback from high LAI to production can lead to unreasonably
high predictions of LAI; the results of allowing LAI to increase beyond about 8 are discussed in the
previous section. This situation can only be prevented by including canopy LAI amongst the data to
which model output is being fitted, or by imposing some reasonable upper limit on LAI when observed
LAI data are not available.
Other areas where potentially nonsensical results easily arise are litterfall and root biomass. In the
case of litterfall, litter is an output from the foliage biomass pool and hence can counteract incorrect
allocation to the biomass pool. It is therefore important to check that the amount of litter predicted is
within reasonable bounds, and that the parameters in the litterfall rate are themselves reasonable. In the
case of roots, errors in the parameters in root turnover (gR in Eq. (9.1)) can mask errors in root
allocation, which in turn affect allocation to stems and foliage, and if quantum use efficiency has been
estimated as part of the calibration of the model, also NPP. Thus, even when biomass data for roots are
available, careful thought needs to be given to the value of gR.
Numerous studies have demonstrated that when appropriately calibrated for a species, 3-PG
provides sound predictions of the time course of canopy development. Of these we mention Sands
and Landsberg (2002) and Almeida et al. (2004a). Sands and Landsberg (2002) developed a set of
parameters for E. globulus based on data from a range of fertilised and (with one exception) not
irrigated sites in Tasmania and southern Western Australia. The available data included observed stem
biomass and canopy LAI at four or more ages from each site, as well as time series for specific leaf area
and branch and bark fraction, individual tree stem harvest data giving stem mass and DBH, and some
litterfall data. Almeida et al. (2004a) developed sets of parameters for clones of E. grandis grown in
The 3-PG Process-Based Model
261

--- Page 274 ---

sub-tropical Brazil. Their data were quite comprehensive and included time series of stem and root
biomass data, canopy LAI, litterfall, soil water content, specific leaf area, basic wood density, branch
and bark fraction, stomatal conductance, and numerous other physiological observations. Whereas
Sands and Landsberg had to assume default parameters for root allocation, these were estimated from
observations in the Almeida et al. study. In both of these studies, 3-PG accurately reproduced the
observed biomass data to which it had been calibrated. For illustrations of the fidelity of the model, we
refer to the original papers.
Despite the simplistic nature of the water balance model, it has been shown to provide results that
are accurate enough for simulations running over several or many years. Law et al. (2000a,b) explicitly
tested the water balance model as part of their study on a Pinus ponderosa stand, on which detailed
measurements were made. They used sensitivity analysis to decide on the optimum maximum water
holding capacity in the root zone, and model predictions of soil water content corresponded well to
measured monthly values. In Brazil, Almeida et al. (2004a) assessed the performance of the water
balance sub-model by measuring soil water in the root zone under a eucalypt plantation for 3 years,
and comparing measured and calculated available water. Early in stand development, and through
most of year 4, the model predicted more available water than was indicated by measurements, but
taken overall it accounted for 70% of the variance in measured available water. Extending that study for
a further 2 years, and comparing 3-PG with a detailed, daily input water use model (UAPE; Soares and
Almeida 2001), which accounted for 82% of the variance in available water, Almeida et al. commented
that ‘‘The differences (between 3-PG and UAPE) were accentuated when rainfall was irregular, or
concentrated over short periods. . .’’. We discuss these issues and possible improvements to the water
balance sub-model in Section 9.5.5.
Further analyses of the overall performance of 3-PG are discussed in Section 9.4 on applications of
the model. We draw particular attention to the review given by Landsberg et al. (2003). All of these
studies show that 3-PG has the capacity to reproduce observed growth patterns across a quite diverse
range of sites and climatic conditions.
9.3.3. Validation
A rigorous analysis of the performance of 3-PG, including assessment of the accuracy with which the
model predicted leaf area index and stem biomass and the influence of the data used to initialise the
stand, was performed as part of the Sands and Landsberg (2002) calibration of 3-PG for E. globulus.
This study did not provide an independent validation of 3-PG, but it did confirm that it had the ability,
when appropriately calibrated for a species, to reproduce observed stand development across a range of
sites. Similar comments apply to other studies such as Almeida et al. (2004a,b) and Esprey et al. (2004),
both for E. grandis.
Landsberg et al. (2003) applied 3-PG to a wide range of experimental and commercial plantings in
Africa, Australia, the USA (Oregon) and Sweden. These included spacing and thinning experiments,
and fertilisation trials. They showed that, given appropriate values for canopy quantum efficiency, the
coefficients of the allometric equations, specific leaf area, and the site fertility rating, 3-PG could, in all
cases, be calibrated to simulate observed data with useful accuracy. The temperature modifier intro-
duced by Sands and Landsberg (2002) was found to be important, particularly for the species from
Oregon and Sweden. Overall the model performed well.
3-PG has also been rigorously tested against flux measurements and the monthly dynamics of LAI,
NPP and ET and found to be consistent with measurements (Law et al. 2000a,b) and with the
predictions of a much more detailed model (CANVEG-A; Siqueira et al. 2006).
262
Chapter 9

--- Page 275 ---

A comparison of the ability of four models, including 3-PG, to predict above-ground biomass
production of 62 permanent sample plots (PSPs) in E. globulus plantations in southern Western
Australia, Victoria and Tasmania was reported by Miehle et al. (2009). Some plots had been thinned,
which was taken into account when applying 3-PG. The required climatic input data were obtained
from SILO, a digest of historic climate data (Jeffrey et al. 2001). However, the soil and fertility data
came from various sources, and their quality probably varied across the 62 plots. 3-PG was initialised at
age 1 year with standard conditions, rather than with observed initial biomass data, and the parameter
values were those obtained by Sands and Landsberg (2002).
Miehle et al. reported that 3-PG tended to underestimate above-ground biomass growth of
E. globulus with an average error of 34% at age 2 years, decreasing to less than 10% at age
8 years. That the under-prediction declined towards the end of the rotation was consistent with the
self-correcting tendency of 3-PG reported by Sands and Landsberg (2002). 3-PG overestimated
biomass production at several sites where mortality was severe, but when observed mortality was
imposed, biomass and DBH predictions were considerably improved. This highlighted the need in
3-PG for a mortality sub-model responsive to environmental factors. Growth was under-predicted at a
few highly productive sites at which it was suspected the stands might be accessing groundwater, but
when water stress was ignored, 3-PG predicted production at these sites quite well. Miehle et al. (2009)
concluded that 3-PG adequately predicted above-ground production of most sites under normal
growth conditions, but questioned its predictive ability under extreme conditions. However, as we
noted, when a plausible ‘‘work around’’ was applied, performance at most of these extreme sites
improved. Miehle et al. also noted that, because of its small input data requirements, 3-PG may be well
suited for modelling exercises where input data are scarce, and for spatial applications.
Pinjuv et al. (2006) also compared the performance of quite different models, and used 3-PG as an
example of a process-based model. Their analysis was based on a large number of measurements of
basal area and stem number across an environmental gradient over 14,000 ha of plantation grown
Pinus radiata in south-eastern New Zealand. Their analysis included the determination of a 3-PG
parameter set for P. radiata. This was based on observations of basal area and stem number at 200
unique plots representative of all elevation ranges and age classes in the study area, and ensured that the
predicted LAI were consistent with those inferred from an independent model (Pinjuv 2006). The
resulting predictions of these data by 3-PG showed no apparent bias. However, when applied to
independent data from within the study area, Pinjuv et al. (2006) concluded that predictions of basal
area were biased at high elevations. This finding suggests to us that the cardinal temperatures in the
temperature-dependent growth modifier may not be optimal; they had not been estimated in this
study. Pinjuv et al. also found poor predictions of stem number at the end of the rotation, and
emphasised that 3-PG needs to include density-independent mortality, in addition to self-thinning.
9.4 APPLICATIONS
When a model has been calibrated for a particular species, it may then be used to explore the
consequences of changing conditions, such as different initial stem number, soil water holding capacity,
or fertility (which may be brought about by fertilisation), or changes in thinning practices. The use of
the calibrated model to explore ‘‘what if?’’ scenarios fulfils one of the original aims of the development
of 3-PG: ‘‘to bridge the gap between conventional, mensuration-based growth and yield and process-
based carbon balance models’’. Yield predictions may be used to assess profitability, determine harvest
schedules and assess the potential of areas where plantations are not currently grown. 3-PG is also
widely used as a tool for spatial analyses, and may be driven by satellite data, or used in association with
soil and climate layers in a Geographical Information System (GIS).
The 3-PG Process-Based Model
263

--- Page 276 ---

Another useful application, particularly in relation to fast-growing plantations, is evaluation of the
possible impacts of drought, the probability of which can be assessed using historical climate data
(although we note that these probabilities may change as a result of climate change). Drought can be
simulated simply by reducing the rainfall in the weather data files. In this context the relationship
between average monthly VPD and average monthly rainfall may be worth investigating because if
VPD increases with decreasing rainfall then, to simulate drought realistically, this effect should be
included.
It may be argued that, unless we are certain about every aspect of a model’s behaviour and its
parameter values, the use of a process-based model as a management tool to assess the probable
responses of forests to management intervention or variations in weather is fraught with risk. However,
the risk associated with using a model based on sound biophysical principles – especially a model that
has been shown in many studies to accurately reproduce observed forest growth – to explore the
implications of management decisions and possible climatic events, is far lower than the risks
associated with decisions based on guesswork or inflexible empirical models. Any model is an
approximation to reality, and no model simulates reality perfectly, so there are, and will remain,
many uncertainties in a model’s behaviour. Decision-makers make decisions based on the best
information available to them, knowing there is an element of risk associated with all decisions. As
well as the uncertainties in any model used, there will also be the normal uncertainties associated with
predicting the future. As a final point, if other models are available, they should also be used so a
consensus can be obtained, and areas of disagreement explored.
In the next section we review work that has been done with 3-PG to illustrate the range of possible
applications, and to provide a basis for considering how it might be improved. We have not attempted
a critical assessment of each of these studies, and reference should be made to the papers cited for
detailed discussions of the methods used, results obtained and the lessons learned about 3-PG, its value
and limitations.
The applications are considered in three categories, which are not mutually exclusive but provide a
useful basis for examining the current and potential uses of 3-PG. They are (1) simulation of the
growth of plantations and even-aged forests with a view to yield prediction and as a basis for
management decisions; (2) simulation of forest growth and productivity over large areas, which
contribute to knowledge about ecosystems and may provide estimates of carbon sequestration as
well as data useful for forest management; and (3) as an analytical tool to identify factors limiting forest
growth in particular areas, and factors that may determine species distribution. These three classes of
application use different types of input data, and most involve some sort of calibration or comparison
of outputs with observations.
9.4.1. Analysis and Prediction of Plantation Growth
3-PG is widely used around the world, most frequently as a research tool, but also – and particularly in
South America – as a management tool. There have been many studies on temperate and sub-tropical
eucalypt and pine plantations using 3-PG, and on boreal species.
Its incorporation into the Canadian hybrid model TRIPLEX (Section 8.2.3c), along with an
empirical growth and yield model and the soil–carbon–nitrogen model CENTURY (Parton et al.
1988; see Section 6.3.1), was a step towards its implementation as a management tool (Peng et al. 2002;
Zhou et al. 2004). Applications of 3-PG in South America as a management tool were pioneered, in
Brazil, by Almeida (2003), and it is now widely used as such in Brazil and Chile. In South Africa, 3-PG
was incorporated into a decision support system aimed at assessing water yield and productivity of
eucalypt plantations (Dye 2005). It is being used by the CSIRO in Australia for contracts ranging across
264
Chapter 9

--- Page 277 ---

site selection, productivity and water use assessment, and land amelioration, and it provides the
forestry module in the FullCam model developed by the Australian Greenhouse Office for carbon
accounting of forests (Paul et al. 2006).
The 3-PG model is clearly well suited to simulate the growth of plantations and has been widely
tested and used for this application. Here we review some of these, grouped by forest type and location.
Further examples can be found in Section 9.4.2 where we consider spatial applications of 3-PG.
a) Temperate Eucalypts
We discussed above the application by Sands and Landsberg (2002) of 3-PG to E. globulus at site in
southwestern West Australia and Tasmania. This was probably the first detailed analysis and applica-
tion of 3-PG to a eucalypt species. The primary outcomes of this study were important incremental
improvements to the model, a soundly based set of parameter values for E. globulus, and most notably
the 3PGPJS (now 3PGXL) implementation of 3-PG as an Excel spreadsheet application (Sands 2004a).
There are numerous other applications of 3-PG in Australia. Many of these are commercial-in-
confidence, and many are spatial in nature and discussed in that context in Section 9.4.2.
Fontes et al. (2006) evaluated the performance of 3-PG against data for E. globulus from an
irrigationfertiliser trial, a spacing trial and a number of PSPs in different regions of Portugal. The
treatments in the irrigationfertiliser trial spanned a range of conditions that would not normally be
encountered in commercial production of eucalypts in Portugal. The PSPs had been re-measured
several times between January 1971 and November 1994, so the data provided a good representation of
the variation in climate and soils across the regions in Portugal where plantation eucalypts are being
grown. To estimate the fertility rating for each PSP, soils were evaluated from soil pits and profiles were
analysed by an experienced soil scientist. Fontes et al. ran initial tests of the model against data from the
experiments using the parameters for E. globulus provided by Sands and Landsberg (2002). They made
changes to the relationships for specific leaf area, wood density and the stem mass/DBH allometric
equation on the basis of Portuguese data. The fit between model outputs and observed values of stem
mass, foliage mass, stand volume and basal area was generally good, except in the case of foliage mass.
A parameter set applicable to all Portuguese sites and conditions was established by tuning the
existing parameter values against the experimental data. An almost identical set of parameters was
obtained, and the model was tested against the PSP measurements. No further tuning was carried out,
and this test indicated that a single set of parameter values would provide good estimates of eucalyptus
growth throughout Portugal. This suggests that the physiology of the trees and the processes that
govern their growth and biomass allocation are strongly conservative, and that the major factors
influencing growth are the same factors that determine site productivity and account for almost all
variation in growth and productivity. Therefore, GIS systems can be used with soil and weather data
layers to estimate wide-scale productivity, examine the effects of events such as droughts, assist in
management decisions, assess potential productivity and assess the probable consequences of manage-
ment actions such as fertilisation and thinning (Fontes et al. 2006).
b) Sub-Tropical Eucalypts in South America
There have been many studies of sub-tropical eucalypt plantations using 3-PG. Here, we discuss
applications in South America by Almeida et al. (2004a,b, 2007), Stape et al. (2004, 2006, 2008) and
Rodrı´guez et al. (2009). In the following section, we discuss applications in South Africa by Dye et al.
(2004), Esprey et al. (2004) and Dye (2005).
Almeida et al. (2004b) established a network of 19 automatic weather stations across the estates of a
large pulp and paper company (Aracruz Celulose) in eastern Brazil (see Almeida and Landsberg 2003).
The data from these, extrapolated to the whole region in a GIS with soil layers, provided the basis for
The 3-PG Process-Based Model
265

--- Page 278 ---

calculating growth rates and wood production. The estate – more than 180,000 ha of fast-growing
E. grandis, E. urophylla and their clones and hybrids – was stratified into soil, climatic and administra-
tive regions. Standard rotation time was about 7 years. More than 500 check plots were established
across the soil, climatic and administrative regions, and measurements were made in each every
6 months. 3-PG was initially calibrated against observations from intensively monitored experimental
plots, but subsequently the equations were adjusted for each administrative region.
This was the first time a process-based model had been used as a management tool in such an
extensive way. The model could be run for any land unit at any time to estimate standing volume for
reporting, economic evaluation and planning. The check plots across the whole estate provided data
against which model predictions could be immediately compared, while they reduced by a factor of 4 or
5 the amount of conventional mensuration necessary. Model calculations provided immediate esti-
mates of productivity, allowed assessment of the potential productivity of new areas and of whether
changes to productivity in any particular area were attributable to climatic or management factors.
Analyses carried out as part of this study demonstrated the importance of using real-time meteorologi-
cal data, rather than long-term averages, when simulating the growth of these short rotation tree crops.
This finding agrees with a similar conclusion made by Mummery and Battaglia (2004) using a different
process-based model (CABALA).
The paper by Almeida et al. (2004a) describes a careful calibration of 3-PG for each of two clones,
and compares the differences between these clones in terms of their optimal parameter values. The
parameter sets established were sufficiently generic to allow the use of the model in the practical
management system described by Almeida et al. (2004b). They were also broadly consistent with those
determined by Sands and Landsberg (2002) for E. globulus. Sensitivity analyses showed that default
values for maximum canopy quantum efficiency, specific leaf area, canopy conductance and branch
and bark fractions could be used for these eucalypts. The differences between the clones could be
attributed primarily to differences in biomass partitioning, and secondarily to differences in stomatal
conductance.
In a later paper Almeida et al. (2007) used 3-PG in conjunction with a daily water balance model
(Soares and Almeida 2001) to evaluate plantation productivity and water use efficiency. The results
provided further evidence for the importance of LAI as a major factor affecting tree water use. Almeida
et al. observed that ‘‘interpretation (of the results) appears to be straightforward because lower LAI
later in the rotation results in less radiation interception and less dry matter production’’, but the
question of what causes the reduction in LAI at later stages in the rotation, when growth conditions
appear to be good, was not answered (see Ryan et al. 1997a).
Stape et al. (2004) calibrated 3-PG with a complete carbon budget from an irrigated plantation of
E. grandisurophylla clones. They then validated the model using 2 years of growth data from 40 pairs
of fertilised and unfertilised stands. The fertility rating was based on an objective fertiliser response
from the paired plots (see Section 6.4.2). Since fertility rating is a key input to 3-PG the use of paired
control and fertilised inventory plots allowed validation of the model for fertilised stands and the
development of a protocol to scale fertility among sites based on the response to fertiliser. Stape et al.
(2006) later developed the twin plot approach in an experiment involving 127 twin plots across more
than 40,000 ha of eucalyptus plantations in eastern Brazil, sampling a range of age classes on different
soils subject to varying rainfall. The plots were measured at 6-month intervals. This approach allowed
them to determine very quickly, at landscape level, the gap between actual and potential plantation
productivity and provided a robust dataset for parameterising and testing physiologically based models
of productivity, such as 3-PG. The twin plots procedure constitutes a conceptual and technical advance
by providing quantitative estimates of soil fertility, and maximum potential growth rates during
periods of high water availability.
266
Chapter 9

--- Page 279 ---

In later work, Stape et al. (2008) used an experimental design similar to those discussed earlier
(Section 6.2.3c) in relation to forest nutrition. This had irrigation, fertilisation and irriga-
tionþfertilisation treatments, and was designed to determine all the components of the carbon balance
of E. grandisurophylla clones in Brazil, and to obtain information on carbon allocation patterns,
canopy quantum efficiency and above-ground light-use efficiency.
Rodrı´guez et al. (2009) parameterised 3-PG for Eucalyptus nitens in Chile, and validated its
performance against data from 46 research plots. They also used an expression given by Battaglia
et al. (1998) and discussed in Section 4.4.3 to estimate potential LAI and its dependence on tempera-
ture and soil water stress from basic physiological data. The correspondence between simulated and
measured MAI was remarkable (r2¼0.997). Rodriguez et al. then used the model in a GIS, with spatial
soil and climate models, to estimate the potential productivity of E. nitens for a 37,000 km2 region
of Chile.
c) Sub-Tropical Eucalypts in South Africa
Application of 3-PG in South Africa was pioneered by Peter Dye, both with eucalypts and pines.
Dye et al. (2004) evaluated the potential of 3-PG as a management tool for stands on sites in South
Africa with a wide range of variations in tree age and growth rates. The success of various studies with
the model to some extent reflected the availability of background information about the physiology
and structure of the species being modelled, but there is a scarcity of such information for the South
African stands. Dye et al. therefore investigated the possibility of developing a parameter set based on
limited field data, default values for eucalyptus species and fitting procedures to estimate maximum
available soil water and the fertility rating. They found that such an approach gives rise to a useful
preliminary set of parameter values, which can be estimated over a period as short as a year. Their study
provides useful insights into the estimation of various parameters important for 3-PG to capture the
effects of wide variations in species and conditions on tree growth.
In an application that combines plot and wide-scale approaches, Dye (2005), working with a team of
20, carried out a major project in South Africa, aiming to develop a decision support tool that affords
tree growers (both small-scale and industrial growers) greater opportunity to maximise forest produc-
tivity, and permits water resource managers to improve predictions of the water use and hydrological
impacts of forest plantations. After reviewing the models available round the world they selected 3-PG
as the basis for their project and modified it to predict additional stand-level variables, e.g. mean tree
height. They also introduced a daily time step for water balance calculations. Their implementation of
the revised model was called Sim-A-Tree (3-PG), and was geared towards spatial applications.
An extensive exercise was undertaken to develop parameter values for four species important in the
South African forestry industry: Eucalyptus grandis, Pinus elliottii, Pinus patula, and Acacia mearnsii.
For each species, data from 30 to 50 sites spanning a range of ages and productivities were used to
develop parameter values, and another sample of sites was used to validate model performance.
Climate and soils data in appropriate format for automatic entry into Sim-A-Tree (3-PG) can be
obtained for any location from GIS data for the whole country.
Esprey et al. (2004), as part of this project, carried out a comprehensive sensitivity analysis of 3-PG,
based on E. grandis data from 31 sites, using a methodology similar to that used by Battaglia and Sands
(1998a). They used stand volume as the output to assess the effects of a range of variations imposed on
various parameter values. They also examined the sensitivity of the model to site initialisation data and
to site and climatic factors.
The 3-PG Process-Based Model
267

--- Page 280 ---

At the conclusion of his report, Dye (2005) stated that:
a software tool is (now) available to assist the non-scientist in understanding and predicting the effects of
site and climatic factors in constraining rates of tree growth and water use. Sim-A-Tree (3-PG) has been
designed to remove the tedium of sourcing and entering the necessary inputs to the model, and to display
the model results in tables and figures that are easily evaluated. Its ease of use is a particularly valuable
feature, which distinguishes it from the great majority of other forestry models available world-wide.
Different scenarios (e.g. varying soil characteristics, climatic conditions and stand properties) may be easily
compared, and will in time contribute to a far deeper understanding of how trees grow and transpire over
time. There is great potential for further improving the utility of Sim-A-Tree to provide additional
information of use to foresters and land managers.
This project provides an excellent example of the way a process-based model can be systematically
developed, tested and applied as a practical tool for forest management and for economic analysis of
the implications of various options and events.
d) Conifers
Much of the development of 3-PG in the original paper by Landsberg and Waring (1997) was based on
data from P. radiata grown in New Zealand and New South Wales, Australia, and their Fig. 11 is shows
the results of the first application of 3-PG to plantations – in this case also P. radiata. We have already
discussed in the section on validation (Section 9.3.3) a comprehensive application of 3-PG to P. radiata
in New Zealand by Pinjuv et al. (2006).
The effects of different planting geometries on growth of P. radiata in Chile were examined by
Rodrı´guez et al. (2002): a homogeneous planting at 1600 trees ha1, two agro-forestry plantings of 625
trees ha1 in clusters of four trees at 22 m spaced 6 m apart, and one planting of 1000 trees ha1 in
bands separated by 7 m, all interspersed with pasture. They applied 3-PG to predict volume growth of
these stands and found a remarkably high correlation between predicted and observed stem volumes
for each of these four treatments (r20.98) for 10 annual measurements from age 7 to 16 years.
Landsberg et al. (2001) applied 3-PG to loblolly pine (Pinus taeda) data from the South-East Tree
Research and Education (SETRES) fertiliserirrigation experiment in North Carolina. A parameter
set for P. taeda was established using the control (unfertilised, unirrigated) treatment and these
parameters were subsequently applied to other treatments. To simulate the effects of fertilisation,
simple changes were made in the fertility rating, and there was excellent correspondence between
simulated values of stem mass and the values obtained from field data (r2¼0.99), and good agreement
for canopy LAI (r2¼0.9). They then used the model to simulate stand growth for an entire rotation
length, and these results corresponded to those obtained with a traditional growth and yield model.
Dye (2001) used 3-PG to model growth and water use of P. patula stands growing at a number of
sites in South Africa. He developed a set of species-specific parameters for P. patula using a mix of
default values based on other species, data from direct experiments, and values that maintained critical
outputs within reasonable ranges. He demonstrated that with these parameters, 3-PG predicted
growth, water use and soil water content at a four different sites, commenting that the overall
performance of the model was encouraging. He concluded that:
the difficulties experienced in finding useful data sets has highlighted the critical need for more detailed
