descriptions of forest stands. In addition to regular measurements of diameter, height and tree density, key
physiological and site data describing LAI, rates of litterfall, the timing of stress, soil available water
capacity. . .are needed.
He also emphasised the potential of satellite observations for providing spatial estimates of above-
ground biomass, leaf area index and the occurrence of drought stress.
268
Chapter 9

--- Page 281 ---

Landsberg et al. (2005) tested the performance of 3-PG in relation to long-term mensuration data
from two sites in Finland (three plots at one site, one at the other) where the Pinus sylvestris stands were
up to 90 years old and had been thinned at approximately 5-year intervals for the last 50 years. They
fitted the model to the data, using aC¼0.055 mol C(mol quanta)1, having derived the necessary
allometric relationships from yield tables. The analysis indicated that, under normal thinning regimes,
a range of different thinning intensities can be adequately described using a simple multiplicative
model relating the proportion of volume and foliage mass removed to the corresponding proportion of
stem numbers. Stand development in terms of basal area, volume and mean stem diameter closely
followed the measured stand characteristics in all the stands. Landsberg et al. fitted the Weibull
probability density function to the stem size distribution data from each plot for each measurement
time after thinning was carried out. Observed and fitted stem size frequencies were compared by
regression analysis. The results were good enough to encourage the use of this type of analysis in
situations where the value of the commercial product from forests is affected by the size distribution of
harvested stems. However, there are some indications from other, unpublished work, that the growth
responses to thinning frequently observed in plantations are not accurately simulated by 3-PG using
this simple approach.
Another application to Scots pine is that of Xenakis et al. (2008) who coupled 3-PG to the soil
organic matter decomposition model ICBM/2N (Andre´n and Ka¨tterer 1997). Their coupled model
includes three soil carbon and nitrogen pools, and 11 new parameters that characterise the decompo-
sition rates of the soil pools, their C:N ratios, the rate at which soil carbon is turned into humus, foliage
nitrogen concentration and labile and refractory pool efficiencies. The fertility rating now becomes an
internal variable of the model, and is defined as the ratio of the plant-available soil nitrogen and uptake
required to meet the demand for nitrogen set by the growth rate of the stand. Xenakis et al. (2008) do
not show how well predictions of the model agree with observed data, but include an assessment of the
uncertainty and sensitivity of the models parameters.
In a comparison of 3-PG and PnET-II (Aber et al. 1995), Law et al. (2000a) compared the
performance of both models in predicting CO2 and water vapour exchange by ponderosa pine
(P. ponderosa) at water limited sites in central Oregon, USA. The forest was mixed age, with 25% of
trees in each of 45- and 250-year-old cohorts, the remainder being of mixed age. The objectives were to
evaluate the models using traditional and eddy covariance techniques, determine the sensitivity of key
model parameters, and determine the seasonal importance of processes controlling carbon and water
exchange. Environmental and physiological data were available at half-hourly intervals. Although this
resolution is too fine for prediction by 3-PG, the data allowed the exploration of the ‘‘why’’ and ‘‘how’’
of observed effects.
Law et al. (2000a) conducted a sensitivity analysis of 3-PG outputs to site factors (soil water capacity
and fertility) and key species-specific parameters (canopy quantum efficiency, conductance and
optimum temperature). The results were used to assign parameter values for ponderosa pine such
that the equilibrium LAI agreed with observed LAI. Although predicted and observed NPP for the two
measurement years were in close agreement, stem biomass exceeded field estimates by a factor of 2.
This was attributed to the fact that the stand was mixed age, whereas the model runs assumed an even-
aged stand. However, as there were no root data available to balance an observed carbon budget, gross
underestimation of allocation to roots and root turnover is also a likely explanation.
Seasonal variation of the estimates of GPP and ET was also examined by Law et al. (2000a) by
comparing monthly output from 3-PG with the flux tower measurements. Although correlation
between observed and predicted GPP was reasonable (r2¼0.61), there were differences in the magni-
tude of the seasonal trend and a general underestimation of annual GPP. A similar pattern was found
for evapotranspiration. Possible contributions to these results are an underestimation in the seasonal
variation in canopy LAI, and since the canopy was open (LAI1.4), soil evaporation would have been
The 3-PG Process-Based Model
269

--- Page 282 ---

significant but was not correctly accounted for in the version of 3-PG in use at that time. However, Law
et al. also suggest the determination of water stress would be improved by the use of a multiple layer soil
water balance model.
An analysis of environmental limitations to the growth of Sitka spruce (Picea sitchensis) in Great
Britain was performed by Waring (2000). Species-specific 3-PG parameter values appropriate to Sitka
spruce were determined largely from the literature. The model was then applied to spruce grown at 12
sites from the far south of England (latitude 50050) to northern Scotland (57260), and at elevations
ranging from 20 to 430 m. A sensitivity analysis of maximum LAI and current annual volume
increment (CAI) to incoming PAR suggested that it was necessary to take effects of slope and aspect
on radiation into account. Waring then adjusted the soil water storage and fertility of each site so that
predicted CAI agreed with observed CAI, and analysed variations in the 3-PG growth modifiers to
determine the factors affecting site productivity. He concluded that variation of solar radiation with
topography accounted for about half of the variation in plantation growth, and unfavourable tem-
peratures reduced growth by only 10–15% because they occur in months when solar radiation is low.
Water stress did not appear to be a limiting factor, although site fertility was limiting at several sites.
This analysis demonstrated the power of a process-based model such as 3-PG to improve the forecasts
of plantation growth in the UK.
Several applications to Douglas fir (Pseudotsuga menzies) growing in Oregon and New Zealand are
discussed below in Section 9.4.2.
e) New Zealand Native Species
3-PG has also been used in two studies of New Zealand species. White et al. (2000) tested its use as a
tool to aid a national monitoring system to quantify changes in carbon stored in indigenous forests and
scrub vegetation. For this purpose, 3-PG was calibrated against plot data for six vegetation types across
the North and South Islands of New Zealand, which resulted in six out of 20 key parameters being
altered: maximum canopy quantum efficiency, optimal temperature for growth, stem and foliage
allometric parameters, and leaf and root turnover rates. Sensitivity analyses were carried out. White
et al. then analysed the extent to which the model can predict stem and total biomass across a range of
forest and scrub types. The results obtained for total stored biomass were very similar to those from a
national biomass survey, with the vegetation types ranked in the same order. White et al. include an
extensive discussion of potential sources of uncertainty and scope for improvement, both of the model
and sampling techniques for determining input data. They comment that ‘‘the similarity between
values of biomass obtained from 3-PG and those estimated by field techniques indicate that the model
provides a legitimate framework for regional modelling. A key benefit is the capacity to apply the
model to large areas, (which) is only possible due to the 3-PG’s generic approach to physiology and
growth that does not, in most cases, require specific information about the physiology of individual
species.’’ White et al. note that ‘‘a single set of physiological variables worked well at predicting biomass
accumulation across all forest and scrub types’’, and conclude that ‘‘while some discrepancies exist
between predicted and field values exist. . .models such as 3-PG can play a role in biomass monitoring
at regional and national scales. . .’’.
Two process-based models were used by Whitehead et al. (2002) to identify environmental variables
limiting productivity in a mature mixed forest dominated by rimu (Dacrydium cupressinum).
A biochemically based canopy photosynthesis model (Whitehead et al. 2001) was used to predict
daily NPP and transpiration for the rimu canopy, and soil and understory evaporation. These data were
then used to parameterise components of 3-PG. The model was initialised with values of stem mass,
LAI and stem number corresponding to 100-year-old rimu. Data for parameterisation of allocation,
leaf loss and root turnover were scarce, and there were no observed root mass data. 3-PG and the
parameter set were not tested against independent measurements of growth, but mensurational data
270
Chapter 9

--- Page 283 ---

were used to set limits for productivity. Growth at the site was very slow, and the fertility rating had to
be very low to simulate the observed growth. Whitehead et al. (2002) concluded that productivity was
seriously limited by nutrient availability, which also limited the establishment of the canopy, and that
effects of temperature and water stress were minor compared to those of fertility. The use of the canopy
photosynthesis model provided independent estimates of the parameters in 3-PG that determine NPP.
9.4.2. Use of the Model as an Analytical Tool
The environmental factors limiting the growth of Douglas fir (P. menzies) in five Coastal Western
Hemlock Biogeoclimatic Ecosystem Climatic zones across Vancouver Island were identified using 3-PG
by Coops et al. (2007). Stem volume predicted by 3-PG for stands varying in age from 20 to 150 years
was matched to standing wood volume calculated from yield tables. To do this, maximum available soil
water holding capacity and canopy quantum efficiency (as a surrogate for fertility) were adjusted so
that modelled growth matched the yield tables. The required soil water capacities were well within the
range reported for Vancouver Island. LAI was also predicted, and maximum LAI was linearly related to
site index and mean current annual increment, as reported by Waring and Running (1998). Coops et al.
ensured that the parameters that were varied to obtain good fits between yield-table data and 3-PG
outputs remained within the range of values reported in previous studies, but also conducted an
additional sensitivity analysis. They found that slope exposure was important: south-west facing slopes
of 30 or more on Vancouver Island receive 40% more incident radiation than similarly inclined north-
east slopes. When combined with other environmental differences associated with aspect, the model
predicted 60% more growth on south-west than on north-east slopes. The close correspondence
between the optimised parameter values and literature values, combined with the capacity of the
3-PG model to match the general mean and range of LAI over Vancouver Island, suggested that the
model was well calibrated and robust, and that the output could therefore be used for further analyses.
These illustrated the limitations on growth caused by suboptimal temperature, high vapour pressure
deficits and other factors. The study indicated that the modelling approach complements ecological
classifications, and offers the potential to identify the most favourable sites for management of other
native tree species under current and future climatic conditions.
Coops et al. (2009) used the 3-PG calibration for Douglas fir discussed above (Coops et al. 2007)
and a climate data base to map 50-year growth of the species across the Pacific North West of the
United States, assuming that this widely distributed species is a good surrogate for other evergreen
conifers. They then analysed the distribution of the four major climatic factors limiting growth, as
revealed by the model, and using almost 4000 plot records which included frequency of occurrence of
tree species across the region, applied decision-tree analysis to assess the factors critical to the presence
or absence of a large number of species, i.e. to determine what factors limited species range. This
approach includes the assumption that the presence or absence of a species at a given site is a function
of the plants’ physiological responses to climate. Coops et al. provide an interesting table of the factors
limiting the growth of six important species in the region, and a useful discussion of the potential of the
procedures they used. They note that combining information on species abundance with predictions of
stand growth meets two of the most critical data needs for sustainable forest management activities,
and that the approach has potential for species mapping and productivity studies.
In another investigation of the growth of Douglas fir, Waring et al. (2008) examined the question of
why the species grows significantly faster in New Zealand than in its native range. Using meteorological
data from forest sites in New Zealand and Oregon they made eight simulation runs for each site with
3-PG. They varied soil water storage and fertility, air temperatures, atmospheric humidity values and
the frequency of frost occurrence by 20% of the observed values that led to the observed growth rates.
These simulations led to the conclusion that the much higher yields recorded in New Zealand can
The 3-PG Process-Based Model
271

--- Page 284 ---

(mainly) be attributed to lower temperature extremes during the summer growing season. The higher
summer temperatures in Oregon result higher humidity deficits, which cause reduction in stomatal
conductance, and hence photosynthesis.
Another study that combined spatial estimates of forest productivity and the use of 3-PG and 3-PGS
as an analytical tool was reported by Nightingale et al. (2008a,b). This was concerned with the
productivity and response to disturbance of tropical rainforests in north Queensland, Australia,
where the combination of high topographic, edaphic and climatic variability results in a very complex
mosaic of vegetation types. Nightingale and her co-workers had available data on stand stem number,
basal area, above-ground biomass, litterfall and species range from 37 plots (but not all were available
from all plots), as well as soil and monthly climate surfaces in a GIS for the region. They developed a
‘‘reasonable’’ 3-PG parameter set on the basis of discussions with local rainforest scientists, as well as
through the literature, and calibrated 3-PG against 12 of the 37 plots, using the appropriate climate and
soil data for each location. They also carried out a sensitivity analysis. The complete parameter set is in
the first paper. Model performance was validated by simulating the characteristics of the 25 plots that
were not used in the calibrations. The results were remarkably good. In the second part of the study
they used satellite data from the Advanced Very High Resolution Radiometer (AVHRR) to estimate the
LAI for a number of regions within the overall study area to carry out scenario analyses to assess forest
growth and the dynamics of carbon accumulation over the region. They evaluated old growth forest
areas, the impacts of cyclones and of commercial tree plantations.
Flores and Allen (2004) carried out a study in Chile which was a combination of analytical and
wide-scale investigations. They used 3-PG to provide estimates of stem wood production for fully
stocked radiata pine plantations located in several regions in Chile. The model was also used to explore
the effects of climate and soil water storage capacity on potential productivity by examining the time
course and geographic distribution of environmental modifiers, specifically soil water and vapour
pressure deficit, on potential productivity.
9.4.3. Spatial Applications
A series of studies by Nicholas Coops and various co-workers shows how the 3-PG model can be used
to simulate forest growth and productivity over large areas. Many of these studies have been made
using 3-PGS (see Section 9.1.5), the version of 3-PG modified by Coops et al. (1998a,b) so that specific,
remotely sensed data can be used as inputs.
The model is now widely used as tool for spatial analysis and, in this mode – whether it is driven by
satellite data, or in association with soil and climate layers in a GIS – we would not necessarily expect
the growth and yield predicted at any particular location to be very accurate. Soil properties, such as
water holding characteristics and fertility, are highly variable spatially, and there will almost always be
significant variation within the individual regions (polygons) assumed by the GIS to be spatially
homogenous. These, with differences in microclimate resulting from topography, will cause variation
in the growth patterns of trees. (See Chapter 10 of Landsberg and Gower (1997) for discussion of GIS,
data layers and remote sensing; also Chapter 10 of Waring and Running (2008) for an overview and
examples of the uses to which these technologies are being put in forest eco-physiology.) No matter
how accurately a model is calibrated, the results it produces are unlikely to correspond precisely to
reality at a point on the ground. Nevertheless, if we are confident that the model represents the first-
order responses of a system, the results should, for the average conditions in polygons, provide
statistically accurate predictions of productivity in those polygons.
Some of the applications discussed above involved spatial elements; here, we discuss a few specific
spatial applications.
272
Chapter 9

--- Page 285 ---

a) A ‘‘Proof of Concept’’ Application
Coops et al. (1998a) used 3-PGS to compare predictions of above-ground NPP for forest sites in
Australia and New Zealand with values for NPP derived from standard forest inventory data collected
by forest managers. Their analysis was limited to a single year, and restricted to comparisons of NPP.
The biomass partitioning procedures in 3-PG were not used, and litterfall, root turnover and self-
thinning routines were not implemented. The fraction of PAR absorbed by the forest canopies was
estimated from a satellite-derived reflectance index – the NDVI (see Section 9.1.5). Coops et al.
obtained monthly estimates of NDVI for nine regions, each including stands of reasonably homoge-
neous forest of at least 88 km – approximately the resolution of the satellite datasets (NOAA
AVHRR) used for the analysis. They used long-term inventory data from government agencies or
private firms in New South Wales, Victoria, Tasmania and New Zealand. Representative plots with a
large number of field measurements were chosen where the terrain was relatively uniform, centred
within the 88 km areas. These included pine and eucalyptus plantations, and native eucalyptus
forests. Weather data were averaged over each of the 88 km cells comprising the study sites, using a
surface fitting package to extrapolate from the nearest meteorological measurement stations. Radiation
was estimated using a procedure based on Bristow and Campbell (1984) and subsequently improved by
Coops et al. (2000). For three sites where solar radiation was measured the correspondence between
measured and estimated values was good (r2¼0.9–0.97). The soil fertility and water holding capacity
required as input data for 3-PG were obtained from national soil series surveys and on-site assessment.
Predictions of total NPP were corrected for the biomass that was assumed to have been allocated
below-ground, using the functions in the full 3-PG model. Above-ground biomass was then corrected
for foliage and branches, and converted to equivalent stem volumes, and hence into MAI values for the
year in question, using the best available information about wood density for the species at each site.
The values obtained using 3-PGS were compared to MAI values obtained from inventory data. The
relationship between predicted and estimated NPP (from measurements) was generally good, with an
overall error of 20%. Biases in the relationship indicated that the MAI estimated by 3-PGS were
slightly higher in low production areas and slightly lower in high production areas than those estimated
from field data.
This study provided a useful ‘‘proof of concept’’, demonstrating that a process-based model, driven
almost entirely by available monthly weather data and satellite-derived images of changing greenness
across landscapes, can be used to obtain reasonable estimates of the productive capacity of a region.
The opportunity therefore exists to include decades of data and evaluate longer-term implications of
climatic variation and other factors affecting forest productivity.
b) Comparison with BIOME-BGC
In another study by Coops et al. (2001), 3-PGS was compared with BIOME-BGC (Running and Hunt
1993), a complex eco-physiological model using daily time steps, at 18 sites in the Siskiyou Mountains
of southwestern Oregon. The sites ranged in elevation from 550 to 2135 m, and had varying slopes and
aspects. Plot-based information was available from each site on species composition, site productivity,
phenology, and seasonal trends in plant water relations. Long-term averages of minimum and
maximum temperature and precipitation were extrapolated from local meteorological stations and
converted into estimates of solar radiation, daytime vapour pressure deficit, and frequency of sub-
freezing temperatures at each sites. State-wide soil survey data were interpreted to estimate soil water
holding capacity and fertility. Satellite-derived data were used to drive 3-PGS and to validate predic-
tions of leaf area by BIOME-BGC.
The two models gave highly correlated, generally very similar, annual estimates of NPP, but 3-PGS
tended to give higher values when NPP is low. This study provided useful insights into the implications
The 3-PG Process-Based Model
273

--- Page 286 ---

of the differences between the models – different time steps (monthly vs. daily), the assumption of a
constant NPP/GPP ratio in 3-PGS whereas values are calculated in BIOME-BGC, the annual course of
NPP, the effects of using data from different satellites, and the effects of soil water holding capacity and
fertility. In particular, the authors provide a discussion on the relative advantages and disadvantages of
daily and monthly time steps.
c) GIS Applications Using 3PG-Spatial
The performance of 3PG-Spatial (see Section 9.1.5) was assessed in two studies by Tickle et al.
(2001a,b) in an application to 50,000 ha of native eucalypt forest in south-east Australia. The study
area largely comprised gently undulating plateau topography falling off into deeply incised valleys and
escarpments with tall eucalypt forests containing Eucalyptus delegatensis (called alpine ash), E. dalrym-
pleana (mountain gum) and E. radiata (narrow-leaved peppermint). The stands varied in age from 16
to 87 years. These studies were thus an application of 3-PG to mixed forests, rather than to
monocultures.
Both studies used spatial surfaces of mean monthly rainfall and maximum and minimum tempera-
tures, with radiation and temperature surfaces corrected for slope, aspect and terrain shadowing effects.
Actual monthly weather data were used from 1970. A detailed soil survey was carried out, using
GIS, digital elevation maps and spatial statistics in a new, quantitative approach to soil and land survey.
A spatial model was used for soil water holding capacity. The amount of phosphorus in the soil to one
metre depth was scaled to values between 0 and 1 to provide the fertility index for the model. The cell size
for the spatial data sets was 2525 m. Biomass allometric ratios were determined for the species in the
study area. The model was run for 100 years up to 1998. 3PG-Spatial was calibrated to the study area
using data from 8 or 24 permanent plots in the study area, using a best-fit set of allometric data.
In the first study (Tickle et al. 2001a) 3PG-Spatial was calibrated using data from eight plots.
Observed stem biomass data were used to determine the parameters in the allometric relationship
between stem mass and DBH. Maximum light-use efficiency and the foliage allometric parameters
were then varied to optimise the fit of predicted DBH to observed DBH, while ensuring that predicted
canopy LAI never exceeded 3. The resulting parameter values were then used in an application of 3PG-
Spatial to the remaining 16 plots across the area. There were strong relationships between predicted and
measured standing stem volume; relationships between predicted and measured DBH and stem
populations were not as strong, although statistically highly significant. The objective of the project
was to produce realistic estimates of potential biomass production for every 0.625 ha cell of the study
area and this was achieved for stand volume using the digital elevation model, soil and climate surfaces.
In the second study (Tickle et al. 2001b), predictions by 3PG-Spatial from all 24 plots for standing
volume, mean DBH and stem number were compared with predictions of the same variables using
two conventional, empirical growth and yield models, and with observed values derived from plot data.
3PG-Spatial gave results slightly better than those obtained with a conventional model that had been
specifically calibrated for these stands: it explained 86, 59 and 89% of the observed variance in standing
volume, DBH and stem number, respectively, whereas the conventional model explained 84, 59 and
78% in the same variables. The second empirical model was intended to be generic but did not perform
well against the observed values, predicting only 6% of the observed variance in stem volume.
The authors acknowledge that there were no wide-scale data available to validate the mapped stand
volume estimates but comment that ‘‘the results presented in this study indicate that it is possible to
use the 3PG-Spatial model to make local-scale predictions of forest growth using high spatial resolu-
tion layers of monthly climatic and soil data and calibration parameters based on measurements from a
small number of forest inventory plots’’.
274
Chapter 9

--- Page 287 ---

d) Other Applications
Bugg et al. (2006) used 3-PG in both point and spatial mode, calibrated against plot data from 30 sites
across central New South Wales, to investigate whether the use of long-term mean climatic data to
model growth across a region adequately represented the effects of climate variability on tree growth in
low rainfall regions. Three scenarios were used to represent different management options: managed
timber plantations, plantations for farm forestry and other purposes, and natural regeneration of
native vegetation. The study showed that long-term climate means do not always provide a realistic
picture of variation in tree growth, as had been found by Mummery and Battaglia (2004). It also
indicated that plantations with longer rotations are less affected by climate variability than short
rotation plantations – a result which is intuitively obvious, but worth reiterating based on data.
Almeida et al. (2009) used calibrations and information from earlier applications of 3-PG to study
Eucalyptus plantation productivity in Brazil (see Almeida et al. 2004a,b) in a GIS with layers for climate,
soil, planting date and clonal type to evaluate the effects of drought on eucalypt productivity across the
same estates. They showed that VPD and available soil moisture accounted for most of the variation in
yields. Across large areas, clonal differences were less convincingly simulated than at the plot level.
There have been several studies at country-wide level using 3-PGS. In one of these, Nole´ et al. (2009)
coupled 3-PGS with a model of soil respiration to predict both gross primary production and net
ecosystem production. They compared monthly and annual output at five sites across Italy with
monthly and annual measurements made by eddy covariance. Analysis of the model predictions in
relation to the measurements, together with a sensitivity analysis, gave the authors confidence in the
model and allowed them to establish a 30-year average meteorological grid across Italy and a map of net
primary production of Italian forests, based on a remotely sensed, land cover classification. Potithep
et al. (2009) calibrated the model against biomass data from two study sites in Japan, and then used
10-day composite values from the MODIS (Moderate Resolution Imaging Radiometer) satellite sensor
to derive vegetation indices which provided values for LAI. These were validated against measurements
that included fish-eye photos and litter collections. Potithep et al. then used 3-PGS with data from
satellite imagery to produce maps of monthly GPP, from May to October, for the island of Hokkaido.
9.5 POSSIBLE IMPROVEMENTS
The above review of 3-PG and its applications has demonstrated that the model accounts for the first-
order processes that determine the growth of forests. It can be calibrated and easily applied to many
forest types and can account for a high proportion of the variation in growth and forest productivity,
not only of even-aged forests and plantations, but also mixed-species forests (Coops et al. 1998a, 2009;
Nightingale et al. 2008a,b). It has also been rigorously tested against flux measurements, and the
monthly dynamics of LAI, NPP and ET were found to be consistent with measurements (Law et al.
2000a,b; Siqueira et al. 2006). Nevertheless, the model could be improved, although we note that it is
highly desirable that any improvements should not make it significantly more complex, nor lead to the
need for additional parameter values that may not be easy to determine.
Some of the changes that were made by Sands and Landsberg (2002) improved the descriptive
power of the model although, except in the case of the temperature modifier, they did not alter the
physical or physiological basis of the calculations that determine biomass production and the effects on
it of environmental conditions. The equations that describe the age dependence of branch and bark
fractions, and the time dependence of litterfall and specific leaf area, are empirical. Equations for stem
height and volume, based on standard mensuration relationships with stem volume and basal area,
have also been introduced (see Section 9.2.8).
The 3-PG Process-Based Model
275

--- Page 288 ---

9.5.1. Light Interception
At the fundamental biophysical level, the use of Beer’s law to describe the radiation absorbed by the
canopy has always been recognised as an oversimplification, particularly in relation to heterogeneous
canopies – such as those of young plantations – and canopies where there is significant foliage
clumping. For a model such as 3-PG, with its monthly time step, there is no benefit to be gained by
using a complex light interception model that may be significantly more accurate than Beer’s law over
short intervals. However, improvement in the estimates of light interception by canopies may be
achieved using a simplified version of the sunshade model (see Section 5.1.b). This will require a
relationship between direct (fdir) and diffuse (fdiff) radiation (MJ m2), such as that presented by
Coops et al. (2000) for Eugene, Oregon. However, such relationships are likely to be unique for any
particular area. If monthly values of fdir and fdiff are substituted for Idir and Idiff in Eqs. (5.13) and
(5.14), these equations can then give an estimate of total absorbed radiation (fabs) for the month.
The procedure in 3-PG is to multiply fabs by canopy quantum efficiency (aC) to calculate the carbon
fixed by the canopy. It is tempting to suggest that this procedure could be improved by introducing a
canopy photosynthesis model based on equations that describe leaf photosynthesis and integrating this
with the light interception model. de Pury and Farquhar (1997), Chen et al. (1999) and Zhou et al.
(2006) all used the two-stream model with the Farquhar and von Caemmerer equations (see Sec-
tion 3.1.4) for this purpose. Chen et al. tested their model for large area applications. Sands (1995b; see
Section 5.2.4) produced a separate (daily time step) model using a non-rectangular hyperbolic light
response with three parameters. He assumed optimal distribution of nitrogen through the canopy, and
also made photosynthesis rates dependent on temperature. Medlyn et al. (2003) used the two-stream
model, but with a rectangular hyperbolic photosynthetic light-response equation, and also assumed
nitrogen optimally distributed through the canopy.
The use of a complex photosynthesis model requires specification of the parameter values in the
photosynthesis equations. These are subject to considerable variation and uncertainty, particularly in
poorly defined conditions, such as the canopies of commercial stands. There is no guarantee that
introducing more parameter values and greater complexity into the 3-PG NPP sub-model would
improve its accuracy in any particular situation. The simpler canopy photosynthesis models of Sands
and of Medlyn et al. may be worth evaluating as options for the 3-PG model.
The real value of detailed canopy photosynthesis models, in relation to 3-PG, lies in using them to
analyse carbon fixation by stands for which there is a great deal of information about leaf area, leaf area
distribution, and leaf N-content. Raulier et al. (1999) presented a useful exercise of this type for sugar
maple, comparing several models of canopy photosynthesis against measurements of leaf gas exchange,
leaf area and light interception. Such exercises, for particular species and well-described stands, can be
used to generate accurate values of aC. This would provide data that can be used to analyse variations in
aC and e under different conditions, leading to improvements in the estimates used for the wider-scale
and longer-term analyses for which 3-PG is well suited.
9.5.2. Open Canopies
Light interception in 3-PG does allow for partial canopy closure of young stands. Currently, the
variable bgc, which is the fractional ground cover of the stand, increases uniformly with stand age to
bgc¼1 at some age at which the canopy is deemed not to be closed. However, it does not allow for
canopies to open at later ages, either through pruning, thinning, extensive litterfall, or declining
allocation to foliage. An alternative approach would be to introduce a critical leaf area index Lgc
below which the canopy is deemed to be partially closed, and define bgc as an increasing function of
276
Chapter 9

--- Page 289 ---

L/Lgc that is unity for L/Lgc1. This procedure is clearly approximate, but is very simple to implement
and parameterise.
Conceptually at least the above procedure could be improved by using a single-tree light-intercep-
tion model based on some estimate of tree height and a standard crown shape. Alternatively, Duursma
and Ma¨kela¨ (2007) provide simple equations for light interception by isolated trees for which crown
surface area can be calculated. Their treatment can also be applied to non-homogeneous stands, which
could include mixed species (see also Bartelink 1998). Whether either of these options would contrib-
ute significantly to the overall accuracy of the simulations made with 3-PG is doubtful; introducing one
of them would only be justified if the objective was to study in detail the growth patterns of young trees.
In this case 3-PG, in its present form, would probably not be the right model to use.
9.5.3. Edge Effects
In its standard form 3-PG implicitly assumes the canopy is homogeneous. However, it should be
configured to operate for block plantings, and we offer some thoughts on this here. A very simple
approach is for total production and water usage to be reduced in proportion to the fraction of the
ground area occupied by trees. However, edge effects on biomass production, and on water availability
and usage, should be considered.
The discussion of Section 5.4.2 provides a simple approach to account for possible increased
production by edge trees, which can be determined using Eq. (5.38). This approach should be
quantified and tested. Also, the summary model of Duursma and Ma¨kela¨ (2007) may have some
relevance.
The other effects that need to be taken into account are an edge effect on evapotranspiration by the
canopy, and bare soil evaporation. Various factors potentially contribute to edge effects on evapotrans-
piration from trees. Proximity to the forest border seems to have little effect on rainfall interception
losses, probably because both canopy storage capacity and rate of evaporation increase near the edge
and the two effects cancel each other (Klaassen et al. 1996; Herbst et al. 2007). Sap-flow observations in
plantation grown E. globulus (Taylor et al. 2001) showed that average daily water use per unit sapwood
area was 2.9 m3 m2 for edge trees but only 1.9 for interior trees. Taylor et al. also concluded that water
availability was partially responsible for the differences between interior and edge trees, and that greater
exposure of edge trees to wind enhanced their transpiration. In a study of the spatial variability of
transpiration in relation to distance from the edge of a mixed deciduous forest, Herbst et al. (2007)
found transpiration was around 350 mm in the interior of the forest as compared to 565 near the edge,
while potential evaporation was around 570 in both cases. They also concluded that edge effects on
water use became negligible only when the area exceeded 100 ha.
A possible approach to modelling the edge effects due to increases in available soil water can be
based on the volume of soil explored by roots. For interior trees, the region of soil available to roots is
determined by the ground area occupied by a tree, and hence by tree spacing. The roots of an edge tree
will explore a much large region outside the planting, but just how large is difficult to predict. It is likely
that the influence will decline exponentially with increasing distance form the tree; Crosbie et al. (2008)
offered a method for determining the area a tree belt occupies for hydrologic purposes.
An estimate of the enhanced production due to edge trees could be based on some weighted average
of production determined using a stand-based model assuming interior trees are part of a homoge-
neous planting at their actual spacing, while edge trees are part of a homogeneous planting at a larger
spacing. The weights applied to these estimates must reflect the relative number of edge and interior
trees. A sensitivity analysis with respect to the assumed spacing of edge trees should be made.
The 3-PG Process-Based Model
277

--- Page 290 ---

9.5.4. Growth Modifiers
No comprehensive analysis has been made of the manner in which the various growth modifiers in
3-PG are applied to canopy quantum efficiency and conductance. Most are applied multiplicatively,
but only the minimum of the soil water and VPD modifiers is used, and to date there has been no
strong evidence that this procedure should be changed. However, the way some modifiers are defined
in terms of their associated environmental factors is worth discussing.
a) Temperature-Dependent Growth Modifiers
The effects of temperature are currently taken into account through two modifiers: fT, being a function
of the average daily temperature; and fF, being a function of the number of frosty days in the month.
We now question whether it would be better to base the modifiers on the average maximum (Tx) and
minimum (Tn) temperatures: that based on Tx would be similar to the current fT but with the cardinal
temperatures simply increased, while that based on Tn would be unity for Tn above some threshold, but
decline to 0 at some critical Tn.
Our reasoning for this is threefold: first, photosynthesis occurs in daylight hours and peaks when
irradiance is high; hence Tx is a better measure of the temperatures experienced during photosynthesis.
Second, it is not just the number of frosts that affect photosynthesis but also their severity; hence a
modifier based on Tn could better account for both intense and frequent frosts. Finally, if two sites
experience the same average temperature but very different diurnal ranges we would expect them to
respond differently to temperatures because the one with the higher diurnal range would have very
different daytime temperatures.
b) VPD-Dependent Growth Modifier
By default, the VPD-dependent modifier fD declines exponentially with increasing vapour pressure
deficit D. This choice was based on fits of the observed dependence of stomatal conductance on D from
numerous studies, e.g. Dye and Olbrich (1993), Leuning (1995). Many studies also suggest that
conductance is insensitive to D for D less than about 1 kPa, e.g. Waring and Franklin (1979), Wang
and Kelloma¨ki (1997) and Oren et al. (1999). However, a closer examination of some studies that do
not report a threshold suggests that an envelope curve should have been drawn around the data, rather
than fitting an empirical function. The envelope curve suggests no sensitivity of stomatal conductance
to D less than about 1 kPa. Accordingly we suggest the use of a VPD-based modifier that shows low
sensitivity for small vapour pressures should be considered.
c) Age-Dependent Growth Modifier
The age-dependent growth modifier fage was used by Landsberg and Waring (1997) to account for
observed age-related declines in productivity. They recognised – as we noted in Chapter 7 – that it is
not age per se that causes this decline, and used arguments based on hydraulic conductivity (see
Section 7.3.1), and the results of Mencuccini and Grace (1996) to justify their use of an empirical
expression, in terms of relative stand age, to account explicitly for the reduction in maximum stomatal
conductance as stands age. In the original study on age-related decline in growth, Ryan and Yoder
(1997) showed that older trees were morphologically and physiologically different from younger trees.
They proposed the hydraulic limitation hypothesis: that the growth decline was caused by stomata
closing in order to avoid xylem embolisms arising from the loss of hydraulic conductivity due to
increasing path length from roots to crown of taller trees. Ryan et al. (2006) revisited this hypothesis
and concluded that any limit to height growth does not appear to be related to the age-related decline
in wood production, and suggest that growth may slow in response to some ‘‘sink limitation’’.
278
Chapter 9

--- Page 291 ---

Magnani et al. (2000) proposed that it was a shift in allocation from foliage to conducting tissue, and
in particular to fine roots, to compensate for the loss of conductivity due to increased height that was
driving the loss of productivity. The biomass allocation algorithm used in 3-PG is based on the
observed allometric structure of trees, and hence should reproduce this shift in allocation. Indeed,
the productivity of stands simulated by 3-PG does decline as trees age. Given also that it is too easy to
use fage to ‘‘explain’’ unknown effects, or to ‘‘fudge’’ a fit to observed data, we caution that the age-
dependent growth modifier should be regarded with suspicion and interpreted with care.
9.5.5. Biomass Allocation
We recognise the empirical nature of the carbon allocation scheme in 3-PG (i.e. Eqs. (9.11)–(9.13)) but
see no prospect, at least in the short term, of a more mechanistic scheme that would be as robust. The
validity of the 3-PG approach is illustrated by the performance of the model in general, and by its
adoption by others (e.g. Peng et al. 2002 in TRIPLEX and Sampson and Ceulemans 1999 in
SECRETS_3PG).
A significant omission in 3-PG is the ability to predict both fine- and coarse-root biomass pools. We
demonstrate in Section 4.5.1 the existence of allometric relationships between both stem and coarse-
root biomass and DBH, and show how these lead to the simple equations (Eqs. (5.42) and (9.1)) that
determine the biomass allocation ratios to foliage, stem and fine and coarse roots, given the ratio pFR of
foliage to fine-root allocation. We also postulate that pFR expresses the functional balance of foliage and
roots, driven by site fertility and soil water status. Santantonio (1989) presented strong relationships
between fine root and foliage mass in P. radiata in New Zealand on good and poor sites, with much
higher fine root production on the poorer sites. He also identified strong relationships between dry
matter partitioning to fine roots and stems. There were no clear relationships between dry matter
partitioning to fine roots and foliage. It therefore seems a useful exercise to develop this approach
further by determining a suitable functional form for pFR in terms of site fertility and available soil
water, and use the ideas presented to disaggregate root biomass into fine- and coarse-root pools (e.g. to
determine the soil volume explored by roots for water and nutrients; see Section 9.5.5). Root turnover
would then be primarily confined to the fine-root pool, under the assumption that coarse roots are
structural. Because fine-root turnover is quite high and pFR will vary with site conditions, so too will the
fine-root biomass. If these variations prove too fast, then pFS would be defined in terms of some
temporal running-average of soil water content.
Observed biomass data for both fine and coarse roots will be needed to parameterise the generalised
model. Data for total roots are hard enough to come by, although Almeida et al. (2004a) demonstrated
that both fine- and coarse-root data can be obtained. We also note the insights into soil respiration and
the allocation of carbon to fine roots, provided by the pathfinding work of Ho¨gberg et al. (2001, 2008)
(Section 5.5.2), which have to be taken into account in any attempt to model fine root growth.
It is well known that tree spacing affects stand structure and form. This was reflected in the finding
in Section 4.3.3e that stem number directly affects the allometric relationships between biomass pools
and DBH. Accordingly, greater structural realism would be obtained with 3-PG if stem number was
included in all allometric relationships, at least in analyses of the growth patterns of plantations and
even-aged monocultures, In Section 9.5.7, we argue that this would also go some way towards resolving
inadequacies in 3-PG’s response to thinning in plantations (Section 9.2.8). As we showed in
Section 4.3.3e it is straightforward to parameterise the allometric relationships from biomass harvest
data from a spacing trial. However, the generality of the relationships shown there, in particular of the
allometric powers of spacing, remains to be established.
The 3-PG Process-Based Model
279

--- Page 292 ---

9.5.6. Water Balance
The water balance sub-model in 3-PG is probably its most criticised component, mainly on the
grounds that it uses monthly rainfall data and a monthly time step, and hence effectively assumes
that all rainfall occurs in one event at the beginning of the month. This is obviously not the case, and
the point was explicitly recognised by Landsberg and Waring (1997) who commented that ‘‘The
problem could be solved by using daily precipitation data and water balances, but this would be a
move back to high data demands and increased complexity. . .’’. It also does not explicitly take into
account evaporation from bare soil in open canopies. However, except when canopy LAI was low, the
model nonetheless gave (perhaps surprisingly) good predictions of monthly water usage and soil water
content.
In the version of 3-PG described in this chapter, we divided the month into a number of periods,
given by the number of rainy days, and assumed rain fell at the start of each of these periods. This is
clearly more realistic, but still assumes that the events are equally spaced and have the same amount of
rain. We also modified the way canopy conductance was determined (see Eq. (9.17)) to take into
account the finding by Kelliher et al. (1995) that bulked conductance, representing the soil surface and
the canopy, was non-zero when LAI was low. These two improvements go a long way towards resolving
the problems arising from the lack of a daily time step for water balance, and from not explicitly
modelling soil evaporation.
A further criticism is that the model is a simple ‘‘tipping bucket’’, single-layered model, and ignores
root distribution. It would be possible to write a multi-layered model, but this not only introduces the
need for reliable data on soil physical properties and how they change with depth, but also the need for
good information on root distribution with depth. Such data and information are only likely to be
available from intensively studied sites, and introducing the need for them into 3-PG cannot be
justified.
Finally, the use of the water content of the entire soil profile to determine the soil water-dependent
growth modifier fy has, rightly, been criticised as unrealistic because it is only the water status of that
volume of soil actually occupied by fine-roots that is sensed by the plant, and from which water is
transpired. This could be resolved if we could estimate average fine-root mass and the volume of soil
explored by fine roots. The water balance should then be restricted to this volume of soil, and the
available soil water in this volume used to determine fy. Since fine-root mass is responsive to site
conditions, the volume of the root zone will also vary dynamically. Changes in the water content of the
root zone would have to take into account both recharge by water moving into the root zone along the
hydraulic gradient arising from transpiration, and through the root zone extending into regions of soil
with a higher soil water content. However, even if the data needed to test such ideas were available, they
would introduce considerable complications and many new parameters and much research would be
required before their introduction into the practical version of 3-PG could be justified.
9.5.7. Site Fertility
The use of the poorly defined fertility rating (FR) in 3-PG is a relatively crude procedure to account for
differences in soil fertility. There is a good case to be made for coupling 3-PG to a soundly based model
of nitrogen availability such as CENTURY (Parton et al. 1988; Section 6.3.1) or SNAP (Paul et al. 2002;
Section 6.3.2) and making FR dependent on N-mineralisation.
The coupling of the growth model CENW (Kirschbaum 1999; see also Section 8.2.2d) to CENTURY
was accomplished by including a soluble nitrogen pool in the plant, litter pools on the soil surface, and
organic matter and nitrogen pools in the soil. Litter decomposition and nitrogen mineralisation rates
280
Chapter 9

--- Page 293 ---

were determined by environmental factors, and nitrogen uptake by the maximum amount of nitrogen
that can be stored in the plant. As discussed in Section 9.4.1d, Xenakis et al. (2008) coupled 3-PG to the
soil carbon balance model ICBM/2N (Andre´n and Ka¨tterer 1997). Both of these approaches introduce
a need for data on various forms of soil carbon, soil C:N ratios and the composition of soil surface litter,
and add significantly to the complexity of the model. The resulting soil-based carbon and nitrogen sub-
model must be parameterised and tested at the soil level, and many of its parameters are likely to be soil
texture dependent. Accordingly, we believe the success and feasibility of including a detailed soil carbon
and nitrogen sub-model into 3-PG is still an open question, and worthy of further study.
In the interim, we believe the simpler approach of analysing the dependence of the current fertility
rating on various soil properties determined from observation pits and conventional soil chemical
analyses, interpreted by experts, will serve to provide values of FR (see earlier discussion on Fontes et al.
2006). For wide-scale applications, where the source of information about soil fertility (and water
holding characteristics) is soil maps, this approach is likely to be more useful than an attempt to model
the dynamics of nutrient availability and uptake. The twin-plot approach used by Stape et al. (2006)
also provides an excellent basis for calibration and deciding on FR values.
9.5.8. Thinning and Pruning
It is commonly observed following pruning that net photosynthesis increases and the pattern of canopy
development changes in favour of foliage growth (e.g. Pinkard et al. 1998a,b; Medhurst et al. 2003).
Similarly, it is commonly observed following thinning that canopy development is enhanced through
branch elongation and an increased biomass allocation to foliage, and that net photosynthesis increases
(e.g. Peterson et al. 1997; Medhurst and Beadle 2001; Han et al. 2006).
The assumption currently incorporated in 3-PG is that when foliage or whole trees are removed
from a stand, the only modifications to growth are those that result from the corresponding changes to
LAI, and consequently in radiation interception and transpiration. This is inconsistent with the
observations summarised in the preceding paragraph. In particular, there is no explicit enhancement
of light-use efficiency (e), nor of allocation of biomass to foliage and stems, both of which may be a
significant effect of thinning.
We noted in Section 9.5.4 the possibility of including spacing in the allometric relationships
determining biomass allocation. If this were done then a decrease in stem numbers would increase
allocation to foliage, which is consistent with the observed responses to thinning in fast-growing
plantations. But we see no explicit way to increase e as a direct response to the intervention. However, if
a thinned or pruned site is water stressed, the decrease in canopy LAI will lead to a decrease in
transpiration which might reduce the water stress and hence increase the soil water-based growth
modifier, which leads to an increase in e.
9.6 CONCLUDING REMARKS
In summary, a simple model such as 3-PG could clearly be made more complicated by incorporating
increasingly complex descriptions of various facets of the growth of trees and their environmental
interactions, This may result in greater accuracy, i.e. a better correspondence between simulation
results and reality. But every increase in complexity requires more empirical information to provide
parameter values or other data. Therefore, the prospective value of improvements has to be weighed
against the likely availability of the information needed to quantify those improvements, and the gains
The 3-PG Process-Based Model
281

--- Page 294 ---

in accuracy that might be expected from them. They also have to be weighed against the likely
availability, and probable quality, of test data. There is no point or justification in producing a
model of greater complexity that purports to describe accurately many of the processes involved in
forest growth, if the data available to test that model relate to the performance of the whole system
rather than at the level of these processes. Similarly, there is little justification for developing an
immensely detailed sub-model – say for the central process of canopy light interception and carbon
fixation – in a model with a monthly time step and only crude procedures for estimating nutrient
uptake and a very approximate estimate of respiration. Recall our assertion in Section 9.3.1, that
parameter values associated with a processes should, wherever possible, be based on independent
measurements of that processes, and at the appropriate spatial and time scale.
As we noted in Chapter 8, the purpose for which models are required should be clear, otherwise it is
very likely that the degree of complexity will not be appropriate to the purpose for which the model is
intended. It is also important to retain balance. Detailed, multi-parameter models are research tools
that may be used to calibrate and test simpler models. As a general principle the introduction of greater
site- and species-specific complexity reduces the generality of models, although it may increase their
accuracy in particular applications.
Many of the potential improvements to 3-PG discussed in this section are simple enough to warrant
further investigation. However, while they do have potential benefits, the use of a multilayer soil water
balance model, or of a root zone for determining soil water stress, or a detailed soil carbon and nitrogen
sub-model for modelling dynamic changes in site fertility would add significantly to the complexity of
the model and of its data requirements.
282
Chapter 9

--- Page 295 ---

CHAPTER 10
Future Developments
Contents
10.1 Measurement and Instrumentation
284
10.2 Remote Sensing
285
10.3 Meta-Analyses
286
10.4 Respiration
286
10.5 Stomatal Control and Hydraulic Limitation
287
10.6 Soil Fertility
288
10.7 Models
289
10.8 Concluding Remarks
290
It is not possible to review all the recent literature in a book of this type, nor would it necessarily be
useful to do so. Most research papers and reports provide new information within established
boundaries (the current paradigm), while relatively few break new ground and contribute new
knowledge and understanding. One of the tasks of the authors of a text such as this is to summarise
the ‘‘state of play’’ - to identify and describe the areas of established and generally accepted knowledge
and to illustrate them by reference to relevant published material. This we have tried to do through the
previous eight chapters (discounting Chapter 1). The other task, which we undertake in this Chapter, is
to try to identify the areas of the field (forest ecophysiology and modelling, in this case) that require, or
are likely to reward, particular attention from the research community concerned with this field. These
may include areas where knowledge and understanding are limited, and where the problems derive
from lack of scientific understanding, as well as areas where scientific understanding may be quite good
but where there are clear opportunities for practical applications that have not yet been developed.
Before proceeding, it is probably worthwhile to re-state a principle that links science and practical-
ity. Science is concerned with understanding the way natural systems function, and in the biological
and ecological fields it is concerned with understanding the mechanisms that drive processes and the
way systems respond to changing external conditions and stimuli. On the basis of that understanding
we hope to be able to predict the behaviour and responses of systems, at the levels of interest, and to
manage that behaviour. Management involves making decisions and taking actions, with the intention
of achieving some specified result or goal. The decisions taken are more likely to lead to the desired
results, or achievement of the goal, if they are based on sound knowledge about the (eco)system being
managed and the way it will respond to change (Landsberg and Gower 1997). In relation to tree
Physiological Ecology of Forest Production
Joe Landsberg and Peter Sands
ISSN 1936-7961, DOI: 10.1016/S1936-7961(11)04010-3
Copyright # 2011 Elsevier Inc.
All rights reserved.

--- Page 296 ---

physiology, it is possible that knowledge of the factors that determine tree growth and the way trees
respond to change may be useful in breeding for improved plantation species, and that process-based
models will be used to explore the likely implications of various selection criteria and strategies. There
is not much sign of this as yet, but it should happen. In relation to forest ecosystems, we are concerned
to predict responses to changes such as rising atmospheric CO2 concentration as well as to manage-
ment actions such as thinning and fertilisation.
In the remainder of this Chapter we outline areas where we think there is need for particular
research focus because our scientific understanding of processes is inadequate, and the areas where we
see exciting opportunities for the application of available knowledge to the business of predicting the
behaviour and responses of forest ecosystems.
10.1 MEASUREMENT AND INSTRUMENTATION
We have not been concerned with the process and techniques of measurement, but we note the (rather
obvious) fact that most advances in our knowledge derive from, or are at least strongly associated with,
the development of improved measurement techniques. These are usually more accurate, more
sensitive, more amenable to automatic recording and are frequently easier to replicate. Until about
the 1970s or 1980s it was common for scientists who wished to study ecophysiology to have to develop
and build the instruments they needed. These included the net radiometers, sensitive anemometers and
fast-response temperature sensors needed for micrometeorological measurements, followed later by
eddy correlation equipment; various methods of measuring the light climate in canopies; pressure
chambers for measuring plant water potential; and porometers and gas exchange equipment, including
soil chambers for respiration measurements. All these are now manufactured commercially, benefiting
not only from the skills of the original developers but also from the ever-increasing power of computer
control and recording systems. Many of the advances that have come about in ecophysiology in recent
years owe a great deal to our improved ability to gather large amounts of data, frequently over periods
of weeks or months, so that the behaviour of the systems under study and their responses to changing
conditions can be thoroughly documented and analysed.
Recent advances in instrumentation and measurement techniques include constantly improving,
virtually fully automated, off-the-shelf units for measuring leaf photosynthesis, dark respiration rates
and conductance at a range of easily controlled temperatures, light intensities and CO2 concentrations.
These have led to an avalanche of measurements on a wide range of plants in all sorts of conditions. The
technology behind these leaf chambers has been modified and extended to soil (surface) respiration
measurements.
Equipment for measuring eddy covariance of CO2 and water vapour concentrations is also com-
mercially available, supported by software that performs most of the calculations necessary to obtain
estimates of the fluxes to and from the underlying surfaces. But there are still significant uncertainties
and frequently large errors in these values, and considerable care and expertise are still required in
processing and interpreting them. Oren et al. (2006) and Baldocchi (2008) provide valuable discussion
of these matters.
The free air carbon dioxide experiments (FACE) that are being carried out at various sites round the
world (Italy; Belgium; Wisconsin, USA; North Carolina, USA; and Australia) involve the long-term
‘‘fertilisation’’ of stands of trees with CO2, with the aim of raising the ambient concentrations over long
periods, to about double the current normal. They are extremely expensive and involve large, multi-
disciplinary teams. It is undoubtedly from this type of research that definitive answers will emerge to
the questions about long-term responses to rising atmospheric CO2 and its effects on carbon fixation
284
Chapter 10

--- Page 297 ---

and forest productivity (see, for example Ainsworth and Long 2005; Norby et al. 2005). Significant
questions about the interactions between rising CO2 and nutrition also require research attention;
much of this has come, so far, in the form of modelling studies (e.g. Medlyn et al. 2000; McMurtrie
et al. 2001), but see Oren et al. (2001).
In the area of water relations, there has been significant progress in sap flux measurement - again,
the required instruments are becoming commercially available - so that the flow of water up stems can
now be routinely measured in the field and related to the leaf area of the trees and the evaporating
power of the atmosphere. Combining such measurements with measurements of stomatal conductance
and plant water potential, as is frequently done, provides ever-improving information on the role of
stomata as regulators of plant water potential and on the hydraulic limits to leaf water supply (Oren
et al. 1999; Ewers and Oren 2000; Sperry et al. 2002; Ewers et al. 2007). We expect to see this approach
extended to an increasing range of species and conditions in the coming years.
10.2 REMOTE SENSING
Earth observing satellites have been used for mapping purposes since the 1970s. However, in recent
years remote sensing technology has become increasingly important as the basis for modelling the
productivity of ecosystems and in particular for determining the spatial variation of the inputs to such
models. In an outline of developments in this area, Waring et al. (2010) said:
Measurements made by remote sensing can characterize the leaf area density and nitrogen/chlorophyll
content of forest canopies, as well as maximum photosynthetic capacity and above-ground structure and
biomass. Combining these with climate data estimated from relationships based on temperature measure-
ments and using an appropriate process-based model, it is possible to calculate, with useful accuracy, carbon
sequestration and wood production by different forest types covering large land areas.
Satellites cannot provide information about soil fertility or rainfall, although sequential measures of
canopy greenness and surface temperatures can provide indications of soil water holding capacity.
LIDAR (laser detection and ranging) and radar, particularly when used in conjunction (Treuhaft et al.
2004), offer the possibility of wide-scale estimates of forest biomass which, if made sequentially, will
allow testing of models.
Granted the increasing importance of remote sensing as a tool for the study of forests - and indeed
all forms of (vegetative) land cover - and their productivity, we note here the importance of combing
satellite and eddy-flux data to test and improve models of terrestrial carbon fluxes, from forests and all
other land cover types. Running (2008) noted that there remains ‘‘distressing disagreement between
land models attempting to simulate future atmospheric CO2 with interactive terrestrial carbon
balances’’. The models he was referring to are linked to global climate models, so the whole system is
extremely complex, involving not only the climate simulation models but also information across the
globe about land use and land cover. Running discussed the need to expand the tower-based flux
measurements to all biomes and climates to improve and expand the information available on
terrestrial carbon flux. It is also important that at some sites there should, periodically, be intensive
field programmes and measurement campaigns in which detailed ecophysiological measurements are
made of processes such as respiration and stand water relations, in close association with flux
measurements, remote sensing and modelling. An outstanding example of this approach was the
NASA-supported BOREAS program (see BOREAS Special Issue of the Journal of Geophysical Research,
vol. 120, Dec. 1997) carried out in the Canadian boreal zone in the 1990s. There are other less
ambitious examples that contribute usefully to the developing body of information about
Future Developments
285

--- Page 298 ---

ecosystem–atmosphere interactions. Fluxes from forests are significant contributors to the global
picture, but of course are only one component of it. However, information about those fluxes and
the factors that cause them to vary is immensely valuable to those who make decisions about the
management of forests.
10.3 META-ANALYSES
The increasing use of meta-analyses is a useful recent development made possible by computers and
communications technology. These allow large amounts of data to be collected, manipulated and
analysed with reasonable ease. As the literature grows ever more voluminous and difficult to keep up
with, these meta-analyses, particularly when they involve contributions from large numbers of people
so that many ‘‘raw’’ data sets are made available, will become increasingly important as the primary
reference sources for developments in particular areas of science. Examples that we have referred to in
earlier chapters are the analyses undertaken by Medlyn et al. (1999, 2002a,b) dealing with the
biochemical model of photosynthesis and the variation in its parameter values as seen in many
experimental and observational programmes. These papers were followed by data collation and
analyses of the same model and the variation in its central parameters by Kattge and Knorr (2007)
and Kattge et al. (2009). Medlyn et al.’s and Kattge et al.’s results provide an invaluable resource for
those concerned with models of canopy photosynthesis, and explore the effects of temperature, foliage
N concentrations and rising atmospheric CO2 on carbon fixation. High-quality research will always
provide additional, valuable information, but it is arguable that there is not a pressing need for new
measurements of photosynthesis at the leaf-level in trees. The variation within any ecosystem will
generally be considerably greater than the errors incurred in any simulation by using parameter values
obtainable from analyses such as these.
Another meta-analysis that we have referred to in this book is the collation by Law et al. (2002) of
results from a large number of FLUXNET sites, where CO2 and water vapour exchanges are being
measured byeddy correlation frominstrumenttowers over grassland, tundraand forest (see Section 2.5.4).
As the data from these sites accumulate, their usefulness increases steadily. No two seasons are identical,
either in terms of weather or the condition of the underlying vegetation, and the accumulation of
information allows first-order effects and trends to be identified, as well as provides ever-more reliable
values of net ecosystem production for different biomes and ecosystems. Baldocchi’s (2008) synthesis
illustrates some of this, and also demonstrates that comprehensive literature reviews and progress
assessment by an expert with an intimate knowledge of the field, in both a technical and an operational
sense, remain immensely valuable.
10.4 RESPIRATION
In terms of priorities for research attention, among the physiological processes governing forest
growth, it seems clear that basic work on respiration will remain important for the foreseeable future.
We noted in Section 5.5.2 that various authors have argued that respiration provides the energy to drive
a wide range of processes underpinning plant growth. Accepting this, it follows that studies on respira-
tion should focus on its role in processes such as phloem loading, growth and the maintenance of
tissues. At the basic process level, the view of Cannell and Thornley (2000) that respiration depends on
substrate concentrations clearly requires further investigation. But it is also important that the body of
empirical data on respiration rates of various plant tissues, in relation to temperature and the
286
Chapter 10

--- Page 299 ---

concentrations of nutrients they contain, should continue to grow. The outstanding exemplar of this
approach is Mike Ryan (Ryan 1990; Ryan et al. 1994a,b, 1995, 1996, 1997b; Lavigne and Ryan 1997).
Litton et al. (2007) surveyed reports of experiments in forest ecosystems where total above- and below-
ground carbon fluxes were documented. They found (inter alia) that, in general, total above- and
below-ground fluxes were tightly correlated; autotrophic respiration is strongly related to production;
there are indications in the data that maintenance processes are linked to metabolic processes; and
there are indications that partitioning to respiration is constant across a wide range of GPP in forest
ecosystems and does not vary with resource availability, competition or stand age.
We also noted, in Section 5.5.2, the path-finding work of Ho¨gberg et al. (2001, 2008) which showed
that the flux of current assimilates to roots is a key driver of soil respiration. The implication of their
work is that soil respiratory losses are closely coupled to the flux of photosynthates from the canopy,
i.e. the seasonal pattern of below-ground CO2-allocation appears to be more important than soil
temperature in determining root respiration. This work has considerable implications for stand
modelling and carbon allocation, and developments in these areas will be dependent on studies on
other species and locations. Mike Ryan (personal communication) now routinely monitors soil
respiration with open-system soil respiration chambers. We can expect that combining results from
these measurements with canopy photosynthesis measurements and/or modelling will provide new
data and insights into carbon allocation patterns and below-ground processes.
The NPP/GPP ratio (carbon use efficiency), currently taken as a constant (Y0.47) in the 3-PG
model with its monthly time step, is, strictly speaking, certainly not constant (see Section 5.5.3; and
also Litton et al. 2007). It depends on stand respiration rates, but attempts to calculate these over
intervals as short as days, based on (probably uncertain) information about the size, substrate
concentrations, internal nitrogen concentrations and changes in temperature of various components
of the stand, are likely to result in errors even larger than those that might be incurred by the
assumption of constant NPP/GPP. We look forward to increasing amounts of empirical information
on respiration rates, and to future meta-analyses that lead to general and statistically strong relation-
ships - between dynamic variables such as carbon fluxes, component masses, temperature and
respiration rates - that can then be used in stand level and regional models.
The improved understanding that we can expect in these areas will undoubtedly improve our ability
to model stand growth and productivity.
10.5 STOMATAL CONTROL AND HYDRAULIC LIMITATION
The importance of stomata, the interface between plants and the atmospheric environment, has long
been recognised. Good values of stomatal and canopy conductance are central to calculations of tree
and stand water use (see Section 2.5.3), as well as to calculations of canopy photosynthesis. A prerequi-
site for the calculation of canopy conductance is a good estimate of leaf area index. At the leaf level,
stomatal responses to vapour pressure deficit and ambient CO2 concentrations are reasonably well
understood and can be described quantitatively (see Section 3.2), but responses to leaf water potential
have proved more intractable.
There has been progress in this area based on the recognition that stomata must act as the regulators
of plant water potential and maintain it at values that can be sustained by the conducting system of the
trees, i.e. at values high enough to avoid catastrophic cavitation (Oren et al. 1999; Sperry 2000). The
recognition that stomata will close when the plant cannot sustain the rate of water loss driven by
vapour pressure deficit has indicated that relationships between stomatal conductance and vapour
pressure deficit must be influenced by the capacity (conductivity) of the plant hydraulic system.
Future Developments
287

--- Page 300 ---

Stomata generally appear insensitive to leaf water potential until some threshold value is reached,
which depends on the hydraulic characteristics of the soil–root–stem–petiole system and the ratio of
root–stem conductivity to leaf area (see Section 7.4, Eqs. (7.29) and (7.31); also Oren et al. 1999; Ewers
and Oren 2000; Ewers et al. 2001). Tyree (1988) provided evidence that the branch system offers the
major resistance to flow through trees. This finding was strongly supported by Waring and Silvester
(1994), who tested the hypothesis that branches of varying length and exposure would exhibit
consistent differences in photosynthesis and stomatal conductance on a clear day. They provided
evidence that differences in branch hydraulic conductance play a major role in explaining observed
differences in photosynthetic rates and stomatal conductance in the foliage at the distal end of the
branches (see also Ewers and Oren 2000).
Ryan and Yoder (1997) postulated that as trees grow taller, stomatal conductance is reduced by
increased resistance to water flow through the stems (the hydraulic limitation hypothesis). Ryan et al.
(2006) collated and assessed the evidence relating to this hypothesis and concluded that it holds for
many - but not all - trees, and that where hydraulic limitation occurs it lowers assimilation rate,
stomatal conductance and tree water use in older, taller trees. Oren et al. (1999) and Ewers et al. (2007)
established that stomatal responses to vapour pressure deficit are proportional to the magnitude of
stomatal conductance for vapour pressure deficits  1 kPa. The relationship is described by Eq. (3.20),
and analysis using a simple hydraulic model indicated that this value of m is consistent with the role of
stomata in regulating both evaporation from leaves and leaf water potential. These findings provided
the basis for an analysis by Novick et al. (2009) of published conductance measurements obtained from
forest species occurring in environments that ranged from boreal to tropical. They showed that the
ratios of sapwood conducting area to leaf area provide a useful surrogate for stem hydraulic conduc-
tivity and were able to establish a generic relationship for diverse species between measurable features
of hydraulic architecture and canopy conductance. It is important, from the point of view of modelling
at stand and regional levels, that this relationship is investigated further.
10.6 SOIL FERTILITY
Soil fertility, and how to describe it in quantitative terms that can be used to define and describe
nutrient availability to plants, remains an area of prime importance in relation to ecophysiology and
tree/stand growth modelling. The CENTURY model (Section 6.3.1; Parton et al. 1988) is widely used
and well tested and is based on sound principles of organic matter decomposition. However, it requires
careful parameterisation and significant amounts of information about litter pools to provide reliable
values for the N concentration in soil solutions. SNAP (Section 6.3.2; Paul et al. 2002) offers a useful
empirical approach to the estimation of nitrogen mineralisation rates, and undoubtedly warrants
further development under a wide range of forest types and in a variety of different environments.
Without information about the amount of N likely to be in the soil solution, elegant calculations of
nitrogen uptake rates (Section 6.2.1) will be of limited value in attempts to model the process at stand
level in relation to soil conditions at sites of interest, which can at best usually only be described by
conventional chemical analysis.
We noted, in Section 6.2.1, that ‘‘Since most nutrient uptake takes place from soil solution, it follows
that uptake will be greatly reduced as soil dries’’. It follows from this that attempts to model the effects
