of soil nutrient status on forest growth should include the soil water content in the root zone, possibly
in the simplest case as a modifier constraining nutrient uptake. SNAP includes a soil wetness parameter
in its mineralisation calculations, and it would make physical sense to include a similar term in nutrient
uptake calculations. The first requirements would be some estimate of the amount of nutrient in the
soil - presumably from conventional soil chemical analyses - and the water content of the root zone.
288
Chapter 10

--- Page 301 ---

From this and the physical characteristics of the soil, the hydraulic conductivity (Section 7.3.3) can be
calculated, and its average value over the time period of interest could be used as the nutrient uptake
modifier. The PCATS model (Smethurst 2004) uses an ‘‘average root’’ approach and the principles of
mass flow and diffusion with a daily time step. There are many possible ways in which nutrient uptake
by tree root systems could be described mathematically and it would not be helpful to present poorly
developed ideas in any detail here.
Another area that warrants attention in the field of nutrition and nutrient uptake is concerned with
the qualitative differences between the effects on plants of different forms of nitrogen (see Section 6.2.1),
although we acknowledge that this is a refinement that may not be of significant concern for some time
to come. A paper by Nordin et al. (2001) provides an example of progress in this area. These authors
refer to a number of other studies on the uptake of different forms of N and on the role of mycorrhizal
fungi in this process. From the point of view of production forestry the most important questions in
relation to nutrition remain:

Is the rate of nutrient supply at a site adequate to ensure high and sustained yields?

To what extent will the impact of forest management practices adversely affect subsequent nutrient
supplies and growth?

What returns, in the form of increased growth rate and greater final yield, might be expected from
the application of a given quantity of fertiliser?

Is the rate of nutrient supply commensurate with stand uptake and growth rates so that minimal
(or zero) amounts are leached into water tables or aquifers? This is an important environmental,
as well as economic, consideration.

Which nutrients need to be applied, in what form, when and how?
In most regions the question about economic returns on fertilisation will be virtually unanswerable
unless there have been empirical fertilisation experiments on the same soil type under the same
management and climate regimes. Even if there have been, they may not provide more than a general
guide, particularly in areas of unreliable rainfall. Even in agriculture, after over 100 years of research on
nutrition, it is not generally possible to make accurate predictions of the likely responses of crops to
fertilisation.
10.7 MODELS
We have used models - in the form of mathematical descriptions of processes - throughout this book.
This is because we believe that the attempt to describe biophysical processes in precise and quantitative
terms is one of the best ways of focussing questions about how systems work, and of ensuring that
descriptions of the processes are quantitatively reasonable. We emphasised in Chapter 8 that the
purposes for which models are developed should be clear from the outset. They will tend to take
different forms if the objective is to provide a framework for assessing current knowledge, and
exploring the implications of gaps in knowledge, or if the objective is to produce a practical tool that
may combine empiricism and simulations of processes. The levels at which models are written may
vary from cells to ecosystems, with commensurate variation in the time steps from seconds to months
or years. As a general principle it is not advisable to try to model a system across more than two levels of
organisation.
At the level of practical forest stand modelling, we endorse the comment made by Robinson and Ek
(2003) that it is reasonable to extract components from disparate forest growth and dynamics
modelling traditions, join them together in some way, and expect to be able to make defensible
Future Developments
289

--- Page 302 ---

predictions. If the predictions are found not to be reasonable there must be a serious problem
(or problems) with the components used. Another principle we have stated at various points is
Ockham’s Razor, synonymous with the principle of paucity: the simplest formulation of a model,
with the smallest number of parameters, is probably the best. It is certainly likely to be the best in terms
of testability.
10.8 CONCLUDING REMARKS
In conclusion, we restate an assertion we have already made in several forms: there is no point or
justification in producing increasingly complex models if the data needed to test them are not available.
The business of forest ecophysiology involves careful experiments and tedious, repetitive and accurate
measurements at levels that range from cells and organs to landscapes. The data emerging from this
research will be used to test and further develop models that cover appropriate ecophysiological levels,
which allow analysis of the implications of the data and heuristic assessment of their implications. The
knowledge emerging will always be, to some extent, uncertain, but will provide continually improving
understanding and a continually improving basis for decision-making about forests and evaluation of
their role and importance in global ecophysiological interactions.
290

APPENDIX 1 DETERMINING SOLAR DIRECTION AND RADIATION
Many models of canopy photosynthetic production integrate photosynthetic rate spatially in the
canopy and over an extended period of time, for example a day. These models therefore require the
direction and intensity of the direct beam radiation and the intensity of the diffuse radiation as a
function of time and day of year. We outlined in Section 2.2.1d how direct beam irradiance and
insolation could be determined in the absence of direct observations. In this Appendix, we present
detailed equations that can be used to determine the direction of direct beam radiation, and the
intensity of direct and diffuse radiation at any time.
Irradiance at any time of day is calculated by first determining the extra-terrestrial irradiance, then
the solar direction at that time, and the transmittance of the beam through the atmosphere. Transmit-
tance is the fraction of the incident energy that is absorbed or scattered along the beam path, and
irradiance at a point on the earth’s surface is the product of extra-terrestrial irradiance and
transmittance.
A comprehensive discussion of solar radiation and its determination is available in books such as
Iqbal (1983).
A1.1. Solar Direction
An understanding of how the position of the sun varies through the day is necessary to understand the
radiation environment at a site. Here, we present only the simpler forms of various formula, ignoring
issues such as the equation of time and atmospheric refraction. However, the formulae given are
291

--- Page 304 ---

accurate enough for physiological calculations, and fully capture the seasonal and diurnal effects of
interest.
a) Path of the Sun Through the Sky
The seasonal changes in the daily path of the sun through the sky are caused by the annual revolution of
the earth in its orbit and by its daily rotation. The effect of these changes is in turn determined by the
site’s place on earth, in particular its latitude l, and the inclination O (23.45) of the earth’s axis of
rotation to the plane of its orbit.
The angle the line joining the centres of the earth and the sun makes with the equatorial plane is
called the solar declination dS. The declination is needed to determine day length and the sun’s zenith
angle, and varies seasonally according to the formula
dS ¼ arcsin 0:4sin 2p dn  82
365




;
ðA1:1Þ
where dn is the day number, or day of the year starting with dn¼1 on January 1st, and dS is in radians.
(The argument x of all trigonometric functions such as sin(x) and cos(x) is an angle expressed in
radians. To convert from radians to degrees, multiply by 180/p.)
When discussing the position of the sun in the context of a growing plant it is sufficient to express
time of day relative to solar noon, that is when the sun is at its highest point in the sky. In this context,
time of day t (hours) is measured by the hour angle o of the sun, where
o ¼ p
12 ðt  12Þ
ðin radiansÞ:
ðA1:2Þ
The hour angle is the angle between the observer’s and the solar meridians, and is negative in the
morning, zero at noon and advances 15 (¼p/12 radians) every hour.
The position of the sun relative to a horizontal surface is expressed by two angles. The first is the
solar azimuth ’S, being the angle from the observer’s meridian to the sun’s meridian, measured in the
horizontal plane. The second is either the zenith angle yS, being the angle between the zenith (the point
directly overhead) and a line from the observer to the sun, or the solar elevation aS, being the angular
elevation of the sun above the horizon. The common sign convention has ’S zero in the south and
positive to the east, and yS and aS positive above the horizon. Ignoring refraction of the sun’s rays by the
atmosphere, these angles are given by
sinaS ¼ cosyS ¼ sindS sinl þ cosdS cosl coso
cos’S ¼ ð sinaS sinl  sindSÞ= cosaS cosl
ðA1:3Þ
(from Iqbal 1983). The position of the sun thus depends on the latitude of the observer (l), the time of
year (through the declination dS), and the time of day (through the hour angle o).
b) Solar Transit, Day Length, and Direction of Sunrise and Sunset
The highest point in the path of the sun across the sky occurs when the sun transits the local meridian
at solar noon. The hour angle is then 0, and the first of Eqs. (A1.3) shows that the maximum solar
elevation aSx in degrees is
aSx ¼ 90  l  dS

Sunrise and sunset occur when the solar elevation is zero. The times at which this occurs can be

hd (hours) is then given by

A1.2. Extra-Terrestrial Radiation
Because the earth’s orbit is elliptical, and hence its distance from the sun is not constant, the extra-
terrestrial irradiance IS0, that is solar irradiance outside the earth’s atmosphere measured normal to the
direction of the sun, varies from a high of about 1412 W m2 in early January to a low of about
1321 W m2 in early July. The simple formula
IS0 ¼ ISC 1 þ 0:033 cosð2pdn=365Þ
ð
Þ
ðA1:7Þ
(Iqbal 1983) gives an excellent approximation to IS0 on any day of the year, where dn is the day and the
solar constant ISC¼13671.6 W m2. The extra-terrestrial irradiance IS on an inclined plane is then
given by the Lambert-cosine law, that is
IS ¼ IS0 cosyS;
ðA1:8Þ
where yS is the angle between the normal to the plane and the direction of the sun. In particular, yS is
the solar azimuth angle.
By integrating IS over daylight hours, the above results can be combined to estimate the daily
insolation Qex (MJ m2 d1) on any day of the year exterior to the earth’s atmosphere, or at any site on
the earth’s surface but ignoring atmospheric absorption and scattering. First, note that the hour angle
oS for sunrise or sunset is given by oS¼(p/24)hd, where hd is the day length on the day in question, and
yS is given as a function of time (i.e. hour angle) through Eq. (A1.3). Integration of IS given by
Eq. (A1.8) over daylight hours then gives

0.0864¼246060/106 converts W m2 to MJ m2 d1. Because of absorption, etc. in the atmo-
sphere, insolation Q0 on the earth’s surface is less than Qex.

A1.3. Transmittance
Transmittance is a measure of the loss of energy due to scattering and absorption of light by molecules,
aerosols and dust in the atmosphere. It is determined by the path of the radiation through the air, and
depends on the length of that path and the properties and constituents of the atmosphere. Transmit-
tance is a multiplicative property, that is if atmospheric factors A and B separately reduce transmission
of a beam by tA and tB, together they reduce transmission of that beam by tAtB.
As both direct beam and diffuse radiation are derived from the direct radiation from the sun, the
irradiances Ib of the direct beam, Id of the diffuse beam, and total irradiance It¼IbþId, all measured in
a horizontal plane, are proportional to the irradiance IS0 of the radiation from the sun external to the
earth’s atmosphere (and also in a horizontal plane). Thus
Ib ¼ tbIS0
Id ¼ tdIS0
It ¼ ttIS0;
ðA1:10Þ
where tb, td and tt¼tbþtd are respectively the direct beam, diffuse beam and total atmospheric
transmittance.
a) Optical Air Mass
Determination of transmittance is accomplished using the concept of optical air mass. This a measure
of the amount of air a beam of radiation encounters on its passage through the atmosphere. The optical
air mass depends on the detailed composition of the atmosphere and on the path of the radiation in the
atmosphere. In particular, it depends on the solar azimuth, angle as this determines path length, and on
atmospheric pressure, as this measures the mass of atmosphere above the observer. Through the
dependence on pressure optical air mass also depends on sea level atmospheric pressure and on the
altitude of the observer.
Consider first the direct beam, and assume the optical air mass along the beam is m. It can be shown
that irradiance Ib along the beam then varies in accordance with Beer’s law
Ib ¼ IS0ekm
ðA1:11Þ
where k is a parameter called the extinction coefficient and measures how effectively one unit of air
mass attenuates direct beam irradiance.
b) Direct Beam Transmittance
Comparison of Eqs. (A1.10) and (A1.11) shows that direct beam transmittance and m are related by
tb¼ekm. Both m and k depend on the wavelength of the radiation, and Iqbal (1983) presents a
detailed discussion of the effects of the spectral composition of radiation, and of different molecular
constituents of the atmosphere. For our purposes it suffices to assume m is an average air mass and k a
corresponding average extinction coefficient.
Air mass is conventionally expressed relative to the air mass m0 experienced by a beam from the
zenith to an observer at sea level through an atmosphere of standard properties (listed in Iqbal 1983).
The actual air mass is then m¼mrm0, where the relative air mass mr depends on the zenith angle of the
beam, atmospheric pressure, and deviations in the composition of the atmosphere from the standard
atmosphere. It follows from the definition tb¼ekm and m¼mrm0 that direct beam transmittance, is
294

tb ¼ tmr
0 ;
ðA1:12Þ
where t0¼e  km0 is the vertical transmittance of the standard atmosphere. Typically, t0 is in the range
0.4–0.7, or up to 0.8 for extremely clear air.
A firstapproximationfor thedependence ofrelativeairmassonzenithangleyS is simply mr¼1/cos(yS),
but this is in error for yS close to 0. Relative air mass is also proportional to P/P0 where P (kPa) is sea-level
pressureandP0¼101.3 kPaissea-levelpressureofthestandardatmosphere.Pressuredeclineswithaltitude
z (m) as e0.0001184z. These factors are discussed in Iqbal (1983). Putting these results together, the relative
optical mass is given by
mrðP; z; ySÞ ¼

ðA1:13Þ
where the expression involving the azimuth angle is an empirical expression for the effects of solar

c) Diffuse Beam Transmittance
To determine the diffuse beam irradiance, we need an expression for the diffuse transmittance td for
use in Eq. (A1.10). Liu and Jordan (1960) show a relationship between diffuse and direct beam
transmittances of clear skies such that
td ¼ 0:271  0:294tb ¼ 0:271  0:294tmr
0 :
ðA1:14Þ
Given a value for t0, Eqs. (A1.10)–(A1.8) and (A1.12)–(A1.14) can now be used to estimate direct and
diffuse beam irradiances.
d) Vertical Transmittance
The determination of the vertical transmittance t0 of the standard atmosphere requires considerably
more detailed data. This is illustrated in Iqbal (1983), who considers the separate contributions to air
mass of the various molecular species in clean dry air, water vapour, ozone, and aerosols, also scattering
by air molecules (Rayleigh scattering) and water vapour and dust (Mie scattering), and attenuation by
aerosols (Mie scattering). Various approaches to the determination of t0 have been used, ranging from
detailed numerical integrations based on realistic descriptions of the atmosphere, to regression analyses
of observed values of t0 to measured properties of the atmosphere.
As an example, the model of Bird and Hulstrom (1981) uses empirical relationships for transmit-
tance as a function of a range of properties of the atmosphere. These include measures of the total
amount of ozone and water vapour in a vertical column of the atmosphere, measures of the aerosol
turbidity at 0.5 and 0.35 mm, the ground albedo, and the aerosol forward scattering ratio. However,
these data are not necessarily easy to determine, nor are they routinely available.
A1.4. Calculating Insolation
The clear sky insolation Q0 at the earth’s surface cannot be expressed in a simple form such as
Eq. (A1.9) for extra-terrestrial insolation Qex. However, the results given above allow us in principle
to calculate total irradiance It at any time of day, and Q0 can be calculated by a numerical integration of
Appendices
295

--- Page 308 ---

total It over daylight hours. Various approaches to the determination of daily or longer-term insolation
Q under cloudy skies first calculate insolation assuming the sky is clear, for example using the equations
presented above, and then use empirical relationships to reduce the clear sky insolation to take into
account cloud cover.
In Section 2.2.1d, we discussed the A˚ ngstro¨m–Prescott approach to correct extra-terrestrial insola-
tion Qex using observed measurements of average cloud cover or hours of bright sunshine hS. These
data are themselves often not available, so Bristow and Campbell (1984) relate Q and the calculated
value of Qex by
Q ¼ t0tcQex;
ðA1:15Þ
where t0 is clear sky transmittance and tc accounts for cloud cover. They give an empirical relationship
for tc as a function of the observed diurnal temperature range DdT (i.e. maximum temperatureminimum
temperature, but with a correction factor on the first day of a wet spell) and of the monthly mean diurnal
temperature range DmT (both in C). Thus
tc ¼ 1  ebDc
dT ;
b ¼ b1eb2DmT
ðA1:16Þ
where b1, b2 and c are empirical constants. By fitting Eqs. (A1.15) and (A1.16) to observed data from
several sites in the United States, Bristow and Campbell found t0 varied with site in the range 0.7–0.77,
but b1¼0.036, b2¼0.154 and c¼2.4 independently of site. The model accounted for 70–90% of
observed variation in daily insolation.
APPENDIX 2 SOME MATHEMATICAL DETAILS OF 3-PG
This Appendix provides equations used in 3-PG that were not given in the main text. A full
mathematical description of 3-PG is included on the PHYSECOL website.

A2.2. Equations for Explicitly Age-Dependent Parameters
The explicitly age-dependent variables in 3-PG are based on the following function:
f ðt; f0; f1; tf ; nÞ ¼ f1 þ ðf0  f1Þeðln 2Þðt=tf Þn;
ðA2:10Þ
where f0 and f1 are the values of f at age 0 and for mature stands, respectively, tf is the age at which
f¼ (f0þf1)/2, and n is a constant that determines the shape of the function. If n¼1, then f(t)
exponentially approaches f1, while the function is increasingly sigmoid in shape as n increases.
The explicitly age-dependent variables in 3-PG are then given by
gNðtÞ ¼ f ðt; gN0; gN1; tgN; ngNÞ
gFðtÞ ¼ f ðt; gF0; gF1; tgF; ngFÞ
sFðtÞ ¼ f ðt; sF0; sF1; tsF; nsFÞ
pBBðtÞ¼ f ðt; pBB0; pBB1; tBB; nBBÞ
rðtÞ
¼ f ðt; r0; r1; tr; nrÞ;
ðA2:11Þ
where the various quantities following the ‘‘;’’ are potentially species-specific parameters, although
generic values suffice for many, especially the n’s. The function given by Eq. (A2.10) has a high degree of
flexibility, and appears to be well-suited to fitting 3-PG age-dependent variables to observed data.
